TITLE: Agricultural Disasters in Neolithic Times
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: With the effects of anthropogenic (i.e. human produced) climate change becoming more and more relevant and global population expected to grow to 10 billion by 2050 understanding previous agricultural disasters and relevant lessons learned will become increasingly relevant. This course will focus on agricultural disasters over the last 10,000 years. Discussions will focus on human, biotic and abiotic factors associated with these disasters and what modern civilization could learn from them. The class will meet weekly for one-hour a week (Tuesday from 10AM-11). Seminars will include lectures and group discussion. Discussions will include reflections on outside reading and lecture material and will attempt to integrate perspectives from multiple stakeholder groups including farmers and citizens of the global north and south. At the conclusion of the course students will have a greater understanding of various social, political and environmental predictors of historic agricultural disasters.
OUTCOMES: The primary goals will be to show students that since the agricultural revolution ~10,000 years ago humans have had multiple "agricultural disasters" that are associated with a variety of factors. This science/research based approach will foster both collaborative and independent knowledge discovery.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each student will give one oral power point based presentation of 10-15 minutes based on novel methods of sustainable food production. The presentation will include potential advantages and potential "unintended consequences" of that potential technology. Research methods related to their oral presentation will be discussed in class in order to train students how to search the literature and how to present data appropriately. Out of class workload: weekly reading of a scientific article will average approximately 1 hour and preparation for a final PowerPoint proposal will be approximately 5-10 hours total over the course of the quarter.
GRADING: Class will be pass/no pass with a grade of 70% or above constituting a passing grade. Each student will be graded on participation (30%) associated with weekly discussions and one oral presentation (25% for the submission of the proposed topic and 45% for the actual presentation). The presentation will discuss insights into lessons learned regarding previous agricultural disasters. Criteria for the presentations will include the ability to integrate at least 2 peer reviewed references from scientific journals into a topic related to the proposed topic.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Rob Atwill’s major research focus is on the epidemiology and medical ecology of infectious diseases potentially transferred between livestock, wildlife and humans; assessing microbial contamination of the environment by livestock and wildlife; with special emphasis on microbial contamination of surface and groundwater from watersheds. 

 

Maurice Pitesky is a faculty member at University of California Cooperative Extension (UCCE) with an appointment in poultry health and food safety epidemiology. Dr. Pitesky earned his BS in biology from UCLA, and his DVM and MPVM from UC Davis. Pitesky is also boarded in Preventative Veterinary Medicine (DACVPM). My MPVM research was focused on Greenhouse Gases and Food Production. Pitesky’s research interests are focused in three major areas: 1) Using “traditional” epidemiological techniques and GIS and spatial statistics to understand how avian diseases move in time and space. 2) Using traditional and “Next Generational” epidemiological and sequencing technologies to gain insights into Salmonella with respect to food safety and virulence 3) Gaining a better understanding of small scale poultry production with respect to environmental sustainability, poultry heath, and food safety.

TITLE: Biochemistry
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Our course will give you hands-on experience in collecting and analyzing biochemistry data in the lab. We have eight experiments that you will conduct with your lab partners. In the course of these experiments, you will learn how to pipet and use various instruments in a biochemistry lab. In addition, we will also discuss how various classes in your freshman and sophomore years apply to upper division biochemistry courses.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn how to use biochemistry equipment to obtain experimental data and how to analyze and interpret the data. Effective communication will be learned as students work with their lab partners to acquire data and discuss experimental results. We will discuss the importance of ethics in reporting scientific data. We will develop confidence in performing biochemical techniques, calculations, and analyses of experimental data. We will develop a perspective of why students take lower division math, physics, chemistry, and English in preparation for upper division biochemistry. Biochemistry is a key component for students preparing for health related fields.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will submit eight lab reports during the quarter. They will write these reports outside of lab and turn them in at the beginning of class the following week. Each lab report will include a brief discussion of what we were trying to learn that day in lab, what skills were mastered, analysis of their experimental data, and a brief discussion of what they learned from their experimental data.
GRADING: Participation in class discussions (10%), handing in eight lab reports (80%), working well in a group using lab equipment (10%). Grading is P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Ken Hilt has taught biochemistry on the Davis campus for 38 years. His courses include BIS 102, BIS 103, BIS 105, MCB 120, MCB 120L , and the freshman seminar for the last twelve years. His interests are in protein chemistry, particularly enzymes and the effect of metals at their active site. Justin Yamada is a staff research associate in Molecular and Cellular Biology. He prepares chemicals and cells for use in the upper division biochemistry and genetics courses. He has assisted Dr. Hilt in teaching the freshman seminar class for the past 7 years.

TITLE: Building Knowledge Work Systems for the AI Era
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
What does it mean to know something when AI can produce a polished answer in seconds? This seminar explores that question by putting students at the edge of a genuinely unsettled problem: in an era when machine-generated and human-generated knowledge are increasingly difficult to distinguish, what is the value of your own thinking, and how can you document it?

Students will investigate how AI is reshaping what counts as knowledge, whose work gets recognized, and why the process behind an answer is often more important than the answer itself. The course centers on weekly experiments using KNOBE, a new simple knowledge-documentation format developed at UC Davis. A KNOBE is a plain-text record that packages a student’s written response together with evidence of how that response was produced: sources consulted, reasoning steps, revisions, questions, uncertainties, and any AI tools used. In this seminar, students will use KNOBE not as a technical platform but as a research method for studying how knowledge work happens in the AI era.

AI use is neither required nor prohibited. Instead, students will learn to document their intellectual process clearly enough that others can understand, evaluate, and build on it. Come ready to test new ways of showing your work, tracing your thinking, and explaining what you know across disciplines and professional contexts.
OUTCOMES:
Students will learn to use KNOBE as a structured research method for studying AI-era knowledge work. Through weekly KNOBE Charts, students will document sources, observations, reasoning steps, uncertainties, revisions, and any AI interactions. They will treat their own learning process as research evidence, not simply as preparation for a final answer.

Students will investigate an urgent, unresolved question: how should human intellectual labor be recognized, documented, and distinguished from machine-generated output? Because norms around AI use, authorship, evidence, and process documentation are still emerging, students’ observations will contribute to a developing field of inquiry.

Students will develop process literacy: the ability to explain not only what they know, but how they came to know it. This skill is relevant across disciplines and professions, including law, medicine, journalism, education, science, policy, design, and business. Student use of KNOBE will also provide feedback to the KNOBE development effort.

Students will share selected KNOBE Charts in small groups, evaluate one another’s knowledge trails, and revise their own documentation practices over time. The final KNOBE Portfolio will serve as a cumulative record of intellectual development across ten weeks.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students complete approximately 10–20 pages of reading per week from short academic articles, journalism, and selected book chapters. Readings introduce questions about AI, knowledge work, authorship, evidence, expertise, documentation, and the changing value of human thinking. They are not graded separately; students use them for weekly KNOBE Charts, discussion, and final reflection.

Each week students submit a 400–600 word KNOBE Chart documenting not only what they think, but how that thinking was produced. Charts may include analytical responses, source trails, reasoning maps, uncertainty logs, AI-use documentation, revision notes, comparison exercises, and reflections. Weekly exercises may ask students to compare human-written and AI-generated responses, trace how a claim changes as evidence is added or challenged, identify where judgment enters a knowledge task, test how AI alters reading or writing, or revise earlier work after feedback.

Students periodically bring selected Charts to small-group peer review, where they evaluate clarity, evidence, uncertainty, AI-use documentation, and usefulness of the knowledge trail. Short low-stakes practice and revision activities build KNOBE fluency. The final project is a KNOBE Portfolio with selected Charts and a 4–5 page reflection connecting process literacy to a chosen field.
GRADING:
Weekly KNOBE Charts: 50%
Engagement, clarity of observation, reasoning, uncertainty, revisions, and AI interactions. Includes low-stakes revision activities.

KNOBE Peer Review and Knowledge-Trail Discussions: 10%
Constructive feedback and respectful discussion.

Final KNOBE Portfolio and Reflection: 20%

In-Class Participation: 20%

Grading Scale: A+ = 97–100; A = 93–96; A- = 90–92; B+ = 87–89; B = 83–86; B- = 80–82; C+ = 77–79; C = 73–76; C- = 70–72; D+ = 67–69; D = 63–66; D- = 60–62; F = below 60.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
I am a faculty member in the Sociology Department and have served on two AI steering committees at UC Davis. As a first-generation college student, I know what it means to navigate institutions whose unwritten rules are not always explained, and to do intellectual work shaped by experiences that are not always visible in the final product.

My scholarly work includes pioneering research on transnational communities, non-criminological approaches to migrant smuggling, and cognitive migration: the movement of thinking, memory, and judgment across people, tools, institutions, and imagined futures. My current research focuses on learning, knowledge work, and process literacy in the AI era, with a related project on the idea of talent. In 2016, I coined the term “Blingularity” to describe the point at which AI-generated and human-generated outputs become difficult to distinguish. I developed the KNOBE protocol, which is central to this seminar, as a practical response to that challenge: a way to make the process behind knowledge visible, traceable, and portable.

I am also a lifelong jazz pianist, and I tend to prefer structured improvisation over following scripts.

TITLE: Communicating Climate Change in the Small
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS: This seminar will explore the role of design, humanities, and social science perspectives in understanding the small-scale experiences of climate change. The course will re-focus the conversation on climate change from global-scaled environmental impacts , to one that recognizes the importance of the personal and everyday ways in which individuals experience these impacts. While many students may already understand the impacts of global environmental change (such as sea level rise, global warming, species and habitat loss, etc.), these impacts vary dramatically from region to region. It is the specific conditions within a region, city, neighborhood, and/or household that can determine whether you are vulnerable or resilient to these impacts. The presence of trees, infrastructure, transportation, or food sources can all play a role in determining a community's resilience. Access to education, health services, or a means of political participation can impact an individual's resilience. Many times, these small-scale nuances are over-looked when important decisions are made for a community's future. Despite that, small-scale interventions that support large-scale awareness can have huge impacts on behavior, policy, and technology, providing opportunities for global impact. In addition, design and humanities projects can give voice to those most vulnerable to climate change including under-represented communities within the political sphere. Thus re-scaling and re-focusing how we talk about climate change is an important effort to building resilience, and the goal of this seminar.
OUTCOMES: Students in this seminar will be introduced to the important role design and the humanities can play in addressing climate change and identify the significance of inclusion and diversification in achieving community resilience. Utilizing personal narratives, photography, drawing, installation, and/or curation, students in this course will explore new ways of communicating their personal experience of change and compare/contrast their experiences with communities around the world. This seminar will also introduce students to the allied environmental design professions and provide the preliminary skill sets associated with observing, evaluating, recording, and communicating the built environment surrounding them. Lastly, students in this seminar will develop their visual and oral communication skills through a range of presentation techniques.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students in this course will be expected to complete and discuss approximately 40 pages of course readings weekly and complete weekly digital and/or analogue sketchbook assignments. This will help students understand seminal text and case studies of humanities perspectives on climate change. As a final project, students will develop an analogue, digital or mixed-media design intervention (e.g. digital narrative, campus installation, film, etc.) expressing their personal understanding of and experience with climate change. Lastly, students will share their work with classmates and compare/contrast its content with communities around the world, presenting their work in a 10-minute oral presentation in class or publishing an online presentation of their work.
GRADING: Students will be expected to complete a weekly sketchbook and reading assignment, totaling 2 hours of work a week. In class discussions will be required to demonstrate comprehension of reading materials and share sketchbook assignments. Students will also produce a final design project towards the end of the quarter. The break-down for overall course grade is as follows: contribution of the student to in-class discussions (30%), maintenance of a personal journal/sketchbook (30%), development of a final design project (40%). Below are the expectations for earning specific grades: 100-90% (A+ to A-) - active and frequent engagement with course discussion, on-time completion of all sketchbook assignments, completion and comprehension of reading assignments, and successful completion of final project; 89-80% (B+ to B-) - engagement with course discussion, on-time completion of most sketchbook and reading assignments, on-time completion of final project; 79-70% (C+ to C-) - occasional engagement with course discussion, completion of at least two-thirds of sketchbook and reading assignments, and partial or late completion of final project. Any grade less than C- is not passing.
INSTRUCTORBIO: N. Claire Napawan is a landscape architect, urban designer, and academic who has designed and studied urban environments throughout the world for the past 11 years. She believes urban resilience requires transdisciplinary collaboration and community participation, and that designers and decision-makers need to explore new techniques for integrating urban communities with their built environments. This includes a particular focus on disadvantaged and vulnerable communities, such as urban youth. Examples of her design and research include: "Smart Sidewalks", the winning proposal for Reinventing Payphones in New York City, which seeks to address the digital divide and improve urban environmental resilience; "#OurChangingClimate", a research and design project and winner of the UC Davis Climate Action Champion Award that empowers youth to develop physical and digital exhibitions to document and share their experiences of climate change; and "FOGWASTE", a public art installation that seeks to bring greater awareness of San Jose’s vital infrastructures to community members and encourage greater urban environmental stewardship. Her projects represent award-wining proposals, commissioned by local municipalities, and/or exhibited at notable venues throughout the U.S. She is co-founder of the design collaborative Group Projects (grouprojects.com) and an associate professor in the Department of Human Ecology.

TITLE: Connecting the Local and the Global: The UN Sustainable Development Goals in Action
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
This interdisciplinary course introduces students to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) as a framework for understanding and engaging with complex global challenges. Students will examine the history, principles, and socio-political context of the SDGs while critically exploring how these goals connect with local realities and lived experiences. Central themes include identity and self-awareness, global systems thinking, glocalization, inclusion, collaboration, and the power of storytelling in advancing sustainable change.

The course is interactive and discussion-based, incorporating a range of educational experiences including guest speakers, case studies, reflective writing, small-group collaboration, and peer-to-peer presentations. Students will engage with real-world examples and practice applying systems thinking to address problems that span disciplines and borders.

Through weekly topics—from exploring "Who am I in the world?" to investigating "Who gets heard?" and “What makes collaboration work?”—students will gain both conceptual tools and practical skills for global citizenship. They will develop their ability to analyze global-local connections, work across differences, and communicate effectively about complex social and environmental issues.

By the end of the course, students will be better equipped to think critically about global development agendas, understand diverse perspectives, and contribute meaningfully to collaborative solutions in their communities and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

  1. Explain the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the 17 SDGs, identifying their global and local relevance.
  2. Analyze how diverse cultural, social, and political perspectives are necessary in addressing complex global and local challenges.
  3. Reflect critically on their own identity, cultural perspectives, and role in global systems.
  4. Identify and evaluate a local issue that has global implications and present a solution-oriented initiative connected to one or more SDGs.
ASSIGNMENTS:
In this course, students will engage in a combination of individual and collaborative assignments designed to foster critical thinking, global-local analysis, and effective communication. Weekly coursework includes approximately 20–50 pages of reading and/or 10–30 minutes of multimedia content, which will serve as the foundation for in-class discussions. To encourage active participation, students will submit weekly reflection reports of around 100 words, responding to prompts that connect course content to weekly themes. Class participation plays a central role and includes contributing to discussions, engaging in small-group activities, and providing peer feedback. Sessions may also feature informal group presentations or facilitated dialogue exercises aimed at building collaboration skills.

The course culminates in a two-part final project:

  1. Project (3–5 minutes documentation as a blog or audio/visual materials): students will work in small groups on a campus or local issue with global relevance (e.g. hunger, migration, or climate resilience) linked to the SDGs. This project will explore stakeholder perspectives and collaborative strategies.
  2. Individual Reflection (300–750 words): each student will submit a written reflection analyzing their learning journey throughout the final project, particularly how their understanding of global challenges, teamwork, and their role as a global citizen has developed.
GRADING:
This is a letter grade course. Course grading will be based on the quality of class participation (20%), weekly written reflections (30%), a final project presentation (30%), and a final self-reflection essay (20%). Students must attend 8 of the 10 class meetings. Class participation will require each student to bring questions and comments to class that are relative to the assignments. Note that there are no grade curves in First-Year Seminars.

Percentages required to earn specific letter grades: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Joanna Regulska, vice provost and dean of Global Affairs and distinguished professor of gender, sexuality and women’s studies at the University of California, Davis, has led large-scale programs that transform institutions, establish partnerships, and substantially improve global services for over 30 years. 

Regulska oversees Global Affairs at UC Davis, where she strives to provide all students with global learning experiences through a Provost’s Priority of Global Education for All; engage faculty, staff and students with the UN Sustainable Development Goals as part of UC Davis’ commitment to solving global challenges.

Regulska serves on the board of directors for NAFSA: Association of International Educators as the vice president for public policy and practice. In 2022, she was honored with the Charles Klasek Award for outstanding service to the field of international higher education from the Association of International Education Administrators. In 2021, Regulska was named an inaugural member of the National Academy for International Education. In 2019, she was honored with the first-ever Senior International Officer Award from the Institute of International Education. 

A respected scholar, Regulska’s research concentrates on women’s political activism, grassroots mobilization, decentralization, democracy and democratization, with a focus on Europe and the Caucasus.

TITLE: Demystifying and Unlocking the Beauty of Chinese Characters
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
Have you ever dreamed of learning Chinese but felt overwhelmed by its writing system? You are not alone! Chinese characters can seem mysterious and puzzled — so different from English or Spanish that you cannot guess their pronunciation or meaning just by looking. No shortcuts, no phonetic clues… just pure pictures!

But fear not — you have come to the right place.

This course is designed to demystify Chinese characters and unlocking their beauty. It provides with you a strong foundation in understanding how they work, where they come from, and what they represent. Through five rich and interconnected chapters, you will explore:

  1. Stroke, Radical, and Structure Analysis: Learn how Chinese characters are built — from basic strokes to the two-dimensional structure — and how a few specific radicals reveal meanings.
  2. The Six Principles of Character Formation: Discover the traditional framework that explains how characters are created and used, such as pictographic, indicative, semantic compound, and semantic-phonetic compound.
  3. Chinese Etymology and Historical Development: Trace the evolution of characters through dynasties and script styles, and understand how history shaped the writing system.
  4. Appreciating Calligraphy Styles: Explore the artistic side of Chinese writing — from oracle bone script to standard script — and learn to recognize the beauty in brushstrokes.
  5. Philosophy and Values Embedded in Characters: Uncover how Confucian, Daoist, and other philosophical ideas are reflected in the Chinese writing system.

By the end of this course, Chinese characters will not be mysterious — they will feel like a story, a system, and a piece of living culture.

No prior knowledge of Chinese language or culture is required to take this course. It is designed for beginners and anyone curious about Chinese characters. In order to create a supportive environment that maximizes learning, the seminar curriculum is designed with non-native speakers in mind.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

  • Describe and analyze Chinese characters: Understand basic strokes, identify character structures, and interpret meanings through radicals.

  • Classify characters using the Six Principles: Categorize Chinese characters based on the six principles of formation and usage, and explain the reasons behind this categorization.

  • Explore the historical development of Chinese scripts and their cultural contexts: Recognize major Chinese script styles and understand the historical and cultural backgrounds from which they emerged, and foster appreciation for their diversity. (Global Diversity)

  • Appreciate calligraphy and embedded philosophies: Develop an appreciation for various calligraphy styles and gain insight into the philosophies and values reflected in Chinese characters. (Global Awareness)

  • Create and present a cultural calligraphy project: Design a final project featuring Chinese calligraphy and share personal reflections on the cultural themes expressed through your work. (Global Action)
ASSIGNMENTS:
The course assignments and activities include the following three categories.

  1. Weekly Preparation
    Before each seminar, students are expected to spend 1 to 1.5 hours completing the assigned materials. This includes reading or watching the weekly theme and completing the corresponding homework assignments.
  2. In-Class Participation
    Active participation in class discussions is essential. The instructor will provide demonstrations and feedback, and students will engage in hands-on practice with Chinese script writing and experience calligraphy techniques during class sessions.
  3. Final Project
    For the final project, students will design a Chinese calligraphy product that reflects the cultural values and philosophical concepts explored throughout the course. Each student will present their design in a 3 to 5 minute presentation, explaining the creative philosophy behind their work and sharing it with their classmates. A showcase or exhibit for the final project will be scheduled to encourage cross-cultural dialogue and community building.
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

  • Class Participation and Discussion: 20%
  • Weekly Reading/Watching Assignment: 30%
  • In-class Script Writing and Calligraphy Work: 15%
  • Final Project: 35%
    • Calligraphy Product and Cultural Reflection: 20%
    • Oral Presentation: 15%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Yutian Tan is a continuing lecturer in Chinese in the Department of East Asian Languages and Cultures at UC Davis. She holds a Ph.D. in Chinese Linguistics from The Ohio State University, an M.Phil. in Linguistics from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, and a B.A. in Chinese from Peking University.

Before joining UC Davis, Dr. Tan served as a visiting instructor at the College of the Holy Cross in Worcester, Massachusetts. At UC Davis, she teaches a wide range of courses in Chinese language (beginning, intermediate, and advanced levels), linguistics, and culture.

A native speaker of both Cantonese and Mandarin, Dr. Tan also studied Japanese for two years during college and is currently learning Spanish through Duolingo. Her research interests include second language acquisition (SLA), Chinese dialectology — particularly Cantonese — and regional cultural studies. She is especially passionate about incorporating active learning strategies into her teaching and enhancing students' visibility and engagement in assessment settings.

Outside of academia, Dr. Tan enjoys ice skating with her daughter.

TITLE: Demystifying Undergraduate Research in Neuroscience
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: The seminar course is designed for students who are interested in undergraduate research in the biomedicine field and neuroscience in particular and those who plan to apply for graduate or medical schools. As a new undergraduate student, you may know little about what “real” biomedical research activities (rather than high school labs) look like in the science laboratories across UC Davis campus and its medical campus. You may also think about joining a research lab to pursue your interest and spark your curiosity toward science but don’t know how. This seminar is intended to demystify biomedical and neuroscience research and provide first-hand experience on applying for undergraduate researcher positions at UC Davis and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
Laboratory research seems puzzling and complicated to newly admitted undergraduate students. Applying for a lab position as an undergraduate student is even more intimidating for those who do not have any prior research experience or lab skills. This seminar will cover topics that are related to biomedical research (with a focus on neuroscience research) such as why you need research experience, how a research laboratory works, how you can get along with other lab members at different training levels,  what common research tools and methods are, and what types of research the Instructors are doing, among others. This seminar will also guide students though how to find a research lab, how to join a laboratory to start the research journey at UC Davis, and how to extract useful and digestible knowledge from reading scientific papers among others.  At the conclusion of this seminar course, students are expected to 1) feel confident about biomedical research through interacting with the instructors, 2) know basic steps of applying for undergraduate  research positions in a research lab, and 3) develop skills of reading and presenting scientific papers.

Students are encouraged to use the Handshake platform (https://urc.ucdavis.edu/handshake) for discovery research positions.
ASSIGNMENTS:
In the first part, the instructors will lead discussion on topics including, but not limited to, research laboratories, research hypotheses and performance, research funding and publishing, undergraduate research opportunities, steps of research a science lab prior to joining a lab, analyzing and reading research papers. The first part will be in the interactive format of questions and answers. Students are highly encouraged to raise questions and provide responses to those raised by their peers.  

The second part is science paper reading and presentation, which is an essential component as an undergraduate researcher in a science lab (equivalent to “journal club” ). Each group of students will be assigned with a research paper or choose their own paper if applicable. All students are required to study the assigned research article. Students within one group are expected to work collaboratively with their team members on researching the articles, preparing a 20-min presentation, delivering the presentation, and leading the class discussion of the presented papers.

At the conclusion of the seminar, each student is required to write a final essay (2 pages maximum) reflecting what they’ve learned from the seminar and what they want to learn if they retake the seminar course.
GRADING:
Attendance for the first class is mandatory for this seminar course. No registration is allowed after the first class.

The grading system is based on “pass/fail”. The pass grade is 70 points or above. Grades will be assessed on class attendance (20%), class engagement and discussion (20%, assessed by the quantity of Questions and Answers raised by each student), collaboration within a group (20%), group presentation (30%), and final reflection essay (10%).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Fuzheng Guo, PhD is an Associate Professor in the Department of Neurology at UC Davis School of Medicine. His research interest has been centered on glial and myelin biology/pathology in the healthy and diseased brain. Dr. Guo's laboratory is well-funded by NIH and non-federal agencies. In addition, Dr. Guo has been actively conducting services to the University and the School of Medicine including medical school admissions. 

Meina Zhu, MD, PhD is a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Neurology at UC Davis School of Medicine. Her research interest is to discover novel cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying brain development and injury with a focus on brain white matter injury.

Please visit www.guo-laboratory.org for more information.

TITLE: Exploring India Through Spices, Color & Cuisine
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: This seminar class will explore three of the most important aspects of the Indian culture—food, spices, and vibrant colors associated with traditional customs such as Rangoli and henna. This seminar will explore global diversity by introducing the students to the significance and use of specific colors within Indian culture and discuss how this reflects similarities and differences in their understanding and use of colors in their own lives. It will highlight the hidden importance of the spices which are used in our day-to-day life. Indian herbs and spices are the major ingredients of the cuisines, loaded with lists of powerful health benefits and alternative medicine to common ailments. When learning about the vibrant colors and their importance in the culture of India, students will learn about traditions such as ‘Rangoli,’ an ancient piece of art where powdered color, colored rice, flowers and other ingredients are used to decorate the entrance of the houses. Students will also learn about popular Indian cuisine. Not only will students learn about the use of these three aspects within the Indian culture, but they will gain practical knowledge during the class. It will be learning an ancient art as well as getting to know the rich culture of India through color, cuisine and spices. The last section of the seminar will let the students learn Indian cuisines like Chicken Tikka Masala, Saag Aaloo, Samosa, Jalebi etc will be discussed and recipes will be taught and shared by the instructor. The instructor will be using audio-visual aids to make the class communicative.
OUTCOMES: It is essential to learn about spices which help to improve Immunity to fight against many diseases. Students will explore complex dimensions of diversity, equity, and inclusion around the world, through the significance of Indian  cuisine and spices. The classes based on cuisine will take the students to the diverse culture of India and they will also learn some popular cuisine which will be an asset to their personal and professional lifelong experience.  Learning cuisine from India will also be a learning of ethics and respect. As food industry is one of the biggest industries all over the world, students may develop an interest which can lead them to their possible career as a food critic or a number of other renowned positions in the food market.  This seminar will be an interactive one by assigning the students role play activities and by engaging them in different listening, speaking, reading and writing activities.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Poster presentation (Based on Rangoli Art , Students will be making Rangoli in groups during the class)
  2. Power point presentation ( Based on spices and its magic, students will make a 7 minutes power point presentation in pairs, which will be presented during the class)
  3. Discussion based on reading assignments (Tasks will be given for reading assignments based on Ancient, medieval and contemporary Indian Culture from the mentioned books, 15 pages a week)

A short three to five minutes video assignment based on cuisine of their choice will be assigned to the students to shoot and upload.
GRADING:
It is a pass no pass class.

Attendance and Participation                          :  20%

2 Quizzes based on assigned reading               : 20%

Video on favorite Indian cuisine                       : 20%

Power Point Presentation                                 : 20%

Rangoli/Henna Poster                                       : 20%

 

 

The grade of pass will be awarded to students for work that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better
INSTRUCTORBIO: Shagufta Fatema, a native of Patna, Bihar, India received her PhD in Indian English Literature. The area of her research is Indian Politics and Indian Political History which is a source of her teaching as well. A former teacher of Communicative English in Maulana Azad College of Engineering and Technology in Patna, Bihar, India and Fulbright Scholar Teaching Assistant  for UC Davis’ Hindi/Urdu Program. She teaches Hindi and Urdu both at University of California, Davis under Middle East South Asia Department. She developed a passion for teaching at a young age and began her teaching career in 2008. She is an easy going person who loves nature, humanity and always tries to find pleasure in little things. She likes to spend quality time with family, friends and especially with kids. In her free time, she can be easily found in her kitchen making some Indian cuisine and experimenting baking. She loves to cook community lunch and invite people to share dishes with her. It gives her immense pleasure to satisfy someone’s hunger pangs.  With devotion towards education and passion towards teaching, she loves to be a good source of inspiration

TITLE: Exploring UC Davis: The best kept secrets
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
The goal of this seminar is to help you discover all the best resources and spots on campus that UC Davis has to offer. I often find that students learn about these resources during their last year, which is a pity. In an effort to transform the university into a place filled with fun, useful, and amazing possibilities for adventures, we will explore campus together and put together a list of all that we discover. The assignments will be a mix of field trips and class discussions, as well as group and individual activities where you’ll report back on your findings. My goal is to make you knowledgeable and comfortable in this large, beautiful campus.

The first five weeks of the quarter will be dedicated to field trips across campus, and you’ll be asked to explore the places individually and post the findings in discussion boards.  You will also be asked to explore your own campus resources and fun spots and to report back in discussion boards. Then, during the second half of the quarter, you will work in groups exploring campus resources and presenting your findings to the class. Be prepared to go explore all over campus for this seminar!

By going out of our way to explore all the secret corners of campus, we build habits of curiosity and inquiry, as well as a sense of community as we share these findings with others. We also establish a different relationship with the university as scholars and human beings in a place dedicated to our success.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop effective communication skills, 
  2. Develop high cognitive skills 
  3. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy, 
  4. Develop focus and depth in one or more discipline, 
  5. Develop leadership skills, 
  6. Develop a global perspective, and 
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Discussion board posts: Every week, you will post your individual findings and discoveries either of the places we’ll visit together or of places that you visit on your own. You will respond to your peers adding comments to their posts. Each discussion board will have specific guidelines for you to follow. 

Class discussion: Be prepared to share your findings of each weekly resource you explore and to answer your peers' questions about them. You need to come prepared with information about the resources (where to find it, what it does, etc.) and with a description of your experience (why you liked it, why you think it's important, etc.)

Group presentation: You will form groups and go explore one resource thoroughly. You will take pictures/videos, talk to employees, practice some activity related to the resource you're investigating, etc. Then, you will put together slides for a 20–30 minutes presentation to the rest of the class, as well as answer your peers' questions. Share the responsibilities in your group! 

For discussion boards and the group presentation, you’ll read up on the resource (official website, LocalWiki, Reddit, etc.). Visiting and reading about the resource will not exceed 4 hours a week.
GRADING:
The class will be graded pass/no pass. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

To obtain a Pass for this class, you will complete 8 out of the 10 discussion boards and at least half of the responses to your peers' posts. You will also participate in your group's presentation and share your findings in an engaging manner. You must participate in at least 8 of our class meetings (which means you may miss two meetings). Check Assignments for details on participation.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a lecturer for the University Writing Program. She teaches upper-division writing classes. She is interested in finding ways to bring inner awareness and peace to her students and hopes that this seminar will help her achieve a better understanding on some of these ways.

TITLE: From Dice to Pixels and Podcasts: Role Playing Games (RPGs), Writing, and Storytelling
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: This course explores the intersections of gaming and writing from Dungeons and Dragons to World of Warcraft and beyond. We consider how the rise of RPG podcasts (e.g., Critical Role, Venture Maidens, The Adventure Zone, and College Humor's Dimension 20) has impacted in-game storytelling. Based on our interactions with different gaming systems, we will discuss how writing organizes the interactive narrative structures of adventures, world building, and game rules. The seminar will consider how elements of narrative adventures (e.g., plot, character, setting) work in different Role Playing Games (RPGs) ranging from classical tabletop systems to massively multiplayer online games (MMORPGs).
OUTCOMES:
• 1). Students should be able to analyze how games, game design, and simulated worlds shape—and are shaped by—social, cultural, and educational contexts. (Think critically)

  • 2). Students should be able to communicate their ideas effectively in writing and collaborate productively with peers to develop, present, and refine shared projects. (Communicate effectively and Establish foundations for lifelong learning)
ASSIGNMENTS:
Gaming Logs: Students will keep a gaming log based on in-class activities (one entry per class meeting).

Group Presentation/Project: Groups of three to five students will present on a gaming system, an adventure scenario, and/or campaign world.

Individual Paper/Story: Students will complete one short (5-8 page) paper or story. This paper may focus on students’ experiences as gamers, an in-world story based on an adventure, or a comparison of traditional and online RPGs.
GRADING:
The course is graded P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. The weight of the three major assignments is:

Gaming Log: 30%

Group Presentation/Project: 30%

Individual Paper/Story: 40%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Carl Whithaus is a Professor of Writing and Rhetoric. His research focuses on the impact of information technologies on literacy and learning. His published books include Teaching and Evaluating Writing in the Age of Computers and High-stakes Testing, Writing Across Distances and Disciplines, and Multimodal Literacies and Emerging Genres. He has a new book coming out in early 2025, Swarms, Viral Writing, and the LocalLinks to an external site.. One of the chapters in that book looks at how writing works around Roll20 and explores questions about Virtual Table Tops (VTT) as collaborative, storytelling spaces.

A long time RPG player, he is fascinated by how gaming, game design, and RPGs/MMORPGs have come to influence professional discussions in fields including computer science, publishing, and education. You can check out the actual play podcasts he's been working on at the HAUS Rules Gaming CollaborativeLinks to an external site..

TITLE: Gals and Pals in Engineering
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
An important tone-setter for most college students is the first one or two years of their undergraduate experience. Negative experiences during this vulnerable period may adversely affect students' sense of belonging in engineering and lead to potential dropout from the major or the university. Through the perspective of examining a series of inequitable experiences towards women engineers, this seminar intends to gather the first-year Gals and Pals in engineering to learn about how to recognize inequitable practices in the academic/professional engineering world and tackle these practices. You may pick up some allies and long-term friends along the way in this supportive community!

We have the following activities planned out in the seminar:

  • Read and reflect on the McKinsey report on women in professional workplace.
  • Read and discuss academic papers on 1) chilly climate in undergraduate engineering education 2) negotiating gender identity with professional engineering identity.
  • Complete case studies: navigating isolative behavior in academic and professional settings in engineering.
  • Learn from professional women engineers in both academia and industry through a Women in Engineering panel.
  • See yourself as an engineer with possible trips to the Engineering Student Design Center (with a 3D printing workshop), the Center for Nano-Micromanufacturing (with a clean room tour), and/or other labs/facilities at the College of Engineering.
  • Write practice emails to engineering professors and professionals to seek help and resources.

As part of the Aggie Connection program, your peer mentor may organize additional social events for everyone participating in this community.
OUTCOMES:
As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students should be able to:

  1. Make informed life decisions. You will learn about the essence of being a college student in engineering!
  2. Align your personal identities with your career goals. You will discover your own engineering identity that incorporates your gender identity. You will also explore ways to improve the current environment experienced by women in engineering.
  3. Demonstrate leadership skills. You will learn about the challenges women engineers face in the current world. You will arm yourselves with respect and empathy in gender equity and be a force of change in the engineering world.
  4. Prepare for lifelong learning. We hope to provide a platform and build a community of allies for women in engineering.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students are expected to actively engage in class discussions involving individual brainstorming and group discussions about the reading assigned for that week. Discussions will be held in groups of 3-4. Weekly assignments include a reflection (approximately 150 words) on a reading that ranges from 6 to 20 pages. In this course, longer readings tend to be industrial or academic reports (thus consuming less time per page) and shorter readings tend to be academic papers (thus consuming more time per page). On average, each student is expected to spend two hours on reading and reflection.

Each student is expected to attend our field trips to engineering labs. The field trips will be held during class periods.

At the end of the course, each student will generate a two-page, single-spaced reflection on the contents covered in the course.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or higher. 30% of the grade is based on weekly discussions and participation; 50% of the grade is based on the weekly reflections; 5% of the grade is based on the practice email written to an engineering professional or faculty; 15% of the grade is based on the final reflection.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Tiffany Chan (she/her) is a current M.S. student in Biomedical Engineering. She is a member of the Cube3 lab (@cube3ucd on Instagram), starting her research journey as an undergraduate student in the lab. She is one of the original designers of the first-year seminar with Dr. Wang and have served as a peer mentor of this course for 4 times.

Dr. Xianglong Wang (he/him) is an Associate Professor of Teaching in Biomedical Engineering (BME). Dr. Wang leads the Cube3 Lab, which is dedicated to improving the sense of belonging in engineering students and quality of education in engineering at UC Davis.

This FYS will be supported by two peer mentors: Anabelle Pang, a second-year undergraduate students in the Cube3 Lab and another peer mentor through the Aggie Connection office.
