TITLE: Genomics and Gene Therapy: How Genes Control You and How You Can Control Them
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: You are now living in a world in which we can alter our DNA. As of 2017, gene therapy is now a thing, and gene editing is in clinical trials. Would you like to learn how this is done and where it's going? This seminar is intended to provide science and non-science majors with an overview of the use and ethics of genome sequencing, gene therapy, and CRISPR/Cas gene editing. Topics to be covered will include an introduction to the human genome, what is a genetic disease, and how recent advances have enabled scientists to attempt to fix genetic diseases. Anyone curious about what information is in the human genome and how that information might be used to improve public health is welcomed to attend.
OUTCOMES: One goal of this course is to develop effective communication skills, both between the instructor and the students, and the students with each other. The content will therefore be delivered in discussions, debate teams, peer feedback on writings, and a tour of the genome center. In addition to learning about our genes and gene therapy, the students will learn to work together to increase their knowledge and share it with others. These interactions, discussions and debates, will works towards our other goal of cultivating ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy.
ASSIGNMENTS:
The seminar will be one hour per week for ten weeks. There will be two informal lectures for orientation and introduction to the basics. The subsequent format will be discussions, debates, and presentations based on reading materials, including articles from scientific journals and popular sources. A 1-credit class averages 2-hours outside of the class per week (20 total hours). All reading materials will be provided. Participants will also write two 1–2-page papers on topics related to gene therapy. One session will be reserved for a tour of the UC Davis Genome Center to see where this work is actually done. The last few sessions will be reserved for group presentations in which 3–4 students will present progress in a particular area of gene therapy. The format and details of the group presentations will be discussed during the class. Here is an outline of topics from last year's class. This year will be even better!

Introduction to the Human Genome

Genetic discrimination, debate teams

Write 1 pg paper on “ FDA shuts down 23andMe”

Introduction to Gene Therapy

Germline gene therapy, debate teams

Tour of Genome Center

Gene therapy group presentations.

Hope you join us!
GRADING: The grade of PASS is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better. Evidence of preparation and active participation in discussions and group presentations will be 30% of the course grade. Another 30% will be based on three take home short quizzes to evaluate if participants are understanding the key concepts as we go along. The remaining 30% will be based on the two writing assignments.
INSTRUCTORBIO: David Segal holds joint appointments in the UC Davis Genome Center, the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Medicine, the Department of Pharmacology, and the M.I.N.D. Institute. This will be his 21st time teaching this seminar. His research interest is in genome and epigenetic editing using CRISPR as therapy for genetic disorders. He is also aware that research scientists often do a poor job of communicating their science to non-scientists, fueling public anxiety about new technological advances. This seminar therefore aims to demystify, discuss, and invite critical thinking on new frontiers of biomedical science.

TITLE: Global Pathfinders: Charting a Path to Global Learning
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
We live in an interconnected world. New students come to UC Davis with many types of global experiences already. You may be from an immigrant family, from a diverse community, multilingual, or an international student. It doesn't matter what your background is, what your major is, or what your career goals are, we ALL need skills and knowledge to successfully operate in a global world. 

Study abroad is one way to experience global learning, but there's so much more that UC Davis has to offer. We have programs for students to go abroad, virtual opportunities, and even things to do right here on campus. Research, internships, community engagement, intercultural workshops, leadership and mentorship programs, and the Global Studies minor are just some of the opportunities you can get involved with. Come learn about scholarships and available funding, free or low-cost programs, and programs you can even get paid for participating in. There are so many global learning opportunities, it's time to get informed now so you can make amazing choices about your involvement for the rest of your time at UC Davis and beyond.

The goal of this 1-credit seminar is to orient students early on to what global learning is and all of the resources the Global Learning Hub has to offer. We will also explore and develop some of the key global and intercultural skills necessary for a global workforce. The skills and knowledge in this course will help prepare you not only for your time at UC Davis, but also for your post-graduation life in our interconnected world.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of the 10-week course, participants will have:

  1. Defined global learning and intercultural learning
  2. Assessed a variety of definitions of and analogies for culture and key elements of cultural dimensions.
  3. Described elements of their own identity and how they inform their values, attitudes and actions
  4. Developed a plan for their own global and intercultural learning and linked it to their UC Davis journey
  5. Explored the concept of SMART goals and applied this concept to their plan
  6. Reflected on the concept of cultural humility and articulated ways to cultivate and practice it
ASSIGNMENTS: There will be weekly readings/videos to watch, totaling a max of 20 pages/week and/or two short videos/week. Students will complete a weekly written reflection of minimum 250 words. Finally, students will have a final brief individual presentation (2-3 minutes).
GRADING:
This course will be graded Pass/No Pass based on the following work: 

  • Participation: Participate in discussions and activities. If you miss more than three classes, you will receive a “Not Pass” grade for the class. If you miss a class, you are required to complete a makeup assignment.
  • Assigned readings and reflections: Complete all required readings/video viewings. Complete all written assignments in order to receive a “Pass” grade.  
  • Final presentation: Presentation of 2 minutes on the goals you’ve outlined for yourself and the vision board you created in class. Attend the final class and present to receive "Pass."
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Instructor of Record: Nancy Erbstein

Nancy Erbstein serves as Associate Vice Provost, Global Education and as faculty member in the School of Education. Her research and teaching focus on how communities produce and disrupt disparities in youth well-being, as well as youth civic engagement as an intervention strategy, situating her work at the intersection of educational reform, public health, youth development and community development. She brings her background in the areas of educational equity, community-engaged research and teaching, and global learning to catalyzing the Global Education for All initiative at UC Davis.

 

Course Collaborator: Kimberly Bellows

Kimberly works in the UC Davis Global Learning Hub as the Global and Intercultural Programs Manager. She is passionate about developing and facilitating programs that help students make connections and see the world through a globally aware lens. She has lived in the Southwest and East Coast of the U.S. and abroad in Chile and Northern Ireland. A native Californian and first gen student, she graduated from UC Davis with a degree in International Relations and a minor in Spanish, and she has a MA in International Education Management from the Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey.

TITLE: High Performance Driving
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Auto racing is one of the most popular sports in the world. Whether it's a local autocross or Formula 1, the driving controls are the same: steering wheel, throttle, brake, gearbox. While simple in principle, the act of high performance driving is deceptively complex. In this course, students will learn the theory and practice of high performance driving. Students will learn from both historical and modern texts, from videos of drivers of varying skill levels, and from telemetry data. We will explore the truths, misconceptions, and compromises that make motor racing such a difficult and rewarding sport.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn the mathematical and physical foundations underlying auto racing from the theoretical ideal racing line to the unusual properties of rubber tires. Students will learn how to examine videos to diagnose common driving errors. Students will learn how one records and reads telemetry data to understand what the driver and vehicle are doing at every instant. By the end of the course, students will be able to debunk many common driving myths based on their sound theoretical knowledge and practical experience. The educational outcomes will include (1) developing effective communication skills from both a driver and pit perspective, (2) developing high cognitive skills in assessing driver and vehicle performance, (3) developing focus and depth in the interdisciplinary fields associated with high performance driving, and (4) preparing for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each week there will be a short written assignment where students will be given a few driving puzzles to solve. The puzzles will contain text and/or video and/or telemetry that describe a racing activity that didn't go as planned (e.g a spin). Solving the puzzle will require integrating information across disciplines. For example, diagnosing whether a spin is due to the car or driver requires inspection of weight distribution, yaw, and change in steering angle. Each student will write a concise report describing what happened and why. In the following session, the class will start by discussing the previous problem. Towards the end of the course, students will also create their own puzzles for other students to solve. It is expected that the weekly assignments will take 2 hours of out-of-class work.
GRADING: Students will be graded on a 100 point scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). 90% of the points will come from written assignments and 10% from class participation (defined as coming to class and being present for discussion).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology Department who specializes in bioinformatics. When he's not developing novel sequence analysis algorithms he likes to be at the race track where he may be driving or coaching (but not spectating). He has written a popular entry level book for racers, "You Suck at Racing: a crash course for the novice driver", and has a blog with over 500 posts about high performance driving.

TITLE: How Wars End
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
Almost all wars end—the Korean War is still ongoing both legally and sometimes more than that—but they end in different ways and with divergent consequences.  The purpose of this course is to actively engage in identifying the factors that lead to the end of wars and using that framework to undertake and present a case study based on both historical and/or contemporary information. The final class meeting will apply what has been learned in the case studies to forecast the conditions that might be necessary for the termination of the war between Russian and Ukraine. 

The course meetings will consist of (1) discussions of the material assigned below and (2) student expositions of what they have learned about the war they are studying, including  sharing  the data sources they have drawn upon that might be of use to other students.  Assigned readings will be available online.
OUTCOMES: The primary goal of the class is to enhance student understanding of the political and military processes that are inherent in the end a war.  Research on line and in the library will familiarize  students with experience in accessing detailed information about how variation in political structures and the resources available to the state frame choices.  There will be an emphasis in introducing social science methods in the analysis of the complex process of conflict and how war aims evolve as wars continue.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each student will conduct independent research on how variation political structures and resources led to a war's end. In the first class meeting students will discuss and vote on how the wars will be assigned.  Case study readings will be assigned and made available on line.  Students will report the results of their research in an appropriately documented  paper of no more than five pages. Rather than having each student deliver a report to the class on their work we will have around table discussion of their conclusions. A voluntary sixth meeting will discuss the Russo-Ukraine war ans forecast its likely outcome and the factors motivating such an outcome.
GRADING: The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- .Grading for the course is P/NP, and the grades will be determined by (1) student attendance and participation in class discussion (10 %) and (2) the components of a paper of no more than five (5) pages (excluding bibliography) analyzing the circumstances that led to the termination of a war (90%).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
I retired from the UC Davis faculty in 2009 as a Distinguished Professor of Political Science.  My primary area of study was international war, a subject on which I published one book and 60+ articles..  At the time of my retirement I was appointed research professor; this currently continues.,  Subsequent to retirement I was recalled as acting university librarian for 18 months and then as associate dean in the College of L and S for three further years

I am the first member of my family to attend college and the second to graduate from high school..

TITLE: Hummingbirds--Conservation of an Urban Pollinating Avian Species
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Pollinators encompass a diverse group of animals that provide important ecosystem services. As examples, pollinators include bees, butterflies, bats and birds. Students interested in urban wildlife and human interactions would benefit from this Freshman Seminar. Students who have some biology or ecology background or interest would benefit most from this seminar. This seminar will explore the hummingbird species of California, what ecosystem services hummingbirds provide, the important factors that help hummingbirds be successful and produce offspring, and how hummingbirds are being studied in science. The goal of this course is to provide students with an overview of the amazing nature of hummingbirds and to enlighten them about the challenges that hummingbirds are facing in an ever changing world
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the different facets of hummingbird natural history and conservation. The goals of this seminar are to (1) cultivate ethics, responsibility, and respect, for the animals that we share planet Earth with and (2) to develop a global perspective for ecosystem health. This seminar is an opportunity to gain a more in-depth understanding about how hummingbirds play a role in ecosystem health and how we can help to protect their populations in the future.
ASSIGNMENTS: For the five sessions, students will be assigned to read 2-3 journal articles per session. Journal articles will focus on avian research techniques, hummingbird biology, health, habitat, migration, or conservation. These topics will then be discussed during the seminar sessions so that students can share their views about what they learned from the assigned reading and how that changed or added to their current perceptions. During the quarter, students will be expected to write a 5 page paper (double spaced, with at least 6 references) on a topic related to hummingbird biology, conservation, health, habitat restoration etc. Students will also be expected to give a 5 minute presentation at the end of class. Writing the paper and giving a presentation is an opportunity for students to experience translatable skills of critically reviewing scientific literature and present it in an educational form. This seminar will meet for a total of five sessions. Each session will be 1 hr 40 min in duration. The tentative dates for the five sessions are 9/24/26, 10/01/26, 10/08/26, 10/15/26 and 10/22/26.
GRADING: This is a pass/no pass course. A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. Course grading will be based on a written 5-page paper (35%), verbal presentation during the last session (35%), and quality of participation in class sessions (30%).  Students must actively participate in discussions for 4 of 5 sessions (unless further absences are excused) to receive a passing grade. Class participation will entail students coming class with questions/comments relative to assigned manuscripts for the seminar session and active participation in the discussion sessions.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Tell is a member of the faculty in the School of Veterinary Medicine. Her research interests include treatment of avian disease, avoidance of drug residues in poultry products consumed by humans, and avian health relative to conservation of bird populations. Dr. Tell obtained her Bachelor of Science from UC Davis, did her residency at the National Zoo, then returned to UC Davis as a faculty member. She is boarded with the American College of Zoological Medicine and the American Board of Veterinary Practitioners (Avian Practice). Dr. Tell is currently the director of the UC Davis Hummingbird Health and Conservation Program and holds a federal permit for banding hummingbirds.

TITLE: Integrated Toxicology, One Health, and Global Sustainable Development
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: One Health is defined as an integrated unifying multisectorial approach that aims to sustainably balance and optimize the health of people, animals and the environment locally, regionally, nationally, or globally. This transdisciplinary course will integrate toxicology, One Health, and the UN sustainable development goals.  A healthy environment is the foundation of One Health and using case studies from around the world, the course will integrate the impact of environmental toxicants on human, animal, and ecosystem health. For grounding, principles of toxicology and the fundamentals of One Health will be covered in the beginning, followed by a review of the UN sustainable development goals. Then multiple and integrated sources and effects of environmental toxicants to humans, animals, and their shared environment will be covered at the global level and the impact on sustainable development emphasized. Discussions will also include but not limited to: the impact of climate change on One Health; animals as sentinels for the environment and public health; exposure to toxicants through water, food, and air from a one health perspective; health of water bodies on One Health and sustainable development; vulnerable communities and community-based impact of environmental toxicants; toxicants as environmental stressors and their interaction with communicable diseases; environmental justice;  and the relevance of conservation to One Health. Discussions will cover effects on individual and population levels. Students taking this course will develop higher-order critical thinking skills, actively discussing complex global One Health challenges and their impact on sustainability of human, animal, and ecosystem health.
OUTCOMES:
Environmental issues are global and complex. This proposal addresses at least two of the UC Davis Global Learning Outcomes: 1) Global Awareness and 2) Global action.

Global awareness

Students will learn to:

a) Evaluate complex and overlapping environmental health toxicology issues in a global context, integrating  environmental toxicological issues and human, animal and ecosystem health; and 

b) Analyze how these systems are constructed, influenced and altered, identifying differential and inequitable consequences, and how this shapes people’s lives around the world, including sustainable development.

Global action

Students will learn to:

a) Create strategies to identify biases, navigate power relations, and continuously monitor intended and unintended consequences when addressing complex global challenges surrounding the topic of climate change, environmental toxicants, and human, plant, and ecosystem health;


b) Apply knowledge, skills, and abilities to contribute to society at multiple levels - locally, regionally, nationally, and globally - demonstrating cultural humility and ethical, informed, and responsible actions; and


c) Explore personal, academic, and professional opportunities that address real-life global challenges through collaboration across intercultural and transdisciplinary contexts in ways that account for cultural diversity, social justice, and planetary sustainability.
ASSIGNMENTS: This is a 2 unit course requiring 4 hours of out of class work per week. Students will be required to attend and actively participate in discussions centered on selected topics. Students will be assigned to read peer reviewed journal articles and/or curated online content on toxicology, One Health, and UN sustainable goals. In-class discussions will be based on these reading assignments. Students are expected to come ready to actively participate in discussions.  Each student will be expected to write a capstone paper towards the end of the quarter on a topic that aligns with the course. Students will discuss their chosen topics with the instructor for approval.  There is no field or laboratory work for this class.
GRADING: This class will be graded as a P/NP . The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. 50% will be based on active class participation, 30 % will be based on a capstone paper, and 20 % will be based on quizzes.
INSTRUCTORBIO: I am a professor of One Environmental Health Toxicology in the Department of Molecular Biosciences, School of Veterinary Medicine. I am originally from Uganda and naturally I have a global perspective on environmental toxicology issues. My job includes teaching research and service. I teach toxicology to veterinary students and I also teach in graduate courses. I am affiliated with the Pharmacology and Toxicology Graduate program, the Forensic Science program, the MPV graduate program, and the One Health Institute. My research is centered on understanding the role of the environment on human and animal health.  In one aspect of my research I investigate mechanisms by which hydrogen sulfide, an environmental/occupational toxicant,  worsens influenza A virus infection. For my other research, I am investigating early biomarkers of exposure to microcystins in animal models. Microcystins are water and food contaminants produced by harmful algal blooms.  Climate change has impact on prevalence of microcystins globally.  I have previously taught Study Abroad courses on One Health which involved taking students to Uganda and Rwanda. I am very passionate about effects of toxicants from a One Health perspective as the health of the environment and that of humans, animals, and ecosystem are intertwined globally.

TITLE: Introduction to Disability Studies
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
This seminar will introduce students to the disciplinary field and the methodological lenses of disability studies. Disability studies scholars explore the many definitions of disability and uncover the social norms that marginalize people who do not fit arbitrary standards of able-bodiedness and neurotypicality. Their work pushes back on ableist policies, ideas, and structures that marginalize disability instead of considering it a universal and integral aspect of human diversity and experience. “Like black studies, women’s studies, and other liberation-movement disciplines, disability studies teaches that it is an unaccepting society that needs normalizing, not the minority group” (Capuzzi Simon).

We will read short readings from disability studies experts and discuss them together, applying them to our own experiences and observations. Then, students will investigate on their own, conducting a collaborative project applying what we discussed in class to a topic of their choice. In our class, the definition of disability will encompass physical, cognitive, and intellectual disabilities. 

Because the topic of disability is applicable to everything, students will find no shortage of topics! The concepts and theories of disability studies can be applied to history, humor, literature and comics, education, movies and TV shows, music, race and ethnicity, social media, language, sexual education, technology, the arts, urban design, politics, science and medicine, architecture, gender theory, and so on. Even our own campus can be inspected through disability lenses and evaluated critically for its efforts towards accessibility and inclusion!

My hope is that students with or without disabilities will fall as much in love with disability studies as I have.
OUTCOMES:
As a result of participating in this seminar, students should be able to 

Communicate effectively on disability studies issues and make word choices that align with liberatory practices;

Observe their environment critically, noticing ableist structures and coming up with potential solutions for increased inclusion;

Collaborate with others during discussions and presentations in ways that foster connections and understanding even when encountering different opinions.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Participation: 

For this class, you will

  • read short readings on disability studies concepts and theories (no more than 2 hours of reading a week and never more than 10 pages of reading),
  • complete weekly discussion board postings reflecting on the readings,
  • discuss the readings during our class meetings.

(Active participation looks different for everyone. We will discuss this in class)

 

Discussion leading: 

Each week, a couple of students will lead discussion on the readings. They will recap the discussion board conversations, share their interpretation of the readings, and come up with discussion questions for their peers.

As discussion leaders, you will read all the posts in that week’s discussion board and

  • recap the discussion board posts to give us a sense of some reflection trends you noticed people had about the weekly reading,
  • have 5–6 prepared discussion questions on the reading,
  • encourage people to share their thoughts on the reading. 

 

Group project:

As a group, you will pick a disability studies keyword, teach it to the class, and create a project that focuses on that keyword by applying the keyword to a topic of your choice. You will present your project to the rest of the class.
GRADING:
This class is graded Pass/No Pass. The grade of Pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. 

To pass this seminar you need to

  • attend at least 8 class meetings and actively participate in the class discussions (definitions vary on what active participation means),
  • complete at least 8 of the readings and discussion board posts,
  • complete the group project and present it to the class,
  • lead discussion during one weekly meeting.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a lecturer for the Writing Center. She teaches upper-division writing courses like Writing in Science (UWP 104E), Writing in Health Sciences (UWP 104F), and advanced composition (UWP 101). She has been interested in disability studies for her entire academic career, culminating in her PhD in Technical Communication and Rhetoric where she analyzed the evolution of the topic of disability in technical communication publications.

TITLE: Learning and Memory in Neurologic Disease
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: As scientists in a neurological surgery lab, our interest involves how brain disease and injury result in behavioral dysfunction such as learning disorders. We then use our understanding of that biology to try to identify and test new treatment strategies to restore the brain to a healthier state. Why does a single insult to the brain, such as an epileptic seizure or traumatic brain injury, have such a significant impact on learning and memory? Are cells dying resulting in deficits? Is it possible that deficits result from changes to brain network function? Could it be a bit of both? Through continual research of different neurological diseases including epilepsy, Alzheimer's disease, traumatic brain injury, schizophrenia etc., it is clear that there is a dynamic relationship between brain injury and cognitive function where many patients, regardless of disease, experience some level of impairment. However, as the mechanism and location of disease within the brain may change, the scope (either severity or type) of learning disabilities also varies. Our research aims to answer questions related to changes in brain function (at the neuronal and circuit level) with the goal of finding a target to reduce cognitive deficits associated with brain injury. In this course, students will learn about various brain disorders and discuss how they alter learning and memory. By utilizing articles published in popular science journals that summarize basic science findings, students will gain insight into current research methodology and learn about the intricate relationship between brain injury and cognition.
OUTCOMES: In this course, students will develop the tools to analyze and interpret scientific articles and will also practice sharing ideas with their peers by way of discussions and presentations. This course will also serve as an introduction to neurological disorders of the brain and discuss the fundamentals of neuroscience, including learning and memory.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet for one hour each week spanning the 10-week quarter. The first meeting will include an introduction to and discussion of some basic ideas related to cognitive neuroscience and different brain disorders. Reading (5-15 pages; ~30 min) assignments will consist of scientific journals and popular science articles (i.e. journals, publications, etc.) and will be assigned for each week’s topic. All students will be expected to read these papers and submit a short summary of the reading each week via presentation slides (6-8 slides; ~30 min). In addition, each week 1 or 2 students will  present their slides to the class (~15 min) and lead the discussion about the assigned reading. Each student will present only one time. I will provide a brief overview at the end of the class that will be relevant to the assignments.
GRADING: Pass/no-pass will be assessed based on class participation (40%), quality of presentation (30%) and quality of the weekly summaries (30%). At UC Davis a passing grade is considered a C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ali Izadi, PhD, is part of the Research Faculty (Assistant Professional Researcher) in the Department of Neurological Surgery. Dr. Izadi’s research is concentrated on developing novel and innovative treatment strategies using adult and juvenile rodent models to treat neurologic disorders such as epilepsy and traumatic brain injury. On a larger scale, he is interested in evaluating similarities and differences in the biological mechanisms of cognitive dysfunction across multiple neurologic diseases. Fundamentally, the goal of his research is to explore mechanisms and identify innovative therapies in the laboratory and to ultimately improve quality of life for patients with neurologic disorders. He has taught this class since 2019.

TITLE: Locksport: Picking locks for fun
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Have you ever wanted to learn how to pick a lock? Take this course, and you'll be able to pick locks. But not any locks. We only pick locks (a) that are not in use and (b) that we have the expressed permission to pick. This is just for fun: we never pick locks in the "real world". Students will learn the various ways locks are constructed and how simple tools can be used to defeat them. Students will practice lock picking first-hand and work their way up the Lockpickers United Belt System to record their expertise. Students will gain an appreciation for the fragility of security through obscurity, the laws and ethics governing lockpicking, and how the locks in their lives keep them safe (or not). At the end of the class, students will be able to make more informed choices when it comes to their own security (and astound their friends and family with their new skills).
OUTCOMES:
1. Roughly half of every class period will be spent in small group work where students have to communicate with each other to work through problems.
  2. We discuss the mathematics of security: for example, the number of pins and the depth of cuts in pin tumbler locks determine the number of possible keys.
  3. Locksport practitioners are very concerned about the unethical side of their sport and are therefore vigilant to ensure their sport remains legal.
  4. Locksport challenges both the mind and body. One cannot see inside a lock, so one must visualize what is happening through a theoretical understanding of lock design. One must also be able to put that theory into practice by feeling and interacting with tiny movements and sounds.
  5. This class isn't designed to develop leadership skills.
  6. We discuss the legality of lock picking around the world. For example, owning lock picks is a felony in Japan.
  7. We introduce the LPU Belt system, which associates student skill with a colored belt (like martial arts). Students are expected to go from White to Yellow to Orange Belt during the course, and will be positioned to continue to higher levels after the course.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class has a partially inverted structure: students watch videos remotely (about 30-60 min per week), come to class for a brief lecture/discussion (20 min), then work in small groups to develop their lock picking skills (30 min). Each week, students will answer a few low-stakes questions online that test and reinforce their knowledge.
GRADING:
Students will be graded on the typical 100 point scale that is used in Canvas.

  • 70% of the grade will be based on weekly quizzes administered through Canvas
  • 15% of the grade will be based on lock picking skill (belt level)
  • 15% of the grade will be based on group activities (judged by participation in class)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Department of Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology who specializes in bioinformatics and genomics. Locksport is one of his many hobbies.

TITLE: Mathematics Of Global Change
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Starting with Copernicus and the displacement of Earth from the center of the heavens, we will explore the use of mathematics as a tool for understanding various forms of global change. Using spreadsheets as a computational tool, basic mathematics (mostly high school algebra and geometry) will be used to model populations, human migration, overshoot, and other forms of global change that are central to the future of Mankind.
OUTCOMES: Students will develop an ability to engage in focused discussion of forces underlying global change followed by efforts to formulate such forces mathematically. This will enable them to develop modeling skills that incorporate such forces and to perform calculations that lead to scenarios  of outcomes. In so doing and discussing outcomes they will develop appreciation for both the uses and limitations of mathematical modeling.
ASSIGNMENTS: Readings of materials to be distributed or available online, written solutions of assigned problems, and submission of spreadsheets modeling various forms of global change.
GRADING:
P/NP: The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

We will calculate student grades using the following breakdown:

  • Final Project: 20%
  • Participation: 20%
  • Evidence of Notes/Class Work: 20%
  • Homework: 20%
  • Journal: 20%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Kurt Kreith is a retired professor. He has taught first year seminars in a variety of areas, with a special interest in the role mathematics can play in understanding our changing world.

Andrew Hood is a former middle and high school math teacher, and currently supervises in the UC Davis teacher credential program.

TITLE: Mind in Motion: Embodying Attention
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Attention is never just one thing. One does not merely pay attention or not. Rather, many forms and styles of attention physically and mentally determine our lived experience. As the basis for knowing all aspects of our world, attention is critical to our activities in it because it forms the foundation from which we grasp new knowledge and upon which we then respond. Facility in the actions of attention includes understanding how to direct it, concentrate and free it as well as how to recognize a diversity of its forms that are principle in our organization of time and space. This class will knit practice and theory by drawing on a variety of physical and somatic movement practices including Contact Improvisation and Body-Mind Centering, as well as theoretical investigations that include body theories of performance and neuroscience. Our purpose will be to tie together the movement of attention with the synergistic movement of the body and the mind. Designed to develop and support both theoretical and practical research, class discussions will be based on weekly readings (drawn from a broad range of sources in the humanities, sciences, and the arts) as well as physical research intended to explicate the attentional decisions at work in social and academic life. Our work will culminate in a greater appreciation that the actions of our body and mind move as one phenomenon. How we recognize its motion is significant to our understanding of the world, our body, our identity, and the university.
OUTCOMES: Students will (1) develop effective communication skills through practicing group work which train in how to listen, think quickly, and respond to others in real time; (2) cultivate ethics, respect and empathy by participating in intense group activities; (3) develop cognitive skills through analyzing individual and group behavior in realtime and problem-solving; (5) prepare for lifelong learning by being introduced to many disciplines of embodied practices of listening to one's body and to others, learning how learn, and group engagement practices that are mostly taught outside the university.
ASSIGNMENTS: Classes will be two hours per week and will include discussion of readings (limited to 10 -15 pages per week) and the practice of individual and group  movement exercises aimed to increase physical/mental awareness and creative response. For a final project students will work in groups to develop their own creative movement and attentional exercsises out of class and share weekly progress. Out of class assignments include reading, developing group project exercises, practicing, and writing up experiences with practice (weekly 2-3 paragraphs posted to Canvas): these will take no more than 4 hours per week total. Students must attend all class sessions (or do a makeup assignment for each assignment missed) and participate in class activities and discussions.
GRADING: The course grade will be: practice reports (30%), final project (presentation exercise and writeup, 40%), class participation (30%). A student needs to achieve at least a C- (70%) in each of the core areas in order to receive a passing grade. Grading: 100-90 (A), 89-80 (B), 79-70 (C), 69-60 (D), <59 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Joseph Dumit is Chair of Performance Studies, and Professor of Science & Technology Studies, and Anthropology. He studies brains, minds, bodies, drugs, media, virtual reality, capitalism, gaming, and improvisation. He has written two books: Picturing Personhood: Brain Scans and Biomedical Identity, and Drugs for Life: How Pharmaceutical Companies Define Our Health. He is also a co-founder of the ModLab for game studies and game development (where he's currently making a game on fracking), and works with the KeckCAVES on 3D development for science and the arts. http://dumit.net  

Dorte Bjerre Jensen’s work as an art worker and educator is anchored in an evolving artistic inquiry into multisensory relations of attention through movement manifested as live art installations, site-specific work, activism, lectures, classes, workshops and writing. Dorte Bjerre Jensen holds an M.F.A. from the Danish National School of Performing Arts and is currently a PhD student at University of California Davis, Performance Studies.

TITLE: Modern Marvel: Bridges Around the World
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: This seminar course aims to: 1) introduce different types of modern bridges and their aesthetics; 2) give a quick peek on bridge disasters in the history; 3) provide a tool's-eye view of just how a mega-bridge like San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge gets built; and 4) provide hands-on design experience via participation in a web-based bridge design contest. Case studies on many famous bridges around the world will also be included.
OUTCOMES: The students will learn about different types of bridges in the world and how they are typically constructed during this Freshman Seminar course. Basic concept such as bridges, structures, design and engineering will be introduced through formal lectures and case studies. The students will also have the opportunity to apply the knowledge in designing their own bridge and compete among their classmates towards the end of the quarter.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly reading materials and an oral presentation on a selected case study. The course grading is Pass/No Pass. Students will be required to participate in the in-class discussions. An individual project will be assigned where the student is required to prepare for one oral presentation on a bridge case study chosen from the list provided by the instructor (bridges other than the ones provided in the list need to be approved by the instructor upon consultation). The project and presentation will be evaluated on three different categories: (1) Content (e.g., covering important aspects such as construction, design, history); (2) Organization (e.g., well structured, offering summary); and (3) Delivery (e.g., effective use of visual tools such as pictures and videos, clearness). The students are also required to participate in a web-based bridge design contest to gain fun hands-on design experience.
GRADING: The course grade will be based on: in-class discussion (20%), preparation on the case study topic (20%), peer evaluation on the student’s project (10%), web-based bridge design contest (20%), and the instructor’s evaluation on the student’s presentation and design (30%). Class attendance (no less than 80%) is mandatory.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Dawn Cheng is a member of the faculty in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering. Her research interests include combined field and laboratory experimental investigation and analytical/computational simulations; design and analysis of bridge components and systems; application of Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites to innovative infrastructures and renewal; design, analysis and durability mechanics of FRP composites; and engineered materials using recycle wastes.

TITLE: Politics of Immigration and Anti-Asian Hate Crimes
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: The seminar will explore the immigrant’s struggle for equality and on the resurgence of anti-Asian hate crimes in recent years.  It will highlight the turmoil unshackled by the 1/27/2017 travel ban from 7 select Muslim nations and will draw a historical perspective from the Chinese American experience. Because the Supreme Court decisions in the anti-Chinese cases helped to establish the federal government’s plenary power over immigration matters, understanding these cases and decisions will cast invaluable perspectives on the current debate over immigration and on the importance of checks/balances in government. Many have interpreted recent immigration policies as a surreptitious way to demean the “unassimilable” immigrants. Such racial prejudice can seep into the equanimity of science and medicine, as evidenced by misfeasance against the Chinese during the 1900 bubonic plague outbreak in San Francisco. Physicians and politicians covered their ignorance of Yersinia by invoking anti-Chinese tropes. The discussion will cast perspectives on the current rise of anti-Asian hate crimes and on the mitigating strategies.
OUTCOMES: The seminar will raise awareness of the impact of Trump’s travel ban, immigration policies, Chinese Exclusion Acts, and errant public health board’s response to the bubonic plague outbreak in 1900 San Francisco. These perspectives will stimulate the students to think about and articulate the genesis and the recent resurgence of anti-Asian hate crimes.  The class will guide students to gather informed perspectives and to articulate their ideas in oral and written presentations. The class will foster critical, independent thinking that will interface multidisciplinary ideas in science, medicine, history, law, and social policy.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Format: The seminar will meet for 2 hours each week.  Instructor will provide background material and lead classroom discussions. However, the course focuses on stimulating students to think and articulate critically. So, from the first class, the instructor will guide students to formulate their own ideas. 

Assignments: 

Weeks 1-4:  Lecture & Discussion. Students will read intensely assigned articles (about 25 pages total each week and write 1-2 page critique.

Weeks 4-6:  Each student will give a 5-10 min oral presentation to the entire class about his/her research topic based on the course content and discussion. After the presentation, the student will submit a 2-3 page essay on the research topic.  Other class members will provide oral and written feedback. 

Weeks 7-10:  Given the feedback, the student will revise his/her presentation and present a revised 15-20 min oral presentation to the entire class.  After the presentation, the student will submit a 2-3 page essay on the research topic.  Other class members will provide oral and written feedback. 

Finals Week: Based on the presentations and class feedback, the students will submit a final 5 page on the research topic.
GRADING: Students will be graded on 1) Essays on assigned reading (10%), 2) 5-10 min presentation (10%), 2) 10-20 min presentation (15%), 3) midterm examination (15%), 4) 5 page report (35%), and 4) class participation (15%). Class participation score will be based on attendance and oral/written feedback to fellow classmates’ presentation. Grading will follow the standard scale: 100-90%=A, 80-89%=B, 70-79%=C, 60-69%=D, <60% = F.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Thomas Jue has served as the instructor of record at UC Davis in the core biochemistry course in the medical school, in graduate courses focusing on metabolism, research methodology, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) in biomedicine, and current techniques in biophysics. He has developed curricula for the graduate programs in Exercise Physiology and Biophysics. He has research expertise in the field of chemical physics and has conducted research in many disciplines including NMR technique development, protein structure-function, the bioenergetics of muscle contraction, altered oxidative vs. non-oxidative metabolic regulation in type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) myocardium and skeletal muscle, sialic acid supplementation and cognitive development, and the linkage between T2DM and Alzheimer’s Disease.

He has previously taught FYS courses: 1) Trump's Legacy on Immigration and on Anti-Asian Hate Crimes and 2) Covid19 and the Dark Reality of Health Disparity.  He has received funding from the UCD Humanities Institute to launch an initiative to study the “Immigrant’s Struggle for Equality” and has delivered  papers on “Wong Kim Ark and the Shape of American Immigration Policy”.  He is currently working on a book with UCD law professor, Gabriel Jack Chin, entitled “Contextualizing Asians in the American Immigration Experience”.

TITLE: So You Want To Go To Law School
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: This course will be an introduction to law school and the practice of law. There will be an emphasis on how law school is different than college. Students will also be exposed to typical law school assignments (on an abbreviated basis) and sample law school exams. Students will have the opportunity to attend an actual 1L class. Students will learn the basics of legal writing and how it is different than typical college prose. Guest speakers may include judges, practicing attorneys, prosecutors, and public defenders. Active participation by students in collegial discussions is encouraged. The course will also include an overview of the process of applying to law school, the LSAT, law school rankings, and the admission process.
OUTCOMES: This seminar will introduce students to the important elements of the application process to law school. Students will also learn the reality of the demands of law school and the actual practice of law. Students will learn the importance of brevity and clarity in legal writing. Guest speakers will include law students, practicing attorneys, and judges.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will be assigned to draft modified legal correspondence, memos, motions, and pleadings. Emphasis will be placed upon using topic sentences and using the standard legal analysis format (Issue, rule, analysis, conclusion).

The first assignment, assigned on the first day of class, is a short personal statement worth 20 points.

Each following weekly assignment is worth 10 points.

Attendance is worth 10 points. One unexcused absence is permitted for the fall quarter.
GRADING:
Pass/Fail. Attend all classes, complete all assignments.

The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Clayton Tanaka is currently a lecturer in the King Hall LLM program. He was formerly the Director of Legal Research and Writing at King Hall, UC Davis School of Law. Mr. Tanaka received his undergraduate degree from the University of California, Davis, and his J.D. from UC Hastings College of the Law. He practiced criminal law at the Office of the California Attorney General for twenty-seven years. During his career, he represented the State of California, primarily on direct appeals, capital litigation, and writs in California state and federal courts. He was also responsible for criminal investigations, trials, evidentiary hearings, review, and assignment of cases and special projects.

TITLE: Songs and Belonging: A South Asian Cultural Ensemble
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
This course warmly invites students to explore South Asian culture through song, community, and shared experience. It is designed as a welcoming space where singing is not about perfection or performance, but about connection, curiosity, and joy. As an instructor who loves sharing South Asian culture, I guide students through songs that people sing in everyday life to express emotions, tell stories, and create a sense of belonging.

The course is experiential and participatory. Students will learn to sing together in a supportive environment, engaging with folk songs, ghazals, devotional music, and popular songs from South Asia and its diasporas. Alongside singing, we will talk about lyrics, language, cultural context, and the feelings these songs carry. No prior singing experience, musical training, or language background is required everyone’s voice is welcome.

By taking this course, students will develop cultural awareness, listening skills, and confidence in using their voice as a form of expression. Singing together helps build community, encourages reflection, and offers a meaningful way to experience culture from the inside. This course is for anyone who is curious, open, and interested in learning through shared cultural practice.
OUTCOMES: As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students will engage actively with South Asian musical traditions to explore culture, identity, and belonging. They will learn to communicate effectively through group singing and discussion, gaining confidence in using their voices in a supportive environment. Students will develop intercultural understanding by examining how songs convey history, emotion, and community across South Asia and its diasporas. Through listening, reflection, and participatory activities, they will think critically about the social and cultural roles of music and consider how art shapes lived experience. By connecting these practices to their own experiences of identity and community, students will establish foundations for lifelong learning, cultivating curiosity, empathy, and creative engagement. This seminar emphasizes experiential learning, encouraging students to discover culture through practice, reflection, and shared musical exploration.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to attend the rehearsals during class time, prepare the reading and pronunciation of songs, listen to links of the songs and of some of the music, read cultural handouts accompanying the songs they will be singing, and might submit a 1-2-page paper at the end of the class. The final group project is a low-key performance at the end of the quarter. Most of the project work will be done in class in preparation for the culminating performance at the end of the quarter. Students can practice the songs at home or in groups, but need to come prepared to class. This will involve listening to the music and the songs on their own for about one to two hours a week. Class participation is key to this course  Most pronunciation preparation will be done in class. Instructors will arrange for Middle Eastern music specialists to come in and demonstrate instruments and musical concepts to students in a practical manner. Students will be expected to do research and write one essay about one of the many presented subjects and/or about the visitors' presentations.
GRADING: Students are expected to attend all rehearsals, actively participate in group singing, and come prepared by practicing pronunciation and melodies of the songs. They will listen to recordings, review cultural handouts, and engage with the music outside class for 1 2 hours per week.Class participation is essential, as most rehearsal, pronunciation practice, and preparation for the final project a low-pressure group performance will happen . Guest South Asian music specialists will demonstrate instruments and musical concepts. Students will also write one 1–2 page reflective essay on a song, cultural context, or guest presentation, worth 20% of the final grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
I am a language instructor at the University of California, Davis, teaching Urdu and Hindi, and sharing South Asian culture through language, literature, and music. As a first-generation student, I understand the importance of belonging, mentorship, and discovering one’s voice in new environments. My own journey has shaped my commitment to creating inclusive and welcoming classrooms where all students feel empowered to participate and express themselves.

In my teaching and work on campus, I focus on experiential learning, helping students engage with poetry, song, and storytelling to explore identity, community, and cultural heritage. While I am not a trained singer, I love guiding students in singing, listening, and reflecting on South Asian musical traditions as a way to connect intellectually and emotionally with culture.

This seminar draws on my interests in cultural expression, intercultural learning, and community-building through music. Students will have the opportunity to experience South Asian culture hands-on, develop confidence in their voices, and reflect on their own sense of belonging all within a supportive, interactive, and joyful learning environment.

TITLE: Strategies for Success in Learning
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
This first-year seminar will teach you strategies for success in learning. This course will help you set goals that align with your core values, develop quarterly and weekly plans to achieve your goals, and connect you to resources for achieving your personal and academic goals. It will also focus on better understanding yourself (for example, how and why you procrastinate), and developing strategies to overcome habits that are holding you back. This course emphasizes reflective learning and assignments will follow that format. The overall goal is to help you develop lifelong skills and stay motivated this quarter. 

Topics include: developing a quarterly plan and weekly plans that you actually follow, the importance of self-care, improving your focus (and minimizing distractions), reflecting and refocusing your goals and plans, motivation, preparing for exams, writing strategies, developing and expanding your resilience, improving communication skills, developing a mentoring network, and celebrating your success.

The format will include in-person lectures and group discussions. Each week students will complete a homework assignment (e.g. creating your strategic plan for the quarter) and write a wellness reflection to keep them on track for achieving their goals this quarter. 

Students from all majors are welcome. The instructor recommends this course for students who:

  • Want to improve their time management skills;
  • Struggle prioritizing goals
  • Struggle to stay motivated and focused (or suspect they may struggle to stay motivated)
  • Struggle with self-care and addressing personal goals (all work and no fun)
OUTCOMES:
1. Increase effective communication through class discussions and assignments submitted in written, audio and video forms. One lecture will focus specifically on providing an overview of the components of communication and how it changes over different media, and is associated with communication assignments (e.g. conversations in office hours, seeking internships and mentors)
  2. Develop cognitive skills. Students will learn the higher cognitive skills necessary to continually observe, evaluate, and develop their own personal strategies for effective learning. They will decide why a strategy is or is not working.
  3. Cultivate responsibility and ethics by developing strategies to complete high-quality assignments in a timely matter. Cultivate acceptance, respect and empathy for others and one-self, learning to understand and grow from limitations and errors, and developing a growth mindset.
  4. This class will improve focus, in order to delve into specific subjects more deeply.
  5. Develop leadership skills, including: goal setting, task prioritization, time management, motivation, and practicing self-care. Goals and time management skills will provide the basis of the course and most lessons will be building on the initial lessons taught through students making a quarter plan and weekly plans. For self-care, we rely on the eight dimensions of the wellness model.
  6. A global perspective will be developed by exploring and understanding different student goals, priorities, and non-school responsibilities, and exploring how to adjust learning styles.
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning. The skills emphasized in this course are critical for successful learning and working in any context.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to actively participate during class activities including reflection, group brainstorming, class discussion, and others. Assignments outside of class will include weekly assignments and reflections related to the in-person lecture, and an asynchronous wellness video. Templates for several assignments will be provided.  Assignments will focus on developing the skill of the week (strategic planning, weekly planning, studying for exams, etc.), and weekly reflections will focus on wellness and can be written (∼250 words), audio or video. Students will prepare and share updates and assignment products each week, and coach each other on weekly discussion boards.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. A passing grade will be awarded for a course grade of 70% or higher. Students will be evaluated on in-class individual and group participation and discussion (10%), and weekly reflections (30%), and weekly assignments (60%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valerie Eviner is a professor of Ecosystem Management and Restoration in the Department of Plant Sciences. Her research, teaching, and outreach depend on collaborations among scientists, land managers, and policy makers to advance our fundamental ecological understanding, while addressing key challenges in our environment and society. Her training includes peer coaching focused on the strategies for success highlighted in this seminar. She continuously fine-tunes these strategies as she juggles her multiple interests of research, teaching, outreach to land managers, collaborations with policy makers, leadership in creating positive change, and outdoor adventures.

TITLE: Surfacing Environmental Anxiety, With a Focus on Impacts of AI
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
The use and applications of AI are growing rapidly. In contrast, our understanding of the impacts of AI use on the environment is still in its infancy. Nonetheless, there is growing concern that AI use may exacerbate existing environmental crises related to climate change.

Environmental crises like climate change can evoke feelings of fear, hopelessness, and anxiety. These feelings may arise as a consequence of directly experiencing effects of climate change. But they can also arise as a consequence of learning about climate change impacts on other human beings or on natural systems. And such feelings can inhibit a willingness to learn about and engage with solutions to challenges posed by climate change.

In this class we will gain experience with surfacing emotions related to environmental crises and explore feelings about how increasing use of AI may worsen the impacts of climate change. The first two weeks of the course will be instructional and help set the stage for student-led, small group presentations and discussions in the remaining weeks.

During the initial instructional phase of the course, students will first learn about Maslow's hierarchy of needs, beginning with the foundation of basic physiological needs and how these may be threatened by climate change impacts that in turn may be exacerbated by AI use. Then we will shift to a basic introduction to elements of emotional resilience applicable to development and maintenance of well-being in an era of accelerating climate change and the potential role of AI use in this acceleration.

After establishing this foundation, the course will transition to weekly small-group, student-led presentations and related discussions. A focus on interactive expressions of vulnerability will help build and reinforce a sense of community and belonging through human engagement rather than reliance on AI for our well-being.
OUTCOMES:
This course will encourage surfacing and expression of feelings related to, and development of skills for coping constructively with, societal, environmental, and ecological impacts of climate change and exacerbation of these by AI use. Each class meeting will begin with a brief (5-minute) period of anonymous expression and sharing in relation to prompts provided by the instructor via Mentimeter. These may ask students to share how they feel about the use of AI and its environmental impacts, or prompt them to practice techniques aimed at coping and emotional resilience such as expressions of gratitude, empathy, and awe. The overview provided during the first two instructional weeks of the course will help prepare students for these expressions. 

Small-group presentations and development of discussion prompts will give students practice with development and application effective oral communication skills. Weekly reading assignments chosen by each week's presenter group will also foster development of higher cognitive skills, including scientific literacy and critical thinking. Development and application of active listening skills will also engender cultivation of respect, empathy, and a sense of community.
ASSIGNMENTS: After the first two weeks of instructional content, students will work in small groups of 2-3 to make a brief (15 minute) presentation on a topic of their own choosing related to the focus of the course. Each week's presenting group may also assign an associated reading drawn from sources such as blogs, peer-reviewed literature, news articles, or book chapters. Guidance provided during the first two instructional weeks will help students distinguish between reliable and unreliable sources for the content of their presentations and associated reading material. This will help students learn concepts related to scientific literacy and critical thinking. Presentations will be followed by a group discussion led by that week's presenters using questions or prompts provided at the end of their presentation or, if based on assigned readings, at least 5 days in advance and posted to Canvas by the instructor. These prompts should focus on positive coping with environmental anxiety. Each group will present on and lead one discussion. Everyone will be expected to participate in weekly discussions. A final, brief written assignment will ask students to reflect on how to better develop agency over their own sense of environmental anxiety around climate change and AI usage.
GRADING:
A passing grade will be issued for scoring at least 70 points out of a total of 100 possible points.

Points will be awarded as follows:

30 points for leading a discussion

30 points for regular participation in discussions

40 points for completing the final written assignment
INSTRUCTORBIO: Eric Post is a professor of climate change ecology in the Dept. of Wildlife, Fish, and Conservation Biology. He studies impacts of climate change on plants, animals, and their interactions, primarily in the Arctic. He founded and serves as director of the Polar Forum at UC Davis, a research collective that seeks to raise awareness of climate change impacts on people and natural systems in the polar regions. He also founded and serves as PI on the APPLES Project, an initiative that provides training in climate change educational and research methods to K-12 teachers across the United States. He has previously taught First Year Seminars on "Surfacing Environmental Anxiety" and a Directed Group Discussion for the University Honors Program on "Climate Change, Misinformation, and their Impacts on Emotional Well-Being".

TITLE: Symmetry in Art and Science
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: This class will explore aspects of symmetry in life, art and science. How do we perceive symmetry? How does it affect the way we see the world? This class will involve discussions that should deepen our understanding and perception of symmetry in our environment. How is symmetry involved in pattern making, in music, in biology? What is the role of repetition? Can symmetry be quantified? The format will involve discussions among the participants with each student expected to bring interesting examples of symmetric situations to the class room. Hopefully the class will involve students from a broad range of majors with interests in the arts and in science. The ability to count, add and subtract will be the only math skills required.
OUTCOMES: The course is designed to facilitate discussion between students in various disciplines with a common interest in symmetry. The class is also designed to increase the participants' awareness and perception of symmetry. The students should emerge form the course with greater appreciation of symmetry and some ability to quantify aspects of symmetry. Students will learn about symmetry operations, patterning, and related ways to quantify symmetry. The instructor will approach the topic from a visual, tactile standpoint, but students will be encouraged to consider other non-visual aspects of symmetry.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class will involve extensive discussion of symmetry and how we perceive symmetry. Students will be expected to bring examples of symmetric objects or situations to class for discussion. They should be prepared to explain the reasons for the examples they have chosen. Each student will be expected to participate in two in-class presentations focused on an aspect of symmetry that they find interesting and to write two 500-100 word essays
GRADING: Participation in classroom discussions will account for 25% of the grades. Another 50% of the grade will be based on the in class presentations.  The two essays will account for 25% of the grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Alan Balch is a Distinguished Professor Emeritus in the Department of Chemistry and was Associate editor of the journal, Inorganic Chemistry. His research involves the study of molecular structure and the interaction of molecular structure with physical and chemical properties of molecules. Much of his work involved the highly symmetric fullerenes (Bucky Balls), which are closed cages comprised of only carbon atoms with a distinct inside and outside. He is also currently involved in the crystallization of luminescent gold(I) complexes, which may have sensor applications and has a group of undergraduate student working on this project in the lab.  He is also interested in the visual arts and is a passionate museum goer. He collects various modern works of art, particularly work in and on paper, a few of which may be relevant to this course.
