TITLE: Talking Tech: Interactions with LLMs and Voice-AI as an object of social science research
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: In this course, we will explore how humans interact with language-based artificial intelligence systems—such as large language models (LLMs) and voice-enabled AI assistants—as a subject of social scientific inquiry. Drawing from linguistics, human-computer interaction studies, psychology, sociology, communication studies, we will examine how people attribute human-like qualities to machines, how trust, empathy, and social norms shape these interactions, and how AI systems might influence behavior, identity, and decision-making. Through readings, discussions, and hands-on analysis, students will critically assess the implications of anthropomorphism, moral agency, and social influence in the age of conversational AI.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

 

  • Define and critically evaluate the concept of anthropomorphism in human-AI interaction.
  • Analyze how individual differences and social contexts influence perceptions of AI systems.
  • Apply social science theories to understand trust, empathy, and moral judgment in AI-mediated communication.
  • Assess the societal and ethical implications of LLMs and voice-AI in domains such as education, healthcare, and customer service.
  • Design and conduct basic empirical investigations into human responses to conversational AI.
  • Communicate insights about human-AI interaction.
ASSIGNMENTS:
There will be weekly materials to read or listen to before class. Then, there will be weekly short assignments for students to complete related to course material. The assignments will be posted on Canvas. Examples: Write a paragraph in response to a chapter we read and discussed in class. Write a proposal for a “thought experiment”: using some study or experiment we discussed in class, propose a modification, adaptation, or extension of it to address an issue raised in class. Find an example of a research article that addresses the topic we discussed in class. Report how you would explain the findings to a lay audience. 

Below is an example:

Assignment 1. Read the assigned and then respond to the questions below (500 words max).This assignment helps you connect ideas from the chapter to your own experiences:

  • Think about your own experience with voice technology (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant, voice in cars, etc.).
  • Write a response on the following:
    • When do you use voice assistants, and why?
    • Do you treat them like tools or conversational partners?
    • Have you ever felt frustrated, amused, or surprised by how they responded?
    • How does your experience compare to Mia’s story in the chapter?
GRADING:
Grading will be based on:

Participation in class (40%)

Weekly Assignments (40%)

Final Week Project (20%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Georgia Zellou is a linguist specializing in phonetics and laboratory phonology. She is a faculty member in the Department of Linguistics at UC Davis, where she also directs the UC Davis Phonetics Lab. Her research investigates how fine-grained phonetic detail is represented and processed in the cognitive system, with a focus on linguistic variation during human interaction with voice-based AI systems.

TITLE: The Origin of Your Identity Through the Lens of Eastern and Western Psychology
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS:
Are you curious about how you came to be who you are? Do you have unusual, perhaps even strange, quirks or fears that confound you as to their origin? Or perhaps you’ve encountered more difficult things, like the death of a loved one, addiction in your family or your own life. If you would like to take a deep dive into these facets of human being and learn more about how Eastern and Western psychology offer complementary perspectives on human development, then this class is for you.

We will do a deep dive into the book, “The Root of Trauma: Resolving Pain & Addiction Through the Lower Chakras,” with the author/psychologist, Dr. Melissa Grabau, to learn about energetics as it relates to who we are and how we function in the world. “The Root of Trauma” draws from work of Bessel Van der Kolk in “The Body Keeps the Score” and further explores how our physical ailments manifest from unprocessed trauma, and follows in the footsteps of Anodea Judith’s work, “Eastern Body, Western Mind,” which integrates Eastern Chakra theory with Western psychological models.

Classroom time is focused on group discussion of readings with frequent small group breakouts. Assessment is based on creativity of ideas in journal entries and peer reviews, as well as contribution to class discussion.
OUTCOMES:
The main objective of this course will be to explore the development of our own identities through a framework that integrates the concept of life force energy (often referred to as ‘prana’ or `chi’) and the Chakras. Other outcomes include:

  • Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of what addiction is, its progressive stages and how it impacts the mind and body.
  • Students will learn about the concept of life force energy and its fundamental importance in our physical and emotional health.
  • Students will learn foundational Chakra theory
  • Students will deepen their appreciation for how early childhood development lays down neural and emotional pathways that fundamentally shape who we come to be and how we experience ourselves and the world.
  • The role of choice and responsibility are emphasized as students are encouraged to take a more conscious role in their unfolding development.
  • Discussion, writing and presentations will provide opportunities to develop communication skills
  • Discussion will be framed around Mutual Invitation and RESPECT guidelines (Responsibility, Empathy, Sensitivity, Ponder, Examine, Confidentiality, Trust).
ASSIGNMENTS:
• Students will be expected to read 40-50 pages of material each week (1 to 1.5 hours)
  • Students will engage in weekly writing that will be reflection paragraphs from their reading (~500 words, ~1 hour)
  • Students will work either in pairs on presenting a topic of relevance to themselves and course or by themselves on a more creative outlet such as poetry (Total could be highly variable, but at least 2-3 hours, one time).
  • Each session will involve small group discussion.
  • Students will also be expected to read and respond (in a meaningful way) to at least three journal entries from fellow students each week (0.5-1 hour)
GRADING:
• Grades will be based on student class-room participation and peer review (25%), weekly writing (65%), and one presentation (10%).
  • Writing and presentation grades will be based on the level of creativity and engagement displayed by the student in applying personal experience/knowledge to what they read.
  • Participation grades are based on the extent to which students engage in discussion in class AND completion of peer reviews with reasonable substance.
  • Letter grades will be assigned according to typical criteria: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Hernes is a faculty member in the Department of Land, Air and Water Resources, specializing in river biogeochemistry, but extending more broadly into any environment that contains naturally formed organic compounds. As an avid outdoor enthusiast, his interest in the environment is not just professional, but personal.

Dr. Grabau is a clinical psychologist who has been working in private practice since 2002. She is a certified yoga teacher and integrates mind-body techniques into her work with clients. More recently she has broadened her understanding of consciousness and completed trainings in psychedelic therapy and ketamine therapy.

TITLE: The Plague Dogs: Who Gets to Decide Which Lives Are Worth Saving?
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS:
Richard Adams is most famous for writing “Watership Down,” which sneakily explores human societies through the guise of rabbits. His followup book was “Plague Dogs,” which not so sneakily explores the ethics of animal testing in the course of an adventure novel that follows the classic “hero’s journey.” What *is* sneaky, however, are the countless vignettes about bureaucracies, politics, reporters, sensationalism, the military, human character, and an exploration of how individuals (Snitter and Rowf are really metaphors for humans) respond to a system of exploitation and oppression, and Richard Adams is ruthless in his critique of how human systems create trauma.

Snitter and Rowf escape from an animal testing facility, and their reward is further exploitation leading to an all-out effort to hunt them down and kill them. In the meantime, they are merely trying to survive and hoping for a miracle.

Classroom time is focused on group discussion of readings with frequent small group breakouts and listening to passages from the wonderful audiobook narrated by Ralph Cosham, whose mastery of local dialects is spectacular. Assessment is based on creativity of ideas in journal entries and peer reviews, as well as contribution to class discussion.
OUTCOMES:
The main objective of this course is to enjoy a novel with subtle depth! Of course we will come back to the theme of animal testing repeatedly, but this is not a textbook! Other outcomes include:

  • Students will learn about the multiple levels of allegory that make up all great art: literal, allegorical, moral, anagogical. (The literal teaches what happened, the allegorical what to believe, the moral what should be done, the anagogical what it all means in the universe)
  • Students will identify cultural/societal themes that translate to our current day.
  • Discussion, writing and presentations will provide opportunities to develop communication skills
  • Discussion will be framed around RESPECT guidelines (Responsibility, Empathy, Sensitivity, Ponder, Examine, Confidentiality, Trust).
ASSIGNMENTS:
• Students will be expected to read 50-60 pages from Plague Dogs each week, depending on the edition (~1.5 hours)
  • Students will engage in weekly writing that will be reflection paragraphs from their reading (~500 words, ~1 hour)
  • Students will work either in pairs on presenting a topic of relevance to themselves and course or by themselves on a more creative outlet such as poetry (Total could be highly variable, but at least 2-3 hours, one time).
  • Each session will involve small group discussion.
  • Students will also be expected to read and respond (in a meaningful way) to at least three journal entries from fellow students each week (0.5-1 hour)
GRADING:
• Grades will be based on student class-room participation and peer review (25%), weekly writing (65%), and one presentation (10%).
  • Writing and presentation grades will be based on the level of creativity and engagement displayed by the student in applying personal experience/knowledge to what they read.
  • Participation grades are based on the extent to which students engage in discussion in class AND completion of peer reviews with reasonable substance.
  • Letter grades will be assigned according to typical criteria: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Hernes is a faculty member in the Department of Land, Air and Water Resources, specializing in river biogeochemistry, but extending more broadly into any environment that contains naturally formed organic compounds. As an avid outdoor enthusiast, his interest in the environment is not just professional, but personal.

TITLE: The Rest of the Old Testament?: The Deuterocanonical Books of the Christian Canon
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS: Why are there different versions of the Christian Old Testament? What are the stories or teachings in the texts that are not included in the Protestant Old Testament? This class covers those texts included in the Roman Catholic and Orthodox Christian Old Testament but not in the Jewish TANAK or Protestant Old Testament. These texts are frequently referred to as “deuterocanonical.” Students will read the texts in question, learn about their historical development and religious significance, as well as discuss the formation of the canons that resulted in their inclusion or exclusion. The course will engage students with interesting readings (likely unfamiliar to many students), fostering critical thinking and analytical skills, while at the same time explicating the differences in the canons. The class will not cover those texts which are universally accepted (those texts are taught in RST 21).
OUTCOMES: Students will learn the content of the Deuterocanonical Christian texts, be introduced to analysis of historical textual and canonical development, and develop understandings of theoretical approaches in the academic study of religious texts.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to complete the readings before attending class. Students will participate in class discussion and group activities during class. Students will write weekly responses to the course readings (not more than 150 words each week). Students will lead group discussions on selected, pre-arranged class meetings.
GRADING: Students will be evaluated on participation (40% of course grade). Class meetings will be heavily focused on discussion, and students will participate in group activities during class to facilitate participation. There will be no exams for the course, but each student will write five weekly responses to questions concerning the course readings (Students will choose the five responses they wish to write out of eight possible weeks. Each response is worth 10% of the course grade). Each student will also lead one class discussion (Leading the discussion will be worth 10% of the course grade).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Wendy Terry is a Lecturer in the Religious Students Department. She frequently teaches survey courses in addition to textually based courses on the Hebrew Scriptures and Christian New Testament. Her primary research is in the history of Christian Spirituality, with an emphasis in the medieval period on the British Isles and in France.

TITLE: The Toolkit for Shared Management and Cooperative Leadership
ELIGIBILITYTYPEDESC: Currently open to first year and transfer students
FOCUS:
“Local people have really begun to find a way that they can use a meeting as a tool for running their own lives. For having someone to say about it.”
— Bob Moses, early civil rights leader of the Student Non-violent Coordinating Committee 

Why don't we all have the basic literacies of being effective in groups? You learned to tie your shoes, you learned to read and write. Now learn to

  • resolve conflict
  • give and take feedback
  • hold space
  • build a team
  • manage time
  • exercise and share power
  • write a budget
  • tell a story
  • share accountability
  • take notes
  • plan an event
  • run a meeting
  • lead a project
  • and fulfill your duty to serve a community.

We will treat these as the basic literacies that everyone needs to fulfill a vision, everything from finishing a group project with classmates to starting a business or non-profit with friends; organizing a community or stewarding a democracy. In this class we will build a curriculum together, spend money together, surface our assumptions about how collective action works, and share ideas, hopes, and plans.
OUTCOMES:
Identify areas for personal leadership development

Execute a development plan

Develop cooperative leadership skills

Identify a preferred style of engagement/leadership/entrepreneurship/management/organizing
ASSIGNMENTS:
The primary mode of engagement with the course will be an hour of reading or other media per week. Assessment will be based on in-class check-ins elaborating a key theme and its personal relevance. Assignments will address the learning objectives with coverage of topics including:

* Reading overviews of competing paradigms of leadership

* Reading on the history of perspectives in management, organizing, and civics that center the work of developing other leaders as the work of leadership

* Completing assessments of personal leadership skills, and reflections on those assessments

* Training materials in conflict resolution, non-violent communication, time management, and other core skills.

 

Students will also be assessed during class with exercises around meeting facilitation, facilitation of challenging subjects, presentation and public speaking skills, argumentation, dialogue,  conflict resolution, non-violent communication. Presentations will give students the opportunity to direct course content. Students will give two 10-minute presentations over the course of the term, one personal and one focused on a relevant topic.
GRADING:
The course will use a letter grade with P/NP option.

50% of the grade will be based on performance on in-class assessments, the other 50% on out-of-class assessments.

The latter out-of-class 50% will be based on assessments of students' 5-minute recordings on weekly reading and media.

The in-class 50% will be divided as follows:

  1. 10% discretionary participation (high but important because of the nature of the course as a leadership development course)
  2. 20% for engagement in in-class facilitation exercises. Each student will have at least two opportunities to lead an activity.
  3. 20% for in-class presentations, 10% each
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Prof. Seth Frey is a scholar of commons governance and an organizer for the cooperative movement. As a professor at the University of California, he specializes in the science of community governance. He earned his Ph.D. in Cognitive Science and Informatics at Indiana University in 2013, and his B.A. from Berkeley. He was a researcher at Disney Research, a part of Walt Disney Imagineering. He also has 20 years' experience in grassroots community economic development, filling development, leadership, and founder roles for housing cooperatives. Seth is from Sunnyvale, CA.
https://enfascination.com/research

TITLE: AI Agents: From Prompts to Multi-agent Teams
FOCUS: We explore how to use AI agents for a more productive and rewarding day. Studies show that generative AI, like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, or DeepSeek, can quickly lead to increases of over 50% in productivity and over 70% in job satisfaction. Studies also show that students working with AI tutors can improve their performance from a C to an A. The key to sustainable success is converting these tools into AI agents that automate concrete demands. This starts with prompt engineering (the art of knowing how to talk with AI), and ends with building multi-agent systems consisting of AI agents with team-based division of labor between them. This course aims to provide students with the big picture of the AI paradigm and equip them with practical skills to reap immediate benefits.
OUTCOMES:
1. Research Process: Students are introduced to and researching appropriate methods and techniques of working with generative AI, i.e., in the form of Large Language Models (LLMs), which include prompt engineering techniques and agentic workflow building. 
  2. Discovery: Students have the opportunity to make relevant discoveries in a fastly changing and very young field, that are previously unknown to both student and instructor and thus require both exploration and evidence-based reasoning. This is done through hands-on exercises.
  3. Broadly Relevant Work: Beyond short-term trends, students get exposed to long-term perspectives of AI-human interaction, including the importance of data, the setting of reward functions through prompt engineering, and hands-on exercises aimed at resolving real-world challenges. 
  4. Collaboration: Student collaboration and communication are emphasized and encouraged as critical research skills. 
  5. Iteration: Students learn the iterative nature of research and scholarship by experiencing how prompts are the basis for agent personalization of AI, while agentic workflows are the basis of contextualization, and multi-agent systems are the basis of AI team orchestration.
ASSIGNMENTS:
25% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

25% is for the weekly assignments on prompt engineering (weeks 2-4). This explores the Personalization of LLMs. Exercises explore how to talk with an LLM? We are implementing a memorable, practical framework for daily application. Hands-on exercise: Explore a personal case study with a complete prompt engineering framework.

25% are hands-on assignments on AI agents (weeks 5-7). This explores the Contextualization of AI Agents. Exercises explore how workflows can be automated with agentic AI? We are designing professional assistants that can take autonomous action in the real world. Hands-on exercise: Create a customized workflow integrating LLMs, databases, and online search.

25% are hands-on assignments on multi-agent teams (weeks 8-9). This explores the Orchestration of teams of AI agents. Exercises explore how we tackle complex tasks by creating a team of diverse AI agents? We are using multi-agent systems to solve the challenges of hallucinations, robustness, biases, and complexity. Hands-on exercise: Experience the power of working with teams of diverse AI agents. Become a human resource manager of AI agents.
GRADING:
P/NP

25% grade is attendance & participation. 

25% are hands-on assignments on prompt engineering (weeks 2-4). 

25% are hands-on assignments on AI agents (weeks 5-7). 

25% are hands-on assignments on multi-agent teams (weeks 8-9).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor of Communication at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis on Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he provided technical assistance to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert and more on AI here: https://Communication.UCDavis.edu/news/UC-Davis-Professor-Martin-Hilbert-delivers-keynote-UC-Tech-2024-conference-addressing-ais

TITLE: Becoming an Extraordinary Human
FOCUS: No one wants to be ordinary. This course focuses on the personal development of the characteristics of human beings deemed extraordinary. Outcomes include enhanced concentration for higher-level cognition, increased capacity to handle stress, development of increased self-confidence, increased mastery of emotional and mental processes, development of physical awareness and control, and development of positive personal characteristics. Physical activities include movements and visualizations that revolve around a unique heart-centered meditation method, namely, Tamarkoz®.
OUTCOMES: Enjoy enhancing the personal attributes which foster the development of (1) Concentration & focus for higher-level cognition and academic success (2) Cross-culturally positive characteristics such as fairness, acceptance, respect, responsibility (3) Stress Mastery (4) Resiliency (5) Physical awareness and control.
ASSIGNMENTS: 1. Reading: The Extraordinary Human (In press) - (20 pages per week, 1.5 hours/week); 2. Completion of the daily, focused journal. (30 minutes per week); 3. Completing a one-time self-assessment survey at the beginning and end of the quarter, namely, the description of personal behavior and changes (2 hours per quarter)
GRADING: Grade breakdown would be 25% for class participation, 40% for assigned reading, 25% of keeping a journal, and 10% for completing the self-assessment survey at the beginning and end of the quarter. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Mohammad Sadoghi is an Associate Professor in Computer Science at UC Davis. He leads the ExpoLab research group that aims to pioneer a new exploratory data platform to unify secure transactional and real-time analytics. Additionally, he has a keen interest in understanding the true nature of the human being and uncovering one’s inner identity. He has been offering courses at UC Davis that focus on stress management and the student’s overall well-being centered around meditation.

Nasim Bahadorani is a wellness expert who specializes in lifestyle health behavior programs. She has her doctorate in public health from Loma Linda University and taught public health courses in the Health Sciences Department at California State University, Northridge. She holds a master’s degree in biomedical science from Midwestern University, College of Health Sciences. Her strong background in biomedical sciences coupled with her experience in public health provides unique expertise in understanding pathways of disease and knowing the strategic models and interventions to prevent them. She is a certified health education specialist and a certified health and wellness coach providing consultation for wellness programs. Her work is focused on lifestyle medicine with integrative approaches to disease prevention and health promotion.

TITLE: Biochemistry
FOCUS: Our course will give you hands-on experience in collecting and analyzing biochemistry data in the lab. We have eight experiments that you will conduct with your lab partners. In the course of these experiments, you will learn how to pipet and use various instruments in a biochemistry lab. In addition, we will also discuss how various classes in your freshman and sophomore years apply to upper division biochemistry courses.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn how to use biochemistry equipment to obtain experimental data and how to analyze and interpret the data. Effective communication will be learned as students work with their lab partners to acquire data and discuss experimental results. We will discuss the importance of ethics in reporting scientific data. We will develop confidence in performing biochemical techniques, calculations, and analyses of experimental data. We will develop a perspective of why students take lower division math, physics, chemistry, and English in preparation for upper division biochemistry. Biochemistry is a key component for students preparing for health related fields.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will submit eight lab reports during the quarter. They will write these reports outside of lab and turn them in at the beginning of class the following week. Each lab report will include a brief discussion of what we were trying to learn that day in lab, what skills were mastered, analysis of their experimental data, and a brief discussion of what they learned from their experimental data.
GRADING: Participation in class discussions (10%), handing in eight lab reports (80%), working well in a group using lab equipment (10%). Grading is P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Ken Hilt has taught biochemistry on the Davis campus for over 35 years. His courses include BIS 102, BIS 103, BIS 105, MCB 120, MCB 120L , and the freshman seminar for the last ten years. His interests are in protein chemistry, particularly enzymes and the effect of metals at their active site. Justin Yamada is a staff research associate in Molecular and Cellular Biology. He prepares chemicals and cells for use in the upper division biochemistry and genetics courses. He has assisted Dr. Hilt in teaching the freshman seminar class for the past five years.

TITLE: Biology of Aging: From contemporary theories to potential counter-aging therapies
FOCUS: Why do we age? Is it preventable? This seminar explores one of biology's most complex questions. We will investigate the contemporary scientific theories of aging, from the role of our genes and cellular damage to the latest research on potential counter-aging therapies.  Through guided readings, in-class discussions, and a final group presentation, you will learn to critically evaluate claims about aging in the popular media, and effectively explain the science of aging to a non-scientist.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the major biological theories of aging and the fundamental genetic and cellular mechanisms that drive it. They will develop the skills to critically assess media claims on anti-aging interventions and effectively communicate scientific findings to a non-scientific audience.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly reading assignments consist of one article from the popular press, and one from the primary scientific literature. Class time will include some lecture to cover basic concepts as well as discussion of the articles and the underlying science. There will be a short quiz before each class to test knowledge related to the readings. In the second half of the course, students will form small groups to research a specific topic in aging or a counter-aging strategy. Each group will present a 10-minute oral presentation to explain the complex science of your topic to a non-scientist audience, such as friends or family.
GRADING:
25% Class participation during in-class discussions
25% Weekly quizzes based on readings
50% Final in-class presentation

There will be no midterm or final exams. There will be quiz in Canvas each week, covering that week’s reading material.

Final grades will follow a straight scale (not curved): P = > 70%; NP = < 70%
INSTRUCTORBIO: Lifeng Xu is an Associate Professor in the Department of Microbiology & Molecular Genetics. She leads a research lab focused on understanding how telomeres and telomerase function in human diseases, including cancer and premature aging. She has taught classes on Recombinant DNA Techniques, Cancer Biology, and General Microbiology Laboratory.

TITLE: Blockchain and Social Change: An exploration of crypto, NFTs, DiFi, and the metaverse
FOCUS:
Blockchain & social change: an exploration of Crypto, Memecoins, NFTs, DiFi, and the metaverse

 

The blockchain is more than Bitcoin and Ethereum. While we'll have to study and understand both, we will explore the full array of possibilities of the blockchain's modern way of storing and processing data. The blockchain allows for a technological paradigm quite distinct from the centralized model championed by the likes of Google, Facebook, and Amazon. In this course, we explore aspects of social change promoted by the blockchain paradigm, and its main applications, including cryptocurrency (e.g. bitcoin, ethereum, cardano, etc), NFTs (Non-fungible tokens), DeFi (smart-contracts, etc), and the metaverse (3D virtual reality, etc), among other more common applications. We research existing and potential applications and how they affect society, including in health care, education, business. We also research the potential downsides of this new technological paradigm. The outcome of this course is a collection of innovative research done by students into the possibilities and potential dangers of the blockchain paradigm in society.
OUTCOMES:
Research Process: students are introduced to doing qualitative research on a new topic, mainly in form of a literature review of a blockchain application.

Discovery: after deciding on which application they want to explore, students research on their own, mainly in form of a literature review. This includes gathering and filtering information from project reports, whitepapers, magazine articles, and news outlets.

Broadly Relevant Work: this exploration informs ongoing research we are doing about the blockchain at our computational communication research lab. Students help to identify important case studies, strengths, weaknesses and threats of this new technological paradigm (which is quickly evolving).

Collaboration: Students work in teams to research a blockchain application (among topics like: crypto & finance; NFTs & art; metaverse & business; blockchain & public health, etc).

Iteration: After choosing an existing application of the blockchain, students go through 3 cycles: two presentation and one final write up. They find their own examples in the literature, and compare what they find with findings of others. At the end, the goal is to identify common characteristics of the blockchain paradigm. Through different iterations on different blockchain applications, students will learn how individual research among complementary applications can contribute to understanding a comprehensive technological paradigm.
ASSIGNMENTS:
20% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

20% is the first team presentation (about week 3-4). Students chose among different blockchain applications, and join a team of 2-4 other students to explore it. The first presentation explains the application.

30% is for the second presentation (about week 6-7). Students research and filter different case studies of the application of the blockchain, including their theoretical intention and their real-world applications. This includes the identification of benefits, challenges and potential downsides.

30% is for the final literature review write up. This consists of a teamwork effort to write up and report on the identified literature and case studies, and present the identified opportunities and threats in the style of an information literature review. In weeks 8-9 we review how to write this final literature review report.
GRADING:
P/NP

20% grade is attendance & participation. 

20% is the first team presentation (about week 3-4). 

30% is for the second presentation (about week 6-7). 

30% is for the final literature review write up.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis in Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems and has become increasingly concerned about the downsides of digital tech. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica in the campaign of Donald Trump a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he created the Information Society Program for Latin America and the Caribbean. Prof. Hilbert has provided technical assistance in the field of digital development to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, Die Welt, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert

TITLE: Campus Book Project: Reading Together
FOCUS:
This seminar focuses on reading, analyzing, and discussing the book selected by the Campus Book Project. This year, that book is Solito: A Memoir, written by Javier Zamora and recounting his journey alone from El Salvador to the United States when he was 9 years old to reunite with his parents. This seminar will function as a book club for the first half of the quarter, where we’ll read and discuss the book together. Then, we will branch out and research topics and other books that connect to the topics in Solito and help us go deeper in the analysis of this work.

We will also have the opportunity to meet the author during a public forum and secure $5 tickets to the Mondavi to the author’s talk. Participating in these events will be optional but highly recommended.

This seminar aims to foster a sense of community through the experience of reading and discussing the book together, practicing sharing ideas, listening to others, and merging different ideas to move us into deeper thinking. The seminar also aims at exploring various lines of inquiry connected to the themes that will emerge from our discussion of the book, thus helping us appreciate the interconnectivity between different types of sources, voices, and themes.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop effective communication skills, 
  2. Develop high cognitive skills 
  3. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy, 
  4. Develop focus and depth in one or more discipline, 
  5. Develop leadership skills, 
  6. Develop a global perspective, and 
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Lead discussion on one of the sections of Solito. Come up with discussion questions and be prepared to share your analysis of that week’s section. While this is not a formal group project, there will be multiple students assigned to the same discussion leading day.

Read a second book (of your choice) that is connected to Solito in either genre, themes, or topics. Present that book to the class and explain how it deepens our understanding of Solito. You might not be able to complete the reading before the quarter’s end, but try to read enough to be able to talk about the book in class.

Research a topic among the various topics and themes within Solito and present to the class how your research provides additional information that complicates our analysis of Solito. This is a group project.

Write a self-evaluation connecting how you have met the educational outcomes through the three main assignments. 

Students should be prepared to read two chapters (about 30 pages) of Solito per week during the first 5 weeks of the quarter. The second book and the research group project should not take more than 2 hours of work per week.
GRADING:
The class is graded pass/no pass. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

To obtain a Pass for this class, you will

  • Attend at least 8 class meetings.
  • Complete the assigned reading and participate in class discussion.
  • Read a second book of your choice and present it to the class.
  • Lead discussion on your assigned day.
  • Participate in the group research project and class presentation.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a continuing lecturer for the Writing Program. She teaches upper-division professional writing courses. She loves to read!

TITLE: Communicating Climate Change in the Small
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS: This seminar will explore the role of design, humanities, and social science perspectives in understanding the small-scale experiences of climate change. The course will re-focus the conversation on climate change from global-scaled environmental impacts , to one that recognizes the importance of the personal and everyday ways in which individuals experience these impacts. While many students may already understand the impacts of global environmental change (such as sea level rise, global warming, species and habitat loss, etc.), these impacts vary dramatically from region to region. It is the specific conditions within a region, city, neighborhood, and/or household that can determine whether you are vulnerable or resilient to these impacts. The presence of trees, infrastructure, transportation, or food sources can all play a role in determining a community's resilience. Access to education, health services, or a means of political participation can impact an individual's resilience. Many times, these small-scale nuances are over-looked when important decisions are made for a community's future. Despite that, small-scale interventions that support large-scale awareness can have huge impacts on behavior, policy, and technology, providing opportunities for global impact. In addition, design and humanities projects can give voice to those most vulnerable to climate change including under-represented communities within the political sphere. Thus re-scaling and re-focusing how we talk about climate change is an important effort to building resilience, and the goal of this seminar.
OUTCOMES: Students in this seminar will be introduced to the important role design and the humanities can play in addressing climate change and identify the significance of inclusion and diversification in achieving community resilience. Utilizing personal narratives, photography, drawing, installation, and/or curation, students in this course will explore new ways of communicating their personal experience of change and compare/contrast their experiences with communities around the world. This seminar will also introduce students to the allied environmental design professions and provide the preliminary skill sets associated with observing, evaluating, recording, and communicating the built environment surrounding them. Lastly, students in this seminar will develop their visual and oral communication skills through a range of presentation techniques.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students in this course will be expected to complete and discuss approximately 40 pages of course readings weekly and complete weekly digital and/or analogue sketchbook assignments. This will help students understand seminal text and case studies of humanities perspectives on climate change. As a final project, students will develop an analogue, digital or mixed-media design intervention (e.g. digital narrative, campus installation, film, etc.) expressing their personal understanding of and experience with climate change. Lastly, students will share their work with classmates and compare/contrast its content with communities around the world, presenting their work in a 10-minute oral presentation in class or publishing an online presentation of their work.
GRADING: Students will be expected to complete a weekly sketchbook and reading assignment, totaling 2 hours of work a week. In class discussions will be required to demonstrate comprehension of reading materials and share sketchbook assignments. Students will also produce a final design project towards the end of the quarter. The break-down for overall course grade is as follows: contribution of the student to in-class discussions (30%), maintenance of a personal journal/sketchbook (30%), development of a final design project (40%). Below are the expectations for earning specific grades: 100-90% (A+ to A-) - active and frequent engagement with course discussion, on-time completion of all sketchbook assignments, completion and comprehension of reading assignments, and successful completion of final project; 89-80% (B+ to B-) - engagement with course discussion, on-time completion of most sketchbook and reading assignments, on-time completion of final project; 79-70% (C+ to C-) - occasional engagement with course discussion, completion of at least two-thirds of sketchbook and reading assignments, and partial or late completion of final project. Any grade less than C- is not passing.
INSTRUCTORBIO: N. Claire Napawan is a landscape architect, urban designer, and academic who has designed and studied urban environments throughout the world for the past 11 years. She believes urban resilience requires transdisciplinary collaboration and community participation, and that designers and decision-makers need to explore new techniques for integrating urban communities with their built environments. This includes a particular focus on disadvantaged and vulnerable communities, such as urban youth. Examples of her design and research include: "Smart Sidewalks", the winning proposal for Reinventing Payphones in New York City, which seeks to address the digital divide and improve urban environmental resilience; "#OurChangingClimate", a research and design project and winner of the UC Davis Climate Action Champion Award that empowers youth to develop physical and digital exhibitions to document and share their experiences of climate change; and "FOGWASTE", a public art installation that seeks to bring greater awareness of San Jose’s vital infrastructures to community members and encourage greater urban environmental stewardship. Her projects represent award-wining proposals, commissioned by local municipalities, and/or exhibited at notable venues throughout the U.S. She is co-founder of the design collaborative Group Projects (grouprojects.com) and an associate professor in the Department of Human Ecology.

TITLE: Could a Robot Ever Be Conscious?
FOCUS: Are robots conscious? If they are, how would you know? How would they know? Is artificial intelligence the same thing as consciousness? If not, what distinguishes the two? What is it like to be a robot? In this course, you will learn how to answer these (and other) questions by studying various theories of consciousness and by applying those theories to episodes of the TV series Westworld. In doing so, we will consider how a diverse array of academic disciplines think about, and study, consciousness and consider each approach by applying it to different episodes of Westworld. Success in the course will require a careful viewing of Westworld Season One. The TV show includes depictions of graphic violence including sexual violence, sexual content, nudity, and coarse or crude language. While there is no textbook for this class, you will need access to all episodes in Westworld Season One, which can be purchased or streamed online. You will be graded on your weekly participation, a one page reflection paper, and a four page final paper.
OUTCOMES: By participating in this course, you will: (a) be able to articulate the major approaches to studying consciousness and the limitations of each approach, (b) be able to explain how consciousness influences our understanding of, and expectations for, robots and artificial intelligence, (c) be able to apply different theories of consciousness to the robot "Hosts" in Westworld, (d) develop and practice effective communication skills, (e) develop high cognitive skills, and (f) develop focus and depth in one or more discipline.
ASSIGNMENTS: You will need access to all episodes in Westworld Season One. Season One can be purchased online via Amazon or iTunes. You can stream Westworld if you have a subscription to HBO Go or HBO Now. You will watch one episode of Westworld each week (1 hour) and prepare to participate in class discussions by doing the weekly readings (all readings less than 20 pages per week). To facilitate focused and lively discussion of the assigned readings, you will prepare one brief Question and Contention (Q&C) paper during the quarter in which you either: (a) forward a contention and supporting argument; or (b) pose an important question and tentative answer. The contention or question must be applied to that week’s episode of Westworld. You will also need to prepare two weekly questions. These questions should reflect points of uncertainty about that week's reading, episode, or the relationship between the reading and the episode. Lastly, you will write a four page final paper where you apply course concepts to the ideas considered in Westworld. Specifically, you must pick a theoretical topic from the reading, and consider it in the context of the TV series.
GRADING: The class is graded using the following letter grade ranges: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). Your grade is earned through class participation (30%), weekly questions (20%) the Question and Contention paper (20%), and the final paper (30%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Richard Huskey (Ph.D. University of California, Santa Barbara) is an Associate Professor in the Department of Communication, principle investigator in the Cognitive Communication Science Lab, researcher in the Computational Communication Research Lab, researcher at the Center for Mind and Brain, and an officer of the International Communication Association's Communication Science and Biology interest group. He studies how motivation influences the attitudes people hold, and the behaviors they adopt. He researches these questions using a variety of methodological techniques including: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), computational methods, and lab-based experiments.

TITLE: Damaging Effects of Pesticide Materials On Low-income Families in Salad Bowl Communities
FOCUS:
In this course we will have conversations and discussions about the astonishing damage caused to low income families in agricultural communities by the use of materials in pesticide sprays. The deliberate location of farm homes for poor families are known to cause birth defects in children due to inhalation of gas phase materials in the pesticides. Furthermore, disposal of toxic materials into graves (landfills) will be discussed  Information will be presented through lectures, readings, discussions and visual media.

 Topics:

  1. Damage caused by Pesticide Materials.
  2. The good and bad of pesticides
  3. Are poor workers in farms exposed to bad sprays
  4. What must be done for this problem
  5. Identify new strategies to deal with known damage
  6. Hurdles on the way to a solution
OUTCOMES:
The educational outcomes for this seminar are:

Students will also learn the skills necessary to alertly observe, evaluate, and develop  personal strategies for effective online learning. I will provide intentional and specific guidance for how to write a 4-6 page paper.

2. We will ask students to write weekly reflections about the readings and/or the topic of their choice, 3.
 
paper topic options will evolve over the quarter with my support and discussions. Gradual evolution will be evaluated and graded a the quarter progresses. 

4. Students will learn about to pesticide materials in sprays in liquid, gas, and gel forms. We will speak of new discoveries of agricultural soil additives and keeping food products clean.

5.Students will be given the choice to explore their own interests and write a paper to enhance their written and oral communication skills. Collaborative team efforts will be encouraged via conversations and technical discussions
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments

Students will assigned topics on pesticide materials and advances on the agriculture horizon in the new world. They will be given complete to freedom to choose their own topic by reading the literature or news worthy articles. When appropriate some research lab experiments will be done by groups or individuals. Field trips to Salinas Valley or fields in and around Davis will be a part of the Freshman Seminar.
GRADING:
This will  be a 2 unit letter graded course.

50% grade based on class participation, giving talks, and engaging in discussions as well as reporting progress on the essay paper

50% will be be based on a 4 to 5  page final essay written on the topic of their choice,
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Professor Risbud has a wide-range of teaching interests specially for Freshman courses. He has 35  years of experience at UC Davis in disciplines ranging from engineering and applied science to geology, food science, physics and chemistry. Due to his interest in teaching Freshman Seminars he has initiated and taught numerous seminars on internships, classical music, and similar topics.  He is experienced  in applying diversity practices to student, staff and faculty recruitment; he served on Chancellor's Committee on Recruitment of Women and Underrepresented Faculty. For nine years he served as the Director of the UC-Davis campus-wide Internship and Career Center. During this leadership role he built industry contacts and created the Diversity Leadership Development Certificate Program (DLDP) in collaboration with staff and faculty of the Leadership and Learning Center, Cross Cultural Center, Women’s Resource and Research Center, LGBT Resource Center, and the Office of Campus Community Relations.

Beyond everything else Professor Risbud enjoys Freshman level teaching of things that excite his curiosity the most: what new materials like pesticides will change our future in negative or positive ways. He wants to convey this message to our Freshman/Sophomore students and get them engaged in this world of research and inquisitive enquiry.

TITLE: Death Positivity – Embracing Death through Design
FOCUS:
This 2-unit seminar introduces students to the Death Positive Movement and the role of mortality in shaping identity, memory, and meaning. By looking at cultural and philosophical perspectives from around the world, students will consider how different societies confront death and how these practices inform the way people construct meaning in their own lives.

Through readings, discussions, and hands-on workshops, students will learn to apply design tools—research, ideation, prototyping, and critique—to create a personal Memento Mori. This artifact serves as a way to explore how design can capture and express identity, hold memory, and give form to meaning in the face of mortality.

Students often describe the course not as easing discomfort with death, but as opening a door to conversations they had never engaged in before. By connecting design practice with reflection on mortality, the seminar encourages students to think more intentionally about how they engage with the present moment.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this seminar, students will:

  • Become familiar with the Death Positive Movement and its efforts to foster open discussions about mortality, reducing stigma and creating space for reflection.

  • Explore cultural practices and beliefs around death from diverse traditions, gaining perspective on how societies shape identity, memory, and meaning in the face of mortality.

  • Apply design processes (research, ideation, prototyping, critique) to develop a personal Memento Mori—an artifact that connects personal reflection with cultural insight.

  • Examine the role of technology and design in contemporary memorial practices, including Thanatechnologies and digital mementos, and reflect on how these tools influence ongoing relationships with memory and loss.

  • Use design as a way to open conversations about mortality, deepening their ability to connect academic exploration with personal experience and leaving them with strategies for thinking about identity, memory, and meaning in their present lives.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Weekly Readings & Reflections (20%)

Write 1–2 page reflections connecting assigned texts on death to personal experiences, cultural practices, and potential design opportunities.

2. Agora Discussion – Philosophies of Death (10%)

Participate in seminar-style debates on Western, Eastern, and Indigenous philosophies of death, exploring how identity, memory, and meaning are shaped.

3. Maps of Meaning Project (Midterm) (30%)

Individually analyze the symbolic, functional, and cultural meanings of a chosen object, then collaborate to design a collective “amalgam object” representing shared insights.

4. Creative Project – Memento Mori (Final) (40%)

Create a personal artifact that reflects themes of mortality, identity, memory, and meaning. Accompanied by a 3-page design statement documenting the concept, process, and personal reflection.

 

Extra Activity – Guest Lecturer: Ann Savageau

Hear from artist Ann Savageau on how art and design can address mortality, culture, and resilience, inspiring students to see design as storytelling.
GRADING:
• Weekly Readings and Reflections: 20%

  • Group Presentation: 10%

  • Creative Projects: 70%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Jean has published articles in international peer-reviewed journals, including “Tinkering Methods: Primordial Thoughts in Design Thinking,” presented at the 22nd International Conference on Engineering Design (ICED 19). In 2019, he was one of 16 graduate fellows selected globally for the “Useful Fictions” Symposium in Paris. There, he collaborated with scientists from École Polytechnique, culminating in a group exhibition at Montmartre that visually represented scientific discoveries.

By focusing on innovative design thinking methods and UI/UX, Jean explores human factors, rapid prototyping, illustration, animation, and design as powerful communication platforms. His ultimate goal is to empower people with the tools and knowledge to pursue meaningful work.

As a first-generation college student, Jean deeply values education and understands its transformative power. He is passionate about guiding students through their academic journeys and is committed to keeping mentorship opportunities open, providing support and guidance to help them unlock their potential. Jean's involvement with nonprofit organizations has been a significant part of his career path and development, reflecting his dedication to using design and technology for positive social impact.

TITLE: Demystifying and Unlocking the Beauty of Chinese Characters
FOCUS:
Have you ever dreamed of learning Chinese but felt overwhelmed by its writing system? You are not alone! Chinese characters can seem mysterious and puzzled — so different from English or Spanish that you cannot guess their pronunciation or meaning just by looking. No shortcuts, no phonetic clues… just pure pictures!

But fear not — you have come to the right place.

This course is designed to demystify Chinese characters and unlocking their beauty. It provides with you a strong foundation in understanding how they work, where they come from, and what they represent. Through five rich and interconnected chapters, you will explore:

  1. Stroke, Radical, and Structure Analysis: Learn how Chinese characters are built — from basic strokes to the two-dimensional structure — and how a few specific radicals reveal meanings.
  2. The Six Principles of Character Formation: Discover the traditional framework that explains how characters are created and used, such as pictographic, indicative, semantic compound, and semantic-phonetic compound.
  3. Chinese Etymology and Historical Development: Trace the evolution of characters through dynasties and script styles, and understand how history shaped the writing system.
  4. Appreciating Calligraphy Styles: Explore the artistic side of Chinese writing — from oracle bone script to standard script — and learn to recognize the beauty in brushstrokes.
  5. Philosophy and Values Embedded in Characters: Uncover how Confucian, Daoist, and other philosophical ideas are reflected in the Chinese writing system.

By the end of this course, Chinese characters will not be mysterious — they will feel like a story, a system, and a piece of living culture.

No prior knowledge of Chinese language or culture is required to take this course. It is designed for beginners and anyone curious about Chinese characters. In order to create a supportive environment that maximizes learning, the seminar curriculum is designed with non-native speakers in mind.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

  • Describe and analyze Chinese characters: Understand basic strokes, identify character structures, and interpret meanings through radicals.

  • Classify characters using the Six Principles: Categorize Chinese characters based on the six principles of formation and usage, and explain the reasons behind this categorization.

  • Explore the historical development of Chinese scripts and their cultural contexts: Recognize major Chinese script styles and understand the historical and cultural backgrounds from which they emerged, and foster appreciation for their diversity. (Global Diversity)

  • Appreciate calligraphy and embedded philosophies: Develop an appreciation for various calligraphy styles and gain insight into the philosophies and values reflected in Chinese characters. (Global Awareness)

  • Create and present a cultural calligraphy project: Design a final project featuring Chinese calligraphy and share personal reflections on the cultural themes expressed through your work. (Global Action)
ASSIGNMENTS:
The course assignments and activities include the following three categories.

  1. Weekly Preparation
    Before each seminar, students are expected to spend 1 to 1.5 hours completing the assigned materials. This includes reading or watching the weekly theme and completing the corresponding homework assignments.
  2. In-Class Participation
    Active participation in class discussions is essential. The instructor will provide demonstrations and feedback, and students will engage in hands-on practice with Chinese script writing and experience calligraphy techniques during class sessions.
  3. Final Project
    For the final project, students will design a Chinese calligraphy product that reflects the cultural values and philosophical concepts explored throughout the course. Each student will present their design in a 3 to 5 minute presentation, explaining the creative philosophy behind their work and sharing it with their classmates. A showcase or exhibit for the final project will be scheduled to encourage cross-cultural dialogue and community building.
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

  • Class Participation and Discussion: 20%
  • Weekly Reading/Watching Assignment: 30%
  • In-class Script Writing and Calligraphy Work: 15%
  • Final Project: 35%
    • Calligraphy Product and Cultural Reflection: 20%
    • Oral Presentation: 15%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Yutian Tan is a continuing lecturer in Chinese in the Department of East Asian Languages and Cultures at UC Davis. She holds a Ph.D. in Chinese Linguistics from The Ohio State University, an M.Phil. in Linguistics from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, and a B.A. in Chinese from Peking University.

Before joining UC Davis, Dr. Tan served as a visiting instructor at the College of the Holy Cross in Worcester, Massachusetts. At UC Davis, she teaches a wide range of courses in Chinese language (beginning, intermediate, and advanced levels), linguistics, and culture.

A native speaker of both Cantonese and Mandarin, Dr. Tan also studied Japanese for two years during college and is currently learning Spanish through Duolingo. Her research interests include second language acquisition (SLA), Chinese dialectology — particularly Cantonese — and regional cultural studies. She is especially passionate about incorporating active learning strategies into her teaching and enhancing students' visibility and engagement in assessment settings.

Outside of academia, Dr. Tan enjoys ice skating with her daughter.

TITLE: Demystifying Undergraduate Research in Neuroscience
FOCUS: The seminar course is designed for students who are interested in undergraduate research in the biomedicine field and neuroscience in particular and those who plan to apply for graduate or medical schools. As a new undergraduate student, you may know little about what “real” biomedical research activities (rather than high school labs) look like in the science laboratories across UC Davis campus and its medical campus. You may also think about joining a research lab to pursue your interest and spark your curiosity toward science but don’t know how. This seminar is intended to demystify biomedical and neuroscience research and provide first-hand experience on applying for undergraduate researcher positions at UC Davis and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
Laboratory research seems puzzling and complicated to newly admitted undergraduate students. Applying for a lab position as an undergraduate student is even more intimidating for those who do not have any prior research experience or lab skills. This seminar will cover topics that are related to biomedical research (with a focus on neuroscience research) such as why you need research experience, how a research laboratory works, how you can get along with other lab members at different training levels,  what common research tools and methods are, and what types of research the Instructors are doing, among others. This seminar will also guide students though how to find a research lab, how to join a laboratory to start the research journey at UC Davis, and how to extract useful and digestible knowledge from reading scientific papers among others.  At the conclusion of this seminar course, students are expected to 1) feel confident about biomedical research through interacting with the instructors, 2) know basic steps of applying for undergraduate  research positions in a research lab, and 3) develop skills of reading and presenting scientific papers.

Students are encouraged to use the Handshake platform (https://urc.ucdavis.edu/handshake) for discovery research positions.
ASSIGNMENTS:
In the first part, the instructors will lead discussion on topics including, but not limited to, research laboratories, research hypotheses and performance, research funding and publishing, undergraduate research opportunities, steps of research a science lab prior to joining a lab, analyzing and reading research papers. The first part will be in the interactive format of questions and answers. Students are highly encouraged to raise questions and provide responses to those raised by their peers.  

The second part is science paper reading and presentation, which is an essential component as an undergraduate researcher in a science lab (equivalent to “journal club” ). Each group of students will be assigned with a research paper or choose their own paper if applicable. All students are required to study the assigned research article. Students within one group are expected to work collaboratively with their team members on researching the articles, preparing a 20-min presentation, delivering the presentation, and leading the class discussion of the presented papers.

At the conclusion of the seminar, each student is required to write a final essay (2 pages maximum) reflecting what they’ve learned from the seminar and what they want to learn if they retake the seminar course.
GRADING:
Attendance for the first class is mandatory for this seminar course. No registration is allowed after the first class.

The grading system is based on “pass/fail”. The pass grade is 70 points or above. Grades will be assessed on class attendance (20%), class engagement and discussion (20%, assessed by the quantity of Questions and Answers raised by each student), collaboration within a group (20%), group presentation (30%), and final reflection essay (10%).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Fuzheng Guo, PhD is an Associate Professor in the Department of Neurology at UC Davis School of Medicine. His research interest has been centered on glial and myelin biology/pathology in the healthy and diseased brain. Dr. Guo's laboratory is well-funded by NIH and non-federal agencies. In addition, Dr. Guo has been actively conducting services to the University and the School of Medicine including medical school admissions. 

Meina Zhu, MD, PhD is a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Neurology at UC Davis School of Medicine. Her research interest is to discover novel cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying brain development and injury with a focus on brain white matter injury.

Please visit www.guo-laboratory.org for more information.
