TITLE: Disability Studies, NeuroDiversity, Mentorship...and How To Be An Ally
FOCUS:
There are 350 inclusive college programs serving students with intellectual disabilities around the country, yet California has very few.

There is only one four-year inclusive college program in California: The UC Davis  Redwood SEED (Supported Education to Elevate Diversity) Scholar Program. 

This is the first of its kind in California!

Our first 8 graduates celebrated this past spring!

Understanding disability, neurodiversity, the history of this often marginalized population will be integral to our course. Students will develop the skills and learn the crucial ways students without disabilities can mentor and ally with them. How can the average undergrad be an ally?

Students will also learn about neurodiversity, the unique assets and challenges of people with intellectual disabilities; their K-12 systemic educational experiences and post-secondary educational options; their path to employment and a living wage.

Students will meet and explore best practices and understand universal design for learning.

Students will also experience active participation in conversations about allyship and mentorship with young adults with intellectual disabilities and advocates.

A summative video project will be required: To introduce SEED Scholars to the UC Davis campus, typical student activities and/or your favorite inside scoop about campus.

*This seminar is ideal - but not limited to - students with these majors/minors: nutrition, human development, neuroscience, education, psychology, behavioral sciences, exercise, and physiology.

For those that excel, students will be offered an opportunity - potentially paid work or work-study - to mentor SEED Scholars.
OUTCOMES:
Students will:

  1. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy by:

- understanding what neurodiversity means

- gaining familiarity with the systemic barriers adults with intellectual disabilities must face every day, including academic, social/emotional, employment, and health

- developing an understanding of self-determination, dignity of risk, ableism, and opportunities for typical students to offer support and allyship to this marginalized and often overlooked population

- seeing the positive societal impact of presuming competence, holding space for, and holding high expectations for someone with an intellectual disability while at the same time understanding that support is often necessary for success.

 

     2. Develop leadership skills by:

- exploring a cross-discipline approach to research and employment opportunities

- developing mentorship skills via a mentor toolkit

- developing the soft skills necessary to work with a diverse group of people in the workplace

- connecting with other students and community members while growing in awareness at the diversity of lived experiences in our campus community

*This seminar is ideal - but not limited to - students with these majors/minors: nutrition, human development, neuroscience, education, psychology, behavior sciences, exercise, and physiology.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will:

- Keep a weekly journal of reflections based on one reading assignment or multi-media presentation per week (Expectation: one page of writing per week)

- Participate in a group discussion board 

- 15 - 30 minute interview with a young adult with an intellectual disability facilitated during a seminar class - students will share key takeaways during class and on discussion board

- Create a 2-3 minute video that would be an accessible support for a beginning Redwood SEED Scholar at UC Davis
GRADING:
This seminar will be a Pass/No Pass grade.

The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

The seminar coursework will be graded with the following weight:

Weekly Journal - 10%

Discussion Board - 10%

Interview - 25%

Class Participation - 15%

Summative Video - 40%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Beth Foraker was a Teacher Education Supervisor in the School of Education's post-graduate Multiple Subject Credential/Masters Program for elementary school teachers. Now, she is the Director for the UC Davis Redwood SEED (Supported Education to Elevate Diversity) Scholars Program for students with intellectual disabilities.This is a partnership with the UC Davis Office of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion, UC Davis Continuing and Professional Education and the UC Davis MIND Institute.

She is an advocate for the full inclusion of people with disabilities in school and in all facets of adult life: employment, housing, community involvement. She works to build continuums of inclusion: preschool through college inclusion opportunities so that inclusive communities can develop and thrive. She works to facilitate cross-discipline collaboration, community collaboration along with amplifying family and self-advocate voices so that full belonging is possible.

TITLE: Discovering East Asia
FOCUS:
East Asia plays a strong role in global affairs. The region is diverse in political, cultural, economic, and ethnic terms. Its parts (e.g. Japan, China, Taiwan, and South Korea) are interconnected as well as linked to other parts of the world in myriad ways. This seminar introduces students to political and cultural issues in East Asia. It serves as a gateway for understanding the region and building academic and research interests on one or more of its parts. Topics of discussion include, among others, China’s global relations, social media, and theater performances, Japanese folklore and women writers, South Korea’s soft power, Taiwanese poetry, and Hong Kong cinema.

This is a team-taught seminar that draws on the research expertise of UC Davis faculty across four different fields: Communications, Comparative Literature; East Asian Languages and Cultures; and Sociology. On the level, the faculty help students understand the political economy of East Asian societies. On another level, they use folk culture, art, and literature as examples to deepen student understanding of the societies.

Students will gain multilayered understanding of East Asia and the lives of its peoples.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students read, write about, and discuss assigned readings. They acquire multiple disciplinary perspectives on political and social institutions as well as cultures and ideas.
  2. Students acquire knowledge of and reflect on the cultural contexts of lives and livelihoods in complex societies against the backdrop of their own cultural context.
  3. Students learn how to assess sources as well as data and evidence.
  4. Students develop skills in critical reading and reasoning.
  5. Students gain confidence in public speaking and communication.
  6. Students engage in teamwork to identify and address global and regional challenges.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Review the assigned material each week..
  2. Write a 400-word response to the material each week. 
  3. Read the weekly responses of your classmates and consider the issues raised.
  4. Mandatory participation in classroom discussion. Everyone speaks! 
  5. An end-of-quarter team presentation of a global-scale challenge in East Asia.
GRADING:
This course uses the P/NP scale. The P grade is awarded for work that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

  1. Students are evaluated for the quality of their Canvas entries (50 percent). Canvas entries that appear after will not be counted.
  2. Students are evaluated for their participation in class discussion and the end-of-quarter presentation (50 percent).
  3. Students who miss three weeks of class after Week 1 will receive NP.
  4. Students who do not organize an end-of-quarter presentation must make it up with additional written work.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Eddy U is Professor of Sociology, Director of East Asian Studies, and the organizer of the seminar. He studies China and East Asia.

Xiaomei Chen is Distinguished Professor Emeritus of East Asian Languages and Cultures. Her focus is Chinese theater and performance.

Michael Dylan Foster is Professor of East Asian Languages and Cultures. He researches Japanese folklore and culture.

Sheldon Lu is Distinguished Professor of Comparative Literature. He has written books on Asian cinema.

David McCourt is Professor of Sociology. His research focuses on international relations.

Cindy Shen is Professor of Communication. She studies social media in China

Michiko Suzuki is Professor of East Asian Languages and Cultures. Her work examines gender, literature, and culture in Japan.

Michelle Yeh is Distinguished Professor of East Asian Languages and Cultures. She studies poetry, literature, and culture in China and Taiwan.

TITLE: Doing Change-Oriented Research: Studying the Impacts of Deportation Raids
FOCUS:
The United States is home to 47.8 million immigrants, the majority of which have government-sanctioned status in the country. Immigrants make invaluable contributions to society and are owed dignity, just as are all humans. Despite this, immigrants are often exploited, abused, imprisoned, and deported--often through degrading and dehumanizing processes. People are losing protected status and being swept up and imprisoned without warning. This causes severe distress to them, their community, family, and friends. Stories circulate of people staying home or otherwise limiting their mobility for fear of being detained. Constrained mobility has a severe impact of people's quality of life. People miss work, medical appointments, school, extracurriculars, and lack access to food, outdoor space, socialization, church, and other essentials.
 
In this course, students will help conduct a study on the impact of current deportation activities on people's mobility. Students will have the opportunity to learn survey and interview study design, implementation, and analysis. They will have the opportunity to talk with community research partners trusted by immigrants to learn best practices in collecting data in ways that are safe, approachable, and helpful to the people taking part in the study. Most importantly, students will be part of a research team witnessing and documenting the serious impacts experienced by immigrants in the United States in this moment.
OUTCOMES:
Students will learn how to conduct research using surveys and interviews.
Students will learn special considerations when researching with vulnerable populations.
Students will draw preliminary conclusions based on analysis of newly collected data.
Students will work with in teams to ensure validity of research instruments and analysis techniques.
Students will learn how this study relates to previous research on immigration and mobility justice.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will read three articles and create an annotated bibliography in the first three weeks of the class. Students will work in groups to conduct a beta test of research instruments and offer suggestions for improvement in week four. Students will analyze preliminary survey results using descriptive statistics and/or coding and present results in a 10-minute presentation in week 6. Students will work in groups to write a draft (week 8) and final research report (week 10), with each student contributing approximately 2 pages.
GRADING:
Grading scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).

Annotated bibliography: 20%

Research instrumentation testing: 20%

Presentation of survey analysis: 30%

Final report: 30%
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Rebolloso McCullough, PhD runs the UC Davis Feminist Research Institute. She seeks to transform research culture by bridging movement work with academia. She is PI on two NSF studies that integrates justice-oriented frameworks from feminist/critical race science studies into STEM graduate education. She researches mobility justice, environmental justice, and community-university relations. McCullough applies her expertise in ethnographic methods, cultural analysis, and power dynamics to create community-driven research partnerships that span the disciplines. She earned her PhD in Cultural Studies with an emphasis on Feminist Theory at UC Davis.

TITLE: Embodied Creativity and Improvisational Games
FOCUS: How can we be creative? In this seminar we will explore creativity through embodied activities, we will write and we will move, we will read and we will play. The course will be taught in a theatre/dance studio. Drawing on many traditions inside and outside the university, including theatre games, movement scores, experimental ethnography, explicative interviewing (microphenomenology), collaborative experiments, we will explore how we act when put on the spot, when we are in a group solving a problem together, and when we attend to our own bodies and those of others while doing these. Improvisational games from theatre and dance are excellent tools for learning how to listen, how to talk and move together, and how to become curious about oneself, others, and the world. They are also fascinating and fun ways to practice forms of creativity.
OUTCOMES: Students will (1) develop effective communicational skills through practicing improvisational games which train in how to listen, think quickly, and respond; (2) cultivate ethics, respect and empathy by participating in intense group activities; (3) develop cognitive skills through analyzing individual and group behavior in realtime and problem-solving; (5) develop leadership skills through engaged public speaking and creative scenario exploration; (6) prepare for lifelong learning by being introduced to many disciplines of embodied and group training that are mostly taught outside the university.
ASSIGNMENTS: Classes will be two hours per week and will include discussion of readings (limited to 10 -15 pages per week) and the practice of individual group improvisational and movement exercises aimed to increase physical/mental awareness and creative response. For a final project students will work in groups to develop their own creative improvisational exercise out of class and share weekly progress. Out of class assignments include reading, developing group project exercises, practicing, and writing up experiences with practice (weekly 2-3 paragraphs posted to Canvas): these will take no more than 4 hours per week total. Students must attend all class sessions (or do a makeup assignment for each assignment missed) and participate in class activities and discussions.
GRADING: The course grade will be: practice reports (30%), final project (presentation exercise and writeup, 40%), class participation (30%). A student needs to achieve at least a C- (70%) in each of the core areas in order to receive a passing grade. Grading: 100-90 (A), 89-80 (B), 79-70 (C), 69-60 (D), <59 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Joseph Dumit is Chair of Performance Studies, and Professor of Science & Technology Studies, and Anthropology. He studies brains, minds, bodies, drugs, media, virtual reality, capitalism, gaming, and improvisation. He has written two books: Picturing Personhood: Brain Scans and Biomedical Identity, and Drugs for Life: How Pharmaceutical Companies Define Our Health. He is also a co-founder of the ModLab for game studies and game development (where he's currently making a game on fracking), and works with the KeckCAVES on 3D development for science and the arts. http://dumit.net  

Ileanna Sophia Cheladyn is a Canadian dance artist and PhD student in sociocultural anthropology. Her practice prioritizes collaboration, care, and delight. She works with improvisation, slowness, and disorientation to invite meaningful shifts in technique, aesthetics, and relations of power. www.ileannacheladyn.com

TITLE: Engaging in Learning Design: Student-Parent centered classrooms
FOCUS:
1. COURSE DESCRIPTION

This course will engage students in participatory design research in Education with the intention to engage students, as the key stakeholders, in designing a classroom that is supportive of student-parents.

This course provides students the knowledge and skills to plan and implement qualitative research projects that are informed by critical and feminist research traditions. The class will focus on existing literature and provide students with entry points to the research process, with special emphasis on parenting students. Topics to be covered include the foundation for current pedagogical practice, application of culturally nuanced theoretical models and applications of critical research methods; methods of qualitative data collection and analysis, and policy analysis.
OUTCOMES:
III. COURSE OBJECTIVES

Overall Goal: Guide students in the development and use of mixed-methods to conduct qualitative reserch, emphasizing the experiences of parenting students

Specific Objectives:

  • To gain an understanding of the qualitative research process and participatory research approaches to research.
  • To gain an understanding of critical theories in education
  • To broaden your understanding of diversity issues in Higher Education
  • To contribute a novel perspective to policy issues in Higher Education
ASSIGNMENTS:
The weekly schedule as well as the page count for the readings and written assignments are below. The readings are also provided in the attached syllabus: 

  • Week 1: Reading: Banking Concept 15pgs

  • Week 2 Reading: Student-Mother Testimonio 16pgs

  • Week 3: Reading: Student Identity 16pgs

  • Week 4: Assignment: Personal history 3pgs

  • Week 5: Reading: RAC Solorzano 15pgs[1] 

  • Week 5: Reading: Photovoice 15pgs

  • Week 7: Assignment: Photovoice Project (multimodal) 4 images, 2pgs

  • Week 8: Reading: Critical Policy Analysis, Syllabus, OSSJA webpage 8pgs

  • Week 9: Assignment: Examining artifacts 4pgs

  • Finals
GRADING:
Attendance and Participation:

Active participation and engagement are crucial for succeeding in this class.

Testimonio: Educational History

Students will write a 3-page paper using testimonio exploring their educational history.

Photovoice Project

Students will do a photovoice project taking a minimum of 4 pictures of places or things that impact your learning and classroom experience.  

Examining Artifacts

Students will engage in qualitative artifact analysis.

Readings will not be graded. Readings give background about research methods The course uses labor based grading. Students receive points for completion of assignments that are equally weighted at 30 points each.  Participation is 10 points.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Daniel Rivas is a Ph.D. student in Education at UC Davis, a first-generation college student, and a father of three. He earned his B.A. in Chicana/o Studies with a minor in Education from UC Davis in 2020. As an undergraduate student, he became actively involved in student advocacy, working to improve policies and support systems for student-parents at both UC Davis and across the University of California system. Drawing from his own journey as a student-parent, as a graduate student, Daniel engages in research that seeks to uplift the voices of other student-parents and that continues to push for institutional change at UC Davis and in Higher Education more broadly. 

Natalia Deeb-Sossa was born in Bogotá, Colombia (land of the Muisca people).  She has worked at UC Davis for 19 years and is currently a Professor in the Chicana/o/x Studies Department. Natalia is the co-chair of of the UC Ethnic Studies Faculty Council.

TITLE: Exploring Diseases From Leaves to Labs
FOCUS: Are you interested in research on campus? Curious about California agriculture? Have you ever wondered about what causes a plant to get sick? Intrigued by plants and their relationships with bacteria, fungi, and viruses? In this seminar, we’ll explore how research is carried out on campus and how that research intersects with California agriculture using Plant Pathology research labs (labs that study plant diseases) as our model. You will learn about the basics of plant biology and microbiology to understand how plants form relationships with other organisms like bacteria, and how these relationships can become deadly. We’ll dive into how scientific research is conducted on campus, answering questions such as, “Who actually does the research? Who decides what experiments are done? How is research funded, and how can you get involved in research as an undergraduate student?” Finally, you’ll meet different plant pathology researchers on campus, learning about how they became scientists and what projects they are working on. The goal of this course is to introduce students to both research and the field of plant pathology, helping students make informed decisions about future careers as scientists in agriculture.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop a network of peers through group-based, interactive course activities
  2. Build connections with graduate students and post-doctoral researchers within the plant pathology department through guest lectures and Q&A sessions
  3. Become familiar with the academic career path and structure of research on campus through guest presentations and additional lectures/discussions
  4. Develop an understanding of basic plant and microbial biology, and learn how these intersect within plant pathology through lectures, discussions, & activities
  5. Develop an understanding of California agriculture and of the impact of plant diseases in our daily lives through lectures, discussions, & activities
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will meet once a week, two hours per week, for ten weeks. During class sessions students will participate in a mixture of lectures, discussions, and activities. There will be five guest speakers who will lead 20-minute presentations, followed by 10-minute Q&A sessions. Each week students will have an out-of-class assignment that will be one of the following: 1.) listen to a plant-pathology related podcast produced by the American Phytopathological Society or the American Society of Microbiology and fill out a worksheet that corresponds to the podcast, or 2.) go on a nature walk to look for healthy and unhealthy plants to take photos of and fill out a questionnaire where they record their observations of the plants. Podcasts assignments are expected to take 1.5-2 hours (approximately 1 hour for the podcast listening and 0.5-1 hour to respond to fill out the worksheet). Nature walk assignments are expected to take 1-2 hours. As a final project, students will create an infographic related to any of the topics they found interesting in the class, with the overarching goal of creating a resource to help promote the awareness of the impact plant pathology has on society.
GRADING: The grading for this course will be pass/fail, and a grade of 70% or higher will be required to pass. Students will be graded based on their participation during class discussions and activities (50%), completion of their weekly assignment (35%), and completion of their final assignment of creating an infographic (15%). Furthermore, in order to ensure students are participating in the weekly assignments and are not just relying on class-time and the final assignment to pass the course, students will be expected to complete at least 70% of the weekly assignments.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Tiffany Lowe-Power is a professor of plant pathology, and her lab primarily studies the Ralstonia solanacearum species complex. Her research blends genetics and genomic approaches to understand different species of bacterial pathogens can infect varying plant hosts. She works with graduate students, post-doctoral scientists, and undergraduates, providing mentorship and guidance to help her mentees navigate both research and career development. Tiffany got her undergraduate degree at the Georgia Institute of Technology, and her PhD at the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

 

Matthew is a PhD candidate within the Lowe-Power lab, and his research focuses utilizes bacterial genetics approaches to understand the physiology of Ralstonia plant pathogens. He started his journey in higher education at Mira Costa Community College in North County San Diego, and he finished his bachelor's degree after transferring to California State University San Marcos. He will be in his 5th year of his PhD program within the plant pathology department and looks forward to sharing his plant pathology expertise with students in the course.

TITLE: Find Your Passion in Life-Learning About Sustainability in Global Context
FOCUS:
Do you want to learn about the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, discuss the interaction between science, society, and the environment, and develop interdisciplinary and global perspectives on sustainability-related topics? Are you ready to use your scientific knowledge, increase your environmental awareness, and start building a healthier future for your community and the planet?  If you say "yes," then this seminar is for you. 

In science, subjects are often presented in an isolated realm of theories with little connection to real-world applications. As a result, students struggle to identify the relevancy of these topics in their everyday lives, leading to a lack of interest or motivation to learn the material presented in these courses. Therefore, this seminar was designed to show students how science is actually strongly connected to the local and global issues happening around us. The seminar will present topics in a way that is relevant to students in three dimensions: individual, vocational, and societal. The discussions that will take place in the seminar are expected to spark an interest in science by providing students with an opportunity to use their knowledge to develop solutions that contribute to global efforts to achieve the UN's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).  In this seminar, students will explore seven Prezi learning environments that connect the UN's SDGs (#2-Zero Hunger, #3-Good Health and Well-being, #6-Clean Water and Sanitation, #7-Affordable and Clean Energy, #13-Climate Action, #14-Life Below Water, and #15-Life on Land) with various science topics typically covered in the undergraduate science curriculum.
OUTCOMES: In this seminar, students will 1) create strategies to apply knowledge, skills, and abilities to collaboratively address global challenges; 2) learn about UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) by exploring the content including articles, videos, and animations on Prezi learning environments; 3) investigate how scientific and technological applications affect our local and global society and environment; 4) develop their communication skills by assuming different roles such as politician, economist, and environmentalist and defending their viewpoints during discussions; 5) develop their critical and analytical thinking skills by creating a realistic and innovative action plan that will help solve a local sustainability problem.
ASSIGNMENTS:
The overarching assignment is to develop a PowerPoint or Prezi presentation discussing a detailed strategy that will be utilized to make their local communities more sustainable and increase public environmental awareness.  Students will have weekly assignments consisting of exploring and reflecting on the presented topics in Prezi learning environments. Active tasks during the seminar will include the following:

BEFORE THE CLASS:

  • Determining key ideas from the learning environments that will be used to defend their viewpoints as politicians, environmentalists, or economists (~30–40 min).
  • Identifying critical questions related to SDGs from small group discussions and bringing them to whole seminar discussions (ideally 2-3 questions).

DURING THE CLASS:

  • Leading discussions around presented issues and transforming them into practical suggestions that can be applied locally or globally (every three weeks).

AFTER THE CLASS:

  • Contributing to the ‘course collection’ of papers, books, and resources built into MS Office 365.
  • Documenting the main threads and ideas from seminar discussions, including new insights and ideas generated.
GRADING: Pass/No Pass. A grade of “pass” will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a letter grade of C– or better.  Grading will be based on the following percentage breakdown: exploring and outlining key issues from each Prezi learning environment (30%), actively participating in weekly discussions (30%), preparing a group presentation that highlights the connections between one SDG and some issues faced in local communities, and proposing a plan to implement a successful strategy with sufficient detail to tackle those issues in the light of discussions that will take place during the seminar (40%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ozcan Gulacar is a Professor of Teaching within the Department of Chemistry, where he mentors undergraduate students in his research group. His research interests focus on chemical education, with an emphasis on problem-solving, cognition, knowledge structures, and education for sustainable development. He is keenly interested in helping students understand the complex interactions among factors influencing their learning and problem-solving success in his general chemistry courses. He collaborates with faculty from engineering and other science departments to explore the students’ challenges with problem solving. Additionally, he frequently instructs the CHE 2 series at UC Davis, which is required for most STEM majors.

TITLE: Frankly Frankenstein: A Tangled Tale of Science and Morality
FOCUS: The seminar will explore a topic at the intersection of modern science and morality.  Modern science has introduced so many technological innovations to alter social progress, yet society tends to lag in developing moral principles to guide the course of science. Specifically, biology has already developed methods to clone animals and to edit genes. These techniques can improve human health but at the same time can sinisterly promote the resurgence of racism as reflected in the past Eugenics movement, which strived to create a super race of people with select traits. Certainly, social probity should constrain such activity.  How does one develop these constraints from an informed vantage?  One must first understand the scientific concepts that empower the cloning of an organism and the editing of genes. One must then develop a framework to support the moral arguments.  Indeed, an 18-year-old Mary Shelley probes this question in “Frankenstein”.  Contrary to the Hollywood’s distorted rendition, the tale centers on a moral issue confronting scientific ambition.  Victor Frankenstein’s passion for science creates the “creature”.  He ignores his moral responsibility as the creator and the father  to address the creature’s yearning for an identity and search for the meaning of life.  In many ways, the Frankenstein story stands as a parable that confronts science with some very human questions.
OUTCOMES: The lectures will trace fundamental concepts underlying cloning and gene editing, outline a brief history of successful cloning, discuss the current practice of cloning, and point to the specter of eugenics.  The class will read and discuss Shelly’s “Frankenstein,” which sets a basis to discuss the moral perspectives, and other articles related to cloning and eugenics. With perspectives developed in the lectures and in the reading assignments, students will develop a framework to think about modern science in the context of a moral order.  The instructor will guide students to gather informed perspectives and to articulate their ideas in oral and written presentations. The class will foster critical, independent thinking that will interface multidisciplinary ideas in science, medicine, history, law, and social policy.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Format: The seminar will meet for 2 hours each week.  Instructor will provide background material and lead classroom discussions. However, the course focuses on stimulating students to think and articulate critically. The instructor will guide students to formulate their own ideas. 

Weeks 1-4:  Lecture & Discussion. Students will read intensely assigned book chapters and articles  and write a 1-2 page critique per week.The critique will answer topic questions.

Weeks 4-6:  Each student will give a 5-10 min oral presentation to the entire class about his/her research topic based on the course content and discussion and submit a 2-3 page essay .  Other class members will provide feedback. Instructor will suggest topics but will encourage students to explore their own research interest.  

Weeks 7-10:  Each student will revise his/her presentation and re-present a revised 15-20 min oral presentation and submit a 2-3 page essay.  Other class members will provide feedback.  

Finals Week: Based on the presentations and class feedback, the students will submit a final 5 page on the research topic. 

For all assignments, the instructor will encourage students to use the writing center/campus resources to aid in  the successful completion of the course.
GRADING: Students will be graded on 1) Essays on assigned reading (10%), 2) 5-10 min presentation (10%), 2) 10-20 min presentation (15%), 3) midterm examination (15%), 4) 5 page report (35%), and 4) class participation (15%). Class participation score will be based on attendance and oral/written feedback to fellow classmates’ presentation. Grading will follow the standard scale: 100-90%=A, 80-89%=B, 70-79%=C, 60-69%=D, <60% = F.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Thomas Jue has served as the instructor of record at UC Davis in the core biochemistry course in the medical school, in graduate courses focusing on metabolism, research methodology, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) in biomedicine, and current techniques in biophysics. He has developed curricula for the graduate programs in Exercise Physiology and Biophysics. He has research expertise in the field of chemical physics and has conducted research in many disciplines including NMR technique development, protein structure-function, the bioenergetics of muscle contraction, altered oxidative vs. non-oxidative metabolic regulation in type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) myocardium and skeletal muscle, sialic acid supplementation and cognitive development, and the linkage between T2DM and Alzheimer’s Disease.

He has previously taught FYS courses: 1) Trump's Legacy on Immigration and on Anti-Asian Hate Crimes and 2) Covid19 and the Dark Reality of Health Disparity.  He has received funding from the UCD Humanities Institute to launch an initiative to study the “Immigrant’s Struggle for Equality” and has written a number of newspaper opinion-editorials on the Asian American experience. He is currently working on a book with UCD law professor, Gabriel Jack Chin, entitled “Contextualizing Asians in the American Immigration Experience”.

TITLE: From Curiosity to Discovery: Essential Research Skills for First-Year Scholars
FOCUS:
This seminar invites you to explore the building blocks of research across academic disciplines—from generating meaningful questions to interpreting and presenting findings. Whether you're thinking about a future research project, preparing for upper-division courses, or simply want to learn how to ask better questions and find trustworthy answers, this course is for you.

You’ll learn how to formulate compelling questions, locate and evaluate reliable sources, understand and summarize scholarly literature, and begin designing your own mini research project. Along the way, we’ll demystify the research process, exploring how evidence-based knowledge is generated and how you can contribute to it—even in your first year of college.

Course activities include guided workshops, small group discussions, peer feedback, and reflective writing. Emphasis is placed on learning by doing—developing your voice as a researcher while gaining tools that support academic success in any major.

No prior research experience is needed. This course is ideal for curious students eager to develop research literacy, strengthen their confidence, and build a foundation for future scholarship, internships, and independent projects. By the end of the quarter, you'll walk away with essential research skills and also with a sense of agency as a scholar-in-the-making.
OUTCOMES:
This seminar equips first-year students with foundational research skills while immersing them in the core elements of authentic scholarly inquiry. Students will gain hands-on experience in the research process—from crafting compelling questions and conducting literature reviews to evaluating evidence and designing small-scale studies.

Discovery is central to the course. Students will investigate open-ended topics where outcomes are not predetermined, encouraging intellectual risk-taking and the generation of novel insights. Their work will be grounded in existing literature but oriented toward broader relevance, with the potential to inform real-world issues, academic discourse, or future projects.

Collaboration is fostered through peer feedback, team-based inquiry, and group presentations. Students will practice articulating ideas clearly and engaging in productive dialogue—skills essential to all disciplines.

Through multiple stages of drafting, refining, and revising, students will experience the iterative nature of research, learning how knowledge evolves through continuous inquiry and reflection.

By the end of the course, students are expected to understand the logic and rhythm of academic research and see themselves as capable contributors to scholarly communities—confident in their ability to ask meaningful questions and pursue evidence-based answers.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will engage in weekly activities designed to build core research competencies and support collaborative inquiry. Assignments will include:

  • Reading (~15–20 pages/week): Selected scholarly articles and accessible summaries to model research design, argumentation, and evidence evaluation.

  • Reflective Journals (6 total, ~300 words each): Personal responses connecting course material to students’ own developing research interests.

  • Short Writing Assignments (3 total, 2–3 pages each): These include a research question proposal, a literature scan, and a brief research design plan.

  • Group Project: Students will work in small teams to design and present a mini research proposal. Deliverables include a written group report (4–5 pages), peer feedback, and a final 10-minute group presentation.

  • Final Reflection Paper (3–4 pages): A personal synthesis of what students have learned about the research process and how it applies to their academic goals.

In-class participation will include guided workshops, peer review, low-stakes writing exercises, and structured group collaboration. No out-of-class fieldwork or labs are required, but optional independent exploration (e.g., library research or observational note-taking) may be encouraged depending on student projects.
GRADING:
Student performance will be assessed as follows: 25% participation and in-class activities, 15% weekly reflection journals, 30% individual written assignments, 20% group project (written report and presentation), and 10% final reflection paper. Grades are based on completion, clarity, effort, and growth in understanding research concepts.


Letter Grade Option: Percentages required for letter grades: 100–90 (A+ to A−), 89–80 (B+ to B−), 79–70 (C+ to C−), 69–60 (D+ to D−), below 60 (F).


Over 50% of the final grade is based on written and oral work. No final exam will be administered.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Azra Jahanitabesh is a Lecturer in Psychology at UC Davis whose work and teaching explore the complex interplay of mental health, social cognition, and human behavior. Her research bridges multiple disciplines, including psychology, behavioral economics, and social cognition, to understand how people perceive, interpret, and respond to challenges in daily life—especially under conditions of uncertainty and stress.

Drawing from her rich, multicultural background as a first-generation scholar, Dr. Jahanitabesh emphasizes inclusivity, resilience, and critical thinking in her courses. She encourages students to engage deeply with diverse perspectives on mental health, moving beyond stigma to appreciate the social, cognitive, and emotional factors influencing well-being.

Her teaching approach fosters interactive learning environments where students critically engage with complex human experiences, examining how social context, culture, and individual differences shape behavior and well-being. Her research, grounded in both quantitative data and lived experience, directly informs her teaching. She brings real-world psychological and social challenges into the classroom to cultivate critical thinking, compassion, and a sense of responsibility. By bridging empirical research with personal and community narratives, she encourages students to grow both as future scientists and as engaged contributors to society.

TITLE: Frontiers in Biology: From Learning and Memory to Extraterrestrial Life
FOCUS: What are the latest developments in biology and how do they advance research, knowledge, and human well-being? This seminar course will provide introductory reading into some of the most exciting ideas and questions in modern biology. The breadth of present-day biology and the range of questions and challenges it tries to tackle have led to its extraordinary diversification into subdisciplines. This seminar will present some of the range of modern biology and explore its frontiers in order to provide undergraduate students with orientation regarding their own choices in learning and research. Topics range from the function of the brain to human evolution, epigenetics, and the possibility of extraterrestrial life in our solar system and beyond. Principal course materials are current news and overview articles in major scientific publications supplemented by relevant original research papers. Both the instructor and students will select topics. The instructor will assign topics for presentation to individual students and provide reading materials to the class. In discussing the topics both basic concepts and novel ideas will be presented.
OUTCOMES: Students will be introduced to i) current concepts and important challenges in biological sciences; ii) modern methods and approaches to resolving these challenges; and iii) some current and foreseeable applications that emerged or will emerge form biological research at the frontier. In the process, students will develop effective communication skills, deepen their understanding of modern biology, and expand their cognitive skills to cultivate lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar group will meet two hours each week for this 2-credit class. Preparation for each seminar will require approximately 10 - 20 pages of outside the class reading. The meetings will consist of classroom presentations with simultaneous discussion of the material and a final 5-page report on a pre-approved, relevant topic of each student’s choosing.
GRADING: Grading encompasses three components; presence (25%), participation in classroom discussions (25%), and a final written report on a chosen seminar topic (50%). Full credit for presence requires attending at least 8 out of 10 sessions. Participation credit requires regular raising of questions and responding to issues presented in the reading material. Essay grading is based on quality of writing and accuracy of scientific content. Percentages that are required to earn specific letter grades are as follows: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Konstantinos Zarbalis is a Professor with the Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine. His research interests center on the genetic basis of neurodevelopmental and craniofacial disorders. He is using methods and techniques in developmental biology to investigate the role of key molecules and environmental factors in vertebrate development and their involvement in disease phenotypes.

TITLE: Global Pathfinders
FOCUS:
We live in an interconnected world. New students come to UC Davis with many types of global experiences already. You may be from an immigrant family, from a particular community, multilingual, an international student, or just interested in engaging the world. No matter what your background is, what your major is, or what your career goals are, we ALL need skills and knowledge to successfully operate in a global world.   

There's so much that UC Davis has to offer to support your global learning. We have programs to engage right here on campus as well as virtual opportunities, in addition to many opportunities for students to go abroad. Research, internships, community engagement, intercultural workshops, leadership and mentorship programs, and the Global Studies minor are just some of the opportunities you can get involved with. Come learn about scholarships and available funding, free or low-cost programs, and programs that even pay students for participating. There are so many global learning opportunities, it's time to get informed now so you can make amazing choices about your involvement for the rest of your time at UC Davis and beyond. 

The goal of this 1-credit seminar is to orient students early on to what global learning is and all of the resources the Global Learning Hub and our campus have to offer. We will also explore and develop some of the key global and intercultural skills necessary for success in a global workforce and civic engagement. The skills and knowledge in this course will help prepare you not only for your time at UC Davis, but also for your post-graduation life in our interconnected world.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of the 10-week course, participants will have: 

  1. Defined global learning and intercultural learning 
  2. Assessed a variety of definitions of and analogies for culture and key elements of cultural dimensions 
  3. Described elements of their own identity and how they inform their values, attitudes and actions 
  4. Developed a plan for their own global and intercultural learning and linked it to their UC Davis journey 
  5. Explored the concept of SMART goals and applied this concept to their plan 
  6. Reflected on the concept of cultural humility and articulated ways to cultivate and practice it.
ASSIGNMENTS: There will be weekly readings/videos to watch, totaling a max of 20 pages/week and/or two short videos/week. Students will complete a weekly written reflection of minimum 350 words. Finally, students will have a final brief individual presentation (2-3 minutes).
GRADING:
P/NP: The Pass (P) grade is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C– (70%) or higher.

10%        Engaged listening and sharing in class: During class meetings, students are expected to participate fully and engage in discussions and activities.

80%        Assignments and reflections: Complete all required readings/video viewings. All written assignments are required and the minimum word count is 350 words. 

10%        Final presentation: At the last class, you will briefly present to the group on the goals you’ve outlined and the vision board you created in the previous class.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Instructor of Record: Nancy Erbstein

Nancy Erbstein serves as Associate Vice Provost, Global Education and as faculty member in the School of Education. Her research and teaching focus on how communities produce and disrupt disparities in youth well-being, as well as youth civic engagement as an intervention strategy, situating her work at the intersection of educational reform, public health, youth development and community development. She brings her background in the areas of educational equity, community-engaged research and teaching, and global learning to catalyzing the Global Education for All initiative at UC Davis.

Course Collaborator: Jess Fareri

Jess works in the Global Learning Hub as a Global and Intercultural Programs Coordinator. In this role, Jess focuses on developing intercultural learning content and programming for graduate students and postdoctoral scholars, while also collaborating with Services for International Students and Scholars to support international student orientation efforts.  She is born and raised in New York and moved to California in 2018; she has also lived in Japan for over two years. She graduated from SUNY Stony Brook with degrees in Linguistics and TESOL and has an MA in International Education Management from the Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey.

TITLE: Global Tea Culture and Science
FOCUS:
This First-Year Seminar is a transdisciplinary exploration of tea, Camelia sinensis, taught by faculty and librarians who are participating in UC Davis’s new Global Tea Institute for the Study of Tea Culture and Science. It is offered in conjunction with GTI's 11th Annual Colloquium: The Art of Tea in Culture and Science, Society and Health – and the Industry. Class discussions will focus on the following:

  • Introduction to course and expectations:  Who drinks tea? What is tea? Where is tea? When is tea? Who (else) drinks tea? What is tea culture in China? What is tea science? Why study tea? How can we study tea? (Burnett, Art History); 
  • Nutrition and the Health Effects of Tea: What we know and what we need to know; Sensory aspects of tea: tea tasting session (Hackman, Nutrition);
  • How can teapots help us understand how culture spreads? the case of the Yixing Teapot (Burnett, Art History);
  • GTI's 11th Annual Colloquium: The Art of Tea in Culture and Science, Society and Health – and the Industry (Burnett);
  • Tea growing/tea farms in Japan and Elements of early Japanese Tea Culture (Sorensen, East Asian Languages and Cultures: Japan);
  • The Economics and Business of Tea in the United States (Hardesty, Agricultural & Resource Economics);
  • California Tea Project from 1960 to the 21st Century (Gervay-Hague, Chemistry);
  • Bug-bitten Teas (Kimsey, Entomology);
  • Human-plant bond and tea for wellness (Fausak, Carlson Library).
OUTCOMES:
With a focus on tea, students will explore complex dimensions of diversity, equity and inclusion around the world with an emphasis on culture and identity. 

They will gain a broad understanding about the complexity of global tea cultures and science across a spectrum of disciplines — from the humanities and social sciences to the sciences and health — and from a variety of perspectives. 

Through regular class sessions and the presentations at GTI's 11th Annual Colloquium, students will learn about tea cultures and societies, and identity in China, Japan, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Malaysia, England, and the US. Through the sessions focusing on California and the US, students will discover how the US is adopting and adapting tea cultures from abroad. This will help them understand some of the forces at work that shape cultures and societies, and the understanding of others.

In addition, students will learn how to do smart searches for literature and source materials across the disciplines. They will hone skills in such areas as textual analysis and oral and written communication.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Homework, Weeks 2-3, 5-10       

Students will come to each class meeting prepared to discuss assigned short readings (20 pp. max/week), and with 1) a typed paragraph describing key points of the previous week’s reading and discussion; 2) 3 questions about the current week’s readings (or related material) that demonstrate they have read and considered all assigned material. Students will upload these documents to CANVAS for grading.

Homework, Week 4

Register online for GTI's 11th Annual Colloquium (UC Davis Conference Center). View and take notes on at least 1 session. Submit these to the instructor the following week in class.

FINAL PROJECT

Imagine there is a Global Tea Institute offering summer scholarships to students to study tea. Write an application of 750-1500-words (about 3 to 5 pages) outlining your project, and explaining why you should be awarded a scholarship. Provide background & scholarly context (including a list of at least 2 scholarly references) for your project, explaining your methodology, and delineating your goals and expected outcomes.

Students' experience generating thoughtful questions about the readings each week, and their notes on the various lectures and presentations, including those at the GTI colloquium, should help them frame the final project.
GRADING:
Standard campus letter grading will be in place. Grading will be based on the following.
33.3% of grade  Pre-Class assignments in the form of written work (discussion questions,
3 minimum) submitted at the start of each class meeting
33.3% of grade  Notes from class discussion
33.4% of grade  Final project: a 3-5 page short paper
 
Grading scale:         100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <59 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Lead Instructor: Katharine Burnett, Ph.D., Professor and Chair, Art History and Founder & Director of UC Davis’s Global Tea Institute for the Study of Tea Culture and Science. Her research explores how cultural values are manifested in art, what it means to collect art, and now also, what we can learn about cultures and societies through studying individual and diverse tea cultures. She has traveled widely in China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Vietnam, Malaysia, Japan, and Colombia to study tea farms, tea production sites, tea houses, and collections of tea wares. A new research project investigates, “Those Crazy Teapots from Yixing: Manifestations of Late Ming Intellectual Values about the Individual Self."

Co-Instructors: 

Erik Fausak, librarian, Carlson Health Sciences Library.

Jacquelyn Gervay-Hague, Ph.D., Professor, Chemistry.

Robert Hackman, Ph.D., Professor, Nutrition

Shermain Hardesty, Ph.D., Extension Economist Emerita, Department of Agricultural and Resource Economics and leader of the University of California’s Small Farm Program. 

Lynn Kimsey Ph.D., Professor, Entomology

Joseph Sorensen, Ph.D., Associate Professor, East Asian Languages and Cultures (Japanese literature)

TITLE: Global Warming - Here and Now
FOCUS:
This course provides an up-to-date understanding of the science of Global Warming, including how we all contribute to it and how we all are affected by it. The primary goal is to relate basic science to the individual.

Global Warming will be explored through three varied activities. These will include:

1) Participating in a careful review of the Summary for Policy Makers of the IPCC Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis and recent updates. Topics will include distant and recent climate change, human influences, modeling the past and future, and expected changes for California in this century.

2) Development of a complete estimate of how much carbon dioxide a typical student adds to the environment in a month. Initially, this will use the excellent tool at https://coolclimate.berkeley.edu/calculator.

3) Analyzing a series of videos, which question the scientific basis and consequences of the current scientific consensus.
OUTCOMES: This course is designed to integrate a basic knowledge of the science of Global Warming with personal and social awareness of this important area. Emphasis will be given to the global nature of the problem, its causes, and the individual nature of many possible solutions.  Students will be expected to demonstrate effective oral and written communication skills. Students will be expected to contribute directly to group activities and actively participate in the weekly discussions.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will contribute directly to three small group activities. These include:

1) preparing at least 3 PowerPoint panels explaining assigned small sections of the primary scientific reports,

2) contributing one or more pages to the assigned portions of the typical student carbon dioxide usage survey, and

3)  providing analysis of critiques of the current consensus concerning Global Warming.

In all three cases specific citations for all utilized materials will be required.

Students will also read the assigned portions of the scientific reports and be prepared to comment on the contributions of the lead participants of that week's assignments.
GRADING: A Pass grade will require individual oral and/or written contributions to each of the three formal assignments as well as full participation in the weekly discussions. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Bryan Weare received his Ph.D. from the University of Buffalo with a dissertation in the new area of climate change. He then spent two years as a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Meteorology at MIT before coming to UCDavis as an Assistant Professor. During his years at Davis he taught various courses in atmospheric science and climate change and published extensively in these areas. Since retirement he has continued research in climate change as well as branched out into historical studies of women and minorities in his home state of Maine.

TITLE: Hablemos de Evolución y Ecología
FOCUS:
Scientific conversations and discussions overwhelmingly use English as the primary language. However the growing Hispanic population emphasizes the need to broaden the scope of scientific discourse. UC Davis is an emerging Hispanic Serving Institution. This course aims to promote Spanish as a language for scientific exchange in the fields of ecology and evolution. The course will emphasize conversation and discussion in Spanish through weekly 1-hour seminars with invited panelists from within and outside UC Davis. The invited panelists will give 20-minute presentations of their research in diverse areas of ecology and evolution broadly defined. Then, the students will engage in a guided Q&A with the panelists, asking questions about their research, their career paths, and how they got where they are. The seminar will be in Spanish, and therefore some fluency is necessary, but different levels of fluency are acceptable and welcome.

Las conversaciones y discusiones científicas utilizan el inglés como idioma principal. Sin embargo, la creciente población hispana, insta a que enfaticemos el español como idioma científico. UC Davis es una institución hispana emergente. Este curso tiene como objetivo promover el español como idioma para el intercambio científico en los campos de la ecología y la evolución. Este curso hará hincapié en la conversación y el debate en español a través de un seminario semanal de 1 hora con panelistas invitados de dentro y fuera de UC Davis. Los panelistas invitados darán presentaciones de 20 minutos de su investigación en diversas áreas de la ecología y la evolución en sentido amplio. Luego, los estudiantes participarán en una sesión de preguntas guiada sobre la investigación de los panelistas, así como su carrera como científicos y cómo llegaron donde están actualmente. El seminario será en español, y cierta fluidez es necesaria, pero diferentes niveles de fluidez son  bienvenidos.
OUTCOMES: We are proposing this FYS as a classic. The course will emphasize using Spanish in scientific conversations. Because many technical terms used in science have an English origin, it is often difficult, even for fluent Spanish speakers, to talk about science without using english words. This course will promote and emphasize the correct use of technical Spanish in spoken and written forms. Thus this course will develop effective communication skills and it will prepare students for lifelong learning. In addition, by exposing students to the career paths of successful scientists from diverse Hispanic backgrounds, it will develop a global perspective of being a multilingual scientist.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Each week, all students will read a research abstract by the panelist and write down three questions in Spanish for the panelist (2 about the research and 1 about the career path). 

Each week a group of students will present the speaker and develop a list of questions to guide the Q&A on both research and the career path of the seminar speaker. 

Students will work collectively on a Spanish dictionary of scientific terms commonly used in Ecology and Evolution related to the research presentations. In addition, students will provide examples on how to use these terms correctly.
GRADING:
50% of grade is attendance, graded by turning in their questions.

30% presentation of the speaker.

20% annotated dictionary.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Jeffrey Ross-Ibarra is a Professor in the department of Evolution and Ecology. Ross-Ibarra's work uses computational approaches to understand the genetics of plant evolution. His work focuses mostly on maize and its wild relatives. He has lived and worked in Mexico, including teaching at the National Autonomous University of Mexico in Mexico City.

 

Santiago Ramírez is a Professor in the Department of Evolution and Ecology. Ramirez's research focuses on the adaptation, speciation, and ecology of insect-plant associations. Ramírez was born and raised in Colombia, where he did his undergraduate degree in Biology. Ramirez moved to the U.S. to pursue a Ph.D. and a career in science. Prof. Ramirez is committed to promoting Hispanic/Latinos/as in science and has established several long-standing scientific collaborations with Latin American scientists.

TITLE: Handling Faults in Systems Around Us (With Emphasis on Electronic Systems)
FOCUS: Faults cause the observed behavior of a system to deviate from the expected behavior. They occur throughout the lifetime of electronic devices. They may occur in the design phase, the manufacturing phase, or during normal operation. The students in this course are first expected to learn the techniques for handling faults in electronic systems. Then, the students are expected to learn handling faults in other systems in engineering, science, biology, etc. Finally, the students are expected to explore the methods of handling faults in systems associated with their field of study, and compare their explored methods with those that are being used in electronic systems.
OUTCOMES: The students in this course will develop focus and depth in the filed of handling faults in all kinds of systems. The students are first expected to learn the techniques for handling faults in electronic systems. Then, the students are expected to learn handling faults in other systems in engineering, science, biology, etc. Finally, the students are expected to explore the methods of handling faults in systems associated with their field of study, and compare their explored methods with those that are being used in electronic systems. In addition to the above, the assignments in this course are established to improve the effective communication skills of students.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be required to prepare a short paper (and a related presentation) on a topic related to the theme of the course which is going to be chosen in consultation with the instructor. The students are expected to do the following tasks throughout the quarter: (1) select the research topic, (2) do the proposed research, (3) prepare a presentation, and (4) write a final paper (5 to 7 pages). The overall workload in this course is expected to be 1 to 2 hours of out-of-class work per week.
GRADING: The course score will be based on the quality of the student work and computed as follows: a 10-minute oral presentation to the class (50%) and a final written research report (50%). The final course grade will be reported as pass/no pass, the grade of pass is awarded to students that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Hussain Al-Asaad is a professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering. His research interests include design verification, testing, and fault tolerant computing. He has published over 55 papers in archival journals and refereed conference/workshop proceedings. He teaches undergraduate and graduate courses on digital systems design, testing, and verification. Al-Asaad is a recipient of the National Science Foundation CAREER Award and the UC-Davis academic senate distinguished undergraduate teaching award.

TITLE: High Performance Driving
FOCUS: Auto racing is one of the most popular sports in the world. Whether it's a local autocross or Formula 1, the driving controls are the same: steering wheel, throttle, brake, gearbox. While simple in principle, the act of high performance driving is deceptively complex. In this course, students will learn the theory and practice of high performance driving. Students will learn from both historical and modern texts, from videos of drivers of varying skill levels, and from telemetry data. We will explore the truths, misconceptions, and compromises that make motor racing such a difficult and rewarding sport.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn the mathematical and physical foundations underlying auto racing from the theoretical ideal racing line to the unusual properties of rubber tires. Students will learn how to examine videos to diagnose common driving errors. Students will learn how one records and reads telemetry data to understand what the driver and vehicle are doing at every instant. By the end of the course, students will be able to debunk many common driving myths based on their sound theoretical knowledge and practical experience. The educational outcomes will include (1) developing effective communication skills from both a driver and pit perspective, (2) developing high cognitive skills in assessing driver and vehicle performance, (3) developing focus and depth in the interdisciplinary fields associated with high performance driving, and (4) preparing for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each week there will be a short written assignment where students will be given a few driving puzzles to solve. The puzzles will contain text and/or video and/or telemetry that describe a racing activity that didn't go as planned (e.g a spin). Solving the puzzle will require integrating information across disciplines. For example, diagnosing whether a spin is due to the car or driver requires inspection of weight distribution, yaw, and change in steering angle. Each student will write a concise report describing what happened and why. In the following session, the class will start by discussing the previous problem. Towards the end of the course, students will also create their own puzzles for other students to solve. It is expected that the weekly assignments will take 2 hours of out-of-class work.
GRADING: Students will be graded on a 100 point scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). 90% of the points will come from written assignments and 10% from class participation (defined as coming to class and being present for discussion).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology Department who specializes in bioinformatics. When he's not developing novel sequence analysis algorithms he likes to be at the race track where he may be driving or coaching (but not spectating). He has written a popular entry level book for racers, "You Suck at Racing: a crash course for the novice driver", and has a blog with over 500 posts about high performance driving.

TITLE: How Would You Clone Yourself--and Should You? Genetics, Developmental Biology and Society
FOCUS: This course will introduce the basic genetic and developmental concepts underlying several modern technological advances that impact human society, including the cloning of whole animals, cloning of cells for stem cell therapy, gene therapy, and genome editing. The instructor will introduce basic scientific concepts through lecture and assigned readings during the first 3 meetings. For subsequent meetings students will give presentations on topics that highlight the current state of knowledge for a given technology, and the entire class will discuss the pros and cons of application of this knowledge from a public perspective. This course will be appropriate for science majors as well as non-majors interested in science.
OUTCOMES: The goal for this course is for students to understand the basic genetic and developmental biology concepts underlying technologies that impact modern medicine; this should allow both science and non-science students to be better informed members of society. Topics will emphasize the intersection of science and technology with society and thus students should also gain an appreciation for different points of view; respect and empathy will be stressed during discussion. Finally, students will gain oral communication and presentation skills, including how to communicate scientific information to non-expert audiences.
ASSIGNMENTS: Reading will be assigned for each week’s class (10 pages per week on average); reading for the first 3 weeks will supplement lecture material on basic genetic and developmental biology concepts while reading for later classes will match the presentation topic. For weeks 4-10, students will work in groups of 2 to prepare a 20 minute presentation that summarizes a current developmental or genetic technology. The instructor will provide a list of potential topics and will also provide written resources that include both scientific background and ethical/societal considerations. Presenters are expected to find and read additional material; they will also coordinate with each other to plan and practice the presentation. The presenters and instructor together will lead the rest of the class in discussion after the presentation. For each week, students will turn in answers to questions from the assigned reading, and are expected to participate in the discussion. In addition, there will be one 2-page written assignment due by the end of the quarter on a topic that was not covered by the student’s presentation.
GRADING: Pass/no pass: A grade of pass will be awarded for work equivalent to a C- or better. Grading will based on the following: Turning in weekly discussion questions and participation in the class discussions (25%), one two-page essay on a topic not covered in the presentation (25%), and one presentation (50%). For the presentations, students in the group are expected to contribute to all aspects of the project including research, preparation of slides, and presenting in class.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Lesilee Rose is a Professor in the Department of Molecular and Cellular Biology. Her research involves the study of early developmental processes in the model organism Caenorhabditis elegans. She currently teaches the upper division course in Developmental Genetics (MCB163), and participates in graduate level courses in Developmental Biology.

TITLE: Hummingbird Ecology
FOCUS: Are you curious about hummingbirds? Would you like to know more about ecology research, but don’t know where to start? In this First Year Seminar you will explore the complexities around hummingbird ecology. Hummingbirds present a unique combination of an extremely high life costs and numerous adaptations to efficiently fuel these demands. We will study their remarkable cognitive abilities, territoriality, aggressive behaviors, courtship and physiology as well as their role as pollinators. We will combine lectures, discussions, analysis of visual materials and visits to the UC Davis Hummingbird Garden.
OUTCOMES:
Upon completion of this course, learners should be able to:

- Have an in-depth perspective of the hummingbird ecology from different subdiscipline perspectives.

- Perform effective field observations.

- Communicate scientific ideas in a discussion based on background information.

 Thus, through a mix between theoretical lectures, discussions and hands-on activities, the main objective of this course is to help students to develop critical thinking tools that allow them to communicate scientific information particularly about hummingbird ecology.   

Specific goals:

  1. Students will actively discuss ideas about hummingbird ecology and how hummingbirds are affected by abiotic (i.e., ambient temperature, precipitations) and biotic conditions (i.e., competition/territory defense, flower abundance, vegetation).
  2. Students will practice collaboration, iteration, creativity, and failure, through the tasks/assignments associated with the course. Students will acquire a more realistic view of what is ecology research.
  3. Students will report gains in their understanding of the process of research. Specifically, students will become familiar with the scientific method through the process of reading about hummingbird ecology research. Through observation-discussion cycles, students will learn how to observe and record hummingbird behavior.
  4. Students will believe more strongly in their capability to do research, by developing skills that allow them to observe, discuss and present scientific information.
ASSIGNMENTS: During five weeks, students will attend lectures on hummingbird biology. Readings and a reading guide will be assigned in these lectures (5-10 pages/week). Grades will be based on participation and analysis. Free-living hummingbird observations will be carried out in two classes around the UC Davis Hummingbird Garden to illustrate the materials reviewed in class. Grades will be based on field reports. Based on the scientific literature reviewed as homework and during lectures, students will collaborate to present scientific literature in the format of a poster for a scientific meeting, this is a 10-minute PowerPoint group presentation.
GRADING:
Class Participation & Attendance                     10%

Scientific paper analysis (2, 15 points each)     30%

Field Reports (2, 15 points each)                       30%

Scientific Poster (30 points)                               30%

Total Points 100                                                  100%

 

This is a Pass/No Pass class. Conversion to letter grade is not possible.

Important: A student needs to achieve at least a C- (70%) in order to receive a passing grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
-Paulina Gonzalez

I was trained as an ecologist in my natal Chile. I arrived at UC Davis as a postdoc in ecophysiology in 2010, and I am now a Lecturer and a research ecologist at the UCD Museum of Wildlife and Fish Biology. In this role I teach a range of courses, and I enjoy hands-on teaching. I also do research in avian ecophysiology (stress) and behavior, especially in hummingbirds. Outside of class, I love birdwatching, making jewelry, and cooking with my friends.

TITLE: Hummingbirds--Conservation of an Urban Pollinating Avian Species
FOCUS: Pollinators encompass a diverse group of animals that provide important ecosystem services. As examples, pollinators include bees, butterflies, bats and birds. Students interested in urban wildlife and human interactions would benefit from this Freshman Seminar. Students who have some biology or ecology background or interest would benefit most from this seminar. This seminar will explore the hummingbird species of California, what ecosystem services hummingbirds provide, the important factors that help hummingbirds be successful and produce offspring, and how hummingbirds are being studied in science. The goal of this course is to provide students with an overview of the amazing nature of hummingbirds and to enlighten them about the challenges that hummingbirds are facing in an ever changing world
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the different facets of hummingbird natural history and conservation. The goals of this seminar are to (1) cultivate ethics, responsibility, and respect, for the animals that we share planet Earth with and (2) to develop a global perspective for ecosystem health. This seminar is an opportunity to gain a more in-depth understanding about how hummingbirds play a role in ecosystem health and how we can help to protect their populations in the future.
ASSIGNMENTS: For the five sessions, students will be assigned to read 2-3 journal articles per session. Journal articles will focus on avian research techniques, hummingbird biology, health, habitat, migration, or conservation. These topics will then be discussed during the seminar sessions so that students can share their views about what they learned from the assigned reading and how that changed or added to their current perceptions. During the quarter, students will be expected to write a 5 page paper (double spaced, with at least 6 references) on a topic related to hummingbird biology, conservation, health, habitat restoration etc. Students will also be expected to give a 5 minute presentation at the end of class. Writing the paper and giving a presentation is an opportunity for students to experience translatable skills of critically reviewing scientific literature and present it in an educational form. This seminar will meet for a total of five sessions. Each session will be 1 hr 40 min in duration. The tentative dates for the five sessions are 1/8/26, 1/15/26, 1/29/26, 2/5/26 and 2/12/26. One session date during January might have to be moved in February extending the course to 2/19/26.
GRADING: This is a pass/no pass course. A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. Course grading will be based on a written 5-page paper (35%), verbal presentation during the last session (35%), and quality of participation in class sessions (30%).  Students must attend 4 of 5 sessions (unless further absences are excused) to receive a passing grade.  Missed sessions must be viewed via recordings.   Class participation will entail each student coming class with questions and comments relative to assigned manuscripts for the seminar session and active participation in the discussion sessions.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Tell is a member of the faculty in the School of Veterinary Medicine. Her research interests include treatment of avian disease, avoidance of drug residues in poultry products consumed by humans, and avian health relative to conservation of bird populations. Dr. Tell obtained her Bachelor of Science from UC Davis, did her residency at the National Zoo, then returned to UC Davis as a faculty member. She is boarded with the American College of Zoological Medicine and the American Board of Veterinary Practitioners (Avian Practice). Dr. Tell is currently the director of the UC Davis Hummingbird Health and Conservation Program and holds a federal permit for banding hummingbirds.

TITLE: I Want to be a Consultant, I Just Don't Know What They Do!
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS: So, here you are - UC Davis - one of the great public universities in the world. Yes the world! Let's make the most of it. I have spent most of my professional life as a consultant. It is an interesting journey. And today it is even more interesting and it is a popular career path right out of college. So we will read sections of a few book - all on reserve in the library, read a few consulting reports, look at some consulting firm both large - Accenture, IBM Global Services - and others both small and in the public sector. What I want you to see is the diverse "skill set" - that is consultant speak - that you will need to develop as an undergraduate: writing, listening, thinking, collaborating, patience, and technical that you need to bring to the interview with your dream firm.
