I want you to leave the seminar challenging what you really want to do with your work life: something that is interesting, something that is meaningful, and something you want to do. It is a personal journey - you will see that in some of our readings. And what you will also see is that every class you take, every club you join, every friend you make will make a difference for you. And you will make a difference for them. We won't find the answers, but I hope you find a way for you to start to think about how you will find your way.
OUTCOMES: Welcome to our seminar on "what consultants do." In addition to introducing you to the working world of a consultant as a career choice we want to help you develop you story telling skills. Not just the presentation of facts, but for you to develop your own approach to tell an exciting story. So whatever class you take along the way you can find even the most mundane aspect of a problem and bring it to life. Whether in a formal presentation, a short paper, or a group discussion, you will feel comfortable in your work. And part of this is learning "how to prepare". So much of what we will do is to teach you how to be best prepared - know your facts - so you can tell the story. To succeed, we will do the following: First, learn the importance of communications skills, both for those who present, and those that listen. So will learn about written, visual, and verbal communication skill in all of our assignments. We will not learn everything, but you will learn “how to learn” about communication skills. Second, for some this will be an introduction to formal group work. We will learn of peer evaluation, team building, the importance of empathy which is a leading characteristic of successful leadership. And third, when we look at those leading consulting firms and the wide variety of work done by the firms, you will see the importance of a broad educational background and the need to “keep on learning” both to stay competitive and to do interesting projects.
ASSIGNMENTS: One of the skills you want to develop is your presentation skills - the consultant need to "tell the story." So most of our work is in the form of presentation in our seminar. Our presentation will be about ten minutes and consist of five to seven slides. You will be proficient in at least two presentation tools like PowerPoint. The first assignment will be in groups of two or three and present profiles of three consulting firms: one large, one in the nonprofit sector, and one in the public sector. This assignment will be 10% of your grade. The second assignment will be on an individual basis: From reading sections in several book on reserve in the library you will present three ideas the you have "taken away" from the reading about what consultants do. The objective is for you to start to see the consultant role. This assignment will constitute 20% of your grade for the presentation and 10% for a short written component. The third assignment will be presentation of a consultant's report primarily from the public sector on what was done and what was reported. You will work in groups of three or four on this assignment. We want to see what consultants do. This assignment will 40% of your grade. The remaining 20% of your grade will come from your selection of two project- 10% each- from the list about ten options that are industry specific. So you have the opportunity to see what consultants do in engineering, healthcare, education, or whatever sparks your interest. These a very short papers you will present to the class. We will meet every two weeks in a classroom setting to discuss our projects. And on the other weeks we - I will be there to help you - work in the library getting material for our presentations and in a campus computer lab to work on the mechanics of your presentation.
GRADING: Your grade is on a pass/no pass basis. Getting your presentation submitted on time just like a consultant will keep you on track for a pass grade. For each assignment you will have a "punch list" - another consultant's term - of what needs to be included. Your presentations will be "pre-graded" by the instructor as if we were in a consulting firm to make sure you are on the right track. The formal grade for your assignments will be done after each presentation. So as a consultant you need to convince your client you have the right solution. So your client - the rest of the seminar members – will evaluate your performance and list five parts of your presentation that were great and five parts that you could change to make the presentation even greater. At McKinsey – a large consulting firm – client presentations are always “tested” with a “red team” – a group of other McKinsey consultants whose purpose in life is to find every flaw in your presentation – so when you go to the client your confident you have “all your ducks in a row” – in other words, we do a lot of peer evaluation. Oh! Timesheets – the only downside of consulting! For your presentations and other project you will keep a very simple timesheet to track how you approached the assignments and to make sure we are balancing out the work.
INSTRUCTORBIO: I am a lecturer in Managerial Economics Program in the Agricultural and Resource Economics Department where my major class is ARE 112 - Management of the Organization that I teach each quarter and once during the summer session. I also teach a supply chain management class and a class on linear programming/economic optimization. I have also been a consultant for over 30 years working on information system installations, strategic planning, cost analysis, and business plan development. I have work primarily in California as well as short-term assignments in Australia, the Pacific Islands, and Russia. In my "non-work" life I was on the board of directors of an acute care hospital, currently on the board of a senior housing project in San Francisco, and a collegiate football and rugby referee.

TITLE: Introduction to Buddhist Meditation
FOCUS: This seminar serves as an introduction to Buddhist meditation. College is a time of exhilarating change and challenges, but with change and challenges come stress; research and experience have established that meditation can reduce stress. In this seminar, we will practice meditation and discuss short weekly readings in Buddhist philosophy from several traditions that address how to work with our mind/body to improve our concentration and to help cultivate energy, imagination, insight, and balance for the good of ourselves and others. The seminar’s primary goal will be to work towards establishing a regular meditation practice throughout your time as a UC Davis student and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students will develop effective communication skills by participating in weekly discussions in a small, supportive classroom community.
  2. Students will learn some core principles of Buddhist philosophy, thereby developing a global perspective and cultivating ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy.
  3. Students will incorporate daily meditation practice into their lives, learning strategies for conflict resolution and focusing their mind/body that will help prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: Meditation journal, to include brief weekly reading responses; one 15-minute presentation, solo or with a partner; final written reflection (2-3 pp.); regular attendance / participation.
GRADING:
Grading will be holistically assessed using a grading contract for P/NP (C- or better). Regular attendance is expected.

In-class meditation activity (25%)

Oral contributions to weekly discussions based on readings and daily meditation (25%)

Weekly Reading Responses (30%)

Meditation Journal + Final Written Reflection (20%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Greg Miller teaches in the University Writing Program and has been an imperfect practitioner of Buddhism for several decades.

TITLE: Introduction to Cancer Biology and Prevention
FOCUS: Cancer is caused by changes to certain genes that alter the way our cells function. Some of these genetic changes occur naturally when DNA is replicated during the process of cell division. But others are the result of environmental exposures that damage DNA, such as tobacco smoke. This seminar is intended to provide an overview for environment and cancer.  Topics will include an introduction of cancer, carcinogen, tobacco-related cancer research, the underlying pathways of cancer development and cancer prevention. Regardless of major, this course may be of interest to students who want to learn more about cancer and the impact of toxins on the human body.
OUTCOMES: This seminar aims to demystify cancer and invite critical thinking on new frontiers of cancer research and biomedical science.  After successful completion of the seminar, students will develop scientific literature reading, comprehension skills, and improve discussion and communication skills.  Students will also be able to discuss and/or explain to lay audiences the implication of cancer research.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet one hour per week for ten weeks.  The seminar includes four courses taught by instructors and six courses consisted of students presentation focusing on the topics related to environment and cancer.  For student presentation, small groups will be set (4-5 student per group) and each student will be assigned with one or two basic research articles (approximately 5-10 pages; 1 hour of reading and 1 hour for preparing 10-15 minute PowerPoint presentation).  Student will need to communicate with their team member regarding the presentation and then present their reading at the classroom (explain results/figures, clarify confusion, discuss findings, etc.).  Students will also be required to write a short final paper (4-5 pages maximum) explaining a scientific finding to a lay audience.
GRADING: The grade of PASS is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better. Grading will be assessed according to three components: (1) Teamwork, e.g. collaboration between group member (20%); (2) oral discussion, e.g. contributor in class discussions, intelligent observations, connections, ideas, or questions in each topic (graded by 40%); 3 a final report discussing a chosen seminar topic (40%).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Jin Zhang, PhD, is an Ajunct Professor in the School of Veterinary Medicine studying the molecular mechanism of cancer Initiation and progression.  She uses a combination of molecular tools and animal models to answer the fundamental questions such as how cancer is developed.

Xinbin Chen, DVM/PhD, is a distinguished professor in the Schools of Veterinary Medicine and Medicine.  His research interest focuses on understanding the biochemical and biological functions of the p53 family of tumor suppressors and the role of gene-environment in cancer initiation and progression.  

Allan Gao, MD PhD, is a professor at Department of Urologic Surgery, School of Medicine.  Professor Gao's research focuses on the seeking to better understand the molecular changes associated with the progression of prostate cancer, identification of diagnostic markers and therapeutic targets to stop the progression of late-stage prostate cancer.

TITLE: Introduction to Condensed Matter Physics
FOCUS: Did you know that one of the biggest branches of modern physics research, is a field called condensed matter physics? This is a fancy name for what is essentially the physics of materials. The physical properties of some materials defy our current understanding, leading to a very active field of research. Yet, the first formal introduction to this field usually happens in your senior year, after taking several upper division physics classes. In this first-year seminar, we will introduce condensed matter physics at the first-year level, with no prerequisites. You will learn why and how physicists are looking into the properties of materials.
OUTCOMES:
To learn about a modern physics research topic

To develop focus and depth in physics, chemistry, and material science

To explore how scientific research leads to new ideas and discoveries

To develop effective communication skills, practice written, oral, and interpersonal communication skills through writing, reading, and presentation

To learn guidelines for UC laboratory safety fundamentals and receive training completion certification, thus demonstrating proactive competency and preparedness

To increase knowledge of what resources and mindset will allow one to be successful as researchers, scientists and students
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments will be due each week and will include:

(i) written summaries of readings of research articles, or review articles chosen by the students among a list prepared by the instructor (up to 10 pages reading, up to 2 pages writing);

(ii) short presentations on various research topics chosen by the students among a list prepared by the instructor (10 minutes). Students will get advice on how to find journal articles on their own.
GRADING:
The mode of grading for this course will be letter grades. Attendance each week is expected, and part of the grade will be based on participation.

Sixty (60) percent of the final grade will be based on successful completion of written assignments, presentation, and readings. Each student will be required to complete several reflective assignments related to material presented in class, discussion seminars, readings and experience.

Forty (40) percent of the final grade will be based on participation, which requires class attendance. Class participation includes actively engaging in class discussions with instructor, guest presenters, peers, and completing assignments.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valentin Taufour is a Professor in the Physics Department at UC Davis.  He received his PhD from CEA Grenoble (France) in 2011 and was a post-doctoral researcher at Iowa State University (2011-2013) and a scientist at the Critical Material Institute at the Ames Laboratory (2013-2016) before coming to UCD.  The goal of his experimental work is to elucidate the physical interactions that are responsible for unconventional properties of new materials.  His research group grows crystals of new materials, often in the form of high-quality single crystals. After creating new materials, they tune them to study how to control and improve their physical properties. The tuning parameters can be chemical substitutions, high-magnetic field and/or high-pressure.

TITLE: Introduction to Food Writing
FOCUS: This seminar explores food writing from the late nineteenth century to the present. We’ll consider food writing as a means for raising broader questions related to pleasure, power, reform, activism, culture, identity, migration, and diaspora. In addition to foundational works in food literature such as Roald Dahl’s Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (1964) and Upton Sinclair’s The Jungle (1906), we will read Kate Chopin’s novella, The Awakening (1899), and John Birdsall’s What Is Queer Food?: How We Served a Revolution (2025). Students can expect to learn more about literary studies, cultural history, food studies, and interdisciplinary research.
OUTCOMES: Students will gain proficiency in the fields of archival research, cultural studies, and literary studies. Students will develop a greater understanding of the relationship between literature and politics by studying roughly two hundred years’ worth of food writing that responds to social, political, and environmental injustice. The seminar will be discussion-based and involve discussing works of fiction, literary criticism, primary documents, and visual art. In addition to reading and discussing key texts of food writing, students will hone communication skills through written assignments as well as archival practices by visiting the UC Davis Library Special Collections department. Given the seminar’s interest in migration and identity, students will develop a global perspective by studying how food production and consumption occur within a transnational framework. Finally, the course will illuminate the complexities of eating, a routine and commonplace activity that affects most every aspect of daily life in Northern California.
ASSIGNMENTS: Reading will include two novels (approximately 250 pages each), a children’s novel (approximately 150 pages), a novella (approximately 100 pages), and a work of cultural history (approximately 200 pages). Readings will average approximately 125 pages per week. Students are responsible for 3 written assignments: a Recipe and Reflection (2 pages); an Interview (3 pages); and a Table Tableau (3 pages). Students will be graded for class discussion, which means attending seminar in a punctual manner, synthesizing material and readings, and engaging course material. There is one out-of-class field trip planned to the UC Davis Special Collections Department at Shields Library.
GRADING: Grades will be evaluated using P/NP. The grade of P (“Pass”) is awarded to students for work that would receive a grade of C- (70%) or higher. The grade of NP (“No Pass”) is awarded to students for work that would receive a grade of D+ (69%) or lower. Grades will be weighted accordingly: a Recipe and Reflection (2 pages; 20%); an Interview (3 pages; 30%); a Table Tableau (3 pages; 30%); and Class Participation (20%). Participation will involve making connections between readings and asking questions using textual evidence.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Chip Badley (he/him) is a lecturer in the English department at the University of California, Davis. He is at work on a book project concerning aesthetics and queer sexuality in nineteenth-century American literature. A former managing editor of Camera Obscura: Feminism, Culture, and Media Studies (Duke University Press), he researches and teaches visual culture, gender and sexuality studies, critical race and ethnic studies, cognitive literary studies, and psychoanalysis. During 2022-23, he was a National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH) Postdoctoral Fellow at the Library Company of Philadelphia, and, in 2023-24, the Hench Post-Dissertation Fellow at the American Antiquarian Society. He is currently beginning a project on food studies in American literature.

TITLE: Introduction to Nano-medicine: Small thing, big effect
DEPARTMENT: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Medicine
FOCUS: Very tiny particles can have big effect. Nano-medicine is playing a more and more important role in health care. The purpose of the course is to provide an overview on nano-medicine. The seminar group will meet for two hours each week. The seminar begins with lectures and discussions organized by the instructor and co-instructor on the very basics of knowledge and classifications about nano-medicine so that students with a wide range of backgrounds may participate. In addition to basic concepts and background information provided early in the course, students will be encouraged to pick up their favorite type of nanoparticles and give short presentations on selected topics, followed by discussions. A facility visit and laboratory demonstration of nano-medicine production will be included subsequently. The scientific level of this seminar is designed for undergraduate students from any major in the life sciences, who have taken general biology courses. This seminar has been offered many times since 2016 and it was well received.
OUTCOMES: The purpose of the course is to provide students with a broad overview of nano-medicine, discussing the fundamental science and classifications of nanotechnology and its applications in biomedical fields. We will discuss the interdisciplinary nature of nanotechnology and how the different basic sciences merge to create the field, as well as give the students real experiences on how nano-medicine is produced. We will help the students to gain an appreciation and basic understanding of nano-medicine, develop effective communication & presentation skills, and prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be assigned to read a newest review journal article on a specific topic per week (approximately 10-20 pages). Also, students will be assigned to write a short review/summary and give a 10-minute presentation on selected topics in oral form in front of the class, followed by group discussions. A facility visit and laboratory demonstration of nano-medicine production will be included in the final class. The lab trip would be scheduled within the regular class time.
GRADING: The grading system is based on “pass/fail”. Briefly, grades will be based on the completion of article reading (30%), class participation and class discussion (40%), and the writing assignment and a ten-minute presentation on a selected topic (30%). The PowerPoint presentation is critically reviewed by the instructor before presentation. The grade of “pass” is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Li is a Professor in the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Medicine. He is also an Active Member of the National Cancer Institute-Designated Comprehensive Cancer Center at UC Davis. Dr. Li’s teaching and research interests are in the areas of nano-medicine, molecular cancer therapy, molecular imaging and translational research. His research group is developing a series of novel multifunctional nano-medicine platforms for early cancer detection, effective cancer therapy and minimizing the side-effects of chemotherapeutic drugs. Significant efforts have also been devoted to the rapid “bench to bed side” translation of these nano-platforms for better patient care.

TITLE: Latino Male Leadership Seminar
FOCUS:
Course Description:

This course provides a space for Latinx male students to cultivate, develop, and take ownership of their Latinx male identity and increase the visibility of Latinx male leaders on campus. Discerning the impact and role of their Latinx male identity in their educational journey is crucial for the academic success of these students. In support of their academic goals, this course will also concentrate on establishing strong community connections among their peers and other Latinx male role models to enhance their social capital. Through this network, they will be introduced and challenged to engage with the different tools and resources to acquire internships, scholarships, and research opportunities to be successful at UC Davis and beyond.

Course Materials:

These materials will be provided on CANVAS. You are not expected to purchase these materials unless you prefer to retain a personal copy for future use beyond this course.

  • bell hooks. The Will to Change: Men, Masculinity, and Love (2004)
OUTCOMES:
Goals:

  1. Retain and support the Latinx male student population.
  2. Deconstruct, reflect, and cultivate their Latinx male identity.
  3. Build community and a sense of belonging.
  4. Acquire competency in navigating campus resources and structures.
  5. Foster mentorship and networking opportunities by facilitating connections with guest speakers and other campus mentors.
  6. Connect students to professional development opportunities.

 

Learning Outcomes

  1. Deconstruct, Reflect and Cultivate
    1. Students will learn how to reflect on their male identity within the Latinx community and how this impacts their academic journey
    2. Students will develop awareness to measure the impact of their identity on themselves and others.
    3. Students will learn how to challenge patriarchal expressions of Latinx manhood to create healthy and safe communities on campus and beyond.
  2. Community and Network
    1. Students will understand the importance of building a sense of belonging and establishing community.
  3. Increasing Knowledge
    1. Students will learn how to identify and navigate resources and structures, both on and off campus.
    2. Students will gain an understanding of how to collaborate with one another and offer guidance on academic enrichment opportunities.
  4. Developing Skills
    1. Students will learn how to develop mentorship relationships and understand the importance of seeking guidance and support.
    2. Students will learn skills to navigate professional development opportunities.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments & Week-by-Week Topics

Week 1: Understanding Latinx Male Identity

  • Assignment: Initial Reflection

Week 2: Impact of Identity on Self and Others

  • Assignment: Podcast write-up on recurring class topics (link: https://www.latinousa.org/2025/01/12/hombrechuckrocha/).

Week 3: Building a Sense of Belonging

  • Brief Assignment: Reflect on personal identity, family role, and how these influence belonging, your idea of masculinity, and your educational journey?

Week 4: Establishing Community

  • Discussion post: on bell hooks’ The Will to Change, Chapter 9.

Week 5: Navigating Resources

  • Assignment: Schedule and document a meeting with an Academic/Campus Resource Advisor.

Week 6: Understanding Identity

  • Assignment: Podcast write-up based on this episode (link: https://www.latinousa.org/2025/01/12/hombrechuckrocha/).

Week 7: Facilitating and Sharing Opportunities

  • Assignment: Reflect on support and resources from your meeting with an advisor.

Week 8: Mentorship and Guidance

  • Discussion post: on bell hooks’ The Will to Change, Chapter 4.

Week 9: Exploring Scholarships/Internships

  • Brief Assignment: Summarize experiences applying for scholarships or internships.

Week 10: Research & Professional Development

  • Brief Assignment: Outline career goals and support systems or strategies for achieving them.
GRADING:
• Attendance, Participation, and Brief Assignments (30%)

  • Initial Reflection paper (10%)

  • Podcast Episode Write-Ups (10%)

  • Book a meeting with an Academic or Campus Resource Advisor (5%)

  • Meeting Reflection with an Academic or Campus Resource (10%)

  • Discussion Posts (10%)

  • Final Write-Up & Presentation (25%):
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Michael V. Singh is an assistant professor in the Department of Chicana/o Studies at UC Davis. He received his Ph.D. from the Berkeley School of Education at UC Berkeley in 2019. Dr. Singh’s research is guided by questions of racial and gender justice in schools, with a focus on Latino men and boys. His book, Good Boys, Bad Hombres: The Racial Politics of Mentoring Latino Boys in Schools, tells the story of a school-based mentorship program for Latino boys in the era of neoliberal multiculturalism. It delves into the complicated and sometimes contradictory racial and gender politics undergirding youthwork.

 

Emilio Sanchez is a first-generation Mexican graduate born and raised in East Oakland, CA. He received his Bachelor's from Santa Clara University and competed his Master's in Higher Education and Student Affairs at the University of San Francisco. Currently, he servers as the Academic Success Advisor for El Centro (Center for Chicanx Latinx Academic Student Success). In addition to advising, programming, and supporting the services at El Centro, he leads the Latinx Male of Color Success Initiative on campus.

TITLE: Learning and Memory in Neurologic Disease
FOCUS: As scientists in a neurological surgery lab, our interest involves how brain disease and injury result in behavioral dysfunctions like learning disorders. We then use our understanding of that biology to try to identify and test new treatment strategies to restore the brain to a healthier state. Why does a single insult to the brain, such as an epileptic seizure or traumatic brain injury, have such a significant impact on learning and memory? Are cells dying resulting in deficits? Is it possible that deficits result from changes to brain network function? Could it be a bit of both? Through continual research of different neurological diseases including epilepsy, Alzheimer's disease, traumatic brain injury, schizophrenia etc., it is clear that there is a dynamic relationship between brain injury and cognitive function where many patients, regardless of disease, experience some level of impairment. However, as the mechanism and location of disease within the brain may change, the scope (either severity or type) of learning disabilities also varies. Our research aims to answer questions related to changes in brain function (at the neuronal and circuit level) with the goal of finding a target to reduce cognitive deficits associated with brain injury; focusing specifically on electrical stimulation as a therapy. In this course students will learn about various brain disorders and discuss how they alter learning and memory. By utilizing articles published in popular science journals that summarize basic science findings, students will gain insight into current research methodology and learn about the intricate relationship between brain injury and cognition.
OUTCOMES: In this course, students will develop the tools to analyze and interpret scientific articles and will also practice sharing ideas with their peers by way of discussions and presentations. This course will also serve as an introduction to neurological disorders of the brain and discuss the fundamentals of learning and memory.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet for one hour each week spanning the 10-week quarter. The first meeting will include an introduction to and discussion of some basic ideas related to cognitive neuroscience and different brain disorders. Reading (5-15 pages; ~30 min) assignments will consist of scientific journals and popular science articles (i.e. newspapers, publications, etc) and will be assigned for each week’s topic. All students will be expected to read these papers and submit a short summary of the reading each week via presentation slides (6-8 slides; ~30 min). In addition, each week 1 or 2 students will  present their slides to the class (~15 min) and lead the discussion about the assigned reading. Each student will present only one time. When appropriate I will provide a brief overview at the end of the class that will be relevant to the assignments.
GRADING: Pass/no-pass will be assessed based on class participation (40%), quality of presentation (30%) and quality of the weekly summaries (30%). At UC Davis a passing grade is considered a C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ali Izadi, PhD, is an Assistant Professional Researcher and member of the Department of Neurological Surgery. Dr. Izadi’s research is concentrated on developing novel treatment strategies (such as electrical stimulation) using adult and juvenile rodent models to treat neurologic disorders such as epilepsy and traumatic brain injury. On a larger scale, he is interested in evaluating similarities and differences in the biological mechanisms of cognitive dysfunction across multiple neurologic diseases. Fundamentally, the goal of his research is to explore mechanisms and identify innovative therapies in the laboratory and to ultimately improve quality of life for patients with neurologic disorders. He has taught this class since 2019.

TITLE: Learning to Grow: An Introduction to Ecological Medicine Through Gardening and Green Care
ROOM: Green Care Lab, Pine Trails Ranch, 35270 County Road 31, Davis, CA
FOCUS:
Interested in gardening but not sure where to start? Want to get outside, move your body, and learn by doing instead of sitting through another PowerPoint lecture? Step off campus and into the Green Care Lab gardens, a living laboratory of plants and animals directed by Dr. Rebecca Calisi-Rodríguez from the Department of Neurobiology, Physiology, and Behavior.

Rooted in the emerging field of Ecological Medicine, this course invites students to explore how human, animal, and environmental well-being are intertwined, and how caring for living systems can, in turn, promote personal and community health. Students will spend class time working hands-on in the Green Care Lab gardens at Pine Trails Ranch in west Davis, assisting with seasonal planting, weeding, composting, and caring for spaces that support research animals, local youth nature programs, and therapeutic wellness activities.

Each class begins with a guided garden orientation, followed by student-selected projects where learning happens through doing. The following week, students reflect on what they did, what surprised or challenged them, and build on their learning by reading an assigned scientific paper and discovering another that connects directly to their own project.

No prior experience is required - just curiosity, teamwork, and a willingness to get your hands dirty. By the end of the quarter, students will have gained confidence in basic gardening, developed self-directed learning skills, and experienced the restorative power of nature firsthand.

This course meets off campus at the UC Davis Green Care Lab gardens, located at 35270 County Road 31, Davis, CA, about a 10-minute drive from campus. Transportation will be provided, or students may choose to drive their own vehicle.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this seminar, students will be able to:

  • Apply disciplinary knowledge by practicing basic horticultural skills and connecting them to ecological and welfare principles.
  • Demonstrate information literacy by locating, evaluating, and citing credible sources related to gardening, sustainability, or plant–animal systems.
  • Develop critical thinking skills by transforming practical observations into researchable questions and integrating findings into applied practice.
  • Communicate effectively in writing through weekly blog posts that synthesize observation, research, and reflection.
  • Engage in civic and community awareness by contributing to a shared knowledge base with applications for animal welfare and sustainable agriculture.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Wednesday Garden Session at the Ranch

Join Dr. Calisi for a brief garden walk-through to review current needs, ask questions, and choose a task that interests you, such as planting, pruning, weeding, composting, or harvesting. Engage in hands-on garden care, learning by doing and asking questions along the way. The goal is not perfection, but comfort, confidence, and curiosity - getting used to working with the land, understanding living systems, and finding joy in the process of growth.

2. Weekly Reflection (Due Mondays 12 PM)

Write a brief reflection (~500 words) describing what you worked on, what you learned, and what surprised, frustrated, or delighted you. Connect your experience to the assigned reading and include a before-and-after photo of your task.

Assigned Reading:
Students will complete ~5 pages per week from short, accessible materials such as excerpts from Braiding Sweetgrass, The Biggest Little Farm, National Geographic, or brief scholarly readings related to weekly themes. Readings highlight diverse voices, from scientists and farmers to writers and Indigenous thinkers, and require no prior expertise.

Source Literacy:
In Week 1, we’ll discuss how to identify reputable sources. Canvas guides and brief feedback on assignments will reinforce these skills throughout the quarter.
GRADING:
Pass:

  • 80% attendance and active engagement in garden work
  • Completion of self-assigned gardening tasks with care and responsibility
  • Weekly reflections demonstrating observation, research, and application (with sources cited)
  • Evidence of growth in skill, knowledge, and reflective practice
  • Respectful communication and collaboration with peers and faculty

No Pass:

  • Excessive absences (>20% without valid reason)
  • Failure to complete gardening responsibilities
  • Neglect of plant or animal welfare
  • Lack of research/blog submissions, or submissions with inadequate effort
  • No demonstrated growth in skills or critical inquiry
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Rebecca Calisi-Rodríguez is an Associate Professor of Neurobiology, Physiology, and Behavior at UC Davis and a contributing scholar in the emerging field of Ecological Medicine, which explores how human, animal, and environmental well-being are interconnected. For decades, her research has examined how stress shapes biology across species, with discoveries spanning behavior, reproduction, genomics, and neuroendocrinology.

In response to the growing mental health crisis intensified by the COVID-19 pandemic, Dr. Calisi-Rodríguez shifted her focus from studying the harms of stress to exploring pathways to recovery and resilience. In 2022, she founded the UC Davis Green Care Lab, located at Pine Trails Ranch in West Davis, to study how interactions with animals, plants, and natural environments can restore health and connection.

Her work expands traditional “One Health” frameworks into the broader realm of Ecological Medicine, emphasizing relational care and the healing power of working with living systems. Through her teaching, she invites students to step beyond the classroom and into the living laboratories of the Green Care Lab, where science, stewardship, and self-discovery grow side by side.

TITLE: Legal Hot Topics
FOCUS: Are you thinking about going to law school? Or do you have an interest in legal issues? In this seminar we will explore current legal issues that are in the news or pending before the U.S. Supreme Court. Possible topics include free speech, equal protection, the free exercise of religion, and voting rights.
OUTCOMES: Through written and in-class discussion, seminar participants will strengthen their communication skills by discussing complex issues and their ability to converse about issues with people who see things differently from them. Reading complex articles and cases will strengthen participants' reading skills.
ASSIGNMENTS: We will explore seven legal topics. The work involves doing assigned reading of several pages each week, posting a several-paragraph-long response to a weekly question on a discussion board, and discussing issues in class.
GRADING: This class is graded P/NP. Each written assignment will receive a grade of P or NP. Because each assignment is graded P/NP, all assignments are equally weighted. If a student receives a grade of NP on an assignment, they will be invited to revise and resubmit that assignment. Students must receive a P on each assignment to pass the class.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Lisa Klotz, a former practicing lawyer, teaches UWP writing courses through the Writing Center.

TITLE: Locksport: Picking locks for fun
FOCUS: Have you ever wanted to learn how to pick a lock? Take this course, and you'll be able to pick locks. But not any locks. We only pick locks (a) that are not in use and (b) that we have the expressed permission to pick. This is just for fun: we never pick locks in the "real world". Students will learn the various ways locks are constructed and how simple tools can be used to defeat them. Students will practice lock picking first-hand and work their way up the Lockpickers United Belt System to record their expertise. Students will gain an appreciation for the fragility of security through obscurity, the laws and ethics governing lockpicking, and how the locks in their lives keep them safe (or not). At the end of the class, students will be able to make more informed choices when it comes to their own security (and astound their friends and family with their new skills).
OUTCOMES:
1. Roughly half of every class period will be spent in small group work where students have to communicate with each other to work through problems.
  2. We discuss the mathematics of security: for example, the number of pins and the depth of cuts in pin tumbler locks determine the number of possible keys.
  3. Locksport practitioners are very concerned about the unethical side of their sport and are therefore vigilant to ensure their sport remains legal.
  4. Locksport challenges both the mind and body. One cannot see inside a lock, so one must visualize what is happening through a theoretical understanding of lock design. One must also be able to put that theory into practice by feeling and interacting with tiny movements and sounds.
  5. This class isn't designed to develop leadership skills.
  6. We discuss the legality of lock picking around the world. For example, owning lock picks is a felony in Japan.
  7. We introduce the LPU Belt system, which associates student skill with a colored belt (like martial arts). Students are expected to go from White to Yellow to Orange Belt during the course, and will be positioned to continue to higher levels after the course.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class has a partially inverted structure: students watch videos remotely (about 30-60 min per week), come to class for a brief lecture/discussion (20 min), then work in small groups to develop their lock picking skills (30 min). Each week, students will answer a few low-stakes questions online that test and reinforce their knowledge.
GRADING:
Students will be graded on the typical 100 point scale that is used in Canvas.

  • 70% of the grade will be based on weekly quizzes administered through Canvas
  • 15% of the grade will be based on lock picking skill (belt level)
  • 15% of the grade will be based on group activities (judged by participation in class)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Department of Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology who specializes in bioinformatics and genomics. Locksport is one of his many hobbies.

TITLE: Love, Sex and Gender in Premodern Europe
FOCUS: We study ideas about love, sex and gender as they impacted lived experience from late antiquity through the Age of Enlightenment in the late eighteenth century. In the Middle Ages a couple could consummate a clandestine marriage by having sex, without the participation of clergy. Clandestine marriages were suppressed in the sixteenth century, as state and church attempted to impose greater control over the sexuality of their subjects. Sex out of wedlock, prostitution, and same-sex relationships, widely tolerated in late antiquity and the Middle Ages, became criminalized in the early modern period. We explore how and why these changes occurred and how they intersected with religion, class, ethnicity, religion and class. Why did early modern people believe that Jewish men menstruated? Why was sex with Satan a necessary legal element of the crime of witchcraft? Why were Renaissance medical textbooks full of examples of women spontaneously turning into men, but a man turning into a woman was considered medically impossible? When and why did the ideal of "romantic love" develop How was the emergence of a modern “gay” identity in the late eighteenth century related to the development of the modern heterosexual male, which happened at the same time? These are some of the topics we explore through a selection of primary source documents and art.
OUTCOMES:
1. communication skills: this seminar is discussion-based. Students will discuss assigned readings. We also view and analyze relevant artistic representations in class. 

2. Cognitive skills: we will be working through a selection of challenging texts.

3. this seminar will introduce students to historical method, analyzing and contextualizing primary sources.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Aproximately 25 pages reading per week. Informed class discussion of assigned readings. 

One 2-3 page precis on on assigned reading.  

Two students work together to identify, analyze and explain a relevant visual representation, such as Ruben's "The Rape of Ganymede", or Bernini's "Ecstasy of Saint Teresa", or Bruegel's "The Battle of Carnival and Lent." The two students give a joint 10 minute PowerPoint presentation on chosen artwork in class.
GRADING:
50 % : informed, substantive participation in class discussions of assigned readings. 

25 %: power point presentation on chosen art work. 

25% : reading precis.
INSTRUCTORBIO: I am a historian of early modern Europe, i.e. from the Renaissance to the French Revolution. I specialize in the history of crime and deviance. My most recent book "Suicide by Proxy in Early Modern Germany" reconstructs a peculiar early modern type of homicide. My signature classes are "Crime and Punishment in Early Modern Europe," and "The History of Witchcraft and Magic." I am a passionate booster of Education Abroad, and teach a UC Davis summer program in Vienna every August. I am the advisor of the History Club. Learn more about me here: https://lettersandsciencemag.ucdavis.edu/news-noteworthy/students-honor-history-professor-who-connects-todays-conspiracies-humanitys-dark

TITLE: Mind in Motion: Embodying Attention
FOCUS: Attention is never just one thing. One does not merely pay attention or not. Rather, many forms and styles of attention physically and mentally determine our lived experience. As the basis for knowing all aspects of our world, attention is critical to our activities in it because it forms the foundation from which we grasp new knowledge and upon which we then respond. Facility in the actions of attention includes understanding how to direct it, concentrate and free it as well as how to recognize a diversity of its forms that are principle in our organization of time and space. This class will knit practice and theory by drawing on a variety of physical and somatic movement practices including Contact Improvisation and Body-Mind Centering, as well as theoretical investigations that include body theories of performance and neuroscience. Our purpose will be to tie together the movement of attention with the synergistic movement of the body and the mind. Designed to develop and support both theoretical and practical research, class discussions will be based on weekly readings (drawn from a broad range of sources in the humanities, sciences, and the arts) as well as physical research intended to explicate the attentional decisions at work in social and academic life. Our work will culminate in a greater appreciation that the actions of our body and mind move as one phenomenon. How we recognize its motion is significant to our understanding of the world, our body, our identity, and the university.
OUTCOMES: Students will (1) develop effective communication skills through practicing group work which train in how to listen, think quickly, and respond to others in real time; (2) cultivate ethics, respect and empathy by participating in intense group activities; (3) develop cognitive skills through analyzing individual and group behavior in realtime and problem-solving; (5) prepare for lifelong learning by being introduced to many disciplines of embodied practices of listening to one's body and to others, learning how learn, and group engagement practices that are mostly taught outside the university.
ASSIGNMENTS: Classes will be two hours per week and will include discussion of readings (limited to 10 -15 pages per week) and the practice of individual and group  movement exercises aimed to increase physical/mental awareness and creative response. For a final project students will work in groups to develop their own creative movement and attentional exercsises out of class and share weekly progress. Out of class assignments include reading, developing group project exercises, practicing, and writing up experiences with practice (weekly 2-3 paragraphs posted to Canvas): these will take no more than 4 hours per week total. Students must attend all class sessions (or do a makeup assignment for each assignment missed) and participate in class activities and discussions.
GRADING: The course grade will be: practice reports (30%), final project (presentation exercise and writeup, 40%), class participation (30%). A student needs to achieve at least a C- (70%) in each of the core areas in order to receive a passing grade. Grading: 100-90 (A), 89-80 (B), 79-70 (C), 69-60 (D), <59 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Joseph Dumit is Chair of Performance Studies, and Professor of Science & Technology Studies, and Anthropology. He studies brains, minds, bodies, drugs, media, virtual reality, capitalism, gaming, and improvisation. He has written two books: Picturing Personhood: Brain Scans and Biomedical Identity, and Drugs for Life: How Pharmaceutical Companies Define Our Health. He is also a co-founder of the ModLab for game studies and game development (where he's currently making a game on fracking), and works with the KeckCAVES on 3D development for science and the arts. http://dumit.net  

Ileanna Sophia Cheladyn is a Canadian dance artist and PhD student in sociocultural anthropology. Her practice prioritizes collaboration, care, and delight. She works with improvisation, slowness, and disorientation to invite meaningful shifts in technique, aesthetics, and relations of power. www.ileannacheladyn.com

TITLE: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction
FOCUS:
As life in college is chaotic, we all need to reduce our stress levels. One way to do that is through Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR), and this seminar will provide a sense of community as we practice MBSR together. Mindfulness can be defined as “paying attention to present moment experience with open curiosity and a willingness to be with what is” (Diana Winston). This kind of attention will help you practice strategies for stress reduction but also train your ability to focus to strengthen your attention during your studies.

We will be following the Palouse course (https://palousemindfulness.com/MBSR/index.html), which “is a blend of meditations, body awareness, and mindful movement: learning through practice and study how your body handles (and can resolved) stress neurologically” (https://palousemindfulness.com/MBSR/week0.html). The course consists of readings, videos, mindfulness practice, and worksheets to keep track of your progress. The Palouse course also offers an optional certificate of completion if students decide to submit their worksheets to them (free of cost). While this course is self-guided, we will be completing it together during the quarter, discussing both the readings and our individual mindfulness practice.

Through our discussion of the readings and through our practice of MBSR together, we will learn a mindfulness practice that will accompany us during our college years and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop effective communication skills through class discussions,
  2. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy,
  3. Develop strong focus abilities through mindfulness practice,
  4. Develop leadership skills through discussion leading,
  5. Prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Complete weekly readings and videos, daily practice, and completing formal and informal worksheets.

Complete informal discussion boards to share your progress.

Lead discussion for one class meeting (read at least two supplementary materials, summarize them to the class, and come up with discussion questions to engage your peers in discussion). While this is not a formal group assignment, multiple students will be assigned on the same discussion day.

Write a one-page description of what you’ve learned in this course.

 

Time commitment: 2 hours of reading each week, plus 30-35 minutes of formal practice each day.
GRADING:
The class will be graded pass/no pass. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

To obtain a Pass for this class, you will

  • Complete at least 10 worksheets.
  • Participate in at least 8 class meetings.
  • Complete at least 8 discussion boards.
  • Lead discussion on the readings during one class meeting.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a continuing lecturer for the Writing Center. She teaches upper-division professional writing courses. She has experience with different types of meditation.

TITLE: Modern Marvel: Bridges Around the World
FOCUS: This seminar course aims to: 1) introduce different types of modern bridges and their aesthetics; 2) give a quick peek on bridge disasters in the history; 3) provide a tool's-eye view of just how a mega-bridge like San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge gets built; and 4) provide hands-on design experience via participation in a web-based bridge design contest. Case studies on many famous bridges around the world will also be included.
OUTCOMES: The students will learn about different types of bridges in the world and how they are typically constructed during this Freshman Seminar course. Basic concept such as bridges, structures, design and engineering will be introduced through formal lectures and case studies. The students will also have the opportunity to apply the knowledge in designing their own bridge and compete among their classmates towards the end of the quarter.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly reading materials and an oral presentation on a selected case study. The course grading is Pass/No Pass. Students will be required to participate in the in-class discussions. An individual project will be assigned where the student is required to prepare for one oral presentation on a bridge case study chosen from the list provided by the instructor (bridges other than the ones provided in the list need to be approved by the instructor upon consultation). The project and presentation will be evaluated on three different categories: (1) Content (e.g., covering important aspects such as construction, design, history); (2) Organization (e.g., well structured, offering summary); and (3) Delivery (e.g., effective use of visual tools such as pictures and videos, clearness). The students are also required to participate in a web-based bridge design contest to gain fun hands-on design experience.
GRADING: The course grade will be based on: in-class discussion (20%), preparation on the case study topic (20%), peer evaluation on the student’s project (10%), web-based bridge design contest (20%), and the instructor’s evaluation on the student’s presentation and design (30%). Class attendance (no less than 80%) is mandatory.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Dawn Cheng is a member of the faculty in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering. Her research interests include combined field and laboratory experimental investigation and analytical/computational simulations; design and analysis of bridge components and systems; application of Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites to innovative infrastructures and renewal; design, analysis and durability mechanics of FRP composites; and engineered materials using recycle wastes.

TITLE: Navigate College, Navigate Life
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS:
This innovative two-quarter seminar is designed to empower first- and second-year students to explore who they are, what they value, and where they’re headed. Winter focuses on designing your college experience and spring expands to designing your career and life aspirations. Through integration and application of a creative problem-solving methodology, known as Life Design, and leadership development practices, you will learn how to create a meaningful college experience and build a foundation for your own life path.

This seminar is designed to be an experiential learning space that will ask you to explore new ways of thinking and challenge you to step outside their comfort zone. Through hands-on exercises, design thinking, small and large group dialogue, peer feedback, critical reflection, storytelling, community- building, mentorship, and academic advising, you will begin cultivating a clearer sense of direction, purpose, and empowered agency which will influence your college experience and how you shape a purposeful life path.

By the end of this seminar, you will emerge with stronger leadership skills and a personalized roadmap that weaves together your values, academic interests, career goals, and aspirations – equipping you to make confident, informed decisions about your future, in your own voice and on your own terms. 

This seminar is a 2-quarter commitment. Winter focuses on designing your college experience and spring expands to designing your career and life aspirations.
OUTCOMES:
Winter - Designing your college experience:

  • Identify and articulate your values, interests, skills, and strengths
  • Create a personal definition of success that honors your values, culture, and community
  • Articulate how your lived experience influences your identity as a leader
  • Define areas to focus on for personal growth
  • Understand the fundamental principles of design thinking and their application to college planning
  • Create prototypes of multiple college pathways
  • Implement small steps to test, reflect, and adapt drafted pathways based upon collected data, new discoveries, and experiences
  • Refine college goals based upon collected data, new discoveries, and experiences

Spring - Designing your career and life aspirations:

  • Align your values, skills, and strengths with career interests
  • Extend your personal definition of success to career and life goals
  • Define areas to focus on for professional skill development
  • Apply design thinking principles to career and life planning
  • Create prototypes of multiple career and life pathways
  • Implement small steps to test, reflect, and adapt drafted pathways based upon collected data, new discoveries, and experiences
  • Employ leadership skills when pursuing academic and professional opportunities
  • Establish a team of mentors who will help guide you on your journey
  • Refine career and life goals based upon collected data, new discoveries, and experiences
ASSIGNMENTS: Active engagement in class activities and group discussions. Journaling, reading assignments, create a portfolio, and class presentation.
GRADING:
This is a pass/no pass course.  Pass based on C- (70%) or better

Grades breakdown is as follow:

30% In-Class Engagement

20% Journaling

20% Reading 

15% Portfolio

15% Presentation

There are no final exams and grading is not curved based
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Lisa Rapalyea holds a PhD in Human Development, an MS in Child Development, & Bachelor’s degrees in Biology & Psychology from the University of California at Davis (UC Davis). She currently is a continuing lecturer / adjunct faculty teaching several developmental courses for Human Development & Family Studies. Her doctoral research focused on the intersection of sports involvement & character, which fully explored character development & how it relates to leadership skills, as well influences on both social & personality development. On a more personal note, Dr. Rapalyea has given guest lectures on her academic journey as a deaf/multiracial/late bloomer, connecting that journey with perseverance & resiliency.

Christie Navarro - Prior to serving as Director of the Lead by Design Initiative, she directed the Center for Leadership Learning (CLL) for almost two decades. Christie is a first-generation college student & earned her Bachelor of Science in Psychology & Master of Arts in Higher Educational Leadership from California State University, Sacramento. Before UC Davis, Christie served the California state representative for the college admission testing organization ACT; as a regional outreach coordinator for UC Merced Early Academic Outreach Program (EAOP); & a case manager for Court Appointed Special Advocates (CASA) of Tulare County.
