TITLE: Navigating Challenges as a Woman in Engineering
FOCUS:
An important tone-setter for most college students is the first year or two years of their undergraduate experience. Negative experiences, such as the lack of community, during this vulnerable period may adversely affect women's sense of belonging in engineering and lead to potential dropout. Nationally, women have dropped out of engineering at a higher rate than men, despite having equal or superior math and physics scores coming out of high school. This seminar was formed to address this issue, better prepare you for the academic environment you are about to enter and possibly gain additional allies to tackle this problem with us.

This course aims to provide a brainstorming and discussion platform for the following topics in the seminar:

  1. Discussion through academic and professional publication: status of women in engineering and the associated climate; how women engineers negotiate with their gender and professional identities
  2. Case studies: how to navigate isolating behavior in academic setting (case study 1) and professional setting (case study 2)
  3. Learn from professional women engineers in both academia and industry through a Women in Engineering panel
  4. See yourself as an engineer through tours of the Engineering Student Design Center and a 3D printing workshop
  5. Gain allies in engineering through a free lunch with students, staff, and faculty in Biomedical Engineering.

The instructor recommends this course to:

  1. Women who have declared an engineering major, intend to transfer into an engineering major, and/or would like to work in an engineering company upon graduation or in the future.
  2. Allies who would like to learn about the experiences of women in engineering.

The instructors are housed in Biomedical Engineering (BME), so some of the examples used in this course may be BME-specific but will apply to all engineering disciplines.
OUTCOMES:
Upon completion of this seminar, we hope for you to:

  1. Develop high cognitive skills. You will discover your own engineering identity that incorporates your gender identity. You will also explore ways to improve the current environment experienced by women in engineering.
  2. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy. You will learn about the challenges women engineers face in the current world. You will arm yourselves with respect and empathy in gender equity.
  3. Prepare for lifelong learning. We hope to provide a platform and build a community of allies for women in engineering.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students are expected to actively engage in class discussions involving individual brainstorming and group discussions about the reading assigned for that week. Discussions will be held in groups of 3-4. Weekly assignments include a reflection (approximately 250 words) on a reading that ranges from 6 to 20 pages. In this course, longer readings tend to be industrial or academic reports (thus consuming less time per page) and shorter readings tend to be academic papers (thus consuming more time per page). On average, each student is expected to spend two hours on reading and reflection.

Each student is expected to attend at least one Women+ lunch hosted by the biomedical engineering (BME) department during the quarter and write a 100-word reflection on their experience at the event.

At the end of the course, each student will generate a two-page, single-spaced reflection on the contents covered in the course.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or higher. 30% of the grade is based on weekly discussions and participation; 50% of the grade is based on the weekly reflections; 5% of the grade is based on the Women+ lunch reflections; 15% of the grade is based on the final reflection.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Tiffany Chan (she/her) is a current M.S. student in Biomedical Engineering. She is a member of the Cube3 lab (@cube3ucd on Instagram), starting her research journey as an undergraduate student in the lab. She is one of the original designers of the first-year seminar with Dr. Wang and have served as a peer mentor of this course for 4 times.

Dr. Xianglong Wang (he/him) is an Assistant Professor of Teaching in Biomedical Engineering (BME). Dr. Wang leads the Cube3 Lab, which is dedicated to improving the sense of belonging and quality of life in engineering students with a focus on BME students.

This FYS will be supported by an undergraduate peer mentor through the Aggie Connection program. Ahva Razavian (they/them) is a 4th-year undergraduate student in the Cube3 lab and will be a peer mentor for this course a second time.

TITLE: Nuclear Science and Society
FOCUS: When you hear about nuclear science, what’s the first thing that comes to mind? Nuclear power plants? Invisible nuclear radiation? How about the ways nuclear science appears in popular culture and the media? In this seminar, students will expand their knowledge of “all things nuclear” by exploring the uses, sources, and impacts of nuclear science in today’s world. Course topics will be a mixture of those selected by the instructor, and those of greatest interest to enrolled students.
OUTCOMES:
In this seminar, students will…

- Develop and practice effective communication skills during in-class discussions and small group presentations on historic nuclear events.

- Explore the complexity of modern application of nuclear technology and the ethical questions surrounding responsibility for radioactive materials and human-generated nuclear waste, from local and global perspectives.

- Deepen their knowledge of nuclear chemistry principles.

- Cultivate curiosity about the cultural and societal relevance of scientific discoveries.

- Consider different perspectives and cultivate empathy around historic events involving radiation-related accidents.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to actively participate during class activities including readings and reflection, group brainstorming, critiques, debate, concept mapping, small-group discussion, and others. Assignments outside of class will include reading (about 15 pages / 30 minutes weekly), videos (10-20 minutes weekly), internet research (about 20 minutes weekly), and weekly reflections and responses to these materials (30 minutes weekly). Reflections may be written (~200 words) or multimedia (creativity encouraged!) Students will prepare and give an individual 10-minute presentation of a historic nuclear event or disaster of their choosing in the second half of the quarter (about 5 h design and preparation).
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. A passing grade will be awarded for a course grade of 70% or higher. Students will be evaluated on in-class individual and group participation and discussion (30%), individual and team-based homework tasks (20%), weekly reflections (written and multimedia) on assigned reading and videos (25%), an individual 10-minute in-class presentation assignment on a nuclear-science event or disaster (15%), and courteous classroom practices such as respectful dialogue and inclusive discussion (10%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Julia Chamberlain is an Associate Professor of Teaching in the Chemistry Department. An active participant in the Scholarship of Teaching and Learning, her research focuses on why learning chemistry can be hard, and how to make it easier to understand. She teaches general chemistry, inorganic chemistry, and coordinates an undergraduate Learning Assistant program for the CHE 2 series. In her free time, she grows food in her backyard and tries not to think too hard about mutually assured destruction.

TITLE: Nutrition, Health and Your Environment
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS: Wise food choices for UC Davis students involve more than just eating what tastes good, is quick or inexpensive! Your mind and body don’t care what your excuse is! If you don’t make wise food choices, you will feel and look tired and unhealthy, have challenges with mental focus and sleep, and the planet may suffer as well. Making wise food choices involves both knowledge and motivation. We will explore the role of nutrition on personal health and performance, as well as on the environment. Topics include optimizing physical and mental energy, phytochemicals (from cocoa and tea), healthy beverages, heart health, performance nutrition, and environmental impact of food production. The seminar will emphasize high cognitive skills through the analysis and discussion of academic and popular articles and media reports concerning food and health claims. Effective communication skills will be developed through small- and large-group discussions, both inside and outside of the class. The teaching format will involve a short lecture, extensive in-class discussions, a weekly out-of-class meeting with a study partner, and a weekly homework assignment.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn how to think critically and express their thoughts in a concise, coherent manner during weekly discussions and homework assignments. Students will develop online library research skills, learn basic concepts of nutrition, engage in weekly goal-setting activities and explore popular health claims regarding foods, beverages and dietary supplements.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet once a week for two hours. Class sessions will consist of a brief lecture by the instructor and extensive discussion among students. Each week, a topic will be given and students will do independent reading to prepare for the next week’s discussion (~3-4 hours; 30-40 pages/week), meet with a partner outside of class to discuss your reading (~1 hour), and write a homework assignment (1-2 pages) summarizing the reading (~1-2 hours).  A mid-term outline of a final paper, and a final paper (4-5 pages) synthesizing a topic of your interest, and exploring at least two facets of the nutrition debate around your topic, is required.
GRADING: A letter grade will be based on participation in weekly discussions and goal-setting homework (30%), weekly writing assignments (1-2 pages) assessing at least two sides of arguments related to the week’s nutrition topic (50%), a mid-term outline of a final report (10%) and a final paper (4-5 pages plus references) on a pre-approved topic of the student’s choosing (10%). Letter grades will be based on the following final percentages: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), and <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Robert Hackman is a research professor and health advocate in the Department of Nutrition. His current research studies focus on the role of fruits, nuts and bioactive plant ingredients in human health and performance. He has been teaching nutrition for more than 40 years, including courses on weight management, sports performance, and a holistic approach to nutrition in health and disease. Dr. Hackman has offered this seminar in past years, which have been well received.

TITLE: Persian Echoes: The Heartbeat of Rumi's Mystical Poetry
FOCUS:
This seminar explores the life, poetry, and spiritual teachings of Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207–1273 C.E.), who is arguably the most celebrated Persian Sufi poet of all time. We will study selections from the Masnavi (The Spiritual Couplets) in English translation to understand the poet’s conceptions of love, suffering, the ego, the journey of the soul, et cetera. By examining these themes, the course invites students to engage with questions of meaning, self-discovery, and the human search for connection in our complex modern world.


The course combines close readings of Rumi’s poetry with seminar-style discussions and reflective discussion posts. Students will be encouraged to share personal reflections and insights while practicing analytical skills through group dialogue and written assignments. This interactive format fosters a supportive environment for both intellectual inquiry and spiritual reflection.


Students will learn how to analyze important poems, develop critical thinking about spiritual language, and explore the cultural and historical contexts of Rumi’s work. Through reflective discussions, students will also engage in introspection, connecting Rumi’s teachings to their own experiences.


By engaging with Rumi’s poetry and ideas, students will cultivate emotional intelligence, deepen their understanding of diverse worldviews, and enhance their ability to interpret complex texts. The course encourages mindfulness, empathy, and openness—qualities essential for personal growth and meaningful interpersonal relationships. Ultimately, students will leave with a richer sense of the heart’s mirror—clearer, more polished, and ready to reflect beauty and truth in their own lives.
OUTCOMES:
This seminar cultivates high cognitive skills by guiding students through layered texts that demand interpretive insight, historical awareness, and emotional sensitivity. Students will develop effective communication skills through frequent discussion, reflective writing, and collaborative exploration of complex ideas.


Rumi’s teachings—centered on love, humility, the inner self, and divine longing—create a powerful context for students to cultivate empathy, ethical reflection, and intercultural understanding. As students wrestle with concepts of ego, suffering, and beauty, they are invited to connect abstract ideas to personal and global questions, deepening their self-awareness and capacity for meaningful dialogue. By examining how Rumi's poetry resonates across time and culture, students prepare for lifelong learning—learning that is not only intellectual, but also ethical and inwardly transformative.
ASSIGNMENTS: Assignments in this seminar are designed to foster reflection, discussion, and personal engagement. Students will be expected to complete no more than 20 pages of reading per week, and they will be assessed on three components: active class participation, discussion posts on Canvas, and a final reflective portfolio. Discussion posts (short weekly reflections) allow students to engage thoughtfully with Rumi’s poetry and ideas in a conversational format. The final portfolio invites students to select and revise their favorite posts, in addition to writing a brief personal reflection on their learning. These assignments are meant to support meaningful exploration of the self and spirit.
GRADING:
Class Participation (30%): intellectually stimulating participation in discussions.
Canvas Discussion Posts (40%): weekly short reflections (250–400 words) on Rumi’s poetry and themes.
Final Reflective Portfolio (30%): A submission including three revised discussion posts and either a 1- to 2-page personal reflection on individual growth or a short essay applying Rumi’s teachings to a contemporary global issue—such as cultural conflict, ecological ethics, or social alienation—demonstrating the connection between spiritual insights and real-world challenges.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Navid Saberi-Najafi holds a PhD in comparative literature, currently serving as Continuing Lecturer at the University of California, Davis. He has 17 years of experience teaching a vast array of language and literature courses: In Iran, he taught English-language proficiency courses, and in the US, he has taught courses on English composition, on comparative literature, and on the Persian language since 2009. At UC Davis, he was one of the co-founders of the Persian program. Before pursuing his graduate studies in the US, Saberi-Najafi was an international relations officer at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad in northeastern Iran. He also served as Persian Literature Examiner Responsible for the International Baccalaureate (Cardiff, Wales branch).


Saberi-Najafi’s interest in Jalal al-Din Rumi’s work led to a recent bilingual interview on the poet’s enduring spiritual legacy. This course was created to offer students a meaningful encounter with Rumi’s poetry in English while opening a window into the rich literary and mystical traditions of Persian. Students who feel drawn to these themes are encouraged to explore Persian language study at UC Davis to experience Rumi’s poetry in its original language.

TITLE: Precision Medicine: Understanding Epilepsy to Develop Personalized Treatments
FOCUS:
Seizures and epilepsy might look the same yet arise from different molecular and cellular mechanisms. This distinction is crucial for understanding why current medications are not very effective. We will trace the trajectory from identifying an epilepsy-linked genetic variant to developing personalized treatments that reverse its effects, emphasizing concepts and decision-making rather than technical detail. Along the way, we will discuss how to predict and verify whether a variant causes a gain- or loss-of-function and how to screen candidate drugs in diverse cell models. We will focus on repurposing of existing drugs, improving them to reduce side effects, and designing entirely new ones. Gene therapy will be discussed briefly.

We will also address ethics and critically weigh when animal models remain necessary alongside human cell models. Throughout, the seminar highlights how scientists think: framing questions, evaluating evidence, and translating basic discoveries into potential treatments. By the end, students will understand why particular neuronal populations and brain regions are differentially affected in genetic epilepsies and how that knowledge guides precision treatment, without assuming prior coursework in neuroscience or biophysics. Throughout the course, students will not only learn about epilepsy and how to treat it, but they

Students will also gain insight into a variety of methods used not only in neurobiology, but many other fields, and practice habits that support lifelong learning. This seminar will interest students drawn to medicine, neuroscience, biology, data-driven problem solving, or the ethical questions around emerging brain therapies.
OUTCOMES: This course introduces the basics of epilepsy, genetics, and their role in developing precision treatments, with an emphasis on ideas over technical detail. Students will practice effective communication through discussion and respectful peer feedback, develop presentation skills for explaining scientific data clearly and effectively, and to collaborate as a team. This seminar also covers how to respond to an epileptic seizure. Ethics and responsibility are integrated throughout, including model choice and limitations (cell vs. animal), equity in access to precision therapies, data integrity and reproducibility, and patient privacy. By the end, students will be able to outline a simple mechanism-to-therapy pathway, interpret core claims from, assigned papers, and reflect on how scientific findings move toward clinical application.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet for one hour each week spanning the 10-week quarter. The first meeting will include an introduction to epilepsy, epilepsy first aid, and epilepsy genetics. The second and third weeks will consist of informal presentations on the basic tools used in epilepsy research, including plasmid design, basic electrophysiology, and a discussion of different animal models. In preparation for these informal lectures, reading materials from scientific journals and mainstream media will be provided. In the following weeks, 2-3 students will present a scientific paper in which a genetic discovery led to a precision medicine approach for the treatment (20-30min). All students are expected to read the paper and discuss it during and after the presentation.
GRADING: Pass/no-pass will be based on active class participation (40%), quality of the group presentation (30%), and understanding of key concepts demonstrated through three take-home quizzes (30%). Active participation includes coming prepared, contributing to discussions, asking questions about the assigned papers, and offering respectful feedback during peer presentations. The group presentation grade reflects clarity of explanation, interpretation of the paper, balanced teamwork, and the ability to answer basic questions. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
John Gray, MD PhD, is an Associate Professor in the Department of Neurology, core faculty at the Center for Neuroscience, and Associate Director of the Institute for Psychedelics and Neurotherapeutics. His lab studies molecular and cellular mechanisms of neuronal and synaptic function and how they are disrupted in neurological and psychiatric disorders. Current projects examine the molecular mechanisms of epileptogenesis after induced seizures and in rodent models of neurodevelopmental epilepsy and autism.  A long term goal of Dr. Gray’s research is to develop novel therapies that not only treat symptoms but harness neuroplasticity to alter disease trajectory. 

Johanna Krueger, PhD, is a postdoctoral fellow at the Institute for Psychedelics and Neurotherapeutics. Her research focuses on uncovering disease mechanisms of genetic epilepsies to enable precision treatments for patients who do not respond to existing medications. Her work spans a broad spectrum of disciplines from determining pathogenic variants in patients to using rodent models of epilepsy for drug screening. Dr. Krueger’s ultimate goal is to understand the molecular basis of epilepsy, the similarities and differences across different types of epilepsy, and why many patients also have other neurological and psychiatric diagnoses, then to translate this knowledge into treatment—or a cure.

TITLE: Prostate Cancer and Drug Treatment
FOCUS:
In the United States, about a quarter million prostate cancer new cases and over 30,000 deaths each year. Prostate cancer is diagnosed more than any other type of cancer in men and is the second leading cause of cancer related deaths in the same population, behind only lung cancer. About 1 man in 8 will be diagnosed with prostate cancer during his lifetime. After initial diagnosis of prostate cancer, radical prostatectomy and radiation are used to treat the primary tumors. When cancer recurs, drugs are used to block androgen (such as testosterone) activity associated with the growth of cancerous tissues. For a long time, the treatment has been hampered by drug resistance-the tendency for drugs to stop working over an extended period of time. These anti-androgen drugs include enzalutamide (XTANDI®), abiraterone acetate (ZYTIGA®) and apalutamide (ERLEADA™). Although all drugs are highly effective initially, patients quickly develop resistance to these drugs through mechanisms that are not completely understood. Therefore, there is an urgent need to identify resistant mechanisms to improve the treatment outcome of castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC).

This course will be a seminar format and the students will learn the basic knowledge of the prostate cancer development and drug treatment. A topic with associated texts will be assigned weekly and explored in small group conversation. Students will be working and learning by groups and discuss the assigned topic and present their views and points.
OUTCOMES:
1. Research Process: From this course, the students will learn the biology of prostate cancer and the treatments that improve outcomes for men with lethal prostate cancer.

2. Discovery: The course will also integrate the data generated from the lab and the students will learn more basic molecular biology technologies and animal studies. 

3. Broadly Relevant Work: From this course, students will broadly understand the prostate cancer disease and gain the knowledge of drug treatment.

4. Collaboration: The students will be assigned by different groups during the course and they are encouraged to discuss the topic and work together to learn the research data and present their views of the research.

5. Iteration: From the course, students will be more familiar with the cancer disease and will have the general idea and knowledge in cancer development and treatment.
ASSIGNMENTS: This is one unit seminar course. The seminar includes 3 sessions taught by the instructor, including the basic information of prostate cancer, the drug treatment, and research ideas. The rest of the sessions consisted of students' presentation focusing on the topics related to prostate cancer and drug treatment. The students are expected to read articles or news related to the prostate cancer and treatment. The students will need to spend some additional time in topic searching and article reading after class (depending on the weekly topic, 2-3 hours per week). If they are reading articles, they are expected to submit reflections of questions and discuss them during the course. Student will discuss the topics with their team members regarding the presentation and they are expected to present their views and opinions during the class (20 mins PowerPoint presentation each student or group).
GRADING:
The student performance will be assessed by P/NP.

The weekly written reflections will be based on the assigned reading, oral presentation and preparation of discussion questions based on the weekly reading.

There is no final exam.

The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Chengfei Liu is an Associate Professor in the Department of Urologic Surgery at UC Davis. Dr. Liu has extensive expertise in clinical oncology and prostate cancer research. He has identified novel resistant mechanisms and developed innovative pharmaceutical approaches to treat advanced prostate cancer patients. He has also generated a number of unique tools that have allowed him to elucidate how resistance emerges in late stage prostate cancer. Dr. Liu's long-term research interest is to bridge the basic and clinical research that ultimately yields novel translational efforts in urologic oncology. His ideas and research will yield new paradigms for protein post translational modification and drug resistance in cancer cells, advance the understanding of cancer biology and provide opportunities for innovative cancer therapeutics. Dr. Liu's current research program focuses on understanding the mechanisms of therapy resistance and progression in lethal prostate cancer with specialized focus on drug development for the treatment of prostate cancer. Dr. Liu has interests in nuclear receptors, chaperone protein modification, ubiquitin proteasome regulation, steroid hormone biosynthesis and metabolism, clinical translational research and tumor immunology.

TITLE: Pyramids of the Ancient World
FOCUS: While most people are familiar with the pyramids of the Giza Plateau, Egypt, fewer are aware that pyramid construction is known in many ancient societies outside of Egypt, including the Sudan, Mexico, Peru, and Iraq. This seminar will use pyramids as a gateway to studying ancient societies. We will examine how and why people constructed pyramids, how long ago these monuments were built, how they differ socially and physically across different regions, how archaeologists study them, and why should we care about preserving them for future generations. The seminar will include some lecture by the professor, one presentation on pyramids from a particular area and/or ancient culture per participant, and discussion among class members.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the scientific method in archaeology, the techniques archaeologists use to study pyramids, and how different countries preserve and present such monuments today, especially within the tourist industry. This will provide a more global perspective on monumental construction in ancient societies, and how those constructions are experienced today. Students will also learn how different ancient societies arrived at structures that are similar in outward appearance (pyramidal in shape), at least superficially, but quite different in terms of how they were made and used. Students will learn about a specific set of pyramids from a particular area of the world in greater detail through a small research project using on-line and/or library resources, and will lead the class in short discussion on this topic to develop their communication and leadership skills.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will read approximately one article per week (5-10 pages each, or about 1 hour of reading per week), focusing on a particular topic, such as the methods of pyramid construction, or preservation of pyramids. They will lead one discussion about a particular set of pyramids (selected and assigned by the professor), summarize this information in a 5-10 minute presentation to the class and lead the class in a 10 minute discussion. For that discussion, they will review what archaeologists know about pyramids from this particular country and time period (done outside of class), and will examine how those pyramids exemplify or relate to the weekly topical reading. This review should run approximately 10 minutes. They will then lead a discussion for 5-10 minutes, by presenting two to three general questions designed to elicit discussion from other class participants, using the pyramids they studied as a focal point of the conversation. Preparation for the discussion will require on-line and/or library research, design and construction of material such as a handout or powerpoint presentation, and generation of a set of questions to guide a short discussion in class. This should take approximately 5 hours of preparation time. These presentations will generally occur in the second half of the quarter. At the end of the quarter, students will prepare a 2-3 page written and formal discussion of the pyramids they researched, relating all of the different weekly topics to these particular pyramids (approximately 5 hours of research and writing time). This writing assignment will be turned in during the last week of class.
GRADING: Grading is based on class participation in discussions (33%), performance leading one discussion group (33%), and a writing assignment (33%). Grading is P/NP. The grade of "Pass" is awarded for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Jelmer Eerkens is a professor of anthropology, specializing in archaeology. He conducts research in a wide range of locations around the world, especially California and Peru, but has also worked on projects based in the Nile Valley region, American Samoa, and Europe. He is interested in the long-term evolution of human societies.

TITLE: Salud publica
FOCUS: This first-year undergraduate seminar introduces students to the fundamentals of public health through a culturally and linguistically responsive curriculum taught entirely in Spanish. Designed for heritage speakers and advanced Spanish learners, the course fosters early academic engagement, bilingual health literacy, and a foundation in core public health concepts such as health equity, epidemiology, social determinants of health, and chronic disease prevention. Through case-based discussions and project-based learning, students will explore the intersection of public health and community realities, particularly within Latinx and Spanish-speaking populations.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

  1. Identify and explain key concepts in public health in Spanish.
  2. Discuss the impact of mental health, chronic disease, and social inequality on health outcomes.
  3. Analyze how social, political, and economic factors shape access to care and health disparities.
  4. Critically evaluate health information sources and understand the role of misinformation.
  5. Apply learned concepts in a final project focused on a health topic of their choice relevant to Spanish-speaking communities.
ASSIGNMENTS:
• Weekly Participation & Discussions (30%): Active engagement in Spanish, small group discussions, and class activities.
  • Short Reflection Assignments (20%): Written or verbal reflections responding to key class topics (2–3 total, in Spanish).
  • Final Project & Presentation (50%): A 15–20 minute group or individual presentation that explores a public health issue in depth (topic chosen by students, presented in Spanish).
GRADING: Each student will be expected to engage with weekly topics, participate actively in Spanish-language class discussions, and complete several short reflective assignments. Each student will also be asked to collaborate on a final public health project and give a 15–20 minute oral presentation in Spanish. Grading will be based on the short reflective assignments (30%), oral assignments (class participation and final presentation) (20%), and the final public health project (50%). Students will receive feedback on each assignment to support growth in both content understanding and Spanish language use.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Alfredo is a second-year medical student at the UC Davis School of Medicine and a graduate of the UC Davis, where they earned an MPH with a focus in Epidemiology. With a background in community-based research, clinical care, and health education, he is committed to expanding health equity for Spanish-speaking and undeserved populations. Prior to medical school, he led quality improvement and access-to-care initiatives at student-run clinics and conducted research on diabetes management and cancer survivorship in binational contexts. He is passionate about teaching and mentorship through influenced by his previous roles as a Teaching Assistant with the Department of Chicana/o Studies.

Mónica Torreiro-Casal holds a Ph.D. in Counseling Psychology from Northeastern University, Boston, MA and a MFT (Marriage and Family therapist master’s degree) with Latinx mental health emphasis from Santa Clara University, CA. She is a Continuing Lecturer for the Chicana/o/x department where she teaches Latinx and Chicanx mental health and psychology classes.Her research, clinical and teaching interests include Latinx and Immigrant Community Mental Health, Feminist Chicanx Psychology and Minority Qualitative Research. She conducts research on immigration and mental health and mentors students on their research projects and academic journeys to contribute to their professional development.

TITLE: Searching for Shakespeare's Soul: Codes, Conspiracy, and Catholicism
FOCUS: This class will investigate the assertion that William Shakespeare was a recusant Catholic. In order to do this, we will first situate Shakespeare in his historical context, learning that he lived in a tumultuous time in English history, especially with reference to religious belief and practice. We will then review evidence that has been uncovered indicating that Shakespeare was a Roman Catholic and that he encoded his writings to challenge the Protestant government of the Tudors. The bulk of our course time will be spent reading and analyzing one of Shakespeare’s plays (to be determined by what, if any, is being performed locally during the spring) for what it might disclose about the author’s religious beliefs.
OUTCOMES: Students will be introduced to and become familiar with: • The historical, religious issues of Elizabethan England, • The scant biographical information available about William Shakespeare, and • The evidence linking Shakespeare to Roman Catholicism. Students will gain critical analytical skill through careful dissection of one Shakespearian play.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will read a combination of primary and secondary material over the course of the term (not to exceed fifty pages of text per class meeting). The majority of the class meetings will be devoted to careful analysis of the selected play: therefore, preparation for those class meetings will be less ‘reading’ and more assessment of a segment of the material for in-class discussion. Students will attend seminar prepared to discuss the material, and will participate in in-class group activities. Students will write five weekly responses to the course readings (not to exceed 150 words each week).
GRADING: Students will be evaluated on attendance and participation (50% of course grade). Class meetings will be heavily focused on discussion, and students will engage in group activities during class to facilitate participation. There will be no exams for the course, but each student will write five weekly responses to questions concerning the course readings (Students will choose the five responses they wish to write out of eight possible weeks. Each response is worth 10% of the course grade).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Wendy Terry is a senior Continuing Lecturer in the Religious Students Department. She frequently teaches survey courses in addition to textually based courses on the Hebrew Scriptures and Christian New Testament. Her primary research is in the history of Christian Spirituality, with an emphasis in the medieval period on the British Isles and in France.

TITLE: Sleep and Circadian Rhythm
FOCUS:
We spend 1/3 of our life sleeping. But why? Answering this question forms the basis of a major experimental goal of our laboratory. The second question we seek to answer revolves around “how” the brain generates and regulates the daily sleep-wake cycles. We feel that these are very important questions given that dysregulation of sleep (and circadian rhythms) are considered major risk factors for myriad of neurological, neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer’s disease. Sleep dysregulation is also a major risk factor in the development of many non-neurological disorders, such as diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Regulation of the sleep-wake cycle is largely an endogenous function of the brain, but many physiological functions can strongly influence the regulation and normal expression of the sleep-wake cycle, suggesting complex regulatory mechanisms.  Given that 1 in 3 adults in the United State have sleep complaints, including an inability to achieve either sufficient amounts of sleep or sleep of high quality, these are important problems to address.

The goal of this course is to not only learn about the neurobiology of sleep and circadian rhythm, but also to discuss new scientific articles and hear about the backgrounds and careers of sleep scientists. The course will begin with two introductory seminars. Thereafter each class period will explore individual themes related to sleep in society (via current press releases, podcasts or TV segments) and how sleep research is addressing these issues (via discussion of a recently published scientific article). Finally, students will have the opportunity to participate in the annual infographic contest organized by the Sleep Research Society (CASH PRIZES!).

The instructors are eager to adapt the course to specific needs. For example, a transcript of the podcasts will be available. The students are encouraged to discuss specific adjustments.
OUTCOMES: Each week, one or two students (depending on the number of students enrolled in the course) will present a scientific article, which is intended to promote the development of effective communication skills. The article will then be discussed by all students in the class to develop both a global perspective and critical thinking skills. The students will moreover develop a deeper understanding of the neurobiology of sleep and circadian rhythms. Finally, the student will be exposed to the diversity of sleep and circadian phenotypes within the world's population and cultivate acceptance, respect, and empathy for those that struggle to obtain normal, healthy sleep.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet for one hour each week spanning the 10-week quarter. The first two meetings will include an introduction to, and discussion of, sleep and circadian neurobiology. The students will be asked to listen to a podcast (or other media) interview of the leading investigator of a recently published scientific article (~60 min) and read the article (~30 min). All students will be expected to read these papers and submit a short summary of the reading each week (1-2 paragraphs; ~30 min). In addition, each week 1 or 2 students will prepare (~45 min) and present in class (~20 min) a discussion about the assigned reading. Each student will present only one time. When appropriate we will provide a brief overview at the end of the class that will be relevant to the assignments. In addition, students will have an opportunity to participate in the infographic contest organized by the Sleep Research Society (optional).
GRADING: Pass/no-pass will be assessed based on the following criteria: class participation (40%), quality of presentation (30%) and quality of the weekly summaries (30%). At UC Davis a passing grade is considered a C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
In 2022, Drs. Anaclet and Gompf were recruited to the Neurological Surgery faculty at the University of California, Davis School of Medicine, where they have since established their lab and continued to pursue their experimental interest as well as develop new collaborations with wonderful new colleagues. Their primary scientific interest are: 1) the neurocircuitry that regulates sleep-wake cycles and 2) the role of sleep in physiology and diseases.

Christelle Anaclet, Ph.D. is an Associate Adjunct Professor. She graduated from Claude Bernard University, Lyon, France. She completed her post-doctoral training at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center and Harvard Medical School and then accepted an Assistant Professor position in the Department of Neurobiology at University of Massachusetts Medical School.

Heinrich Gompf, Ph.D. is an Professional Researcher. He graduated from Oregon Health & Science University.  He completed his postdoctoral studies at UPenn, and then at Harvard Medical School (Beth Israel). In more recent years, he has teamed up with Dr Anaclet at UMass Medical School to continue their studies and collective interest in the neurocircuitry that regulates both sleep and circadian rhythms.

TITLE: Social Media Marketing with AI Agents
FOCUS:
We will design an integrated digital marketing strategy with current cutting-edge AI tools.

We start by learning about the social media business model, which is based on predictive analytics that guides recommender AI. To be able to compete in this data-driven world, the common solution, nowadays, consists of using AI (in the form of generative AI - think ChatGPT!) to generate campaigns for social media's AI: we fight (AI) fire with (AI) fire! The good news is that Large Language Models (LLMs), like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, DeepSeek, and Llama, all speak plain English, so no coding required!

We will research, explore, and discuss the different aspects required to build a successful social media strategy and use LLMs to catch the newest trends in a fast-changing environment.

After learning about prompt engineering for social media marketing campaigns, we explore the creation of marketing AI agents, and end with building entire teams of AI-multi-agent systems to help us in our campaign. The goal is to create and fine-tune postings for YouTube, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok, and other social media platforms to implement our social media strategy.

We measure its impact and discuss the lessons learned about what works and what doesn't work for our purposes.
OUTCOMES:
1. Research Process: Students are introduced to and researching appropriate social media marketing campaign methods and techniques.  
  2. Discovery: Students have the opportunity to make relevant discoveries in a fastly changing and very young field, that are previously unknown to both student and instructor and thus require both exploration and evidence-based reasoning.  
  3. Broadly Relevant Work: Students focus on learning to work with generative AI, which is of relevance beyond just the classroom setting, aiming at real-world marketing with these new tools. 
  4. Collaboration: Student collaboration and communication are emphasized and encouraged as critical research skills. 
  5. Iteration: Students learn the iterative nature of research and scholarship; new knowledge creation for social media channels builds on existing knowledge of traditional marketing techniques.
ASSIGNMENTS:
20% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

60% is the weekly hands-on lab assignments.

Session 1: The Social Media Business Model

Session 2: Explore different Large Language Models (LLMs)

Session 3: Personalization of LLMs: How to talk with an LLM?

Session 4: Contextualization of LLMs: Agentic Function calls

Session 5: Contextualization of LLMs: Workflow automation with AI Agents

Session 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration: AI teams for complex tasks

20% is for the final project, which consists of a series of real-world social media posts, elaborated with a multi-agent AI workflow. 

Session 7: Social Media Posts: first exploration

Sessions 8 - 10: Social Media Posts: final project
GRADING:
P/NP

20% grade is attendance & participation. 

60% is weekly hands-on labs. 

20% final project.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor of Communication at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis on Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica in the campaign of Donald Trump a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he provided technical assistance to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert

TITLE: Society and Artificial Intelligence - Engaging with Algorithms
FOCUS: Technology has introduced not only new means of social, economic, and political  interaction, but also pernicious modes of surveillance and algorithmic intervention that raise an ever-evolving set of moral questions about its relationship to race, gender, age, privacy, and interpersonal and human-machine relationships. This class surveys current debates over algorithms in society, what gets called "artificial intelligence", and assisted creativity. Many positions advocate a type of panic without understanding the technologies as part of an evolving technosphere. This interactive class invites students to see that they can reclaim agency over technology. It provokes conversations around identity and identity transformation, surveillance, recognition, classification, creativity, and conversation. It does this in part through examining how artists are engaging in innovative responses to the unequal effects of assisted surveillance, algorithmic justice, deep fake images and deep fake writing.
OUTCOMES: Learning outcomes include (1) a critical understanding of the new fields of algorithm and surveillance studies, (2) an introduction to contemporary art that works with algorithms and surveillance technologies. Students will also develop (3) skills in academic teamwork, (4) critical online research, (5) ability to engage in arguments and deep-fake arguments, (6) creating and evaluating artistic interventions.
ASSIGNMENTS: Classes will be organized into modules, each looking at a different genre of AI/machine learning assisted surveillance, recognition, conversation, image generation, and text generation. Assignments will include critical analysis, critical engagement research, and online research (equivalent to 15 pages of reading per week and one page creative or analytic writing assignment 40%). Scholarly approaches to AI will be given as readings each week, and class time will be split between projects and discussion (30%). For the final project, students will work in teams to develop intimate engagements with an AI technology, and implement them in storyboard, art prototype, or programming design, depending on their skill sets (equivalent to 5 page paper, 30%). Guest speakers will make short presentations and then students will facilitate a discussion. Attendance each week is expected (and missed classes will be made up with reading/writing assignments).
GRADING: The course grade will be based on reading assignments and reflection postings (40%), class participation (30%), and final project (30%). Grading: 100-90 (A), 89-80 (B), 79-70 (C), 69-60 (D), <59 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Joseph Dumit is Chair of Performance Studies, Prof Science & Technology Studies, and Anthropology. Studies brains, bodies, drugs, data, media, virtual reality, capitalism, gaming, and improvisation. Co-created “Data Studies” major track for STS. Written books: Picturing Personhood: Brain Scans and Biomedical Identity, and Drugs for Life: How Pharmaceutical Companies Define Our Health. Co-founder the ModLab for game studies and VR development for science and the arts.  http://dumit.net

TITLE: Strategies for Success in Learning
FOCUS:
This first-year seminar will teach you strategies for success in learning. This course will help you set goals that align with your core values, develop quarterly and weekly plans to achieve your goals, and connect you to resources for achieving your personal and academic goals. It will also focus on better understanding yourself (for example, how and why you procrastinate), and developing strategies to overcome habits that are holding you back. This course emphasizes reflective learning and assignments will follow that format. The overall goal is to help you develop lifelong skills and stay motivated this quarter. 

Topics include: developing a quarterly plan and weekly plans that you actually follow, the importance of self-care, improving your focus (and minimizing distractions), reflecting and refocusing your goals and plans, motivation, preparing for exams, writing strategies, developing and expanding your resilience, improving communication skills, developing a mentoring network, and celebrating your success.

The format will include in-person lectures and group discussions. Each week students will complete a homework assignment (e.g. creating your strategic plan for the quarter) and write a wellness reflection to keep them on track for achieving their goals this quarter. 

Students from all majors are welcome. The instructor recommends this course for students who:

  • Want to improve their time management skills;
  • Struggle prioritizing goals
  • Struggle to stay motivated and focused (or suspect they may struggle to stay motivated)
  • Struggle with self-care and addressing personal goals (all work and no fun)
OUTCOMES:
1. Increase effective communication through class discussions and assignments submitted in written, audio and video forms. One lecture will focus specifically on providing an overview of the components of communication and how it changes over different media, and is associated with communication assignments (e.g. conversations in office hours, seeking internships and mentors)
  2. Develop cognitive skills. Students will learn the higher cognitive skills necessary to continually observe, evaluate, and develop their own personal strategies for effective learning. They will decide why a strategy is or is not working.
  3. Cultivate responsibility and ethics by developing strategies to complete high-quality assignments in a timely matter. Cultivate acceptance, respect and empathy for others and one-self, learning to understand and grow from limitations and errors, and developing a growth mindset.
  4. This class will improve focus, in order to delve into specific subjects more deeply.
  5. Develop leadership skills, including: goal setting, task prioritization, time management, motivation, and practicing self-care. Goals and time management skills will provide the basis of the course and most lessons will be building on the initial lessons taught through students making a quarter plan and weekly plans. For self-care, we rely on the eight dimensions of the wellness model.
  6. A global perspective will be developed by exploring and understanding different student goals, priorities, and non-school responsibilities, and exploring how to adjust learning styles.
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning. The skills emphasized in this course are critical for successful learning and working in any context.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to actively participate during class activities including reflection, group brainstorming, class discussion, and others. Assignments outside of class will include weekly assignments and reflections related to the in-person lecture, and an asynchronous wellness video. Templates for several assignments will be provided.  Assignments will focus on developing the skill of the week (strategic planning, weekly planning, studying for exams, etc.), and weekly reflections will focus on wellness and can be written (∼250 words), audio or video. Students will prepare and share updates and assignment products each week, and coach each other on weekly discussion boards.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. A passing grade will be awarded for a course grade of 70% or higher. Students will be evaluated on in-class individual and group participation and discussion (10%), and weekly reflections (30%), and weekly assignments (60%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valerie Eviner is a professor of Ecosystem Management and Restoration in the Department of Plant Sciences. Her research, teaching, and outreach depend on collaborations among scientists, land managers, and policy makers to advance our fundamental ecological understanding, while addressing key challenges in our environment and society. Her training includes peer coaching focused on the strategies for success highlighted in this seminar. She continuously fine-tunes these strategies as she juggles her multiple interests of research, teaching, outreach to land managers, collaborations with policy makers, leadership in creating positive change, and outdoor adventures.

  3. Cultivate responsibility and ethics by developing strategies to complete high-quality assignments in a timely matter. Cultivate acceptance, respect and empathy for others and one-self, learning to understand and grow from limitations and errors, and developing a growth mindset.
  4. This class will improve focus, in order to delve into specific subjects more deeply.
  5. Develop leadership skills, including: goal setting, task prioritization, time management, motivation, and practicing self-care. Goals and time management skills will provide the basis of the course and most lessons will be building on the initial lessons taught through students making a quarter plan and weekly plans. For self-care, we rely on the eight dimensions of the wellness model.
  6. A global perspective will be developed by exploring and understanding different student goals, priorities, and non-school responsibilities, and exploring how to adjust learning styles.
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning. The skills emphasized in this course are critical for successful learning and working in any context.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to actively participate during class activities including reflection, group brainstorming, class discussion, and others. Assignments outside of class will include weekly assignments and reflections related to the in-person lecture, and an asynchronous wellness video. Templates for several assignments will be provided.  Assignments will focus on developing the skill of the week (strategic planning, weekly planning, studying for exams, etc.), and weekly reflections will focus on wellness and can be written (∼250 words), audio or video. Students will prepare and share updates and assignment products each week, and coach each other on weekly discussion boards.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. A passing grade will be awarded for a course grade of 70% or higher. Students will be evaluated on in-class individual and group participation and discussion (10%), and weekly reflections (30%), and weekly assignments (60%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valerie Eviner is a professor of Ecosystem Management and Restoration in the Department of Plant Sciences. Her research, teaching, and outreach depend on collaborations among scientists, land managers, and policy makers to advance our fundamental ecological understanding, while addressing key challenges in our environment and society. Her training includes peer coaching focused on the strategies for success highlighted in this seminar. She continuously fine-tunes these strategies as she juggles her multiple interests of research, teaching, outreach to land managers, collaborations with policy makers, leadership in creating positive change, and outdoor adventures.

TITLE: Sufism: The Art and Science of Self-Knowledge
FOCUS:
What is Sufism?
Sufism is a 1400-year-old discipline that teaches the individual to explore and discover their true self. Sufism teaches that each individual, underneath their social, cultural, and physical roles, and their definitions and labels of self, has a unique and stable identity. Who are you, really, beneath all the coverings of family, community, and society?

What is Tamarkoz®?
Tamarkoz, unique to MTO Shahmaghsoudi®, is the Sufi art and science of self-knowledge through concentration and meditation. As a meditative practice, Tamarkoz involves deep breathing, mind and body relaxation, slow meditative movements (Movazeneh®), visualization, energy work, and heart meditation. The main difference between Tamarkoz and other meditative traditions is its emphasis on the heart. In Sufism, the heart is considered to be the gateway between our physical and spiritual realms and the source of our true knowledge and wisdom.

Anyone can practice and benefit from Tamarkoz, since Sufism has no cultural, religious, or racial boundaries, and has no physical prerequisites. Tamarkoz grants physical, mental and spiritual benefits, providing a healthier, more balanced body, a powerful way to deal with stress, and an opportunity to explore the mysteries of the heart and discover the treasures within yourself.

This seminar includes both lectures on Sufism and the practice of Tamarkoz. The lectures provide context for the Tamarkoz practice. Tamarkoz students have reported that they are better able to enjoy, appreciate, and benefit from Tamarkoz when they have an understanding of the principles, history, and philosophy of Sufism, and of the role Tamarkoz plays in achieving the aim of self-knowledge. In the practice portion, you will be guided through mind relaxation exercises; deep breathing exercises; Movazeneh (slow movement meditation); deep relaxation; visualization; and heart concentration, the hallmark of Tamarkoz.
OUTCOMES:
-To foster self-reflection and self-exploration
-To learn the principles of Sufism
-To experience the key practices of Sufi meditation and to learn about its health benefits
-To learn how to stay relaxed and balanced during stressful times
-To develop enhanced concentration and focus
-To cultivate creativity, kindness, tolerance, acceptance, and empathy
-To develop inter-cultural perspective, and
-To prepare for lifelong learning
ASSIGNMENTS:
(1) Regular Attendance: presence and participation in class are essential since the course is experiential.

(2) Assigned weekly readings from the required short books, Sufism: A Bridge Between Religions & Manifestation of Thought. One chapter per week from each book, about 16 pages per week. On average, we expect 30-45 minutes of reading per week. Each week, it is further required to write at least a paragraph reflecting on the assigned reading.

(3) Self-reflection Exercise: To submit two self-reflection exercises using any form of creative expression, such as poetry, essay (250 words), or any kind of arts, to reflect on the weekly reading and daily meditation practices. We expect students to spend 2.5 hours completing each self-reflective exercise. (5 hours per quarter).
GRADING:
Your grade breakdown consists of

-20% for class participation,

-20% for the weekly assigned readings & weekly reflection writings,

-20% for the daily practice of meditation and keeping a meditation log,

-40% for self-expression exercise
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Mohammad Sadoghi is an Associate Professor in Computer Science at UC Davis. He leads the ExpoLab research group that aims to pioneer a new exploratory data platform to unify secure transactional and real-time analytics. Additionally, he has a keen interest in understanding the true nature of the human being and in uncovering one’s inner identity. He has been offering courses at UC Davis that focus on stress management and the student’s overall well-being centered around meditation.

Nasim Bahadorani is a wellness expert who specializes in lifestyle health behavior programs. She has her doctorate in public health from Loma Linda University and taught public health courses in the Health Sciences Department at California State University, Northridge. She holds a master’s degree in biomedical science from Midwestern University, College of Health Sciences. Her strong background in biomedical sciences coupled with her experience in public health provides unique expertise in understanding pathways of disease and knowing the strategic models and interventions to prevent them. She is a certified health education specialist and a certified health and wellness coach providing consultation for wellness programs. Her work is focused on lifestyle medicine with integrative approaches to disease prevention and health promotion.

TITLE: Symmetry in Art and Science
FOCUS: This class will explore aspects of symmetry in life, art and science. How do we perceive symmetry? How does it affect the way we see the world? This class will involve discussions that should deepen our understanding and perception of symmetry in our environment. How is symmetry involved in pattern making, in music, in biology? What is the role of repetition? Can symmetry be quantified? The format will involve discussions among the participants with each student expected to bring interesting examples of symmetric situations to the class room. Hopefully the class will involve students from a broad range of majors with interests in the arts and in science. The ability to count, add and subtract will be the only math skills required.
OUTCOMES: The course is designed to facilitate discussion between students in various disciplines with a common interest in symmetry. The class is also designed to increase the participants' awareness and perception of symmetry. The students should emerge form the course with greater appreciation of symmetry and some ability to quantify aspects of symmetry. Students will learn about symmetry operations, patterning, and related ways to quantify symmetry. The instructor will approach the topic from a visual, tactile standpoint, but students will be encouraged to consider other non-visual aspects of symmetry.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class will involve extensive discussion of symmetry and how we perceive symmetry. Students will be expected to bring examples of symmetric objects or situations to class for discussion. They should be prepared to explain the reasons for the examples they have chosen. Each student will be expected to participate in two in-class presentations focused on an aspect of symmetry that they find interesting and to write two 500-100 word essays
GRADING: Participation in classroom discussions will account for 25% of the grades. Another 50% of the grade will be based on the in class presentations.  The two essays will account for 25% of the grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Alan Balch is a Distinguished Professor Emeritus in the Department of Chemistry and was Associate editor of the journal, Inorganic Chemistry. His research involves the study of molecular structure and the interaction of molecular structure with physical and chemical properties of molecules. Much of his current work involves the highly symmetric fullerenes (Bucky Balls), which are closed cages comprised of only carbon atoms with a distinct inside and outside. He is also currently involved in the crystallization of luminescent gold(I) complexes, which may have sensor applications.  He is also interested in the visual arts and is a passionate museum goer. He collects various modern works of art, particularly work in and on paper, a few of which may be relevant to this course.

TITLE: Symmetry in the World Around Us
FOCUS:
Many natural and man-made shapes and patterns have symmetrically repeating elements. A symmetry of a geometric figure is a motion which does not change the figure's appearance. These motions form a group in the mathematical sense, because if one such motion is followed by another, the combined motion also does not change the figure's appearance. We will study 2D point groups of motions which keep a figure's center fixed. For example, the symmetry group of a square has 8 motions: the rotations of 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees about the center (the first of these does not actually move anything), and the reflections in the horizontal line, the vertical line, and the two diagonal lines through the center. We will also look at point groups for 3D figures, for example, of a soccer ball sewn from twelve black pentagons and twenty white hexagons, or a baseball cover sewn from two leather pieces along a curved line.

We will study frieze groups for repeating patterns along a strip, and "wallpaper" groups for patterns repeating along two directions in a plane. Along the way, we will study regular and semi-regular polyhedra (3D shapes bounded by flat regular polygon faces), consider kaleidoscopic patterns generated by two mirrors, and tilings which fill a plane by regular polygons, some of which can be produced by three mirrors or four mirrors.
OUTCOMES: Students will become familiar with symmetries of patterns, and the methods of analyzing and classifying them, giving a new way of thinking about the shapes and patterns they see in the natural and human-constructed world around them. They will get experience in preparing and presenting talks using slides with text and images, and in leading discussions. They will also learn some basic concepts of group theory in mathematics.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each student will choose an aspect of symmetry and give a 15 minute oral presentation in class on it using projected slides, and lead class discussion on it. This will involve library and Internet research. Students are encouraged to obtain example images from books or the Internet for their presentations, and to include questions for class discussion. The preparation for this presentation should take at least 10 hours. There will also be homework on (a) creating the group table for the symmetries of an equilateral triangle, and finding as many as possible (by yourself, without consulting references) of (b) the 7 frieze patterns, (c) the 11 semiregular plane tilings, (d) the 13 Archimedean semiregular polyhedra, and (e) the 17 wallpaper symmetry groups. You should spend about 2 hours on each of these assignments. I do not expect each single student to find all the 7, 11, 13, and 17 examples in (b), c(d), (d), and (e), respectively, but hope that among the whole class, we can find almost all of them.
GRADING:
Grades will be based 20% on participation in class discussion, 20% on homework, and 60% on a class presentation on some aspect of symmetry.

This course is graded Pass/No-Pass (P/NP), and the grade of pass is awarded to students for work that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Nelson Max is an emeritus Distinguished Professor of Computer Science, with research interests in scientific and mathematical visualization, computer animation, and producing realistic computer graphics images. His PhD in mathematics prepared him for the group theory used to analyze symmetry. He has always been interested in geometry and visual patterns, which is why he studied mathematics, and then computer graphics.

TITLE: Talking Tech: Interactions with LLMs and Voice-AI as an object of social science research
FOCUS: In this course, we will explore how humans interact with language-based artificial intelligence systems—such as large language models (LLMs) and voice-enabled AI assistants—as a subject of social scientific inquiry. Drawing from linguistics,  human-computer interaction studies, psychology, sociology, communication studies, we will examine how people attribute human-like qualities to machines, how trust, empathy, and social norms shape these interactions, and how AI systems might influence behavior, identity, and decision-making. Through readings, discussions, and hands-on analysis, students will critically assess the implications of anthropomorphism, moral agency, and social influence in the age of conversational AI.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

 

  • Define and critically evaluate the concept of anthropomorphism in human-AI interaction.
  • Analyze how individual differences and social contexts influence perceptions of AI systems.
  • Apply social science theories to understand trust, empathy, and moral judgment in AI-mediated communication.
  • Assess the societal and ethical implications of LLMs and voice-AI in domains such as education, healthcare, and customer service.
  • Design and conduct basic empirical investigations into human responses to conversational AI.
  • Communicate insights about human-AI interaction.
ASSIGNMENTS:
There will be weekly materials to read or listen to before class. Students will post ideas and questions on the Canvas discussion board before class.

 

There will be a short assignment related to course material. Examples include: Write a paragraph in response to a chapter we read and discussed in class. Write a proposal for a “thought experiment”: using some study or experiment we discussed in class, propose a modification, adaptation, or extension of it to address an issue raised in class. Find an example of a research article that addresses the topic we discussed in class. Report how you would explain the findings to a lay audience in 2 sentences. Create a project demonstrating public science communication (e.g., infographic, short video, short podcast).
GRADING:
Grading will be based on:

Participation in class (40%)

Discussion board posts (20%)

Assignment (20%)

Project (20%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Georgia Zellou is a linguist specializing in phonetics and laboratory phonology. She is a faculty member in the Department of Linguistics at UC Davis, where she also directs the UC Davis Phonetics Lab. Her research investigates how fine-grained phonetic detail is represented and processed in the cognitive system, with a focus on linguistic variation during human interaction with voice-based AI systems.

TITLE: Tamarkoz Meditation
FOCUS: We are witnessing a nation faced with stress epidemic, where 1 in 3 college freshmen show signs of mental health disorders. Overcoming stress is crucial in paving the way to promote a healthier lifestyle filled with joy & creativity. This experiential seminar presents a comprehensive stress management technique that revolves around a unique heart-centered meditation method, namely, Tamarkoz®. The Tamarkoz® method is the art of self-knowledge through concentration and meditation. Learning meditation is like learning how to swim. You cannot do it by reading a book. You have to get in the water and practice. True learning requires systematic training. This course will provide a "taste", it teaches how to attain the relaxation necessary for reaching the real state of concentration and meditation.
OUTCOMES: The beneficial effects of meditation are well documented in a massive amount of research literature. Participants report a number of positive results, with particular emphasis on stress reduction. People who meditate properly are calmer, less tense, less reactive to stress, healthier, feel better, have greater self-esteem, and above all are more creative and joyful than those who do not. Meditators also report a sense of increased well-being and enjoyment of life. Research has clearly demonstrated that meditation is helpful in a number of stress-related conditions such as high blood pressure, headaches, back problems, cardiac rehabilitation and pain reduction. Recent research indicates it is often more effective than psychotherapy in the treatment of some problems. The exercises included in the course can be of immediate practical value, and students usually enjoy doing them. To provide students with the following: (1) A foundation and working knowledge of basic theories of meditation; (2) The opportunity to participate as an ongoing member of a meditation group; (3) Working knowledge of meditative techniques; (4)An understanding of meditative principles; (5) The ability to practice deep breathing; (6) The ability to perform stretching and energy balancing exercises; (7) Working knowledge of stress reduction techniques; (8) Knowledge of the various types of meditative approaches and relevant therapeutic factors; (9) A safe environment, in which students feel free to examine and change behavioral habits; and (10) The capacity to sit still and silent for 30 minutes
ASSIGNMENTS: The basic outline of the course includes lecture, discussion, observation, analysis, physical exercises, meditative exercises, small group activities, and demonstrations will be included. Requirements are as follows. (1) Regular Attendance: presence and participation in class are essential since the course is experiential. (2) Daily Practice at home is required. This is one class in which everyone will know whether or not you have done your homework, for it will be physically obvious. You will be expected to demonstrate proficiency in the exercises, including the movements and the capacity to sit with your eyes closed without moving, being very still. Start with 5 minutes of meditation the first week, then increase at least 3 minutes each week thereafter, until you are meditating for 30 minutes each day. (3) Meditation Journal: Keeping a daily journal of your meditation and its results will help you recognize the effectiveness of the procedures, and aid the instructor in helping you deal with any problems.
GRADING: Students are expected to write a journal for at least 5 minutes daily (35 minutes per week) and daily practices of 10-20 minutes daily (1-2 hours per week) consisting of deep breathing, mind relaxation, Movazeneh® exercises (the art of balancing and harmonizing the body), visualization, progressive relaxation, and meditation. Your grade breakdown would be 25% for class participation, 50% for daily practices, and 25% of keeping a meditation journal.
