TITLE: The Arboretum: A Living Laboratory
FOCUS:
The UC Davis Arboretum and Public Garden is a 5,300 acre urban greenspace incorporating stream, forest, grassland and urban ecosystems, demonstration gardens, scientific collections, over 1.5 miles of waterway, and over 3 miles of walking and biking trails. Managing the waterway is a significant task, employing many full-time staff and interns each year. A major component of waterway management is tracking and improving water quality. The goals of this CURE are to 1) educate students on the factors that influence water quality, including how water quality is measured and managed, and 2) provide Arboretum waterway managers with robust water quality data to inform decision-making. 

In this CURE, students will learn about the structure and operation of the Arboretum waterway, study different metrics of water quality, perform field and lab analyses on Arboretum water, and present their results as a report on waterway health to Arboretum waterway managers. Class sessions will incorporate a combination of activities including short lectures, walking tours, field sampling, lab measurements, data analysis and visualization, and written and oral reports. Students will be trained on water quality sampling methods such as pipetting and plating for bacterial counts and using meters to measure conductivity, dissolved oxygen, pH, and temperature. Data generated by students in the class will be provided to Arboretum waterway managers to assist management efforts and be expanded upon in future quarters of this CURE.
OUTCOMES:
The primary research outcome is to provide spatially and temporally robust water quality data to Arboretum waterway managers. Learning goals are to:

  • Describe the history of the Arboretum waterway, its hydrology, and the biotic and abiotic factors that drive water quality
  • Operate water quality meters
  • Plate water for bacterial assays (including performing dilutions, pipetting, and counting bacterial colonies)
  • Present data visually through graphs and calculate summary statistics
  • Communicate research progress and findings orally and in writing 


We will offer this CURE through the entire AY23/24 (to different cohorts of students). FQ23 will test multiple protocols and evaluate metrics of water quality. For example, different media grow different types of microorganisms, and thus provide a different view of the microbial community. There are also a variety of abiotic metrics of water quality, such as conductivity, pH, dissolved oxygen, and temperature. Students will review the advantages and limitations of different methods in terms of time and resource investment in performing the analyses and the aspect of water quality they represent. The final course product will be a report on the different analyses performed including a recommendation on the standard protocol to be used in the course moving forward.
ASSIGNMENTS: Assignments include field activities, lab journals, collecting/analyzing data, demonstrating proficiency in sampling techniques, and presenting written and oral results. Field activities include observational, field, and laboratory methods, and graphing of data and interpretation of results. Students will record data in a class archive for use by future iterations of the course. While the data to be collected are set; students have flexibility in the questions they ask for their data analysis projects. The class dataset includes 7 metrics sampled at 9 locations in the Arboretum, allowing students to consider a variety of questions, including spatial trends, correlation between metrics, and comparison of course data to previous sampling in the Arboretum. Students may also propose a hypothesis for future iterations of the course to test (e.g. seasonal trends), including predictions of data if the hypothesis is supported, and factors that could influence the predicted trends. Through these activities, students will be expected to demonstrate proficiency in techniques such as field sampling, pipetting, using a light microscope, performing dilutions and bacterial counts. The final product of the course is an oral presentation on a portion of the class data (to be shared with the Arboretum), and a final reflection paper.

INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Laci Gerhart is an Associate Professor of Teaching (LSOE) in Evolution and Ecology. Her previous pedagogical research has included analyzing student engagement with video-based homework assignments, and the role of app-based participatory science projects in field courses. This CURE project was born out of her experiential field course EVE 16: Wild Davis in which students tested Arboretum water for fecal coliform bacteria. Her goal is to not only connect students to original research experiences, but also to provide Arboretum waterway managers with valuable data to inform decision-making. 

 

Dr. Brett Poulin is an Assistant Professor in Environmental Toxicology.His research focuses on the interface between water quality and ecosystem health, with an emphasis on metal contaminants that impact wildlife and humans. His active research projects span from the Florida Everglades to Arctic Alaska. His goal is to use the Arboretum as a living laboratory to connect new undergraduate students with research on their campus that will shape management of a local urban waterway.

TITLE: The Dynamic World of Fluorescent Biosensors: How live-cell movies and big data are changing biochemistry
FOCUS: This course will explore the initial discovery of the green fluorescent protein (GFP) from the jellyfish Aequorea victoria and the many innovations in cell biology that this fascinating protein has made possible. It will follow this invaluable tool through its many applications in biology and biotechnology research, with particular focus on the development of GFP-based biosensors that monitor intracellular signaling and metabolic pathways in living cells. The course will incorporate readings from the primary literature that describe the engineering of biosensors and their uses in living physiological systems such as mice or zebrafish to reveal the spatiotemporal organization of biochemical activities. We will also cover the computational developments in image analysis that have made it possible to collect and analyze very large biosensor datasets from thousands of cells. The application of these tools to make advancements in cancer research and immune cell function will be a major point of discussion.
OUTCOMES:
Educational outcomes:

1) Students will develop focus and depth in a basic concept of biochemistry, which is the  relationship between the structure and function of proteins, using examples from the well-developed field of fluorescent protein and biosensor engineering. 

2) Students will develop effective communication skills by creating presentations for the class to share what they have learned from exploring their independent questions and interests within highly accessible databases.  

3) Students will prepare for lifelong learning by learning about a thread of biological research that has spanned nearly 40 years, underscoring the persistent and process of human intellectual advancement, which requires overcoming various obstacles.
ASSIGNMENTS: This course will be have two assignments, which will each be a short (10-15 minute) presentation to the class. In the first, students will choose a fluorescent protein from the Fluorescent Protein database (https://www.fpbase.org/) or a biosensor from the Fluorescent Biosensor Database (https://biosensordb.ucsd.edu/) and provide a short description of how it was developed and subsequently used in cell biology. In the second, students will create a proposal for a variation on an experiment they have learned about in class, describing how they can design a similar biosensor-based approach to collect data on a biological question of interest to them. Depending on enrollment, these presentations may be individual or group-based.
GRADING:
Course grading will be as follows:

Presentation 1: 40%

Presentation 2: 40%

Class interaction and participation: 20%
INSTRUCTORBIO: John Albeck, PhD, is a professor of Molecular and Cellular Biology at the University of California, Davis. His work is focused on identifying systems-level regulatory properties that govern the behavior of cells in both normal tissue and in cancers. Since 2013, his research group at UC Davis has brought together biologists and engineers to study how multiple pathways are integrated to control cellular metabolism, proliferation, and death.  Their approach is centered on time-lapse image analysis and computational modeling of biochemical signaling networks in individual cells. Their most recent work uses biosensors to investigate metabolic regulation at the single-cell level.

TITLE: The Early Years: How Children Learn to Read and Write
COLLEGETITLE: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences
FOCUS: Academic success, particularly in the areas of reading and writing, is often predicated upon the early literacy foundation that children develop in their early years. Research has consistently shown that the language and literacy skills children acquire in the preschool years have an effect on their reading and writing success in kindergarten and elementary school. This seminar will explore different approaches for supporting young children’s emerging language and literacy skills and provide an introduction to the design, implementation and evaluation of quality early learning environments that support the development of these skills. Sample topics include: language development and communication, supporting English Language Learners, routines that support language development, designing the early literacy environment, storybook reading with young children, and early writing development. Emphasis will be placed on observations of young children engaged in hands-on learning in real-world settings, including the National Association for the Education of Young Children’s accredited preschool programs at the Early Childhood Lab School, Center for Child and Family Studies (CCFS).This seminar class is designed to be experiential, and learning will occur through in-class activities and discussions.  Class attendance is mandatory.
OUTCOMES:
Goals:

• Students will develop an understanding of early language and literacy development and consider best practices for supporting children’s emerging skills.

• Students will consider an interdisciplinary approach to early learning.

• Students will develop important oral and written communication skills necessary for the successful participation in future academic work.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will complete weekly readings intended to promote group discussion, critical thinking, and real-world connections. Students will submit five written assignments (1 -2 pages each) designed to develop observation skills, practice stylized writing, and make meaningful connections between research and applied program practice. In small-groups, students will plan and prepare a hands-on learning experience to be donated to an early childhood program in our community that would benefit from such recourses. An Applied Project Plan and in-class presentation (20 minutes) will be completed by students summarizing course content and presenting their applied project.
GRADING:
Course grade will be based on quality of written work (35%), a final presentation (30%), and quality of group participation (35%). The grade of ‘pass’ is awarded for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grad of C- or better.

For the final group project, each student will turn in a written Applied Project Plan for individual evaluation. Within small groups, students will assign roles and responsibilities for creating the in-class presentation. Each student will be expected to participate in the in-class presentation.  Students will assign a participation grade to group members that the instructor will take into account.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Jennifer Gonzalez has been a Child Development Demonstration Lecturer in the Department of Human Ecology since 2008. As part of her work for the university, she designs, implements, and evaluates an experimental early childhood program based on current research in child development and early education. Her applied research interests include early learning environments, language and literacy, and social-emotional development. In addition to her on-campus work, she is an instructor with Family Child Care At It's Best (WestEd) and consults as an early learning expert for the School Readiness Department at the Sacramento County Office of Education.
SCHOOL: College of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences

TITLE: The Fabulous Singer Featherweight (Vintage Sewing Machine):  History, Mechanics, and Use
FOCUS: The Singer Featherweight comprises a series of lockstitch domestic sewing machines that were produced from 1933 to 1968.  The Singer Manufacturing Company started producing these sewing machines pre-war and they have continued popularity among sewers. Students who are interested in reduce, reuse, and recycle and the history of a vintage sewing machine series would benefit from this Freshman Seminar. It would also potentially help them add an activity to balance their overall life. Students who have some sewing experience or working with machines would benefit most from this seminar. This seminar will explore the history of the Singer Featherweight series, changes in the components of the machines over time, and will get time to use a machine. The goal of this course is to provide students with an overview of the history of a piece of machinery that was used in the everyday household historically and can still be used decades later to produce fabric items that can benefit society.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the history and production of Singer Featherweights. Goals of this seminar are to (1) cultivate ethics, responsibility, and respect, for reuse, reduce and recycle (2) learn about the mechanics of the machines (3) have the experience of using the machine to inspire them to explore sewing as a means to enhance life balance. This seminar is an opportunity to gain a more in-depth understanding of a vintage sewing machine that continues to hold its popularity.
ASSIGNMENTS: For the five sessions (2 hours total per session with a 10-minute break), students will be assigned to read 1-2 chapters from the book "Featherweight 221: The Perfect Portable." The chapter topics will then be discussed during the seminar sessions and students will be able to look at various machines so they can appreciate the design and functional changes over the years. During the quarter, students will be expected to design and produce a fabric postcard that they can work on during class sessions while they are experimenting with the different machines. The expectation is that they will have a finished product at the end of the seminar. This seminar will meet for a total of five sessions. Each session will be 2 hours in duration (10 am -11:50 AM) with a 10-minute break. Dates for the class will be Tuesdays, January 6th, 13th, 27th, February 3rd and 10th, 2026. One session in January might have to be moved thus extending the course to February 17th, 2026.
GRADING: This is a pass/no pass course. A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. The course grading for this seminar will be based on creating a fabric postcard (50%) according to the pattern instructions and verbal and quality of participation in class (50%). Class participation will entail students coming to class with questions and comments relative to the assigned reading for the seminar session, active participation in discussion sessions, and completing in-class activities (drawing/picking out a design, selecting/cutting fabric, sewing fabric).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Tell is a member of the faculty in the School of Veterinary Medicine. Her research interests include the treatment of avian disease, avoidance of drug residues in poultry products consumed by humans, and avian health relative to the conservation of bird populations. Dr. Tell obtained her Bachelor of Science from UC Davis, did her residency at the National Zoo, then returned to UC Davis as a faculty member. She is boarded with the American College of Zoological Medicine and the American Board of Veterinary Practitioners (Avian Practice). Dr. Tell has been sewing since 1990 and has taught an honors art science fusion course on the UC Davis campus. She is interested in helping students be aware of the reduction of consumable products and to use fabric art as an outlet for creativity and overall mental health.

TITLE: The Fig
FOCUS: There are a lot of people who are interested in growing their own vegetables and fruits in their backyards. Their main motivation is often the reduction of cost and/or the production of quality organic food. In recent years, there is a significant increase in the interest of growing fig trees. This is the case due to the following facts: (1) figs are rarely available in markets (due to low shelf life), (2) fig trees are easy to propagate from cuttings, (3) figs have a massive number of varieties that produce fruits that are easily distinguishable among other varieties by taste, color, and size, and (4) figs can be easily adapted to grow in containers and hence can be grown in colder climates where they are often moved indoors while they are dormant in winter. Nowadays, fig trees and cuttings are significantly traded among online stores such as eBay and amazon. The students in this course will explore and learn various topics about figs. Every student in the class will research a selected topic and produce a paper and a presentation about that topic.
OUTCOMES: The students in this course will develop focus and depth in the field of figs. The students will learn several topics about figs including: The history of the fig, fig production around the world, fig varieties, fig tree propagation methods, characteristics of figs, diseases, nutritional content, trading fig trees/cuttings, and others. The students will also participate in an experiment to propagate fig cuttings and produce new fig trees. Finally, the assignments in this course are established to improve the effective communication skills of students.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be required to prepare a short paper (and a related presentation) on a topic related to the theme of the course which is going to be chosen in consultation with the instructor. The students are expected to do the following tasks throughout the quarter: (1) select the research topic, (2) do the proposed research, (3) prepare a presentation, and (4) write a final paper (5 to 7 pages). The overall workload in this course is expected to be 1 to 2 hours of out-of-class work per week.
GRADING: The course score will be based on the quality of the student work and computed as follows: a 10-minute oral presentation to the class (50%) and a final written research report (50%). The final course grade will be reported as pass/no pass, the grade of pass is awarded to students that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Hussain Al-Asaad is a professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering. His research interests include design verification, testing, and fault tolerant computing. He has published over 55 papers in archival journals and refereed conference/workshop proceedings. He teaches undergraduate and graduate courses on digital systems design, testing, and verification. Al-Asaad is a recipient of the National Science Foundation CAREER Award and the UC-Davis academic senate distinguished undergraduate teaching award. Al-Asaad has significant experience of growing various fruit trees, and he is currently growing and evaluating around 700 varieties of figs mostly grown in containers.

TITLE: The Hidden Technology: Process Control
FOCUS:
Have you ever wondered how a complex system like a self-driving car stays in the correct lane, maintains the speed limit, or avoids rear-ending other vehicles? How the thermostat in your home maintains a “comfortable” temperature, or how a simple toilet re-fills to the same level after each flush? In reality, each of these seemingly different processes utilizes the all too common, but seldom seen, concept of feedback control.

In this interactive class, we will explore topics such as these, while developing problem-solving and trouble-shooting skills through an exploration of this critical engineering discipline. We will learn how feedback control is an intuitive and iterative process that is used in everyday life to solve problems. For example, one may set a goal for themselves (i.e. get a good grade in a class), but one’s actions may lead to an outcome that diverges from the desired goal (i.e. one does poorly on an exam). This “feedback” motivates new actions that eventually bring about the original goal (i.e. one studies more and does better on future exams). 

Each lecture will introduce process control concepts within the field of engineering and the natural world, with interactive exercises where students will work as teams to explore a variety of control problems.
OUTCOMES:
Students will learn about the field of feedback control, and how it is an intuitive and iterative process spanning both natural and engineered systems. This seminar aims to: (1) Help students gaining a better understanding in applying knowledge, common sense and intuition to solve problems using the principles of feedback control, (2) Give students some exposure to engineering, problem solving, and troubleshooting in a non-math heavy environment, (3) Create a supportive environment to engage with others through analyzing and exploring interesting problems, and (4) Help students develop effective written and oral communication skills.

The attainment of the educational outcomes of the seminar will be measured using several assessment instruments including homework assignments, class discussions and oral presentations.
ASSIGNMENTS: Lecture time will include an in-class small group work exercise where the basic ideas in the lecture will be applied. This will be followed by an interactive feedback session, with the homework for the week being: (1) to individually update and improve the in-class working example with the feedback and any other insights gathered while working on the problem, and (2) to write up the problem in a intuitive way. The homework will be submitted as a 2-page paper written in a "technical memo" style that summarizes the student's key findings and insights learned. There will be weekly content requirements, including 15-20 pages of reading (from scholarly articles) and 10-20 minutes of online videos, to help the students prepare for each class and engage more effectively in the in-class group problem-solving exercises. Adequate preparation for class ranges from coming to class with some ideas and questions about the topic discussed in lecture, to watching informative videos prior to class. Near the end of the term, students will be asked to make a 10-minute power-point presentation that demonstrates an application of the class content and ideas to a process control system of interest to the student.
GRADING: Grading will be based on weekly homework (50%), in-class participation and preparation (25%), and an in-class 10-minute power-point presentation to be given near the end of the term (25%). The homework will be graded based on the submitted written reports, with an emphasis on feedback to the students about ways to improve their communication and analysis of the topic. The oral presentation will be graded based on both the content and presentation quality. Grading scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <59 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Nael El-Farra is a Professor in the Department of Chemical Engineering at UC Davis, and has research interests in the areas of process and energy systems engineering, with emphasis on process control, monitoring and optimization. He has published a book and over 200 peer-reviewed research articles in these areas. He regularly teaches upper division courses in the chemical engineering curriculum and has received several teaching awards for his enthusiasm and teaching style that makes these complex topics approachable to students.

TITLE: The History of the Humble Chicken and the Role it Played and Plays in Human History
FOCUS: Chickens have played a vital role in human history helping humans’ transition from hunter gatherers to playing a key role in agriculture as a source of protein and manure for crops. Additional contributions include their uses in religious ceremonies and “entertainment” in cock fighting events. Chickens have also contributed to pandemics and trade wars. In short, we can learn a lot about human history by studying the history of the humble chicken. This course will discuss some of these events and even pontificate about the future of the chicken and how humans with “creator” like techniques and tools may change the chicken over the next century and beyond.
OUTCOMES: The two educational outcomes this course will integrate will be the development of a global perspective and developing depth in multiple disciplines. Specifically the history of the chicken crosses multiple disciplines over the last 100,000 years including agriculture, anthropology, geography and biology among others. Appreciating how all these topics connect from a multi-disciplinary and global perspective will allow students to better understand the value of a broad education.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each student will give one oral PowerPoint based presentation of 10-15 minutes based on the domestication of poultry (e.g. chickens, turkeys, pheasants) and how it positively and/or negatively impacted humans. The presentation will include potential advantages and potential "unintended consequences" of that domestication Research methods related to their oral presentation will be discussed in class in order to train students how to search the literature and how to present data appropriately. Out of class workload: weekly reading of a scientific article or similar text will average approximately 1 hour and preparation for a final PowerPoint proposal will be approximately 5-10 hours total over the course of the quarter.
GRADING: Class will be pass/no pass with a grade of 70% or above constituting a passing grade. Each student will be graded on participation (30%) associated with weekly discussions  and one oral presentation (70%) described in the assignments section. Presentations will include at least 2 peer reviewed references from scientific journals.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Maurice Pitesky is a faculty member at University of California Cooperative Extension (UCCE) with an appointment in poultry health and food safety epidemiology. Dr. Pitesky earned his BS in biology from UCLA, and his DVM and MPVM from UC Davis. Pitesky is also boarded in Preventative Veterinary Medicine (DACVPM). My MPVM research was focused on Greenhouse Gases and Food Production. Pitesky’s research interests are focused in three major areas: 1) Using “traditional” epidemiological techniques and GIS and spatial statistics to understand how avian diseases move in time and space. 2) Using traditional and “Next Generational” epidemiological and sequencing technologies to gain insights into Salmonella with respect to food safety and virulence 3) Gaining a better understanding of small scale poultry production with respect to environmental sustainability, poultry heath, and food safety.

TITLE: The Psychological Causes and Effects of Binge Watching
FOCUS: This course will focus on research that explores the psychological causes and effects of binge-watching and how it impacts emotional well-being, socializing patterns, and life satisfaction outcomes. Through lecture, discussions about research, and group activities, students will gain a deeper understanding of how pervasive binge-watching is in society and assess their own experiences with it and if it has changed their behaviors in any way. There is a plethora of current research that is dedicated to this phenomenon and its ability to impact the emotional well-being of people, including increasing isolation, escapism, and dissociation. It can also increase one's feelings of connection with others who actively binge-watch the same shows. The course objective will be for students to gain awareness of the frequency of binge-watching and the potential negative or positive outcomes of engaging in it.
OUTCOMES:
• Develop high cognitive skills pertaining to the current literature focused on binge-watching and how it effects emotional well-being and or social patterns. Students should be able to write a reflective essay on their experience with binge-watching. 
  • Develop effective communication skills and present to the class a specific example of a TV or movie series they have watched and how they felt about the experience. 
  • Develop the ability to effectively communicate the causes and effects of binge-watching on socializing patterns and feelings.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Class participation - actively participate in group discussions and activities during class. Students' ability to verbally articulate themselves in group settings that focus on binge-watching research, will showcase their capacity to effectively communicate complex concepts and ideas. 

2. Read class articles -  1 article will be discussed every other week in class. Comprehending articles and their findings will assist in building the skill of functioning at a high level of cognition. 

3. List of shows that student has binge-watched - students will be asked to submit a list of shows they have binge-watched throughout their lives that they can remember. 

4. Reflective Essay -  This reflective essay will exhibit students' high-level cognitive skills and communication competency through expressing how binge-watching effects their behavior, perceived well-being, and socializing patterns if they engage in this phenomenon. This will also highlight if students' experience with binge-watching patterns and behaviors are in congruence with current research. Students will asked to answer 4 prompts when writing this essay. 

5. Non-Binge-watching assignment - Students will be asked to start a show that only comes on once a week so they cannot binge-watch the show. Students will submit the name of the show and discuss the experience.
GRADING:
1. Class participation - 20 points. Students must actively engage in discussion about the literature and relevant topics.

2. Reading articles - 20 points. Verbally engage with classmates regarding literature. 

3. List of shows that they have binge-watched over their lifetime - 20 points

4. Write a reflective essay pertaining to your experience with binge-watching and if any of the adverse or positive outcome pertain to you. - 20 points 

5. Non-binge watching assignment and discussion - 20 points. Students will choose a series or movie to watch that does not have the ability to binge-watch and compare/contract the experience.
INSTRUCTORBIO: I'm Dr. Yasmine V. Jefferson and I have been unofficially studying human behavior since I was a kid. I didn't officially start studying human behavior until I was 20 years old. Psychology changed my life and helped me understand so much about myself and others. In my free time I watch horror movies, listen to EDM, and travel as much as possible. However, California is my favorite place to be.
COURSECOLLABORATORDEPARTMENT: College of Biological Sciences Dean's Office

TITLE: Time Management: Productivity, Mindfulness, and Stickers!
FOCUS:
Time management is more than just a way to stay on top of our responsibilities and get things
done more efficiently (and indeed part of the “hidden curriculum” of higher education).
Developing the ability to manage our time thoughtfully and intentionally can also help unlock
heightened self awareness, improved mental health, and a more grounded sense of purpose.


In this course, we will read and discuss material unpacking the concept of time management and
offering various time management strategies. We will pair these readings with key assignments
that help us to understand what our strengths and weaknesses are in the realm of time
management, as well as how time management intersects with our mental health. Our weekly
work use the “Bullet Journal” approach to staying organized (including giving ourselves
stickers—that’s right, stickers—for our accomplishments), and within this approach we will test
out various concrete time management strategies and share our experiences and insights with one another through weekly discussions.
OUTCOMES:
This course builds toward multiple educational outcomes. By the end of the course, students
should have developed the following skills/insights:
• understanding of students’ personal obstacles to productivity and mindfulness
• ability to identify, map out, and track goals and priorities
• awareness of one’s own peak productivity time
• ability to apply the basic principles of time management (and productivity) to daily life
• understanding of how time management influences mental health and vice versa
• awareness of on-campus resources
• ability to develop and maintain a personal time management system
ASSIGNMENTS:
The course will involve an average of 30 pages of reading per week (mostly, though not
exclusively, from Oliver Burkeman’s book Four Thousand Weeks: Time Management for
Mortals), plus three assignments of 1-2 pages each that link together (backwards calendar
assignment, time diary assignment, and mental health diary assignment), as well as weekly
"Bullet Journal" entries where students apply the concepts from class to their weekly
productivity systems. Students are also required to be active participants in the weekly seminars.
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would
receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. Grading will be calculated as follows:
Participation: 50%
Backwards calendar assignment: 10%
Time diary assignment: 10%
Mental health assignment: 10%
Weekly Bullet Journal entries: 20
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Amber Boydstun is a Professor of Political Science, an Affiliated Professor of Communication,
and a Chancellor’s Fellow at the University of California, Davis. She uses lab experiments,
large-scale media studies, and manual and computational text analysis to study how issues make
the news, how issues are framed in the news, and how media attention and framing influences
public opinion. She has authored two books and many journal articles, as well as articles for the
popular press, such as The Washington Post and the Columbia Journalism Review. She is also a
co-founder of Women Also Know Stuff, an organization that works to amplify the scholarship of
women political scientists.


As a scholar of political communication, she is deeply interested in the decisions that humans
make with regard to, for example, which policy issues to care about, which politicians to trust,
and which media to consume. But as a human being herself, she is even more interested in the
ways that we all navigate the complexities of life, including decisions both big and small. She’s
also a big fan of animals in general, and giraffes in particular.

TITLE: Violence and the Vote: Measuring and Mapping Election Violence Around the World
FOCUS:
Why do some elections turn violent while others remain peaceful? Who are major perpetrators of election violence? Are the drivers of violence the same in different parts of the world? In this first-year seminar, students will explore how political scientists define, detect, and measure election violence from intimidation and attacks on candidates to clashes between rival supporters. We will discuss key concepts and debates about how violence shapes democracy, legitimacy, and citizen participation across different regions of the world.

The course combines academic readings and discussions with hands-on data analysis. Students will learn how researchers build and code event-level datasets by working directly with the ACLED (Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project) database. Students will identify and categorize incidents of election violence and classify perpetrator types such as state forces, party militias, or unidentified armed groups, using real data from past elections in Africa, Asia, Latin America, and the US.

By the end of the quarter, students will have produced their own validated election violence dataset and short research brief on several different elections. Along the way, they will gain practical experience with social-science data, improve their critical reasoning about evidence, and see how concepts of democracy and conflict can be studied empirically.

This seminar is ideal for students interested in politics, international affairs, data analysis, or social-scientific research.
OUTCOMES:
This seminar advances UC Davis’s Global Learning Outcomes in Global Awareness and Global Diversity by guiding students to examine how and why election violence happens in different parts of the world. Students will explore how political competition, community identities, and regime type shape patterns of violence.

Through discussion and hands-on data analysis using the Armed Conflict Location & Event Data (ACLED) project, students will learn to code and interpret subnational event data, identifying variations in both violence intensity and perpetrator type from state security forces to party militias and nonstate armed groups.

Students will develop skills in data organization, spatial reasoning, and comparative analysis, while critically evaluating how data collection methods and language affect our understanding of violence across different societies. By the end of the course, students will be able to interpret subnational conflict patterns and reflect on how global processes such as democratization, inequality, and state capacity manifest unevenly across local contexts.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will begin the quarter by exploring how scholars define and measure election violence through short readings (about 20–25 pages per week) and focused writing or presentation assignments. Early exercises (1–2 pages each) will ask students to explain how key concepts are operationalized in political science research.

In the second half of the course, students will shift from reading about data to creating and interpreting it. Working in small research teams, they will use the ACLED (Armed Conflict Location & Event Data) database to code and classify real election violence events from selected countries. Students will identify who commits violence, where it occurs, and how intensity varies locally, using a coding guide and collaborative discussion.

Each student will complete several validated election violence datasets for specific elections, as well as a final 5-page research brief summarizing what they learned about how election violence takes different forms in different contexts and a 10-minute presentation of their findings to the class. 

By combining reading, writing, and hands-on data analysis, students will gain experience with real social science research and see how political violence is studied across diverse global contexts.
GRADING:
In-class participation, including presentations and discussion: 25%

Validated datasets for assigned elections: 50%

Final written reflection: 25%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
I am an Associate Professor of Political Science at UC Davis, where I study how ordinary people experience and respond to political violence. Much of my research focuses on election violence, including why it happens, how it affects voters, and how communities and policymakers can prevent it. I began this work in Zimbabwe and collaborate with partners including opposition parties, civil society organizations, and development agencies to better understand how party politics, local power, and global institutions shape violent elections and how to promote free, fair, and peaceful elections.

Methodologically, I use tools like WhatsApp-based surveys and field experiments to reach hard-to-access populations, and I help design ethical research practices for studying contentious politics.

In this seminar, I look forward to introducing students to the excitement of hands-on data analysis and showing how careful research can illuminate pressing global challenges for democracy and peace.

TITLE: Writing and Performing Stand-Up Comedy
FOCUS: In this course, we will practice writing and performing stand-up comedy. As we develop material, we will study the work of professional comics, learn the mechanics of writing and delivery, workshop our jokes, and prepare for an end of the quarter performance, where students will do a 3-5 minute set. Students will be required to see one night of live comedy during the quarter (the stand-up comedy club has several free shows each term). Within a week of the performance, they will turn in a 250-word review/analysis. Students will watch a comedy special each week and write a brief response.
OUTCOMES: In an attempt to better write and perform, we will study the basic structures of jokes and storytelling, onstage persona, timing, and generating ideas.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be reading Judy Carter's The Comedy Bible. They will be asked to watch a few performances (one live) outside of class and to write up short reviews. The majority of the work will come from the weekly writing and workshopping of jokes. At the end of the quarter, we will find a time when we can come together to do a live performance of students' 3-5 minute sets.
GRADING: Participation (this includes giving frequent, constructive criticism to your peers as we workshop sets & classroom discussion): 25%. Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. Do not be late. Writing (this includes your joke writing and revision & your review/analyses of comedy): 50%. Group Performance: 25%.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Karma Waltonen, a lecturer in the Writing Center, teaches upper division writing courses and freshman seminars on The Simpsons, Margaret Atwood, and Doctor Who. She leads a summer abroad course in Oxford on British cinema and mentors the stand-up comedy club at UCD. In 2015, she won the AF Excellence in Teaching Award.

TITLE: Addressing Food Security in the Developing World: How to make more chickens???
FOCUS: The course will focus on a question I was asked by an NGO based in Uganda: "Can you help us get more chickens for egg and meat production in Uganda?" This question which was posed in the form of a "yes/no" question is of course very complicated and forced me to think about several different aspects of food security, agricultural economics, available infrastructure, culture and human behavior among other issues. Therefore the course will be a series of interactive lectures that goes over the basics of international work, how to get data, utilize decision sciences and how to implement a plan with the goal being that at the end of the quarter the students will each have a short presentation of what they would focus on.
OUTCOMES: The primary goal is for the students to recognize the complexity of the question (how do we get more poultry in Uganda) and to appreciate that the solutions are complex and require a multi-disciplinary approach. Ideas covered will include: Poultry Production, Crop Production, Vaccine Access , Working in the developing world, ag economics, cultural considerations, political considerations and structure.
ASSIGNMENTS: The students will have 1 assignment. They will have to present a short (~12-15 minutes with 3 minutes for questions) powerpoint based presentation focusing on a specific aspect of food security development. Preparation for the assignment will require around 10 hours of basic research into the literature and slide preparation.. Among other issues they will have to demonstrate how they will collaborate (in country and beyond) and how their approach dovetails with the current norms and infrastructure in Uganda's food system. In addition a weekly journal article will be assigned which will require approximately 20 pages of reading per week.
GRADING: Class will be pass/no pass with a grade of 70% or above constituting a passing grade. Each student will be graded on participation (30%) and one oral presentation (70%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Maurice Pitesky is a faculty member at University of California Cooperative Extension (UCCE) with an appointment in poultry health and food safety epidemiology. Dr. Pitesky earned his BS in biology from UCLA, and his DVM and MPVM from UC Davis. Pitesky is also boarded in Preventative Veterinary Medicine (DACVPM). My MPVM research was focused on Greenhouse Gases and Food Production. Pitesky’s research interests are focused in three major areas: 1) Using “traditional” epidemiological techniques and GIS and spatial statistics to understand how avian diseases move in time and space. 2) Using traditional and “Next Generational” epidemiological and sequencing technologies to gain insights into Salmonella with respect to food safety and virulence 3) Gaining a better understanding of small scale poultry production with respect to environmental sustainability, poultry heath, and food safety.

TITLE: Advanced Techniques in Biomedical Science Research
FOCUS:
"Advanced Techniques in Biomedical Science Research" introduces first-year students to how modern biomedical discoveries are made. This seminar provides an accessible, hands-on overview of experimental approaches used to study the molecules of life, DNA, RNA, proteins, metabolites, and lipids, and how these techniques help us understand normal biology and diseases such as cancer. 

The course combines short lectures, collaborative presentations, and guided discussions of selected scientific literature. Students will explore key experimental themes, including gene expression analysis, protein characterization, metabolomic and lipidomic profiling, and methods for examining cells and tissues. Through these topics, students will learn how researchers design experiments, generate data, and interpret findings in the context of broader scientific questions. 

Participating in this seminar will help students develop foundational research literacy and technical awareness early in their academic careers. Students will practice interpreting real data, comparing experimental methods, and evaluating how different techniques contribute to scientific understanding. The course also emphasizes practical skills, reading primary literature, asking research-driven questions, communicating scientific concepts, and working collaboratively. 

This seminar is ideal for students interested in exploring research opportunities on campus. By the end of the course, students will have a clearer understanding of the scientific process, stronger analytical skills, and greater confidence engaging with biomedical research in both academic and laboratory settings.
OUTCOMES:
This seminar is designed to develop students’ high cognitive skills and strengthen their disciplinary focus in biomedical science. Students will be introduced to core experimental concepts and will demonstrate increasing ability to interpret figures, evaluate experimental methods, and analyze data across modalities such as genomics, proteomics, and metabolomics. These outcomes will be demonstrated through structured discussions, an individual research proposal, and a group presentation requiring explanation of experimental design, interpretation of results, and identification of methodological strengths and limitations. 

Students will apply the scientific method by formulating research questions, developing testable hypotheses, and selecting appropriate experimental approaches. Understanding of how experimental choices influence research outcomes will be demonstrated through the design and justification components of the written proposal and through comparative analysis of techniques in the group presentation.

The course also advances effective communication skills. Students will demonstrate clear and logical communication of scientific ideas through written work and oral presentations tailored to a peer audience.

In addition, the seminar prepares students for lifelong learning by fostering independent engagement with scientific literature, synthesis of ideas within a broader research context, and the ability to ask informed, evidence-based questions. These competencies support continued intellectual growth in research and biomedical science.
ASSIGNMENTS:
This seminar integrates structured readings, guided discussions, a collaborative project and an individual research assignment to support the development of strong analytical and communication skills. Students will read approximately 10 to 12 pages per week, including short review articles and selected figures or excerpts from primary research papers. To prepare for discussion, students will complete brief reading notes (approximately 30 minutes per week) that highlight key concepts and questions. 

Individually, each student will develop a one-page, hypothesis-driven mini research proposal that integrates the experimental approaches introduced in the course. Each student will also deliver a 10-minute oral presentation explaining a selected research technique or concept, demonstrating how the method works and when it is used. 

As a group activity, teams of three will prepare a 15-minute presentation comparing two modern biomedical techniques, focusing on their principles, strengths, limitations and applications. Active and informed participation during weekly discussions is expected, with emphasis on interpreting figures, evaluating methodology and engaging peers constructively. 

The total out-of-class workload will be limited to approximately four hours per week for this class.
GRADING:
Letter grade: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). 
Grades will be based on the following components: 30% one-page research proposal, 30% individual oral presentation, 20% group presentation, and 20% participation in class discussions. Participation is assessed through preparedness, engagement with readings, and contributions to group work. No final exam or grade curve will be used.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Ching-Hsien Chen is an Associate Professor in the Department of Internal Medicine at the UC Davis School of Medicine and will serve as the Instructor of Record for this seminar. She received her Ph.D. in Cell Biology in 2011 and leads a research program that applies advanced biomedical techniques, including multi-omics analyses, genetic manipulations and pharmacologic approaches, to investigate the molecular mechanisms that drive cancer and fibrosis progression. Dr. Chen has extensive experience mentoring undergraduate and graduate researchers and will guide all aspects of the seminar, including instruction, discussion and evaluation. 

Dr. Reno Jihao Xu is a Postdoctoral Fellow in the Department of Internal Medicine at the UC Davis School of Medicine. Although postdoctoral scholars cannot serve as Instructors of Record, Dr. Xu will collaborate in leading selected class activities under Dr. Chen’s direction and with her presence in class. He earned his Ph.D. in Biochemistry, Molecular, Cellular and Developmental Biology in 2024 at UC Davis and brings hands-on expertise in molecular, cellular and biochemical techniques used in modern biomedical research. His role will focus on supporting demonstrations, facilitating discussions and providing practical insights into experimental methods.

TITLE: AI Agents: From Prompts to Multi-agent Teams
FOCUS: We explore how to use AI agents for a more productive and rewarding day. Studies show that generative AI, like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, or DeepSeek, can quickly lead to increases of over 50% in productivity and over 70% in job satisfaction. Studies also show that students working with AI tutors can improve their performance from a C to an A. The key to sustainable success is converting these tools into AI agents that automate concrete demands. This starts with prompt engineering (the art of knowing how to talk with AI), and ends with building multi-agent systems consisting of AI agents with team-based division of labor between them. This course aims to provide students with the big picture of the AI paradigm and equip them with practical skills to reap immediate benefits.
OUTCOMES:
1. Research Process: Students are introduced to and researching appropriate methods and techniques of working with generative AI, i.e., in the form of Large Language Models (LLMs), which include prompt engineering techniques and agentic workflow building. 
  2. Discovery: Students have the opportunity to make relevant discoveries in a fastly changing and very young field, that are previously unknown to both student and instructor and thus require both exploration and evidence-based reasoning. This is done through hands-on exercises.
  3. Broadly Relevant Work: Beyond short-term trends, students get exposed to long-term perspectives of AI-human interaction, including the importance of data, the setting of reward functions through prompt engineering, and hands-on exercises aimed at resolving real-world challenges. 
  4. Collaboration: Student collaboration and communication are emphasized and encouraged as critical research skills. 
  5. Iteration: Students learn the iterative nature of research and scholarship by experiencing how prompts are the basis for agent personalization of AI, while agentic workflows are the basis of contextualization, and multi-agent systems are the basis of AI team orchestration.
ASSIGNMENTS:
25% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

25% is for the weekly assignments on prompt engineering (weeks 2-4). This explores the Personalization of LLMs. Exercises explore how to talk with an LLM? We are implementing a memorable, practical framework for daily application. Hands-on exercise: Explore a personal case study with a complete prompt engineering framework.

25% are hands-on assignments on AI agents (weeks 5-7). This explores the Contextualization of AI Agents. Exercises explore how workflows can be automated with agentic AI? We are designing professional assistants that can take autonomous action in the real world. Hands-on exercise: Create a customized workflow integrating LLMs, databases, and online search.

25% are hands-on assignments on multi-agent teams (weeks 8-9). This explores the Orchestration of teams of AI agents. Exercises explore how we tackle complex tasks by creating a team of diverse AI agents? We are using multi-agent systems to solve the challenges of hallucinations, robustness, biases, and complexity. Hands-on exercise: Experience the power of working with teams of diverse AI agents. Become a human resource manager of AI agents.
GRADING:
P/NP

25% grade is attendance & participation. 

25% are hands-on assignments on prompt engineering (weeks 2-4). 

25% are hands-on assignments on AI agents (weeks 5-7). 

25% are hands-on assignments on multi-agent teams (weeks 8-9).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor of Communication at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis on Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he provided technical assistance to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert and more on AI here: https://Communication.UCDavis.edu/news/UC-Davis-Professor-Martin-Hilbert-delivers-keynote-UC-Tech-2024-conference-addressing-ais

TITLE: AI-Powered Protein Engineering for Biomedical Applications
FOCUS:
Proteins are the fundamental building blocks of life, intricate molecules that carry out vital biological processes with extraordinary precision. The ability to predict and design protein structures has been a long-standing challenge in computational biology, one that has now been revolutionized by artificial intelligence. Over the past four years, advanced artificial intelligence methods, particularly deep learning models like AlphaFold, have achieved remarkable accuracy in predicting protein structures, unlocking insights that previously demanded years of laboratory experimentation. Additionally, innovative tools such as RFdiffusion now enable researchers to design entirely new proteins with specific biological functions, opening doors to revolutionary advances in medicine and biotechnology.

This seminar offers students a unique opportunity to explore this rapidly evolving field. We will begin with an introduction to protein structure and function, followed by an overview of cutting-edge AI-driven methods for protein structure prediction and de novo protein design. Finally, students will gain hands-on experience with computational tools used for engineering therapeutic proteins—such as antiviral proteins, cancer treatments, and therapies for autoimmune diseases.

With the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry awarded to pioneers in this revolution, now is the perfect time to discover how AI is transforming protein science. This course will provide a first glimpse into the future of computational biology, offering students the chance to engage with technologies that are reshaping medicine and biotechnology.
OUTCOMES:
By participating in this seminar, students will:

  • Understand the biological significance of proteins as molecular machines.
  • Learn about the protein folding problem and AI-driven solutions.
  • Use AI-powered servers (e.g., AlphaFold) for structural modeling.
  • Explore therapeutic relevance of proteins as drug targets.
  • Understand principles of de novo protein design using AI tools.
  • Analyze protein structures using visualization software (e.g., ChimeraX).
  • Conduct literature-based research on therapeutic targets.
  • Propose and present a computational design idea for a regulatory protein.

This seminar will introduce students to the transformative power of AI in protein science and equip them with practical skills and a forward-thinking perspective on the future of biotechnology.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will engage with a combination of educational videos, short readings, and a multi-week project to apply the concepts covered in the seminar.

  • Weekly Activities: Each week, students will watch short educational videos (~10 minutes) and answer 3–5 brief questions to reinforce key concepts. Some weeks may include short readings (1–2 pages) on foundational topics in protein biology and AI-driven structure prediction.
  • Final Project: Over the course of the seminar, students will progressively develop a structured project focused on a medically relevant protein. This project will include:
  1. Selection of a protein of interest and literature research on its biological function and therapeutic relevance.
  2. Computational modeling of its structure using AI-based tools.
  3. Structural analysis to understand key molecular features.
  4. Preliminary design of a regulatory protein that could modulate its function.

The final deliverable will be a three/four page report, including:

  • A two-page written summary covering the protein’s biological role, therapeutic significance, structural features, and a proposed design strategy.
  • A one/two page figure overview with structural representations of the modeled protein and the designed regulatory protein.
GRADING:
The grade of PASS is awarded to undergraduate students for work that would otherwise receive a C+ (70%) or better.

Student performance will be assessed as follows:

  • Weekly Activities (30%) – Video-based questions and short readings.
  • Final Project (70%):
  • Literature research & biological relevance (30%) – Justification of protein selection, biological function and therapeutic importance.
  • Structural prediction & analysis (20%) – Accurate modeling and interpretation of key structural features.
  • Protein design (20%) – Conceptualization and explanation of a regulatory de novo designed protein prototype.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Vladimir Yarov-Yarovoy is a professor at the University of California, Davis, specializing in computational protein design, ion channel biophysics, and AI-driven drug discovery. His research focuses on developing novel subtype-selective modulators of voltage-gated ion channels for the treatment of chronic pain, epilepsy, and cardiac arrhythmias. Voltage-gated ion channels are essential for neuromodulation and cardiac excitability, making them prime therapeutic targets. However, current drugs often lack specificity, resulting in limited efficacy and significant side effects. Dr. Yarov-Yarovoy utilizes cutting-edge computational approaches, including deep learning, molecular modeling, and structure-based drug design, to engineer next-generation ion channel modulators with enhanced selectivity and therapeutic potential. His lab integrates AI-based methods such as AlphaFold, Rosetta, and molecular dynamics simulations to predict, model, and design novel protein-based therapeutics. Through this seminar, students will explore how artificial intelligence is revolutionizing drug discovery and gain hands-on experience with computational tools used in biomedical research. Dr. Yarov-Yarovoy has extensive experience in computational biophysics and protein engineering and is dedicated to mentoring students in emerging technologies at the intersection of AI, structural biology, and medicine.

TITLE: Becoming an Extraordinary Human
FOCUS: No one wants to be ordinary. This course focuses on the personal development of the characteristics of human beings deemed extraordinary. Outcomes include enhanced concentration for higher-level cognition, increased capacity to handle stress, development of increased self-confidence, increased mastery of emotional and mental processes, development of physical awareness and control, and development of positive personal characteristics. Physical activities include movements and visualizations that revolve around a unique heart-centered meditation method, namely, Tamarkoz®.
OUTCOMES: Enjoy enhancing the personal attributes which foster the development of (1) Concentration & focus for higher-level cognition and academic success (2) Cross-culturally positive characteristics such as fairness, acceptance, respect, responsibility (3) Stress Mastery (4) Resiliency (5) Physical awareness and control.
ASSIGNMENTS: 1. Reading: The Extraordinary Human (In press) - (20 pages per week, 1.5 hours/week); 2. Completion of the daily, focused journal. (30 minutes per week); 3. Completing a one-time self-assessment survey at the beginning and end of the quarter, namely, the description of personal behavior and changes (2 hours per quarter)
GRADING: Grade breakdown would be 25% for class participation, 40% for assigned reading, 25% of keeping a journal, and 10% for completing the self-assessment survey at the beginning and end of the quarter. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Mohammad Sadoghi is an Associate Professor in Computer Science at UC Davis. He leads the ExpoLab research group that aims to pioneer a new exploratory data platform to unify secure transactional and real-time analytics. Additionally, he has a keen interest in understanding the true nature of the human being and uncovering one’s inner identity. He has been offering courses at UC Davis that focus on stress management and the student’s overall well-being centered around meditation.

Nasim Bahadorani is a wellness expert who specializes in lifestyle health behavior programs. She has her doctorate in public health from Loma Linda University and taught public health courses in the Health Sciences Department at California State University, Northridge. She holds a master’s degree in biomedical science from Midwestern University, College of Health Sciences. Her strong background in biomedical sciences coupled with her experience in public health provides unique expertise in understanding pathways of disease and knowing the strategic models and interventions to prevent them. She is a certified health education specialist and a certified health and wellness coach providing consultation for wellness programs. Her work is focused on lifestyle medicine with integrative approaches to disease prevention and health promotion.

TITLE: Biochemistry
FOCUS: Our course will give you hands-on experience in collecting and analyzing biochemistry data in the lab. We have eight experiments that you will conduct with your lab partners. In the course of these experiments, you will learn how to pipet and use various instruments in a biochemistry lab. In addition, we will also discuss how various classes in your freshman and sophomore years apply to upper division biochemistry courses.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn how to use biochemistry equipment to obtain experimental data and how to analyze and interpret the data. Effective communication will be learned as students work with their lab partners to acquire data and discuss experimental results. We will discuss the importance of ethics in reporting scientific data. We will develop confidence in performing biochemical techniques, calculations, and analyses of experimental data. We will develop a perspective of why students take lower division math, physics, chemistry, and English in preparation for upper division biochemistry. Biochemistry is a key component for students preparing for health related fields.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will submit eight lab reports during the quarter. They will write these reports outside of lab and turn them in at the beginning of class the following week. Each lab report will include a brief discussion of what we were trying to learn that day in lab, what skills were mastered, analysis of their experimental data, and a brief discussion of what they learned from their experimental data.
GRADING: Participation in class discussions (10%), handing in eight lab reports (80%), working well in a group using lab equipment (10%). Grading is P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Ken Hilt has taught biochemistry on the Davis campus for over 35 years. His courses include BIS 102, BIS 103, BIS 105, MCB 120, MCB 120L , and the freshman seminar for the last ten years. His interests are in protein chemistry, particularly enzymes and the effect of metals at their active site. Justin Yamada is a staff research associate in Molecular and Cellular Biology. He prepares chemicals and cells for use in the upper division biochemistry and genetics courses. He has assisted Dr. Hilt in teaching the freshman seminar class for the past five years.

TITLE: Biology of Aging: From contemporary theories to potential counter-aging therapies
FOCUS: Why do we age? Is it preventable? This seminar explores one of biology's most complex questions. We will investigate the contemporary scientific theories of aging, from the role of our genes and cellular damage to the latest research on potential counter-aging therapies.  Through guided readings, in-class discussions, and a final group presentation, you will learn to critically evaluate claims about aging in the popular media, and effectively explain the science of aging to a non-scientist.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the major biological theories of aging and the fundamental genetic and cellular mechanisms that drive it. They will develop the skills to critically assess media claims on anti-aging interventions and effectively communicate scientific findings to a non-scientific audience.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly reading assignments consist of one article from the popular press, and/or one from the primary scientific literature. Class time will include some lecture to cover basic concepts as well as discussion of the articles and the underlying science. There will be a short quiz before each class to test knowledge related to the readings. In the second half of the course, students will form small groups to research a specific topic in aging or a counter-aging strategy. Each group will present a 10-minute oral presentation to explain the complex science of your topic to a non-scientist audience, such as friends or family.
GRADING:
25% Class participation during in-class discussions
25% Weekly quizzes based on readings
50% Final in-class presentation

There will be no midterm or final exams. There will be quiz in Canvas each week, covering that week’s reading material.

Final grades will follow a straight scale (not curved): P = > 70%; NP = < 70%
INSTRUCTORBIO: Lifeng Xu is an Associate Professor in the Department of Microbiology & Molecular Genetics. She leads a research lab focused on understanding how telomeres and telomerase function in human diseases, including cancer and premature aging. She has taught classes on Recombinant DNA Techniques, Cancer Biology, and General Microbiology Laboratory.

TITLE: Blockchain and Social Change: An exploration of crypto, NFTs, DiFi, and the metaverse
FOCUS:
Blockchain & social change: an exploration of Crypto, Memecoins, NFTs, DiFi, and the metaverse

 

The blockchain is more than Bitcoin and Ethereum. While we'll have to study and understand both, we will explore the full array of possibilities of the blockchain's modern way of storing and processing data. The blockchain allows for a technological paradigm quite distinct from the centralized model championed by the likes of Google, Facebook, and Amazon. In this course, we explore aspects of social change promoted by the blockchain paradigm, and its main applications, including cryptocurrency (e.g. bitcoin, ethereum, cardano, etc), NFTs (Non-fungible tokens), DeFi (smart-contracts, etc), and the metaverse (3D virtual reality, etc), among other more common applications. We research existing and potential applications and how they affect society, including in health care, education, business. We also research the potential downsides of this new technological paradigm. The outcome of this course is a collection of innovative research done by students into the possibilities and potential dangers of the blockchain paradigm in society.
OUTCOMES:
Research Process: students are introduced to doing qualitative research on a new topic, mainly in form of a literature review of a blockchain application.

Discovery: after deciding on which application they want to explore, students research on their own, mainly in form of a literature review. This includes gathering and filtering information from project reports, whitepapers, magazine articles, and news outlets.

Broadly Relevant Work: this exploration informs ongoing research we are doing about the blockchain at our computational communication research lab. Students help to identify important case studies, strengths, weaknesses and threats of this new technological paradigm (which is quickly evolving).

Collaboration: Students work in teams to research a blockchain application (among topics like: crypto & finance; NFTs & art; metaverse & business; blockchain & public health, etc).

Iteration: After choosing an existing application of the blockchain, students go through 3 cycles: two presentation and one final write up. They find their own examples in the literature, and compare what they find with findings of others. At the end, the goal is to identify common characteristics of the blockchain paradigm. Through different iterations on different blockchain applications, students will learn how individual research among complementary applications can contribute to understanding a comprehensive technological paradigm.
ASSIGNMENTS:
20% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

20% is the first team presentation (about week 3-4). Students chose among different blockchain applications, and join a team of 2-4 other students to explore it. The first presentation explains the application.

30% is for the second presentation (about week 6-7). Students research and filter different case studies of the application of the blockchain, including their theoretical intention and their real-world applications. This includes the identification of benefits, challenges and potential downsides.

30% is for the final literature review write up. This consists of a teamwork effort to write up and report on the identified literature and case studies, and present the identified opportunities and threats in the style of an information literature review. In weeks 8-9 we review how to write this final literature review report.
GRADING:
P/NP

20% grade is attendance & participation. 

20% is the first team presentation (about week 3-4). 

30% is for the second presentation (about week 6-7). 

30% is for the final literature review write up.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis in Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems and has become increasingly concerned about the downsides of digital tech. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica in the campaign of Donald Trump a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he created the Information Society Program for Latin America and the Caribbean. Prof. Hilbert has provided technical assistance in the field of digital development to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, Die Welt, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert

TITLE: Bookmaking: A Historical Study and Hands-on Experience of Making Books
FOCUS: For the book lover, curious historian, and/or crafty creator - this course will look at the history of bookmaking and the book restoration process. In this seminar, we will start with discussing the first Egyptian scroll created around 3000 B.C. and travel around the world to look at the development of books in places like China, the Islamic World, Europe, the U.S., etc. We will discover the various forms books have taken over time and look at long-held traditions of dispersing books such as the popular street book vendors in Morocco and Paris. This course will also look at the creation or discovery of first books like the Buddhist text called the Diamond Sutra, the first printed book in Japan by Empress Shotoku, and Gutenberg's Bible. While we are learning all this exciting information about books, we will be creating our own books. We will start with simple matchbooks, then move on to more difficult books such as Hi Brag books, On-the-Go books, and finishing with a more substantial hardback bullet journal. Lastly, we will learn a few book restoration techniques and practice them on some older books. Students will need to purchase the JOFAMY 17 Pieces Hand Bookbinding Tools, Practical Bookbinding Kit on Amazon.com: https://www.amazon.com/Binding-Bookbinding-Materials-Webbing-Storage/dp/B0DL9BF8VS/ref=sr_1_18?crid=149CRKQ9FY7QK&dib=eyJ2IjoiMSJ9.hSJYBjX6UchX5mtnS_VBWg_PESGkqiwbaCHA9_utpn8STNKflj5F5dCmbdYrGyPYXhSM4ABE6PvsJ4PuLIlb4zYnEqmFZtmlF64Qr0o72Ffc7lAxMoU-qCgUAgokvz8CfZqTv_ByzjDsIC6JuIucZRaHl9He0f2Plb760bIZ3ZhX-lmcfq7lYciO3rUQBZcA2hHimyUV2CdPpg1lUUN5kGJb1OgsbRUeo323SC9bI5B3D3Zg75fX6fokB6b77mkxiCvBtmdytKbr6Rrwwyk-b2cE5WYbFkYE8_PnL20gRKs.VB7yQwGQls8HYJA7BX58oFMGF_2C7AJA3kAxHQaLK8E&dib_tag=se&keywords=bookbinding%2Bkit&qid=1769470149&sprefix=bookbinding%2Caps%2C251&sr=8-18&th=1
OUTCOMES: In this seminar, students will 1) develop a global perspective of bookmaking, 2) develop an appreciation for the complexities involved in making a physical book, 3) use math and writing skills to create the structure and content of approximately 8 books of different designs, 4) improve critical thinking skills while learning the art of book restoration and practice restoring an older book.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will have weekly readings of approximately 15 pages or short videos totaling 30-40 minutes to watch for which they will write short, informal journal entries of about 100 words each that include 2 questions to prepare for each class discussion. Each week, a team of 2 students will give a 10-minute presentation on a topic relating to the books of the week, which requires library research of at least three sources. We will make two books of a particular style per week with each style expanding students' knowledge of bookmaking (some of these are very easy to make and take little time). Students will then be responsible for filling the contents with poetry, short stories, photos, etc.) before the next class. The last, major project, will be an art book that encompasses the various book styles learned during the quarter and contains approximately/the equivalent of 15-20 total pages.
GRADING: Students will write short, informal weekly journal entries that include 2 questions for class discussion (20%), create 10 small book projects (50%), team presentation (10%), and a bullet-journal (20%). All work must be completed for a passing grade - averages of C- (70 minimum) and above (100 maximum) will receive a grade of "Pass."
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Elisabeth Lore holds a Ph.D. in Comparative Literature from UC Davis where she specialized in French, Caribbean, and Multilingual Literature. Her research interest focuses on Mediating Literature, a genre in which the author’s implied intent is to negotiate relationships between members of conflicting cultures resulting from immigration.
