She currently teaches for the UCD Writing Center and has taught comparative literature, French, and humanities classes for UCD, Sacramento City College, American River College, and Foothill College. In her past teaching experience with the Hubert H. Humphrey Fellowship program at the UC Davis Extension Center, her international students taught her many things about their home countries such as China, Japan, Afghanistan, Mexico, Armenia, Romania, Pakistan, Iran, South Korea, Fiji, Argentina, Columbia, and many African countries, just to name a few. Over the years, she has had the privilege of traveling to over 27 different countries to do research, work, and visit family and friends. She enjoys sharing these experiences with her students in each of her classes.

TITLE: Community-Sustaining Research and First Gen Students
FOCUS: This course connects students with community-sustaining research methods, theory, and practice. Students will  inquire into how their research interests and inquiries can impact local communities, how researchers used scientific and other academic research practices as tools of domination, and consider liberatory research methodologies and action-based alternatives including community-sustaining research, culturally-affirming pedagogies, participatory action research, and community wealth models for research. The course aims to connect students’ academic goals and interests with their communities. Through this course, students will learn applied research methods, action-based learning practices, and community-sustaining praxis. Throughout the course, students will learn about systems and structures of power, community spaces of resistance, and consider how those spaces emerge in the context of research. Students will develop theoretical and methodological knowledge alongside skills that include quantitative and qualitative analytical skills, the ability to communicate to diverse audiences, digital literacy, and intellectual self-confidence. In addition to these skills-based goals, students will also consider how researchers’ power, privilege, and positionality impact equity and the process of gathering data as well as analyzing, interpreting, and sharing findings. Ultimately, this course helps students to attend to the question: how do liberatory research practices offer first-generation students and researchers an opportunity to create a counterbalance to historically marginalized research spaces?
OUTCOMES:
As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students should be able to:

  • Recognize the historical role that systemic injustice contributed to community distrust of researchers and the lack of research that would serves specific communities 
  • Identify possible solutions that support community goals in conversation with community "stakeholders"
  • Demonstrate responsive communication and collaboration with diverse individuals and perspectives
  • Demonstrate digital literacy skills through multiple modes including, but not limited to: 
    • Community mapping 
    • Social media
    • Data collection
  • Practice critical reflection and principled action
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will be assigned a scholarly-peer-reviewed article to read every week and be expected to write a brief summary and reflection.

Students will work in groups to identify a problem in their community (broadly defined). They will work individually to write up a historical analysis, and as a group to develop a digital community mapping project and a research plan. This work will take place in class and outside of class.

Students will have smaller assignments designed to support their work, and receive feedback on their writing, research, and questions.

At the end of the quarter, they will present their findings and their digital map.
GRADING:
Students will receive letter grades. Much of the course's work will be done and overseen in class. Students' workloads will likely look similar to the following distribution:

50% Readings

-weekly reading, summary, brief reflection (1 page per week max)

50% Research Project

- Identify a community, a challenge they face, and gaps in research: (1-2 pages)

- Historical Analysis (1-2 page paper)

- Research Proposal (2-3 pages)

- Community Mapping Project (digital project)

- Presentation

Percentages required for specific letter grades:

100-90: A

89-80: B

79-70: C

69-60: D

<59: F
INSTRUCTORBIO: Vanessa Madrigal-Lauchland (vlauchland@ucdavis.edu) lectures in the Chicana/o/x Studies Department at the University of California, Davis, and is a postdoctoral fellow in the Ethnic Studies Department at the University of California, Berkeley. She is a first-generation scholar from the Central Valley, and works to create sustainable pathways into and through higher education. Her dissertation, The Eugenic Barrio: Housing, Policing, and Education in Stockton, California, 1900-1958, explores how federal, state, and local leaders embedded eugenic ideology into American institutions. Her current research recovers narratives of resistance to this institutionalization of racism by tracing the development of Black Power and Chicano Power epistemologies among youth in Stockton, California, in the 1960s.

TITLE: Computational Modeling and Machine Learning in Healthcare for Cardiac Arrhythmia Risk Prediction
FOCUS: Cardiotoxicity in the form of abnormal heart rhythm is a critical regulatory concern in healthcare and has resulted in withdrawal of multiple therapeutic agents from development or the marketplace. It has been attributed to unwanted drug interactions with cardiac ion-conducting membrane proteins called ion channels, especially a potassium channel called hERG, a notorious drug anti-target. However, not all such interactions result in cardiac arrhythmia, which may lead to withdrawal of safe and efficacious medications. In this course, we will discuss how state-of-the-art computational modeling techniques along with machine-learning (ML) and other artificial intelligence (AI) enabled approaches can be used to accurately predict cardiac arrhythmia outcomes due to unwanted drug – ion channel interactions as well as deleterious genetic mutations. Students will learn multiple molecular modeling and simulation techniques such as AlphaFold protein structure prediction, OpenEye FRED and AutoDock Vina molecular docking, atomistic molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, introduction into multiscale functional modeling as well as several ML classifier methodologies. We will be using a combination of lectures with interactive visualizations as well as hands-on activities to predict ion channel protein structures, build small-molecule ligand libraries, perform molecular docking, set up, run and analyze MD simulations, optimize and test ML classifier models. Taking this course will help students to learn state-of-the-art computational modeling and AI/ML approaches and how they can be used to solve challenging biomedical problems.
OUTCOMES:
By participating in this seminar, students will:

  • Understand biological significance of cardiac ion channel and their role in the heart electrical activity.
  • Learn how cardiac ion channel mutations and their unwanted drug interactions interfere with the normal cardiac function including specific examples.
  • Explore what clinical, cellular and molecular manifestations of drug-induced cardiac arrhythmia are and how they can be predicted using computer modeling and artificial intelligence / machine learning (AI/ML) approaches.
  • Gain familiarity and hands-on experience with different molecular modeling and simulation methodologies including de novo and homology protein structure prediction (e.g., AlphaFold 3 and RosettaCM), molecular docking (e.g., OpenEye FRED, AutoDock Vina), molecular dynamics simulations (e.g., Gromacs), and combined methods (e.g., SILCS).
  • Learn different molecular modeling and simulation data visualization and analysis techniques (e.g., using ChimeraX, VMD, MDAnalysis).
  • Explore different ML optimization and classifier approaches (e.g., Bayesian ML, logistic regression, random forest, neural networks). Write simple ML code and/or perform ML model optimization using Jupyter notebooks.
  • Learn about modern multiscale computer modeling and AI/ML approaches for predicting drug-induced arrhythmia risks and enhance critical thinking skills by evaluating them.
  • Prepare for lifelong learning in computational biology by gaining hands-on experience with emerging computer modeling and AI/ML-driven methods.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will engage with a combination of lecture materials, interactive demos, several scientific paper and book chapter readings, and short hands-on projects to apply the concepts covered in the seminar.

We will alternate lecture and hands-on activity weeks.

Each lecture week, students will listen to a lecture, watch interactive demos, and answer 2–3 brief questions to reinforce key concepts. Some weeks may also include readings of a scientific paper or a part of a book chapter (up to 10 pages). on foundational topics in cardiac ion channel biology and pharmacology as well as computer modeling and AI/ML methodology.

For hands-on activities we will learn how to use modern molecular modeling and simulation tools and AI/ML code to build in silico models for predicting drug-induced arrhythmia risk. The models we will be exploring will be similar to ones described in our recent paper (Rouen et al. Biophysical Journal 2026 https://www.cell.com/biophysj/fulltext/S0006-3495%2825%2903493-9). Each hands-on activity will be dedicated to a specific part of this computational pipeline (protein structure modeling, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulation or its combination with docking,  machine learning classifier)
GRADING:
The grade of PASS is awarded to undergraduate students for work that would otherwise receive a C+(70%) or better.

Student performance will be assessed as follows:

40% - Questions based on conceptual lectures, demos, and short readings.

60% - Hands-on activities, which include accurate modeling / simulations (30%), interpretation of key findings in a short report and/or short oral presentation (30%), with the focus for both parts on:

  • Ion channel structural prediction & analysis (15%)
  • Molecular docking (15%)
  • Molecular dynamics simulations (15%)
  • Machine learning classifier models (15%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: I am an Associate Professor at the Department of Physiology and Membrane Biology as well as Department of Pharmacology at the UC Davis School of Medicine. The research in my laboratory focuses on using computer modeling and simulation techniques, such as molecular docking and molecular dynamics (MD), to study interactions of small-molecule drugs, hormones, peptides, lipids and other ligands with membrane proteins of biomedical significance such as ion channels. In particular, we have been studying interactions of various pharmaceuticals with cardiac voltage-gated ion channels KV11.1 (also known as hERG), NaV1.5 and CaV1.2 in different conformational states to assess drug induced arrhythmia risk from their chemistry using all-atom MD simulations enhanced by artificial intelligence / machine learning (AI/ML) approaches. This is a crucial component of a multi-scale safety pharmacology pipeline we have been developing in collaboration with Profs. Colleen Clancy's and Vladimir Yarov-Yarovoy's labs (see e.g., Yang et al Circ. Res. 2020; 126:947-964). Recently we extended this pipeline to include multiple cardiac ion channel protein targets in different conformations, diverse set of drug molecules, several docking, MD simulation, and AI/ML approaches (see Rouen et al. Biophysical Journal 2026 https://www.cell.com/biophysj/fulltext/S0006-3495%2825%2903493-9). This study will form a conceptual basis of the seminar.

TITLE: Could a Robot Ever Be Conscious?
FOCUS: Are robots conscious? If they are, how would you know? How would they know? Is artificial intelligence the same thing as consciousness? If not, what distinguishes the two? What is it like to be a robot? In this course, you will learn how to answer these (and other) questions by studying various theories of consciousness and by applying those theories to episodes of the TV series Westworld. In doing so, we will consider how a diverse array of academic disciplines think about, and study, consciousness and consider each approach by applying it to different episodes of Westworld. Success in the course will require a careful viewing of Westworld Season One. The TV show includes depictions of graphic violence including sexual violence, sexual content, nudity, and coarse or crude language. While there is no textbook for this class, you will need access to all episodes in Westworld Season One, which can be purchased or streamed online. You will be graded on your weekly participation, a one page reflection paper, and a four page final paper.
OUTCOMES: By participating in this course, you will: (a) be able to articulate the major approaches to studying consciousness and the limitations of each approach, (b) be able to explain how consciousness influences our understanding of, and expectations for, robots and artificial intelligence, (c) be able to apply different theories of consciousness to the robot "Hosts" in Westworld, (d) develop and practice effective communication skills, (e) develop high cognitive skills, and (f) develop focus and depth in one or more discipline.
ASSIGNMENTS: You will need access to all episodes in Westworld Season One. Season One can be purchased online via Amazon or iTunes. You can stream Westworld if you have a subscription to HBO Go or HBO Now. You will watch one episode of Westworld each week (1 hour) and prepare to participate in class discussions by doing the weekly readings (all readings less than 20 pages per week). To facilitate focused and lively discussion of the assigned readings, you will prepare one brief Question and Contention (Q&C) paper during the quarter in which you either: (a) forward a contention and supporting argument; or (b) pose an important question and tentative answer. The contention or question must be applied to that week’s episode of Westworld. You will also need to prepare two weekly questions. These questions should reflect points of uncertainty about that week's reading, episode, or the relationship between the reading and the episode. Lastly, you will write a four page final paper where you apply course concepts to the ideas considered in Westworld. Specifically, you must pick a theoretical topic from the reading, and consider it in the context of the TV series.
GRADING: The class is graded using the following letter grade ranges: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). Your grade is earned through class participation (30%), weekly questions (20%) the Question and Contention paper (20%), and the final paper (30%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Richard Huskey (Ph.D. University of California, Santa Barbara) is an assistant professor in the Department of Communication, principle investigator in the Cognitive Communication Science Lab, researcher in the Computational Communication Research Lab, researcher at the Center for Mind and Brain, and an officer of the International Communication Association's Communication Science and Biology interest group. He studies how motivation influences the attitudes people hold, and the behaviors they adopt. He researches these questions using a variety of methodological techniques including: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), computational methods, and lab-based experiments.

TITLE: Counterstory in Practice: First-Gen Narratives through Zines
FOCUS:
This first-year seminar introduces counterstory as a rhetorical practice through the creation of individual zines. Counterstories challenge dominant narratives by centering lived experience, cultural knowledge, and alternative ways of knowing-making them especially powerful for first-generation students navigating higher education. Students will examine how zines have historically functioned as accessible, low-cost platforms for marginalized voices and will analyze examples drawn from cultural, activist, and educational contexts.

Throughout the quarter, students will engage in guided reflective writing, low-tech design workshops, and peer discussion as they compose an individual zine that tells a first-generation story in their own voice. Emphasis is placed on process, revision, and rhetorical choice rather than artistic skill. All readings and examples are provided on Canvas at no cost. The course concludes with the compilation of all individual zines into a course anthology, creating a collective record of first-generation narratives at UC Davis.
OUTCOMES:
Seminar Goals:
• Understand counterstory as a rhetorical method for challenging dominant narratives
• Reflect critically on first-generation identity, access, and belonging in higher education
• Practice multimodal composition using writing, layout, and visual rhetoric
• Develop revision and reflection skills transferable to academic writing
• Articulate rhetorical choices related to audience, purpose, and representation
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will complete short readings, reflective writing, and creative design work, for this seminar. Weekly readings will frame our work and will typically total 15 pages provided on Canvas.

Each week, students will write a short reflection or journal entry (about 250 words) responding to readings or class discussions. Early in the quarter, students will complete a short-written response (1–2 pages) analyzing how an existing zine or narrative tells a story and reaches an audience. The main project is an individual zine (4–8 pages) that students develop step by step through drafting, peer feedback, and revision. The course ends with a short reflection essay (about 2 pages) explaining the choices made in the zine. Students will participate in discussions and workshops during class. There are no exams or required off-campus activities.
GRADING:
Assignments 
20% Participation and discussion 
25% Weekly journals and short analysis 
45% Individual zine project (concept, draft, final)
10% Final reflection

The instructor will provide specific guidelines and rubrics for all assignments, and provide feedback on student work. Percentages required to earn specific letter grades are as follows: 100-90 (A to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valerie Smith is a Lecturer in the University Writing Center, where she teaches a variety of writing courses focused on rhetoric, digital communication, and multimodal composition. She is currently completing her Ph.D. in Technical Communication and Rhetoric. Her teaching examines how storytelling, technology, and design shape communication, access, and participation in academic and public contexts. As a first-generation scholar, she brings particular attention to reflection, rhetorical awareness, and inclusive teaching practices that support students from diverse backgrounds.

TITLE: De Noche, De Día… ¡Arriba La Jotería! Identity, Culture, and Queer Latinx Futures
FOCUS: De Noche, De Día… ¡Arriba La Jotería! celebrates the resilience, creativity, and pride of queer and trans Latinx communities. In this seminar, we explore identity, culture, and activism “day and night,” examining histories of resistance, current issues, and popular culture representations. Together, we’ll analyze films, music, art, and digital media while reflecting on personal and collective experiences. This seminar will combine discussions, short lectures, interactive activities, and weekly journals intended to engage students in a multimodal learning environment. Students will develop communication and critical thinking skills, engage in respectful dialogue, and learn strategies to navigate higher education as queer and trans Latinx individuals. We will connect identity development to leadership, ethics, and global perspectives that ground students in a critical approach to enact change. By the end, students will have a deeper understanding of queer and trans Latinx history and culture, practical tools for success at UC Davis, and a stronger sense of how to lead and thrive in diverse communities. This course is for anyone interested in identity, justice, and building a more inclusive future.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this seminar, students will be able to:

  1. Communicate their ideas with confidence and clarity in discussions, journals, and creative projects.
  2. Think critically about queer and trans Latinx history, culture, and current events.
  3. Demonstrate respect, empathy, and openness when engaging with diverse perspectives and lived experiences.
  4. Explore queer Latinx studies through history, media, and higher education contexts.
  5. Apply leadership skills to advocacy and community engagement on campus.
  6. Recognize connections between local experiences and global movements for justice.
  7. Develop critical reflection and curiosity that support lifelong learning beyond the course.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Weekly Reflection Journals(8 total, ~250 words each) – Students will respond to prompts connected to weekly topics, readings, and media. These journals encourage personal reflection, critical thinking, and connection between course content and lived experience.

Readings & Media Engagement – Each week, students will complete readings and media analysis consisting of film clips, music videos, or art respective to each weeks topic. 

Media Analysis Final Project – By the end of the quarter, students will select a cultural text (e.g., film, TV episode, music video, visual art) representing queer and/or trans Latinx experiences. They will prepare a 4-5 page written analysis (double-spaced) applying concepts from the course.

Class Participation & Discussion – Active engagement in class discussions, small group activities, and peer discussions are expected. Students will demonstrate attentive listening, respect for diverse perspectives, and thoughtful contributions related to topics covered.
GRADING:
Percentages required to earn specific letter grades: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)

Weekly Reflection Journals (40%) (8 total, ~250 words each) – Students will respond to prompts connected to weekly topics, readings, and media. 

Media Analysis Final Project (30%) – By the end of the quarter, students will select a cultural text (e.g., film, TV episode, music video, visual art) representing queer and/or trans Latinx experiences. 

Class Participation & Discussion (30%) – Active engagement in class discussions, small group activities, and peer discussions are expected.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Ruben Zecena is an interdisciplinary scholar specializing in contemporary Latinx Literature and Culture. He received his PhD in Gender & Women's Studies from the University of Arizona. He is completing his first monograph, Impossible Possibilities: The Unruly Imaginaries of Queer and Trans Migrants. The book explores the cultural productions of LGBTQ migrants as a blueprint from which to question and re-imagine the contours of national belonging. It is under advance contract with University of Texas Press.

Rodrigo Bonilla is the Director for the Chicanx Latinx Retention Initiative and CCLASS, where he supports the UC Davis Chicanx Latinx Student Community. He is a first generation college graduate, arriving from Mexico at the age of 9. He has a B.S in Agriculture, B.S in Economics and a B.A. in Spanish from Washington State University. As a student, Rodrigo was involved in many service-oriented organizations including Residence Life, Admissions and the Multicultural Student Services Mentoring Program. After receiving his M.S. in International Agricultural Development at UC Davis Rodrigo, was an Academic Counselor and the Student Affairs Officer for the Gender, Sexuality & Women’s Studies and the American Studies Departments.

TITLE: Digital Activism and the Rhetoric of Global Change
FOCUS:
This seminar examines how digital activism operates rhetorically across global contexts. Students will explore how social movements use digital platforms-such as social media, websites, and visual campaigns-to persuade audiences, construct identities, and mobilize support across national and cultural boundaries. Rather than focusing on technical production, the course emphasizes rhetorical analysis, ethical communication, and audience awareness.

Through global case studies related to human rights, environmental justice, reproductive justice, and access, students will analyze how arguments are shaped through language, images, hashtags, and platform affordances. Readings, podcasts, and videos (provided free on Canvas) introduce key concepts such as rhetoric, circulation, positionality, and ethics in digital communication. Throughout the course, students will reflect on their own positionality-as digital media users, cultural participants, and emerging global citizens-and consider how identity, location, and access shape both the creation and circulation of activist messages. The course culminates in an individual project in which students either analyze or rhetorically remix a global digital activism campaign to demonstrate ethical and audience-aware communication.
OUTCOMES:
As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students will be able to:

  • Identify global systems of digital communication and explain how platforms shape activism, visibility, and power across cultural and national contexts.
  • Analyze global examples of digital activism using rhetorical concepts such as audience, purpose, platform, and circulation.
  • Examine how positionality (identity, culture, location, and access) influences participation and representation in digital spaces.
  • Apply ethical and rhetorical frameworks to evaluate activist messages and digital campaigns.
  • Name and experiment with strategies for global action by producing a rhetorically grounded analysis or remix of a digital activism campaign.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will complete readings, media analysis, and writing assignments. Weekly materials will typically include 15–20 pages of reading or equivalent media (articles, videos, podcasts), all provided on Canvas.

Students will write weekly discussion posts or short reflections (250–300 words) that apply rhetorical concepts from the course to global examples of digital activism and invite reflection on positionality and ethical communication. Early in the quarter, students will complete a short rhetorical analysis paper (2–3 pages) focused on a digital activist campaign or artifact.

In class, students will participate in guided discussion and small-group mapping activities that explore how activist messages circulate across platforms and global contexts. These activities support learning but do not require graded group submissions.

The final assignment is an individual project, in which students choose either to conduct a deeper rhetorical analysis of a global digital activism campaign or to rhetorically remix or redesign an existing campaign to communicate more ethically and inclusively. Final projects are equivalent to 4–5 pages of written work and are accompanied by a 1–2 page rhetorical reflection explaining rhetorical choices and learning outcomes. There are no exams or required off-campus activities.
GRADING:
25% Participation and weekly Canvas reflections (250 words)
20% Short rhetorical analysis (2–3 pages)
45% Final project: analysis or rhetorical remix/redesign
10% Rhetorical reflection justifying design and communication choice

The instructor will provide specific guidelines and rubrics for all assignments, and provide feedback on student work. Percentages required to earn specific letter grades are as follows: 100-90 (A to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Valerie T. Smith is a Lecturer in the University Writing Center, where she teaches a variety of writing courses focused on rhetoric, digital communication, and multimodal composition. She is currently completing her Ph.D. in Technical Communication and Rhetoric. Her teaching examines how storytelling, technology, and design shape communication, access, and participation in academic and public contexts. As a first-generation scholar, she brings particular attention to reflection, rhetorical awareness, and inclusive teaching practices that support students from diverse backgrounds.

TITLE: Disability Studies, NeuroDiversity, Mentorship...and How To Be An Ally
FOCUS:
There are 350 inclusive college programs serving students with intellectual disabilities around the country, yet California has very few.

There is only one four-year inclusive college program in California: The UC Davis  Redwood SEED (Supported Education to Elevate Diversity) Scholar Program. 

This is the first of its kind in California!

Our first 8 graduates celebrated last year!

Understanding disability, neurodiversity, the history of this often marginalized population will be integral to our course. Students will develop the skills and learn the crucial ways students without disabilities can mentor and ally with them. How can the average undergrad be an ally?

Students will also learn about neurodiversity, the unique assets and challenges of people with intellectual disabilities; their K-12 systemic educational experiences and post-secondary educational options; their path to employment and a living wage.

Students will meet and explore best practices and understand universal design for learning.

Students will also experience active participation in conversations about allyship and mentorship with young adults with intellectual disabilities and advocates.

A summative video project will be required: To introduce SEED Scholars to the UC Davis campus, typical student activities and/or your favorite inside scoop about campus.

*This seminar is ideal - but not limited to - students with these majors/minors: nutrition, human development, neuroscience, education, psychology, behavioral sciences, exercise, and physiology.

For those that excel, students will be offered an opportunity - potentially paid work or work-study - to mentor SEED Scholars.
OUTCOMES:
Students will:

  1. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy by:

- understanding what neurodiversity means

- gaining familiarity with the systemic barriers adults with intellectual disabilities must face every day, including academic, social/emotional, employment, and health

- developing an understanding of self-determination, dignity of risk, ableism, and opportunities for typical students to offer support and allyship to this marginalized and often overlooked population

- seeing the positive societal impact of presuming competence, holding space for, and holding high expectations for someone with an intellectual disability while at the same time understanding that support is often necessary for success.

 

     2. Develop leadership skills by:

- exploring a cross-discipline approach to research and employment opportunities

- developing mentorship skills via a mentor toolkit

- developing the soft skills necessary to work with a diverse group of people in the workplace

- connecting with other students and community members while growing in awareness at the diversity of lived experiences in our campus community

*This seminar is ideal - but not limited to - students with these majors/minors: nutrition, human development, neuroscience, education, psychology, behavior sciences, exercise, and physiology.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will:

- Keep a weekly journal of reflections based on one reading assignment or multi-media presentation per week (Expectation: one page of writing per week)

- Participate in a group discussion board 

- 15 - 30 minute interview with a young adult with an intellectual disability facilitated during a seminar class - students will share key takeaways during class and on discussion board

- Create a 2-3 minute video that would be an accessible support for a beginning Redwood SEED Scholar at UC Davis
GRADING:
This seminar will be a Pass/No Pass grade.

The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

The seminar coursework will be graded with the following weight:

Weekly Journal - 10%

Discussion Board - 10%

Interview - 25%

Class Participation - 15%

Summative Video - 40%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Beth Foraker was a Teacher Education Supervisor in the School of Education's post-graduate Multiple Subject Credential/Masters Program for elementary school teachers. Now, she is the Director for the UC Davis Redwood SEED (Supported Education to Elevate Diversity) Scholars Program for students with intellectual disabilities.This is a partnership with the UC Davis Office of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion, UC Davis Continuing and Professional Education and the UC Davis MIND Institute.

She is an advocate for the full inclusion of people with disabilities in school and in all facets of adult life: employment, housing, community involvement. She works to build continuums of inclusion: preschool through college inclusion opportunities so that inclusive communities can develop and thrive. She works to facilitate cross-discipline collaboration, community collaboration along with amplifying family and self-advocate voices so that full belonging is possible.

TITLE: Druids: Religion, Wisdom, and Violence
FOCUS: Very few ancient people groups capture more contemporary imagination than do the druids. This is perhaps in part due to the sketchy historical records concerning their lives and ways. What we do know is that the druids were the religious leaders of their peoples. As the religious specialists in the Celtic cultures during the Iron Age, the Druids were the keepers of their peoples’ ancient wisdom. For the same reason, they were also practitioners of extreme acts of violence, including human sacrifice. This connection of wisdom and violence seems incongruous for many in contemporary western culture. Consequently, many modern treatments of druidic practice and culture are incomplete, favoring an idealized and romanticized view of Celtic life under the druids. This class will take a Religious Studies perspective on the historical record of the druids. Working with interdisciplinary methods, students will learn about and critique treatments of druidic religion and culture beginning with the historical record from the Romans and ending with the new religious movements that claim connection to the druids and the ancient world.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn what historical record remains for us concerning ancient druidic life and practice, including descriptions of their ritual activity and mythic heritage. Students will also learn a variety of approaches to studying religion with the druids as our case study. This approach will introduce students to historical, literary, sociological, and critical methods.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will read a combination of primary and secondary material each week (not to exceed fifty pages of text per class meeting).

Students will attend seminar prepared to discuss the material, and will participate in in-class group activities.

Students will write weekly responses to the course readings (not to exceed 150 words each week). There will be no exams for the course, but each student will write five weekly responses (out of eight possible) to questions concerning the course readings.

Students will lead group discussions on selected, pre-arranged class meetings. Each student will lead one class discussion with a partner. The date and topic of each student’s discussion will be determined during the first or second week of class. The instructor will model leading the discussion on the first two class meetings. Students will produce an outline/handout of the reading on their chosen date and will prepare discussion questions for the class. Student-led time will constitute approximately 20 minutes of each class meeting. The instructor will provide information/material to augment student reading and activites for the balance of each class meeting.
GRADING:
This class is P/NP. Grade breakdown:

40%: attendance and participation. Class meetings will be heavily focused on discussion, and students will participate in group activities during class to facilitate participation.

40% Weekly Responses. Students will choose the five responses they wish to write out of eight possible weeks. Each response is worth 10% of the course grade.

10%: Lead Discussion.

Students who receive a C- (70%) or better will Pass the class.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Wendy Terry is a Lecturer in the Religious Students Department. She frequently teaches survey courses, intentionally including units on druidic sacrifice and myths whenever possible, in addition to textually based courses on the Hebrew Scriptures and Christian New Testament. Her primary research is in the history of Christian Spirituality, with an emphasis in the medieval period on the British Isles where the druidic heritage remains evident in Christian writing on nature and God.

TITLE: Earthquakes in California: Are we ready for the big one?
FOCUS:
The subject matter deals with the earthquake issue in California and our state of preparedness from an engineer’s perspective Through informal lectures, case studies, and visual and shake table demonstrations, the students are expected to gain a basic appreciation of the damage that earthquakes can inflict on societies, and the measures that are taken to construct communities and structures that are more earthquake resistant. The course will begin with a general description of earthquakes, and the processes that cause them. This will be followed by three case studies that will involve discussion of three major earthquake that have shaped the history of California and earthquake engineering. Based on these case studies and current data from geological and other sources, the seminar will then look towards the future, discussing the likelihood and impact of a major future earthquake event. This will be followed by an introduction to earthquake engineering (through discussion, visuals and physical demonstration of structural shaking). Five of the lectures (by design) will involve heavy student participation. These will alternate with faculty led lectures during which new content is presented. At the each of each faculty led lecture, the students will be asked to conduct internet research on specific topics and present and discuss their findings (during the next lecture) with other students to refine their understanding of the various seismic events that have shaped California’s history. The discussions will be moderated by the instructor, and the instructor will call on all students to contribute to discussion, challenge their points of view and encourage critical discourse. 

The students are not expected to have any prior knowledge of engineering or geology, since these aspects will be covered in the instructor-led lectures, albeit from a very general perspective
OUTCOMES:
The main aim of the seminar will be for students to develop a basic understanding of the earthquake hazard and risk that we face in California, and how these can be mitigated. At the completion of the seminar, the students will be expected to be familiar with geological and seismic history of California, and the type of measures (at the structural, infrastructural and societal levels) that will help California return to normalcy when the big one strikes.

Students will be evaluated on the quality of presentation as well as frequency of participation. The following educational outcomes are targeted

(1) critical thinking and condensation of internet research into meaningful, pithy insights

(2) oral presentation

(3) written presentation
ASSIGNMENTS:
The students will be required to do research, equivalent to roughly 15 pages of reading a week, and then give presentations 5 times during the quarter. These presentations will be based on class materials, internet research (including assigned videos/reading), as well as other reading. Each of these will be accompanied by one brief 1-page paper. These talks will focus on various earthquakes within California. 

There will be a final talk and paper, which will focus on big picture issues facing California due to the earthquake risks; this will be accompanied by a 2 page final paper. In addition, students will be expected to participate in discussion during class each week.
GRADING:
A grade of pass will be awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.

The grading breakdown will be as as follows.

40% (five in class presentations and accompanying papers - 8% each)

20% (attendance)

20% (final paper and presentation) 

20% (in class participation and discussion)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Amit Kanvinde is a member of the faculty in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering. His research interests include earthquake engineering, design and simulation of steel structures, fracture and fatigue of steel structures. He currently teaches courses on linear and nonlinear structural analysis, design of steel structures (graduate and undergraduate), and mechanics of materials.

TITLE: Environmental Peacebuilding in the Middle East
FOCUS:
In times of political and geopolitical tension, environmental projects can address urgent humanitarian needs, as well as create important precedents and opportunities for supporting trust between groups, peacebuilding, and regional stability. The course aims to support the instructors' scientific work aimed at mapping previous and current, as well as planned, environmental peacebuilding initiatives in the Middle East and beyond. 

The class will include understanding main theories and frameworks regarding envionmental initiatives and peacebuilding efforts in general, as well as specific examples regarding previous and current attempts for environmental collaboration in the region, and examples of successful cross-border initiatives of the same nature around the world. It will also explain the specific nature of the environmental needs and challenges of main actors in the region, including those affected by recent wars and conflicts in the Middle East in the last several decades.  

Students will have small in-class assignments and will be expected to participate during class discussions and exercises. In addition to small in-class assignments, main assignments will be a mid-quarter short paper (3 pages) on a current cross-border environmental initiative in the Middle East, and then a medium paper (5 pages) on another initiative, including a longer explanation of challenges and solutions, and incorporation of two class readings. The class will not include tests or quizzes.
OUTCOMES:
Process: Students in this course will develop a strong foundation in case study research methods. They will learn to locate and evaluate diverse sources, including academic articles, news and online media, social media content, organizational websites, and official reports. Students will also practice engaging directly with the field by surveying or interviewing NGO representatives, experts, and other relevant stakeholders. A core skill they will acquire is qualitative thematic analysis—learning how to code segments of text, identify recurring patterns, and synthesize them into meaningful themes that generate fresh, practical insights.

Discovery: The course emphasizes discovery and exploration. Students will investigate current events and innovative projects emerging from the Middle East, a region frequently discussed yet often misunderstood. They will examine lesser-known forms of collaboration that hold potential relevance for peacebuilding and humanitarian work. Along the way, students will encounter theoretical frameworks from sociology, social psychology, sustainable development, game theory, political studies, and fields focused on creative and technological innovation.

Finally, students will learn to apply these analytical tools to future scientific endeavors and receive guidance on producing rigorous academic writing, particularly on geopolitical issues. Regular collaborative activities will deepen their understanding of environmental and cross-sector partnerships while fostering shared knowledge-building.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Short in-class writing assignments, 3-page mid-quarter paper, 5-page final paper. 

Short in-class collaborative assignments: In the in-class assignments, students will apply class theory to the conflict or cooperation between Israelis and Palestinians (past, present, future, or hypothetical). These are half-page assignments that focus on engagemet and project-building, but will also aid to build towards the papers and add knowledge to the project. 

 

3-page paper: Students will describe the collaborative environmental initiative (between Palestinians and Israelis) that they have chosen, using 3 sources from the course (theory and case description) and 3 external sources, including the initiative website, Google Scholar and/or government websites. 

 

5-page final paper: Students will describe another, different, initiative in the same way, but will also write two more pages describing the environmental problem and its social, environmental, and economic impacts. They will also explain if and how the initiative has been effective in its activity, and what are the challenges or barriers to increased effectiveness, conflict resolution, and restorative justice.
GRADING:
The class does not include quizes or tests, and students will receive ample support for the paper writing process.

 

Short in-class assignments - 20% (2 points per meeting, total 10 meetings)

Midterm paper (3-page): 30%

Final Paper (5-page): 50%

 

Students should plan to attend class in person, with allowances for illness, emergencies, and other extenuating circumstances.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Teff-Seker has been researching peace and peacebuilding in the Middle East for the past 20 years. She has published multiple articles regarding environmental cooperation in marine and desert environments, as well as regarding environmental collaboration and peacebuilding in the Middle East, East Asia, Europe, and beyond, and is recognized as an international expert in this specific field. She has advised experts and governments on this particular topic, as well as environmental planning more broadly, and belongs to several European and international science and policy networks. Beyond the topic of this course, which is her newest project, to be presented at an international conference in May 2026, she is also conducting a large-scale project on transboundary cooperation and management of marine environments, and is teaching multiple courses at the UCD Sociology department, including courses on environmental justice and ethics, gender and sexuality, and violence and inequality, among others.

TITLE: Exploring Contemporary China
FOCUS:
China holds an intriguing and influential place in the global political economy. The country shares close yet uneasy relations with its Asian neighbors, the United States and Europe, and Latin American and African countries. Within China, politics, culture, and society have changed markedly in the last four decades. This seminar helps students understand contemporary China through examining key issues. The course is organized around three themes: state power and authority, culture and infrastructure, and health. How the individual experiences life across the Greater China region is an underlying focus.  

Among other topics, we will study China’s image and status in international relations, China’s digital information system, Chinese cinema in the world, China's control of Xinjiang, mental health in China, and Taiwan literature and culture. Students will gain knowledge of China’s contested boundaries and multidimensional understanding of Chinese society. This seminar draws on the expertise of UC Davis faculty across five different fields: Anthropology, Communication, East Asian Languages and Cultures, Comparative Literature, and Sociology.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students read, write about, and discuss assigned readings. They acquire multiple disciplinary perspectives on political and social institutions, cultures and ideas, and conflicts and inequalities.
  2. Students acquire knowledge of and reflect on the cultural contexts of lives and livelihoods in complex societies against the backdrop of their own cultural context.
  3. Students learn how to assess sources as well as data and evidence.
  4. Students develop skills in critical reading and reasoning.
  5. Students engage with a diversity of ideas and analysis sincerely and respectfully.  
  6. Students gain confidence in public speaking and communication.
  7. Students engage in teamwork to identify and address global issues.
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Review the assigned material each week.
  2. Write a 400-word response to the material each week. 
  3. Read the weekly responses of your classmates and consider the issues raised by them.
  4. Mandatory participation in classroom discussion. Everyone speaks! 
  5. An end-of-quarter team presentation of a current issue related to China.
GRADING:
This course uses the P/NP scale. The P grade is awarded for work that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

  1. Students are evaluated for the quality of their Canvas entries (50 percent). Canvas entries that appear after the class meeting will not be counted.
  2. Students are evaluated for their participation in class discussion and the end-of-quarter presentation (50 percent).
  3. Students who miss three weeks of class after Week 1 will receive NP.
  4. Students who do not organize an end-of-quarter presentation must deliver additional written work.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Eddy U is Director of East Asian Studies and Professor of Sociology

Sheldon Lu is Distinguished Professor of Comparative Literature

David McCourt is Professor of Sociology

Cindy Cuihua Shen is Professor of Communication

Michelle Yeh is Distinguished Professor of East Asian Languages and Culture

Li Zhang is Professor of Anthropology

TITLE: Exploring UC Davis: The best kept secrets
FOCUS:
The goal of this seminar is to help you discover all the best resources and spots on campus that UC Davis has to offer. I often find that students learn about these resources during their last year, which is a pity. In an effort to transform the university into a place filled with fun, useful, and amazing possibilities for adventures, we will explore campus together and put together a list of all that we discover. The assignments will be a mix of field trips and class discussions, as well as group and individual activities where you’ll report back on your findings. My goal is to make you knowledgeable and comfortable in this large, beautiful campus.

The first five weeks of the quarter will be dedicated to field trips across campus, and you’ll be asked to explore the places individually and post the findings in discussion boards.  You will also be asked to explore your own campus resources and fun spots and to report back in discussion boards. Then, during the second half of the quarter, you will work in groups exploring campus resources and presenting your findings to the class. Be prepared to go explore all over campus for this seminar!

By going out of our way to explore all the secret corners of campus, we build habits of curiosity and inquiry, as well as a sense of community as we share these findings with others. We also establish a different relationship with the university as scholars and human beings in a place dedicated to our success.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop effective communication skills, 
  2. Develop high cognitive skills 
  3. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy, 
  4. Develop focus and depth in one or more discipline, 
  5. Develop leadership skills, 
  6. Develop a global perspective, and 
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Discussion board posts: Every week, you will post your individual findings and discoveries either of the places we’ll visit together or of places that you visit on your own. You will respond to your peers adding comments to their posts. Each discussion board will have specific guidelines for you to follow. 

Class discussion: Be prepared to share your findings of each weekly resource you explore and to answer your peers' questions about them. You need to come prepared with information about the resources (where to find it, what it does, etc.) and with a description of your experience (why you liked it, why you think it's important, etc.)

Group presentation: You will form groups and go explore one resource thoroughly. You will take pictures/videos, talk to employees, practice some activity related to the resource you're investigating, etc. Then, you will put together slides for a 20–30 minutes presentation to the rest of the class, as well as answer your peers' questions. Share the responsibilities in your group! 

For discussion boards and the group presentation, you’ll read up on the resource (official website, LocalWiki, Reddit, etc.). Visiting and reading about the resource will not exceed 4 hours a week.
GRADING:
The class will be graded pass/no pass. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

To obtain a Pass for this class, you will complete 8 out of the 10 discussion boards and at least half of the responses to your peers' posts. You will also participate in your group's presentation and share your findings in an engaging manner. You must participate in at least 8 of our class meetings (which means you may miss two meetings). Check Assignments for details on participation.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a lecturer for the University Writing Program. She teaches upper-division writing classes. She is interested in finding ways to bring inner awareness and peace to her students and hopes that this seminar will help her achieve a better understanding on some of these ways.

TITLE: Feminist Climate Modeling: Care & repair
FOCUS:
In this first year seminar, we will explore and repair leading climate models and modeling frameworks that are used by the UN and world leaders to organize climate policy meetings. Questions we will explore include: What is climate modeling and why is it important? How can historical bias become congealed into climate models, impacting their accuracy and usability? How has fossil-fuel-funded meddling impacted climate models, and what can we do to repair impacted models and modeling frameworks?

Activities will include discussing and assessing leading climate models including I-PAT, STIRPAT, and the SSPs. Students will learn historical context for the models we’ll be working with. We will engage in group discussions and activities in which we strategize repairs for leading climate models. You will also receive training in feminist understandings of science, and feminist approaches to constructing scientific knowledge. 

You will also participate in at least 2 climate data hack-a-thons in which you will join other UC Davis student-researchers and affiliated student researchers from UC Berkeley students in efforts to mount repairs to leading climate models. Hack-a-thons will which will occur from 1:30pm-6:30pm on Sat 10/4, Sat 10/18, Sun 11/2, and Sat 11/15. Again, you must join and participate in at least two of the scheduled hack-a-thons in order to receive credit for this course.
OUTCOMES:
Learn introductory concepts in feminist science studies

Understand the role of climate modeling in research and policy

Understand how history and systems of domination have skewed climate models

Discover what changes to climate models can help repair historical bias

Collaborate with a broader research community with a shared goal

Experience the importance of iterative knowledge--both in building on what is previously known of climate modeling and in repeating modeling exercises to ensure accuracy

Participate in a hack-a-thon and be better equipped to participate in future exercises of this sort
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments: 

Students will participate in in-class activities analyzing models to give them the skills needed to complete further assignments.

Students will participate in 2 hackathons on Saturday afternoons (you may choose 2 out of 4 dates). Afterwards, students will write a critical modeling analysis on the part(s) of the climate models they worked on (~2-3 pages)

As a group, we will develop a final modeling framework. Students will write a 1-2 page review about this modeling framework.
GRADING:
60% of grade - 2 critical modeling analysis write-ups of the part(s) of the climate models you worked on during your two required hack-a-thons (~2-3 pages)

25% of grade - 1 consensus write-up regarding your personal review of the final modeling framing we develop as a group (~1-2 pages)

15% of grade - In-class activities analyzing models

Grading scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Rebolloso McCullough, PhD, is the Associate Director at the Feminist Research Institute At UC Davis. She researches mobility justice, environmental justice, and knowledge justice and applies her expertise in ethnographic methods, cultural analysis, and power dynamics to create community-driven research partnerships that span the disciplines. She earned her PhD in Cultural Studies at UC Davis.

TITLE: Find Your Passion in Life-Learning About Sustainability in Global Context
FOCUS:
Do you want to learn about the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, discuss the interaction between science, society, and the environment, and develop interdisciplinary and global perspectives on sustainability-related topics? Are you ready to use your scientific knowledge, increase your environmental awareness, and start building a healthier future for your community and the planet?  If you say "yes," then this seminar is for you. 

In science, subjects are often presented in an isolated realm of theories with little connection to real-world applications. As a result, students struggle to identify the relevancy of these topics in their everyday lives, leading to a lack of interest or motivation to learn the material presented in these courses. Therefore, this seminar was designed to show students how science is actually strongly connected to the local and global issues happening around us. The seminar will present topics in a way that is relevant to students in three dimensions: individual, vocational, and societal. The discussions that will take place in the seminar are expected to spark an interest in science by providing students with an opportunity to use their knowledge to develop solutions that contribute to global efforts to achieve the UN's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).  In this seminar, students will explore seven Prezi learning environments that connect the UN's SDGs (#2-Zero Hunger, #3-Good Health and Well-being, #6-Clean Water and Sanitation, #7-Affordable and Clean Energy, #13-Climate Action, #14-Life Below Water, and #15-Life on Land) with various science topics typically covered in the undergraduate science curriculum.
OUTCOMES: In this seminar, students will 1) create strategies to apply knowledge, skills, and abilities to collaboratively address global challenges; 2) learn about UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) by exploring the content including articles, videos, and animations on Prezi learning environments; 3) investigate how scientific and technological applications affect our local and global society and environment; 4) develop their communication skills by assuming different roles such as politician, economist, and environmentalist and defending their viewpoints during discussions; 5) develop their critical and analytical thinking skills by creating a realistic and innovative action plan that will help solve a local sustainability problem.
ASSIGNMENTS:
The overarching assignment is to develop a PowerPoint or Prezi presentation discussing a detailed strategy that will be utilized to make their local communities more sustainable and increase public environmental awareness.  Students will have weekly assignments consisting of exploring and reflecting on the presented topics in Prezi learning environments. Active tasks during the seminar will include the following:

BEFORE THE CLASS:

  • Determining key ideas from the learning environments that will be used to defend their viewpoints as politicians, environmentalists, or economists (~30–40 min).
  • Identifying critical questions related to SDGs from small group discussions and bringing them to whole seminar discussions (ideally 2-3 questions).

DURING THE CLASS:

  • Leading discussions around presented issues and transforming them into practical suggestions that can be applied locally or globally (every three weeks).

AFTER THE CLASS:

  • Contributing to the ‘course collection’ of papers, books, and resources built into MS Office 365.
  • Documenting the main threads and ideas from seminar discussions, including new insights and ideas generated.
GRADING: Pass/No Pass. A grade of “pass” will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a letter grade of C– or better.  Grading will be based on the following percentage breakdown: exploring and outlining key issues from each Prezi learning environment (30%), actively participating in weekly discussions (30%), preparing a group presentation that highlights the connections between one SDG and some issues faced in local communities, and proposing a plan to implement a successful strategy with sufficient detail to tackle those issues in the light of discussions that will take place during the seminar (40%).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ozcan Gulacar is a Professor of Teaching within the Department of Chemistry, where he mentors undergraduate students in his research group. His research interests focus on chemical education, with an emphasis on problem-solving, cognition, knowledge structures, and education for sustainable development. He is keenly interested in helping students understand the complex interactions among factors influencing their learning and problem-solving success in his general chemistry courses. He collaborates with faculty from engineering and other science departments to explore the students’ challenges with problem solving. Additionally, he frequently instructs the CHE 2 series at UC Davis, which is required for most STEM majors.
