TITLE: Folklore & Fairytales: the Literary Worlds of Taylor Swift
FOCUS: This 1-unit interdisciplinary seminar explores Taylor Swift’s lyrics as modern literature. We’ll examine how her songs reveal personal experiences, draw on classic literary traditions, pull from folklore and mythology, and shape how we think about narrative, emotion, and self-expression. Through close readings, discussion, listening sessions, and creative analysis, we’ll uncover storytelling techniques, hidden references, and the ways Swift’s artistry influences popular culture. By the end, you’ll gain a fresh appreciation for her songwriting and her unique place at the crossroads of music, literature, and cultural impact. I believe music is a credible and fun way to get to experience and learn about the world in relation to ourselves and those around us. The seminar hosts a fun and engaging atmosphere for students who may not have similar spaces in their majors to come together and think about music, literature, and culture.
OUTCOMES: 1) develop critical thinking and analytical skills through interdisciplinary explorations of contemporary music, classic literature, and popular culture 2) refine and practice collaborative participation skills through small and large group discussions with peers and instructor 3) craft and hone critical, empathetic, and reflective writing skills 4) have fun! The seminar is meant to be a welcoming and accepting space where we can meet and discuss literature, music, and their impacts on popular culture.
ASSIGNMENTS: a few short, 1-2 page critical responses weekly, in-class discussions, informal reflective in-class journal writings, and lots of music listening! Emphasis on a seminar-style course with less lecture and more reflective writing and discussion. It’s a space to come together for an hour per week, chat, write, discuss, and reflect on the seminar topic.
GRADING: The course is graded P/NP. Each assignment is graded on a complete/incomplete basis. If the task meets all requirements, it will receive a complete. If it does not meet all requirements, it will receive an incomplete along with feedback for how to revise and a deadline for resubmission. Students can have no more than 3 incomplete assignments and receive a Pass in the course.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Dr. Moore is a dedicated educator and scholar whose work focuses on literacy equity and social justice. She believes writing is more than a skill—it’s a tool for empowerment. She weaves activism into her teaching philosophy, empowering students to use writing as a catalyst for advocacy and societal change. Mentorship is important to her, and she works alongside students to help them grow as confident writers, critical thinkers, and active participants in the world around them. Dr. Moore earned her PhD in Rhetoric and Composition from the University of Nevada, Reno; an MA in English from Sacramento State University; a BA in Literature from the University of California, Santa Cruz; and an AA in Liberal Arts from American River College.

TITLE: Frontiers in Biology: From Learning and Memory to Extraterrestrial Life
FOCUS: What are the latest developments in biology and how do they advance research, knowledge, and human well-being? This seminar course will provide introductory reading into some of the most exciting ideas and questions in modern biology. The breadth of present-day biology and the range of questions and challenges it tries to tackle have led to its extraordinary diversification into subdisciplines. This seminar will present some of the range of modern biology and explore its frontiers in order to provide undergraduate students with orientation regarding their own choices in learning and research. Topics range from the function of the brain to human evolution, epigenetics, and the possibility of extraterrestrial life in our solar system and beyond. Principal course materials are current news and overview articles in major scientific publications supplemented by relevant original research papers. Both the instructor and students will select topics. The instructor will assign topics for presentation to individual students and provide reading materials to the class. In discussing the topics both basic concepts and novel ideas will be presented.
OUTCOMES: Students will be introduced to i) current concepts and important challenges in biological sciences; ii) modern methods and approaches to resolving these challenges; and iii) some current and foreseeable applications that emerged or will emerge form biological research at the frontier. In the process, students will develop effective communication skills, deepen their understanding of modern biology, and expand their cognitive skills to cultivate lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar group will meet two hours each week for this 2-credit class. Preparation for each seminar will require approximately 10 - 20 pages of outside the class reading. The meetings will consist of classroom presentations with simultaneous discussion of the material and a final 5-page report on a pre-approved, relevant topic of each student’s choosing.
GRADING: Grading encompasses three components; presence (25%), participation in classroom discussions (25%), and a final written report on a chosen seminar topic (50%). Full credit for presence requires attending at least 8 out of 10 sessions. Participation credit requires regular raising of questions and responding to issues presented in the reading material. Essay grading is based on quality of writing and accuracy of scientific content. Percentages that are required to earn specific letter grades are as follows: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Konstantinos Zarbalis is a Professor with the Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine. His research interests center on the genetic basis of neurodevelopmental and craniofacial disorders. He is using methods and techniques in developmental biology to investigate the role of key molecules and environmental factors in vertebrate development and their involvement in disease phenotypes.

TITLE: Grand Engineering Challenges in the 21st Century
FOCUS: The world is getting a better place! More people have access to clean water, medicine and healthcare, transportation, education, food, etc. We aim to develop a global awareness through an exploration of significant engineering achievements of the 20th century, including electrification, mechanized agriculture, high-performance materials, and space exploration and how they changed and shaped our lives today. We will then focus on the 14 grand challenges identified by the National Academy of Engineering, which are crucial for improving the quality of life globally. These challenges will be discussed under the themes of sustainability, health, security, and joy of living. Through class discussions, reading assignments and videos, the students will gain global awareness and will be equipped to think broadly about their future roles as global citizens and professionals by understanding the global effects of these challenges.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

1. Explain major engineering achievements and challenges, and identify areas for further innovation that align with their interests or career aspirations.


2. Analyze how engineering challenges have global and interdisciplinary implications, considering ethical, environmental, and social dimensions.


3. Develop problem-solving approaches by applying critical thinking skills to real-world case studies, laying the foundation for future research, innovation, or advocacy.

4. Explore emerging technologies and strategies that engineers are using to tackle 21st-century challenges and consider how they might contribute through coursework, internships, or extracurricular projects.

5. Engage in lifelong learning and professional development by identifying resources, organizations, or research areas where they can continue to develop their understanding and skills in solving engineering challenges.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Two types of assignments will be used:

1. Short writing assignments: students will either read a short article or watch a short video and then write a 200-word summary of the content.

2. Perusall assignments: Perusall is an online platform that creates a social learning experience for the students. In this platform, the students can annotate readings and videos that are share with them. 

To pass this course, students are required to submit at least 7 assignments. Active participation in class discussions and activities is also encouraged.

Assignment evaluation: The instructor will evaluate the submissions by reading them and making sure the key concepts of the videos or readings are articulated.
GRADING: To pass this course, students are required to submit at least 7 assignments.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Saeidi is an assistant professor of teaching in the department of Materials Science and Engineering. He teaches senior design in which he talks about the engineering design process. This process entails identifying a problem, brainstorming potential solutions within given constraints, and ultimately determining the most practical solution. Once the solution is chosen, it is executed. He believes that every engineering project contributes to addressing at least one of the engineering grand challenges and understanding these challenges provides engineers with a broader perspective.

TITLE: Habits to Improve our Mental Wellbeing
FOCUS:
Our daily habits can help us create internal peacefulness, but we often do not pay attention to them or don’t practice healthy habits regularly. Small changes in our daily habits and practices can make a huge difference to our mental wellbeing, thus positively  affecting all the aspects of our life. This seminar will ask students to pay close attention to their daily lives and to purposefully practice habits that lead to a healthier mental wellbeing. We will discuss some of the habits that have led people to a better quality of life, we will practice them during the quarter, and we will learn from each other’s experiences through our class discussions.

This seminar will be particularly helpful for first-year students because it will teach them habits that will help them be successful and healthy during their college career. Periods of stress during the (sometimes) overwhelming quarter system can lead to vicious circles for students and lead them into unhealthy patterns that increase stress and an unhealthy lifestyle. This seminar will discuss strategies to help first-year students notice their unhealthy patterns and change their habits. We will read short texts and watch videos from experts on habits such as meditation, gratitude, good sleeping and healthy habits, and exercise. Additionally, we will discuss and use the many resources that UC Davis already has in place to help students lead healthier lives.
OUTCOMES:
1. Develop effective communication skills, 
  2. Develop high cognitive skills 
  3. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy, 
  4. Develop focus and depth in one or more discipline, 
  5. Develop leadership skills, 
  6. Develop a global perspective, and 
  7. Prepare for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Participation: For this class, you will

  • pick one or two quarter habits to practice individually all quarter and 
  • complete weekly discussion board postings.Every week, we will explore a different habit as a class, in addition to the individual ones you have picked for the quarter. As a class, we will practice the habit for a week, reflect on it in a discussion board, discuss our experience with it in class.

Discussion leading: Each week, a couple of students will lead discussion and be in charge of the whole class meeting. As discussion leaders, you will lead discussion on the weekly and the quarter habits. 

Quarter’s pledge: At the end of the quarter, you will write a pledge where you pick one habit for next quarter. The pledge should be at least 350-word long. You will share your pledge with your peers in a discussion board.

Feedback on quarter’s pledge: You will read you peers’ pledges and provide encouragement and ideas on how to be successful with the habits your peers have picked for next quarter.
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. 

To pass this seminar you need to

  • attend most class meetings and participate in the class discussions,
  • complete the discussion leading assignment in class and participate when others are doing their discussion leading,
  • complete all of the discussion board posts; most of them need to be completed on time,
  • complete the next quarter’s pledge and response on Canvas.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Faye is a lecturer for the University Writing Program. She teaches upper- and lower-division writing classes. She is interested in finding ways to bring inner awareness and peace to her students and hopes that this seminar will help her achieve a better understanding on some of these ways.

TITLE: Handling Faults in Systems Around Us (With Emphasis on Electronic Systems)
FOCUS: Faults cause the observed behavior of a system to deviate from the expected behavior. They occur throughout the lifetime of electronic devices. They may occur in the design phase, the manufacturing phase, or during normal operation. The students in this course are first expected to learn the techniques for handling faults in electronic systems. Then, the students are expected to learn handling faults in other systems in engineering, science, biology, etc. Finally, the students are expected to explore the methods of handling faults in systems associated with their field of study, and compare their explored methods with those that are being used in electronic systems.
OUTCOMES: The students in this course will develop focus and depth in the filed of handling faults in all kinds of systems. The students are first expected to learn the techniques for handling faults in electronic systems. Then, the students are expected to learn handling faults in other systems in engineering, science, biology, etc. Finally, the students are expected to explore the methods of handling faults in systems associated with their field of study, and compare their explored methods with those that are being used in electronic systems. In addition to the above, the assignments in this course are established to improve the effective communication skills of students.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be required to prepare a short paper (and a related presentation) on a topic related to the theme of the course which is going to be chosen in consultation with the instructor. The students are expected to do the following tasks throughout the quarter: (1) select the research topic, (2) do the proposed research, (3) prepare a presentation, and (4) write a final paper (5 to 7 pages). The overall workload in this course is expected to be 1 to 2 hours of out-of-class work per week.
GRADING: The course score will be based on the quality of the student work and computed as follows: a 10-minute oral presentation to the class (50%) and a final written research report (50%). The final course grade will be reported as pass/no pass, the grade of pass is awarded to students that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Hussain Al-Asaad is a professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering. His research interests include design verification, testing, and fault tolerant computing. He has published over 55 papers in archival journals and refereed conference/workshop proceedings. He teaches undergraduate and graduate courses on digital systems design, testing, and verification. Al-Asaad is a recipient of the National Science Foundation CAREER Award and the UC-Davis academic senate distinguished undergraduate teaching award.

TITLE: High Performance Driving
FOCUS: Auto racing is one of the most popular sports in the world. Whether it's a local autocross or Formula 1, the driving controls are the same: steering wheel, throttle, brake, gearbox. While simple in principle, the act of high performance driving is deceptively complex. In this course, students will learn the theory and practice of high performance driving. Students will learn from both historical and modern texts, from videos of drivers of varying skill levels, and from telemetry data. We will explore the truths, misconceptions, and compromises that make motor racing such a difficult and rewarding sport.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn the mathematical and physical foundations underlying auto racing from the theoretical ideal racing line to the unusual properties of rubber tires. Students will learn how to examine videos to diagnose common driving errors. Students will learn how one records and reads telemetry data to understand what the driver and vehicle are doing at every instant. By the end of the course, students will be able to debunk many common driving myths based on their sound theoretical knowledge and practical experience. The educational outcomes will include (1) developing effective communication skills from both a driver and pit perspective, (2) developing high cognitive skills in assessing driver and vehicle performance, (3) developing focus and depth in the interdisciplinary fields associated with high performance driving, and (4) preparing for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: Each week there will be a short written assignment where students will be given a few driving puzzles to solve. The puzzles will contain text and/or video and/or telemetry that describe a racing activity that didn't go as planned (e.g a spin). Solving the puzzle will require integrating information across disciplines. For example, diagnosing whether a spin is due to the car or driver requires inspection of weight distribution, yaw, and change in steering angle. Each student will write a concise report describing what happened and why. In the following session, the class will start by discussing the previous problem. Towards the end of the course, students will also create their own puzzles for other students to solve. It is expected that the weekly assignments will take 2 hours of out-of-class work.
GRADING: Students will be graded on a 100 point scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F). 90% of the points will come from written assignments and 10% from class participation (defined as coming to class and being present for discussion).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology Department who specializes in bioinformatics. When he's not developing novel sequence analysis algorithms he likes to be at the race track where he may be driving or coaching (but not spectating). He has written a popular entry level book for racers, "You Suck at Racing: a crash course for the novice driver", and has a blog with over 500 posts about high performance driving.

TITLE: How We Learn Language
FOCUS:
The most amazing thing that humans ever learn is how to communicate through language.

This seminar delves into how we learn language. We will cover topics like parenting and infant language skills, bilingualism, deafness, language disorders, and language deprivation. We will watch movies (like CODA), shows (like the docuseries Babies), listen to podcasts (like RadioLab), and read news and research articles to support the learning of these topics. We will also look for how social media reports about topics in language. Students will get the chance to learn about topics that aren't typically covered in other psychology courses while building science communication skills.
OUTCOMES: The objectives of this course are to 1) Achieve a greater amount of depth in an area of developmental psychology, 2) Develop science communication and writing skills, and 3) promote critical thinking and consumption of psychological science.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Before class: Students will read, watch, or listen to materials and post their thoughts on a Canvas discussion board where they can also interact with their fellow classmates. Students may also be asked to find articles and other content on their own to share with the class. 

Short assignments may include choosing a popular kids show like Miss Rachel and finding research articles to explain why that particular show is or is not supportive of a specific area of language development. 

The course will culminate in a final project where students will create public-facing content such as a short video, podcast, infographic, etc. aimed towards a specific audience (e.g. new parents, educators, clinicians etc) addressing a topic related to diversity in language development. Students will start with identifying a common issue or misconception that is present in the world (e.g. Will raising my child bilingual cause language delays?) and work to create a piece of engaging content rooted in science to address that question.
GRADING:
Participation in class (40%)

Discussion board and Persuall engagement (25%)

Assignments (20%)

Final project (15%)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Professor Graf Estes is a faculty member in the Psychology Department. She teaches Cognitive Development (PSC141), Developmental Psychology (PSC140), and graduate student courses about Language Development. She is the director of the Language Learning Lab at the Center for Mind and Brain. She studies how young children learn language and how their environments support that learning.

Jenna DiStefano is a Ph.D. candidate in Psychology. Her research investigates early language and cognitive development in deaf and hearing children.

TITLE: Hummingbird Ecology
FOCUS: Are you curious about hummingbirds? Would you like to know more about ecology research, but don’t know where to start? In this First Year Seminar you will explore the complexities around hummingbird ecology. Hummingbirds present a unique combination of an extremely high life costs and numerous adaptations to efficiently fuel these demands. We will study their remarkable cognitive abilities, territoriality, aggressive behaviors, courtship and physiology as well as their role as pollinators. We will combine lectures, discussions, analysis of visual materials and visits to the UC Davis Hummingbird Garden.
OUTCOMES:
Upon completion of this course, learners should be able to:

- Have an in-depth perspective of the hummingbird ecology from different subdiscipline perspectives.

- Perform effective field observations.

- Communicate scientific ideas in a discussion based on background information.

 Thus, through a mix between theoretical lectures, discussions and hands-on activities, the main objective of this course is to help students to develop critical thinking tools that allow them to communicate scientific information particularly about hummingbird ecology.   

Specific goals:

  1. Students will actively discuss ideas about hummingbird ecology and how hummingbirds are affected by abiotic (i.e., ambient temperature, precipitations) and biotic conditions (i.e., competition/territory defense, flower abundance, vegetation).
  2. Students will practice collaboration, iteration, creativity, and failure, through the tasks/assignments associated with the course. Students will acquire a more realistic view of what is ecology research.
  3. Students will report gains in their understanding of the process of research. Specifically, students will become familiar with the scientific method through the process of reading about hummingbird ecology research. Through observation-discussion cycles, students will learn how to observe and record hummingbird behavior.
  4. Students will believe more strongly in their capability to do research, by developing skills that allow them to observe, discuss and present scientific information.
ASSIGNMENTS: During five weeks, students will attend lectures on hummingbird biology. Readings and a reading guide will be assigned in these lectures (5-10 pages/week). Grades will be based on participation and analysis. Free-living hummingbird observations will be carried out in one to two classes around the UC Davis Hummingbird Garden to illustrate the materials reviewed in class. Grades will be based on field reports. Based on the scientific literature reviewed as homework and during lectures, students will collaborate to present scientific literature in the format of a poster for a scientific meeting, this is a 10-minute PowerPoint group presentation.
GRADING:
Class Participation & Attendance                     10%

Scientific paper analysis (5, 10 points each)     30%

Field Reports (1, 20 points)                               30%

Scientific Poster (30 points)                               30%

Total Points 100                                                  100%

 

This is a Pass/No Pass class. Conversion to letter grade is not possible.

Important: A student needs to achieve at least a C- (70%) in order to receive a passing grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
-Paulina Gonzalez

I was trained as an ecologist in my natal Chile. I arrived at UC Davis as a postdoc in ecophysiology in 2010, and I am now a Lecturer and a research ecologist at the UCD Museum of Wildlife and Fish Biology. In this role I teach a range of courses, and I enjoy hands-on teaching. I also do research in avian ecophysiology (stress) and behavior, especially in hummingbirds. Outside of class, I love birdwatching, making jewelry, and cooking with my friends.
