TITLE: Hummingbirds--Conservation of an Urban Pollinating Avian Species
FOCUS: Pollinators encompass a diverse group of animals that provide important ecosystem services. As examples, pollinators include bees, butterflies, bats and birds. Students interested in urban wildlife and human interactions would benefit from this Freshman Seminar. Students who have some biology or ecology background or interest would benefit most from this seminar. This seminar will explore the hummingbird species of California, what ecosystem services hummingbirds provide, the important factors that help hummingbirds be successful and produce offspring, and how hummingbirds are being studied in science. The goal of this course is to provide students with an overview of the amazing nature of hummingbirds and to enlighten them about the challenges that hummingbirds are facing in an ever changing world
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the different facets of hummingbird natural history and conservation. The goals of this seminar are to (1) cultivate ethics, responsibility, and respect, for the animals that we share planet Earth with and (2) to develop a global perspective for ecosystem health. This seminar is an opportunity to gain a more in-depth understanding about how hummingbirds play a role in ecosystem health and how we can help to protect their populations in the future.
ASSIGNMENTS: For the five sessions, students will be assigned to read 2-3 journal articles per session. Journal articles will focus on avian research techniques, hummingbird biology, health, habitat, migration, or conservation. These topics will then be discussed during the seminar sessions so that students can share their views about what they learned from the assigned reading and how that changed or added to their current perceptions. During the quarter, students will be expected to write a 5 page paper (double spaced, with at least 6 references) on a topic related to hummingbird biology, conservation, health, habitat restoration etc. Students will also be expected to give a 5 minute presentation at the end of class. Writing the paper and giving a presentation is an opportunity for students to experience translatable skills of critically reviewing scientific literature and present it in an educational form. This seminar will meet for a total of five sessions. Each session will be 1 hr 40 min in duration. The class will be held the first five Thursdays of the quarter.
GRADING: This is a pass/no pass course. A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. Course grading will be based on a written 5-page paper (35%), verbal presentation during the last session (35%), and quality of participation in class sessions (30%).  Students must attend 4 of 5 sessions (unless further absences are excused) to receive a passing grade.  Missed sessions must be viewed via recordings.   Class participation will entail each student coming class with questions and comments relative to assigned manuscripts for the seminar session and active participation in the discussion sessions.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Tell is a member of the faculty in the School of Veterinary Medicine. Her research interests include treatment of avian disease, avoidance of drug residues in poultry products consumed by humans, and avian health relative to conservation of bird populations. Dr. Tell obtained her Bachelor of Science from UC Davis, did her residency at the National Zoo, then returned to UC Davis as a faculty member. She is boarded with the American College of Zoological Medicine and the American Board of Veterinary Practitioners (Avian Practice). Dr. Tell is currently the director of the UC Davis Hummingbird Health and Conservation Program and holds a federal permit for banding hummingbirds.

TITLE: Imagination Practices in Community Engaged Research and Artmaking
FOCUS:
In this seminar, we will explore and engage the theories and practices of community engaged research in the arts, humanities, and design. This seminar is led by staff at Imagining America: Artists and Scholars in Public Life (IA), a national consortium based at UC Davis that brings together scholars, artists, designers, humanists, and organizers to imagine, study, and enact a more just and liberatory ‘America’ and world. The seminar will draw from IA’s national work to share examples of projects that strengthen and promote public scholarship, cultural organizing, and campus change to inspire collective imagination, knowledge-making, and civic action on pressing public issues.

 

This is a participatory seminar where students will actively engage with one another, with guest speakers, and with the three instructors of the course. Activities will include group readings and discussions on theories of public scholarship, guest speakers including community and university leaders engaged in interdisciplinary research, collective artmaking projects addressing social issues, and interactive group work with one another. Students will have the unique opportunity to curate or design creative engagement projects that will be part of the 2026 IA National Gathering (a 450+ person immersive three day event), taking place in Davis, CA October 23-25, 2026.
OUTCOMES:
Collaborate on creative projects that will be featured in the IA national conference in Davis

Learn introductory concepts in public scholarship and community engagement in the arts, humanities, and design 

Discover the challenges and opportunities in community research via national case studies

Explore students’ own histories, strength, challenges, and personal-professional hopes and dreams as engaged citizens in the world

Connect and learn directly with community leaders, researchers, and artists carrying out participatory research and artmaking projects

Collaborate on creative projects that will be featured in the IA national conference in Davis
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will participate in class discussions and reflection activities on key readings and learnings (15% in-class participation)

Students will process and reflect on examples of community engaged research and artmaking from national and regional leaders (30% midterm exam)

Students will collaborate on a creative final project informed by seminar learnings and in connection with the theme for the 2026 IA National Gathering (25% proposal; 30% final project)
GRADING:
30% of grade - Final creative engagement project 

25% of grade - Creative concept for final project

30% of grade - Midterm exam and reflection on community engagement practices in the arts, humanities, and design 

15% of grade - in-class activities and participation 

Grading scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Erica Kohl-Arenas, Ph.D. is an Associate Professor in American Studies at the University of California, Davis and the Faculty Director of Imagining America: Artists and Scholars in Public Life. She is a scholar of cultural organizing, social movements, freedom struggles, and the politics of institutionalization, professionalization, and private philanthropy. Kohl-Arenas’ research has focused primarily on the radical imaginations and deferred dreams of social movements that become entangled with the politics of institutionalization and funding. 

 

Stephanie Maroney, Ph.D. is the Managing Director of Imagining America. She has years of experience in arts and humanities administration and interdisciplinary learning environments. Stephanie has taught courses in public humanities scholarship, theories and methods in community-based research, role-playing games and society, arts and cultures of fungi, radical storywork and Inuit food security, technology and society, and critical nutrition studies.


Anuj Vaidya, Ph.D. is the Communications Director for Imagining America. He brings over two decades of experience in media and community-engaged art practice, arts education, and public scholarship. Anuj has experience with 3rd i Films, the oldest South Asian media arts outfit in the US, and his own eco-media practice pays due attention to the material, social, and intellectual impacts of our storytelling and media technologies.

TITLE: Inclusion and Exclusion in Food Cultures
FOCUS: Food is a major way through which we experience community, learn about culture, and connect with each other. At the same time, we also judge people based on what they eat and food gatherings can be used as an excuse to overlook deeper structural divisions. This course will enable students to explore how food has shaped their identity and understanding of the world. Students will learn about the role of food in culture and share their own food traditions. We will also explore the role of nutrition and how moralism gets tied up with what someone does (or doesn't) eat. The value of this course comes from teaching students how the simple act of eating can be a microcosm of the complex world we inhabit. We will explore how to cultivate joy and connection in sharing a meal, while also acknowledging the hardship, history, and conflict that food can represent. After this class, students will never look at food the same way again.
OUTCOMES: Students will leave this class with greater knowledge of food cultures, respect for foodways different than their own, able to communicate more effectively the relationship between specific foods and sociocultural/political realities, greater critical thinking skills for analysis of foodways, an introductory understanding of food studies, enhanced speaking skills from introducing a dish from their culture to the class, and a lifelong appreciation for the complexity of the food we put in our bodies.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Read and write a short response to three 5-10 page articles on foodways.

Watch and write a short response to four half-hour podcasts or short videos on foodways.

Prepare a dish to share with the class representing their food traditions. If you do not have access to a kitchen or needed cooking materials, the instructor can provide you with access. Funds will be available to purchase ingredients and needed items. 

Deliver a ten-minute presentation on their food tradition attending to its cultural meaning, their lived experience, and its material makeup.
GRADING:
Responses to articles/podcasts/videos: 50%

Food preparation and sharing: 20%

Presentation on your food culture: 30%

Grading scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Sarah Rebolloso McCullough, PhD runs the UC Davis Feminist Research Institute. She seeks to transform research culture by bridging movement work with academia. She is PI on two NSF studies that integrates justice-oriented frameworks from feminist/critical race science studies into STEM graduate education. She researches mobility justice, environmental justice, and community-university relations. McCullough applies her expertise in ethnographic methods, cultural analysis, and power dynamics to create community-driven research partnerships that span the disciplines. She earned her PhD in Cultural Studies with an emphasis on Feminist Theory at UC Davis.

TITLE: Introduction to Buddhist Meditation
FOCUS: This seminar serves as an introduction to Buddhist meditation. College is a time of exhilarating change and challenges, but with change and challenges come stress; research and experience have established that meditation can reduce stress. In this seminar, we will practice meditation and discuss short weekly readings on Buddhist philosophy from several traditions that discuss how to work with our mind/body to improve our concentration and to help cultivate patience, inner calm, and kindness to ourselves and others. The seminar’s primary goal will be to work towards establishing a regular meditation practice throughout your time as a UC Davis student and beyond.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students will develop effective communication skills by participating in weekly discussions in a small, supportive classroom community.
  2. Students will learn some core principles of Buddhist philosophy and how these can be applied to daily living.
  3. Students will be able to incorporate daily meditation practice into their lives, learning strategies for conflict resolution and focusing their mind/body.
ASSIGNMENTS: Meditation journal, to include brief weekly reading responses; one 15-minute presentation, solo or with a partner; final written reflection (2-3 pp.).
GRADING:
In-class meditation activity (25%)

Oral contributions to weekly discussions based on readings and daily meditation (25%)

Weekly Reading Responses (20%)

Discussion leader presentation (10%)

Meditation Journal + Final Written Reflection (20%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Greg Miller teaches for the The Writing Center and has been an imperfect practitioner of Buddhism for several decades.

TITLE: Introduction to Buddhist Meditation
FOCUS: In this seminar, the focus will be on establishing a regular meditation practice to support your life as a student. We will work on building community and supporting each other in developing a regular meditation practice. In addition, we will explore introductory Buddhist teachings on meditation, with a focus on Zen Buddhism. Most importantly, learning to make meditation a regular part of your life can help you cope with stressors and support your academic life at the university.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students will develop effective communication skills by participating in weekly discussions in a small, supportive environments.
  2. Students will be able to incorporate a short daily meditation practice into their lives and learn strategies for calming and focusing their minds and bodies.
  3. Students will understand how the basic principles of Buddhist philosophy can be applied to our daily lives.
ASSIGNMENTS:
• Weekly reading responses and meditation journal (200 words x 10).
  • Final 2-page reflection on your meditation experience this quarter, what you learned about yourself and/or about meditating and/or about Buddhism, and your future meditation goals
GRADING:
Grading will be holistically assessed using a grading contract for P/NP (C- or better). As long as you thoughtfully complete each assignment according to the stated criteria, you receive a “complete” for that assignment.

  • Weekly reading responses (50%)
  • Oral contributions to weekly discussions based on readings and daily meditation (40%)
  • Final Written Reflection (10%)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Lisa Sperber teaches professional writing in the University Writing Program. She first started practicing meditation as a college student, and in the past six years has become dedicated to a regular Zen meditation practice.

TITLE: Introduction to Stem Cell Research
FOCUS: This introductory course provides an overview of stem cells and stem cell research. Students will be introduced to fundamental concepts in stem cell biology and explore different types of stem cells, including pluripotent and adult stem cells. The course will briefly cover experimental methods commonly used in stem cell research, as well as applications of stem cells, and stem cell–derived products in therapeutic development and drug screening. This course aims to build a foundational understanding of stem cells by offering insight into the fields of stem cell research and regenerative medicine. Guest speakers working in stem cell–related fields will be invited to share their perspectives on careers in stem cell research.
OUTCOMES: Please note after considering the elements defining a CURE seminar, the instructors have decided to change this proposed FYS to a classic seminar. In this classic FYS, stem cell biology and experimental methods in stem cell research will be covered in the lectures. By the end of the quarter, the students are expected to be able to define the properties of stem cells, name the types of stem cells and applications for the stem cells, and understand the common experimental methods used in stem cell research.
ASSIGNMENTS: The students are expected to read published research and review papers. One class presentation will given by each group of students (pending class size) in a journal club-style presentation on the selected research paper. Four written assignments summarizing the reading material will require the students to identify 1) what is known on the topic, 2) what is the proposed hypothesis, and 3) what new findings have been discovered in the scientific paper.
GRADING: The students will receive a letter grade for the class based on class participation (20%), four written assignments (40%), and a class presentation (40%). The letter grade will be assigned according to standard policy: A+ to A- for 100-90, B+ to B- for 89-80, C+ to C- for 79-70, D+ to D- for 69-60, and F for <60.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Deborah Lieu, Ph.D. is an associate professor in the Institute for Regenerative Cures with over 19 years of research experience in human pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes. Her lab focuses on the mechanisms underlying the differentiation and maturation of human induced pluripotent stem cell (hiPSCs) into pacemaking cardiomyocytes for engineering biopacemakers and contractile cardiomyocytes for cell replacement therapy after a heart attack. 

Ping Zhou, Ph.D. is an associate adjunct professor and the Director of Stem Cell Core in the Institute for Regenerative Cures. Her research focuses on stem cells with special emphasis on pluripotent stem cells, mesenchymal stem cells and hematopoietic stem cells to create innovative cell therapies for liver disease, vascular diseases and hemophilia.

TITLE: Join the Maker Revolution
FOCUS: Have you ever dreamed of building a robot? Or using a 3D printer to bring an idea to life? A manufacturing revolution through the "maker culture" of do-it-yourself technology has been taking place in the last decade. The ability to prototype small objects using a 3D printer and to use small, inexpensive computers makes it possible for everyone to design, build, and control small machines, such as a simple robot or a garden watering system. We will discuss the tools that make this possible and learn to use some of them. Students will learn some simple electronics and computer programming in order to use an Arduino microcontroller to get input from sensors (switches, thermometers, etc.) and control output to devices (LEDs, motors, etc.). They can also learn how to design an object that can be fabricated on a 3D printer. Students will work together in groups on a final project that incorporates these devices.
OUTCOMES: Students will develop high cognitive skills and prepare for lifelong learning by applying the scientific method to determine the project design. Students will learn elementary dc and ac electronics and simple programming in C in order to connect simple circuits to the Arduino microcontroller. (No background in either circuits or programming will be assumed.) This will allow the Arduino to acquire input signals from sensors like pushbuttons and to control analog outputs like lights. They will use the Arduino to control a piezoelectric buzzer and make music. If they want to use the 3D printer to make parts, they will use computer-aided design (CAD) software to design a 3D object. They will use these skills to build individual or group final projects of their choice, given limitations in materials available in the laboratory. Examples of possible projects are a robot with wheels and sensors, a controllable lamp with varying colors, an alarm system, a temperature controller, a computer game, and a water irrigation system. Students will work to develop effective communication skills through the oral presentation of the final project. They will also develop their writing skills by writing a final paper that describes the project in detail.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will read about the Arduino microcontroller and write small programs weekly. In the first half of the course, they will work in pairs during class time to wire up simple circuits to the Arduino and then test their programs on the hardware in the lab. They can use free 3D design software to design an object and build it on the lab's 3D printer. In groups of 1 to 4 people, students will design and construct a final project. The concept of the project must be discussed with the professor for assessment of feasibility that will depend on the availability of components in the lab or additional parts that can be purchased quickly and inexpensively. In the last half of the course, students will work on designs outside of class and then work together to build the final projects during class. At the final class, each group will present its project to the class and explain the design and building process. Each student will write an individual final report of about 5 double-spaced pages explaining the details of the project. The total work for the class will be 6 hours/week, including the 2-hour class time.
GRADING:
Students will be evaluated on their participation in the programming and circuit lab exercises (1/4), on the quality of the final project and presentation (1/2), and on their final paper of approximately 5 double-spaced pages (1/4).

Percentages required to earn specific letter grades: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Shirley Chiang is an experimental condensed matter physicist who specializes in surface physics studies using high resolution microscopy techniques. She received her Ph.D. degree from U.C. Berkeley and was a Research Staff Member at the IBM Almaden Research Center before coming to U.C. Davis in 1994. She won the UC Davis Distinguished Teaching Award in 2001. Her current research interests include the surface structures of metals on semiconductors. She enjoys building things, including computers, machines, and experimental apparatus.

TITLE: Language, Cognition, and Crossword Puzzles
FOCUS: What is a four-letter word ending in "T" that answers the cue "many a nanny"? is it "BRIT" or "GOAT"? The enjoyment these kinds of puzzles provide are rooted in some linguistic properties of the words used in the cues and the answers: "nanny" is polysemous, meaning either a female caregiver (a "babysitter" in American English), or a female goat. By examining the relationships between cues and answers in crossword puzzles, students will learn about the terms and concepts used in linguistic analysis. Semantic terms like synonym and antonym, the logical structure of definitions, grammatical terms like prefix and suffix, or even more complex ideas about the probability distributions of word and letter combinations, all of these can be used to understand the knowledge that puzzlers need to have access to when solving (or creating) a crossword puzzle). In this course, students will engage with puzzle solving as a way to gain insights into their own cognitive processes, and the different types of cultural, linguistic, and literacy skills that they must deploy to perform the task. The crossword puzzle has an interesting history by itself, which will be reviewed to provide context for their puzzle solving explorations.
OUTCOMES: Students will be expected to learn different technical terms and concepts in linguistics, and to be able to apply them to describe the types of cues used in crossword puzzles. The cues will be used as a source of data that will require analysis, so students will develop their analytical skills. At the same time, because the object of analysis is a particular type of expression (a definition, often cryptic), developing awareness of the rhetorical "tricks" that puzzlers use will enhance the students' literacy and communicative skills. Part of the work will consist in examining the process of solving the puzzle through introspection, which would help in the development of metacognitive awareness.
ASSIGNMENTS: This seminar is a hands-on puzzle solving course. The target is to solve at least one puzzle from the New York Times each week. All students will try to solve the same puzzle before each meeting, keeping a detailed diary of the mental processes they use to understand the cues and find the answers. During class, the whole group will work on the answers collectively, and the instructor will direct the students to research the specific technical linguistic terms that may help them develop explicit analyses of the intuitive issues they bring up. Solving the puzzle and writing the entries should not take more than two hours a week. At the end of the course, students will turn in a short essay (5 pages) where they relate a linguistic concept to a number of cues instantiating the same linguistic problem.
GRADING: A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. Grading will be based on participation, submission of weekly journal entries, and final paper.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Raúl Aranovich is a theoretical linguist working on the interfaces between syntax, morphology, and semantics. His research focuses on grammatical mismatches between these levels. Professor Aranovich specializes in the grammars of Spanish and other Romance languages, but also Austronesian and Bantu languages. He employs empirical methods using natural language processing and corpus linguistics tools, which recently have lead him to work on ontologies and language graph representations for linguistics. More recently, he has turned his attention to the history of linguistics, trying to develop a contextualized epistemology of the discipline. He earned a Ph.D. in linguistics from UC San Diego in 1996, under the direction of Profs. S.-Y. Kuroda and John Moore. He has been at UC Davis since 2001, and is currently full professor. He held faculty positions at the Ohio State University and the University of Texas in San Antonio before joining UC Davis.

TITLE: Learning and Memory in Neurologic Disease
FOCUS: As scientists in a neurological surgery lab, our interest involves how brain disease and injury result in behavioral dysfunctions like learning disorders. We then use our understanding of that biology to try to identify and test new treatment strategies to restore the brain to a healthier state. Why does a single insult to the brain, such as an epileptic seizure or traumatic brain injury, have such a significant impact on learning and memory? Are cells dying resulting in deficits? Is it possible that deficits result from changes to brain network function? Could it be a bit of both? Through continual research of different neurological diseases including epilepsy, Alzheimer's disease, traumatic brain injury, schizophrenia etc., it is clear that there is a dynamic relationship between brain injury and cognitive function where many patients, regardless of disease, experience some level of impairment. However, as the mechanism and location of disease within the brain may change, the scope (either severity or type) of learning disabilities also varies. Our research aims to answer questions related to changes in brain function (at the neuronal and circuit level) with the goal of finding a target to reduce cognitive deficits associated with brain injury; focusing specifically on electrical stimulation as a therapy. In this course, students will learn about various brain disorders and discuss how they alter learning and memory. By utilizing articles published in popular science journals that summarize basic science findings, students will gain insight into current research methodology and learn about the intricate relationship between brain injury and cognition.
OUTCOMES: In this course, students will develop the tools to analyze and interpret scientific articles and will also practice sharing ideas with their peers by way of discussions and presentations. This course will also serve as an introduction to neurological disorders of the brain and discuss the fundamentals of neuroscience, including learning and memory.
ASSIGNMENTS: The seminar will meet for one hour each week spanning the 10-week quarter. The first meeting will include an introduction to and discussion of some basic ideas related to cognitive neuroscience and different brain disorders. Reading (5-15 pages; ~30 min) assignments will consist of scientific journals and popular science articles (i.e. journals, publications, etc) and will be assigned for each week’s topic. All students will be expected to read these papers and submit a short summary of the reading each week via presentation slides (6-8 slides; ~30 min). In addition, each week 1 or 2 students will  present their slides to the class (~15 min) and lead the discussion about the assigned reading. Each student will present only one time. I will provide a brief overview at the end of the class that will be relevant to the assignments.
GRADING: Pass/no-pass will be assessed based on class participation (40%), quality of presentation (30%) and quality of the weekly summaries (30%). At UC Davis a passing grade is considered a C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ali Izadi, PhD, is an Assistant Professional Researcher and member of the Department of Neurological Surgery. Dr. Izadi’s research is concentrated on developing novel treatment strategies (such as electrical stimulation) using adult and juvenile rodent models to treat neurologic disorders such as epilepsy and traumatic brain injury. On a larger scale, he is interested in evaluating similarities and differences in the biological mechanisms of cognitive dysfunction across multiple neurologic diseases. Fundamentally, the goal of his research is to explore mechanisms and identify innovative therapies in the laboratory and to ultimately improve quality of life for patients with neurologic disorders. He has taught this class since 2019.

TITLE: Legal Hot Topics
FOCUS: Are you thinking about going to law school? Or do you have an interest in legal issues? In this seminar we will explore current legal issues that are in the news or pending before the U.S. Supreme Court. Possible topics include free speech, equal protection, the free exercise of religion, and voting rights.
OUTCOMES: Through written and in-class discussion, seminar participants will strengthen their communication skills by discussing complex issues and their ability to converse about issues with people who see things differently from them. Reading complex articles and cases will strengthen participants' reading skills.
ASSIGNMENTS: We will explore seven legal topics. The work involves doing assigned reading of several pages each week, posting a several-paragraph-long response to a weekly question on a discussion board, and discussing issues in class.
GRADING: This class is graded P/NP. Each written assignment will receive a grade of P or NP. Because each assignment is graded P/NP, all assignments are equally weighted. If a student receives a grade of NP on an assignment, they will be invited to revise and resubmit that assignment. Students must receive a P on each assignment to pass the class.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Lisa Klotz, a former practicing lawyer, teaches UWP writing courses through the Writing Center.

TITLE: Locksport: Picking locks for fun
FOCUS: Have you ever wanted to learn how to pick a lock? Take this course, and you'll be able to pick locks. But not any locks. We only pick locks (a) that are not in use and (b) that we have the expressed permission to pick. This is just for fun: we never pick locks in the "real world". Students will learn the various ways locks are constructed and how simple tools can be used to defeat them. Students will practice lock picking first-hand and work their way up the Lockpickers United Belt System to record their expertise. Students will gain an appreciation for the fragility of security through obscurity, the laws and ethics governing lockpicking, and how the locks in their lives keep them safe (or not). At the end of the class, students will be able to make more informed choices when it comes to their own security (and astound their friends and family with their new skills).
OUTCOMES:
1. Roughly half of every class period will be spent in small group work where students have to communicate with each other to work through problems.
  2. We discuss the mathematics of security: for example, the number of pins and the depth of cuts in pin tumbler locks determine the number of possible keys.
  3. Locksport practitioners are very concerned about the unethical side of their sport and are therefore vigilant to ensure their sport remains legal.
  4. Locksport challenges both the mind and body. One cannot see inside a lock, so one must visualize what is happening through a theoretical understanding of lock design. One must also be able to put that theory into practice by feeling and interacting with tiny movements and sounds.
  5. This class isn't designed to develop leadership skills.
  6. We discuss the legality of lock picking around the world. For example, owning lock picks is a felony in Japan.
  7. We introduce the LPU Belt system, which associates student skill with a colored belt (like martial arts). Students are expected to go from White to Yellow to Orange Belt during the course, and will be positioned to continue to higher levels after the course.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class has a partially inverted structure: students watch videos remotely (about 30-60 min per week), come to class for a brief lecture/discussion (20 min), then work in small groups to develop their lock picking skills (30 min). Each week, students will answer a few low-stakes questions online that test and reinforce their knowledge.
GRADING:
Students will be graded on the typical 100 point scale that is used in Canvas.

  • 70% of the grade will be based on weekly quizzes administered through Canvas
  • 15% of the grade will be based on lock picking skill (belt level)
  • 15% of the grade will be based on group activities (judged by participation in class)
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Korf is a Professor in the Department of Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology who specializes in bioinformatics and genomics. Locksport is one of his many hobbies.

TITLE: Love, Sex and Gender in Premodern Europe
FOCUS: We study ideas about love, sex and gender as they impacted lived experience from late antiquity through the Age of Enlightenment in the late eighteenth century. In the Middle Ages a couple could consummate a clandestine marriage by having sex, without the participation of clergy. Clandestine marriages were suppressed in the sixteenth century, as state and church attempted to impose greater control over the sexuality of their subjects. Sex out of wedlock, prostitution, and same-sex relationships, widely tolerated in late antiquity and the Middle Ages, became criminalized in the early modern period. We explore how and why these changes occurred and how they intersected with religion, class, ethnicity, religion and class. Why did early modern people believe that Jewish men menstruated? Why was sex with Satan a necessary legal element of the crime of witchcraft? Why were Renaissance medical textbooks full of examples of women spontaneously turning into men, but a man turning into a woman was considered medically impossible? When and why did the ideal of "romantic love" develop How was the emergence of a modern “gay” identity in the late eighteenth century related to the development of the modern heterosexual male, which happened at the same time? These are some of the topics we explore through a selection of primary source documents and art.
OUTCOMES:
1. communication skills: this seminar is discussion-based. Students will discuss assigned readings. We also view and analyze relevant artistic representations in class. 

2. Cognitive skills: we will be working through a selection of challenging texts.

3. this seminar will introduce students to historical method, analyzing and contextualizing primary sources.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Aproximately 25 pages reading per week. Informed class discussion of assigned readings. 

One 2-3 page precis on on assigned reading.  

Two students work together to identify, analyze and explain a relevant visual representation, such as Ruben's "The Rape of Ganymede", or Bernini's "Ecstasy of Saint Teresa", or Bruegel's "The Battle of Carnival and Lent." The two students give a joint 10 minute PowerPoint presentation on chosen artwork in class.
GRADING:
50 % : informed, substantive participation in class discussions of assigned readings. 

25 %: power point presentation on chosen art work. 

25% : reading precis.
INSTRUCTORBIO: I am a historian of early modern Europe, i.e. from the Renaissance to the French Revolution. I specialize in the history of crime and deviance. My most recent book "Suicide by Proxy in Early Modern Germany" reconstructs a peculiar early modern type of homicide. My signature classes are "Crime and Punishment in Early Modern Europe," and "The History of Witchcraft and Magic." I am a passionate booster of Education Abroad, and teach a UC Davis summer program in Vienna every August. I am the advisor of the History Club. Learn more about me here: https://lettersandsciencemag.ucdavis.edu/news-noteworthy/students-honor-history-professor-who-connects-todays-conspiracies-humanitys-dark

TITLE: Metal Micronutrients in Metabolic Diseases: a Course in Data-Driven Biomarker Discovery
FOCUS: Did you know that metals are an essential part of your health? Metals like copper, iron, and zinc are key components to your diet, yet they are present in low amounts and our bodies need to find specific ways to control them. It is important for us to understand the role that these metals play in our body and how they might go haywire in diseases. In this computer-based Course-based Undergraduate Research Experience (CURE), students will discover connections between the chemistry of metals, biochemistry of proteins, and how this might impact human health and nutrition. Students will work to discover metal-based biomarkers in diseases related to nutrition, metabolism, and obesity through experimentally-derived datasets. While the course does not include wet-lab experience, the course may utilize data acquired from wet-lab experiments. The seminar will offer students the ability to analyze large datasets, draw correlations across multiple scientific disciplines, and gain the opportunity to contribute to problems associated with metabolic and hormone disorders. The seminar has a long-term goal for students to contribute to open-access digital community resources (CoRes) in biochemistry and nutritional biology that will impact data analysis and interpretation in biomedical research. Community college transfer students are encouraged to enroll
OUTCOMES:
Research Process: Students will develop critical research abilities with respect to engaging with and performing computational analytical methods on large-scale biological databases and scientific literature to discover metal-protein connections in metabolic diseases  

Discovery: Working with real experimental data from the instructor's lab and real human data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) databases, students will identify novel metal-based biomarkers that have not been previously characterized, making authentic scientific discoveries.

Broad Relevance: The seminar connects student work to broader impact through contributions to open-access digital community resources like NHANES dartabases in biochemistry and nutritional biology that will inform future biomedical research.

Collaboration: Teams will collaboratively develop biological networks, with multiple groups integrating their findings into comprehensive maps linking clinical data with protein interactions.

Iteration: Students will engage in iterative learning cycles, first analyzing assigned literature networks and previously-analyzed data , then applying those skills to team projects, and finally synthesizing discoveries into the culminating project.

By course end, students will:

  • Navigate and analyze biological databases confidently
  • Read and interpret scientific literature across chemistry, biochemistry, and nutrition
  • Build and analyze biological networks
  • Communicate scientific findings through written reports and oral presentations
  • Understand how metal micronutrients influence human health and disease
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will complete both individual and collaborative assignments demonstrating mastery of research literature analysis, biological network construction, and scientific communication.

Reading: ~15 pages every two weeks of scientific literature and tutorials on bioinorganic chemistry, metal micronutrients, and network biology (total: ~75 pages over 10 weeks).

Individual Work:

  • Pre-course reflection (Week 1, <1 page)
  • Literature worksheet analyzing assigned scientific paper (Week 2, 2-3 pages)

Group Work: Students work in teams of 3-4 throughout the quarter:

Iteration Project (Weeks 3-5):

  • Group presentation on assigned literature (Week 3, 10 minutes)
  • Biological network construction using databases/tools (Weeks 4-5)
  • Written report summarizing network analysis and comparison to literature (1-2 pages)
  • Oral presentation of findings (10 minutes in teams)

Final Collaborative Project (Weeks 6-10):

  • Analysis of wet-lab data and construction of metal-protein biological networks
  • Integration of multiple team datasets into comprehensive clinical-biological maps
  • Written report in journal style including introduction, methods, results, discussion, and proposed validation experiments (2-3 pages)
  • Final oral presentation synthesizing discoveries (15 minutes in teams)

Class Participation: Live synchronous attendance required (1 hour/week). Students engage in whole-class discussions and breakout room teamwork during each session.

Out-of-class time: 1-2 hours/week for reading, database work, team meetings, and writing.
GRADING:
The seminar  will be graded as P(100-70%) / NP(69-0%) and it will not be curved. Participation in class discussion will be graded individually whereas the other elements will receive team grades.

  1. Participation in class discussions (10%)
  2. Participation and contribution to team projects (20%)
  3. Iteration project (20% total)
    1. Written report (10%)
    2. Oral presentation (10%)
  4. Final project (50% total)
    1. Data analysis and/or network map (20%)
    2. Written report (15%)
    3. Oral presentation (15%)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Prof. Marie Heffern graduated from the University of Southern California with a B.S. degree in Chemistry, a B.A. degree in Religion, and a minor in Ancient Religions and Classical Languages. She completed a Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Northwestern University. Her research interest focuses on understanding the roles metals play in health, and specifically, how metals in our diets can inform and redefine therapies and nutritional strategies related to metabolic diseases, specifically those related to obesity. 

Dr. Jennifer Park earned her B.S. in Chemistry from The Ohio State University and completed her Ph.D. in Chemistry at The Pennsylvania State University. Her current research focuses on elucidating the roles of metals in hormone biology, particularly how oxytocin with metals influence diseases, including cancer and neurodegenerative disorders.

TITLE: Modern Marvel: Bridges Around the World
FOCUS: This seminar course aims to: 1) introduce different types of modern bridges and their aesthetics; 2) give a quick peek on bridge disasters in the history; 3) provide a tool's-eye view of just how a mega-bridge like San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge gets built; and 4) provide hands-on design experience via participation in a web-based bridge design contest. Case studies on many famous bridges around the world will also be included.
OUTCOMES: The students will learn about different types of bridges in the world and how they are typically constructed during this Freshman Seminar course. Basic concept such as bridges, structures, design and engineering will be introduced through formal lectures and case studies. The students will also have the opportunity to apply the knowledge in designing their own bridge and compete among their classmates towards the end of the quarter.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly reading materials and an oral presentation on a selected case study. The course grading is Pass/No Pass. Students will be required to participate in the in-class discussions. An individual project will be assigned where the student is required to prepare for one oral presentation on a bridge case study chosen from the list provided by the instructor (bridges other than the ones provided in the list need to be approved by the instructor upon consultation). The project and presentation will be evaluated on three different categories: (1) Content (e.g., covering important aspects such as construction, design, history); (2) Organization (e.g., well structured, offering summary); and (3) Delivery (e.g., effective use of visual tools such as pictures and videos, clearness). The students are also required to participate in a web-based bridge design contest to gain fun hands-on design experience.
GRADING: The course grade will be based on: in-class discussion (20%), preparation on the case study topic (20%), peer evaluation on the student’s project (10%), web-based bridge design contest (20%), and the instructor’s evaluation on the student’s presentation and design (30%). Class attendance (no less than 80%) is mandatory.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Dawn Cheng is a member of the faculty in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering. Her research interests include combined field and laboratory experimental investigation and analytical/computational simulations; design and analysis of bridge components and systems; application of Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites to innovative infrastructures and renewal; design, analysis and durability mechanics of FRP composites; and engineered materials using recycle wastes.

TITLE: Navigating a Genome – Exploring what makes us uniquely human
FOCUS:
The human genome project released the first draft-sequenced genome in 2001 and, more recently, a complete "telomere-to-telomere" genome is now available. These revolutionary accomplishments have ushered in a genomics era. But what exactly is a genome? And how can we use it to better understand human traits and improve health? In this course, students will explore these questions while contributing to real research in the Dennis lab, investigating what makes us uniquely human.

This is a hands-on research course combining computational and wet lab techniques. Students will work collaboratively in teams to learn essential research skills including pipetting, performing molecular biology experiments (PCR, gel electrophoresis, qPCR), navigating genome browsers, interpreting scientific papers, analyzing and displaying genomic data, and presenting research findings. The course format includes 2 hours of in-class activities per week plus outside work on readings, assignments, and projects.

This class is designed specifically for first-year students (freshman and transfer) with no previous biology coursework or lab experience. Students from all majors are welcome—no science background required!

By taking this course, students will gain practical research experience, develop technical and analytical skills highly valued across STEM fields, learn to work collaboratively on real scientific problems, and build confidence in their ability to contribute to research. Students will experience the full research process—from asking questions and designing experiments to analyzing data and communicating results—while discovering whether research might be part of their future path.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this course, students should be able to:

(A) Apply research methods and tools to investigate genomic questions, including performing molecular biology techniques, navigating a genome browser, performing analysis and displaying genomic data using R, and using databases to perform literature review and characterize gene functions.

(B) Explore and uncover new knowledge by addressing the age-old question "what makes us uniquely human?" through analysis of genomic data and experimental results.

(C) Contribute to ongoing real-life research in the Dennis lab that is broadly relevant to the academic community and advances our understanding of the human genome.

(D) Work effectively in teams with fellow classmates and instructors to complete research tasks, share findings, and support collective learning. Experiments will be performed in groups, with final projects completed in these same groups.

(E) Engage in the cyclical nature of research by refining protocols, building upon previous results, and learning from failures as part of the scientific process. For example, students will learn how to use a human assembly to design primers, which will subsequently be used to perform PCR, with the optimal PCR primers employed in qPCR. Gene copy-numbers estimated from qPCR will be verified using orthogonal sequence-data approaches.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Weekly class workload will include two hours of in-class exercises and, on average, four hours of outside work that includes reading papers, preparing presentations, writing, and performing assignments. Students will be assembled into teams and collaborate with fellow students and instructors.

  1. Intro to genome assembly
  2. Practical use of a genome assembly and primer design
  3. Intro to wet lab (practice micropipetting) and how to read a scientific paper
  4. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) wet lab
  5. Databases and intro to scientific writing
  6. Gel electrophoresis & qPCR
  7. Gene expression & R tutorial
  8. qPCR data analysis & copy number
  9. Example science talk and prepare scientific presentations
  10. Scientific presentations of results

Note: Weeks 4 and 6 include buffer time for protocol refinement and iteration in weeks 5 and 7, respectively.

Term Projects

Term projects will comprise two major assignments due at the end of the term completed in an an assigned group:

  1. Written report (approximately 5 pages): A literature review and summary of research findings that students will build on iteratively throughout the quarter.
  2. Oral presentation: Final research results presented to the class in a 10 min group presentation.
GRADING:
This course uses typical UC Davis letter grading (A+ through F).

Grade Breakdown:

  • 25% Pre-class weekly assignments (reading papers, preparation activities)
  • 25% Post-class weekly assignments (genetic/genomic and molecular biology lab work from in-class assignments/activities; including weekly findings in written report)
  • 10% Written report (literature review and research findings)
  • 20% Oral presentation (final research results)
  • 20% Attendance and participation (active engagement in class activities and discussions)

Letter Grade Scale: Percentages required to earn specific letter grades: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Megan Dennis is an Associate Professor of Medicine in the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Medicine, affiliated with both the UC Davis Genome Center and MIND Institute. Her research group focuses on the genetics of human brain traits, conditions, and evolution. Dr. Dennis is a first-gen college grad who began her research career washing dishes in a microbiology lab >20 years ago. After earning her bachelor's degree in Biochemical Engineering at Michigan State University, a PhD in Human Genetics at Oxford University, and completing a postdoctoral fellowship at the University of Washington, she now leads a research group at UC Davis. Dr. Dennis is excited to show new undergrads how fun research can be and that anyone can become a scientist.

Sean McGinty is a PhD candidate in the Integrative Genetics and Genomics graduate group at the UC Davis Genome Center. After earning his bachelor's degree in Genetics and Genomics from UC Davis, he joined Dr. Dennis' lab. He researches structural variants impacting expression of human-specific genes. Using sequencing data and computational methods, he seeks to understand how this understudied variation contributes to traits and diseases across primates. He looks forward to introducing first-year students to genomics.

TITLE: Navigating Challenges as a Woman in Engineering
FOCUS:
An important tone-setter for most college students is the first year or two years of their undergraduate experience. Negative experiences, such as the lack of community, during this vulnerable period may adversely affect women's sense of belonging in engineering and lead to potential dropout. Nationally, women have dropped out of engineering at a higher rate than men, despite having equal or superior math and physics scores coming out of high school. This seminar was formed to address this issue, better prepare you for the academic environment you are about to enter and possibly gain additional allies to tackle this problem with us.

This course aims to provide a brainstorming and discussion platform for the following topics in the seminar:

  1. Discussion through academic and professional publication: status of women in engineering and the associated climate; how women engineers negotiate with their gender and professional identities
  2. Case studies: how to navigate isolating behavior in academic setting (case study 1) and professional setting (case study 2)
  3. Learn from professional women engineers in both academia and industry through a Women in Engineering panel
  4. See yourself as an engineer through tours of the Engineering Student Design Center and a 3D printing workshop
  5. Gain allies in engineering through a free lunch with students, staff, and faculty in Biomedical Engineering.

The instructor recommends this course to:

  1. Women who have declared an engineering major, intend to transfer into an engineering major, and/or would like to work in an engineering company upon graduation or in the future.
  2. Allies who would like to learn about the experiences of women in engineering.

The instructors are housed in Biomedical Engineering (BME), so some of the examples used in this course may be BME-specific but will apply to all engineering disciplines.
OUTCOMES:
Upon completion of this seminar, we hope for you to:

  1. Develop high cognitive skills. You will discover your own engineering identity that incorporates your gender identity. You will also explore ways to improve the current environment experienced by women in engineering.
  2. Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy. You will learn about the challenges women engineers face in the current world. You will arm yourselves with respect and empathy in gender equity.
  3. Prepare for lifelong learning. We hope to provide a platform and build a community of allies for women in engineering.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students are expected to actively engage in class discussions involving individual brainstorming and group discussions about the reading assigned for that week. Discussions will be held in groups of 3-4. Weekly assignments include a reflection (approximately 250 words) on a reading that ranges from 6 to 20 pages. In this course, longer readings tend to be industrial or academic reports (thus consuming less time per page) and shorter readings tend to be academic papers (thus consuming more time per page). On average, each student is expected to spend two hours on reading and reflection.

Each student is expected to attend at least one Women+ lunch hosted by the biomedical engineering (BME) department during the quarter and write a 100-word reflection on their experience at the event.

At the end of the course, each student will generate a two-page, single-spaced reflection on the contents covered in the course.
GRADING: This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or higher. 30% of the grade is based on weekly discussions and participation; 50% of the grade is based on the weekly reflections; 5% of the grade is based on the Women+ lunch reflections; 15% of the grade is based on the final reflection.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Tiffany Chan (she/her) is a current M.S. student in Biomedical Engineering. She is a member of the Cube3 lab (@cube3ucd on Instagram), starting her research journey as an undergraduate student in the lab. She is one of the original designers of the first-year seminar with Dr. Wang and have served as a peer mentor of this course for 4 times.

Dr. Xianglong Wang (he/him) is an Assistant Professor of Teaching in Biomedical Engineering (BME). Dr. Wang leads the Cube3 Lab, which is dedicated to improving the sense of belonging and quality of life in engineering students with a focus on BME students.

This FYS will be supported by an undergraduate peer mentor through the Aggie Connection program. Ahva Razavian (they/them) is a 4th-year undergraduate student in the Cube3 lab and will be a peer mentor for this course a fourth time.

TITLE: PEAR: Promoting Equal Access to Research in the Life Sciences
FOCUS: Are you a Life Science student who is interested in getting involved in research? This course will help you identify and effectively apply for opportunities and plan for your future in science. Each week we will explore different parts of the research process, focusing throughout on training you to find and get involved in internships, research lab work, and other external form of research (e.g., summer programs, study abroad opportunities, etc.). We will also practice valuable professional skills, including developing and honing your specific scientific interests, effectively contacting prospective professor and/or graduate student mentors, creating a CV, and planning for potential career paths after college.
OUTCOMES: This seminar is particularly focused on helping students to develop effective communication skills, to develop focus and depth in their life science discipline of interest, and to start on the process of gaining non-course practical research or internship experience in preparation for future careers. By the end of this course, students should have a more focused idea of their interests in life science, and knowledge of all the research opportunities available to them as an undergraduate (both inside and outside of UC Davis). Students should also develop more effective communication skills, and they will practice fundamental scientific writing skills (cold emailing, applications, proposals etc.) and a tentative plan for their future in science.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Every week students will have a written assignment that will be due the day before seminar. These written assignments do not necessarily have a correct answer and are meant primarily for students to engage with the topic discussed that week. These assignments vary from critiquing reach-out emails to exploring external funding opportunities, culminating in a final proposal appropriate for an undergraduate. This final proposal will be a 1-2 page paper with a research question, hypothesis, methods, and budget. The proposal must be on a shortened timeline appropriate for an undergraduate (< 4 years) and consider possible limiting factors like handling permits, material costs, and fieldwork. Students will receive feedback on all assignments. Listed below are examples of the assignments:

Week 1: Reading response to Russel et al. 2007

Week 2: Drafting and critiquing reach-out letters to professors

Week 3: Proposal idea brainstorm

Week 4: Researching conferences

Week 5:  Drafting a CV and proposal outline feedback

Week 6: Planning for summer research: Identifying REUs, etc

Week 7: Finding a graduate program practice

Week 8: Identifying non-academic career paths

Week 9: Half-page response for each students' future framework

Week 10: Proposals due
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.

Students will submit eight weekly written assignments worth 15 points each (120 points total), and complete a final project worth 30 points. The written assignments are graded for completeness, with a substantive effort earning 15 pts if turned in on time, 10 pts if turned in within a week of the due date, and 5 pts if turned in before the end of the quarter. 

Students must attend at least 8 sessions to pass the course.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Ishana Shukla is a PhD candidate in Wildlife, Fish and Conservation Biology. She holds an MSc and has previously given workshops and taught courses on professional skills in STEM, field safety, and incorporating inclusive practices in a work setting. Her research primarily focuses on the coexistence between reintroduced predators, local communities, and existing wildlife. She has taught this proposed seminar three times as a course at UCSC and UCD, and seeing that students responded so positively last year, hopes to teach it again. She is interested in revealing the "hidden curriculum" for participating in research, which especially affects underrepresented minority students and those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. 

 

Rob Furrow is an Assistant Professor of Teaching in Wildlife, Fish and Conservation Biology. He teaches a wide range of STEM courses, including introductory biology, introductory statistics, data literacy, natural history, and behavioral ecology. He has performed research in topics including evolutionary modeling, human cultural evolution, bird vocalizations during migration, but he now focuses on education research about how students best learn introductory and quantitative material and how instructors can create effective, inclusive learning environments.  He is invested in helping all of his students identify and build skills for meaningful and exciting careers.

TITLE: Poisons and Medicines through the Ages
FOCUS: Throughout history, humankind has employed chemicals for both beneficial and harmful effects. Natural or synthetic chemical compounds, termed ‘xenobiotics’ are not normally found in humans, and can include both drugs and poisons. Both the study of poisons (toxicology) and the study of drugs and medicines (pharmacology) examine how xenobiotics interact with the organism. This seminar course will provide an overview of the principles, history, and applications of toxicology and pharmacology and give students tools to properly interpret scientific studies. This seminar is appropriate for students of all backgrounds, however the scientific level of the seminar is appropriate for students who have taken a general biology course. No prior knowledge of specific chemicals or medicines is required. Each week, a new topic relevant to either toxicology or pharmacology will be introduced. Primary and secondary scientific literature will be used to facilitate discussion, and students will be provided with tools to interpret scientific studies. This course is intended as an introductory-level overview, exploring both the history of various xenobiotics, as well as broaching medicine in the modern age. Discussion topics may include political poisons (Medici family), opioids, vaccines, penicillin, lead and arsenic.
OUTCOMES: The primary goal of this seminar is to provide an in depth exploration of a topic that is relevant to many, but is often not discussed in a traditional biology class.  Pharmacology and Toxicology are disciplines that are rarely pursued at the undergraduate level but for which there is abundant historical and current interest. After taking this seminar, students will be able to engage in conversations about therapeutics and toxins and will have context for understanding how these chemicals can impact human health. Students will have a basic understanding of how to read and extract critical information from primary scientific literature, developing effective communication skills and cognitive ability. Students will have opportunities to engage with their peers, will gain confidence participating in group discussions and will practice research and writing skills essential for lifelong learning.
ASSIGNMENTS: This seminar will meet for one hour each week. The first sessions of the quarter will introduce basic principles of pharmacology and toxicology and will consist of both lectures and hands-on activities. In subsequent sessions, a different chemical of interest (medicine or toxin) will be selected each week and discussed within the context of discovery and use within a historical context. Students are expected to read one scientific paper/review article and/or the posted powerpoint and syllabus links each week and come prepared with discussion questions for each class. This will require between 6 and 20 pages of out of class reading. Participation in weekly discussions and activities is required. Students must prepare a brief written 1-3 page summary of the history, uses and biological effects of a chemical compound of their choice using 3 additional cited literature sources and prepare a small oral presentation on the topic.
GRADING: The grading system is Pass/No Pass- a grade of 70% or better is required.  NO final examination. Weekly attendance is expected. Students will be expected to read provided material ahead of class to participate in the discussion. Participation in weekly discussions (25% asynchronous group discussion using Perusall or Canvas Discussion Threads where students both post a question and respond to one; 25% in person discussion), a short oral presentation (20%), and a brief written summary of a drug or toxin related topic using 3 additional sources from the literature (30%) will be the basis of the grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Laura Van Winkle is a Professor in the School of Veterinary Medicine and teaches in the Pharmacology and Toxicology Graduate Group at UC Davis. Her research focuses on lung health and disease with an emphasis on toxicants that impact early life as well as aging. She is also board certified in general toxicology and has worked in the biotechnology industry in the area of pre-clinical pharmacology and drug development.

Samuel Arriola is a 2nd year PhD student in the Pharmacology and Toxicology graduate group at UC Davis. As an undergraduate, Sam received his BS in Biology from Oregon State University, where he became interested in the field of toxicology working in a microplastics ecotoxicology lab. Now as a graduate student, Sam studies the chemical chloropicrin and is focusing on elucidating its mechanism of toxicity and how the lung recovers from injury.

TITLE: Reading LGBTQIA Memoir
FOCUS:
In this course we’ll be reading and discussing personal essays and memoirs–some graphic, some solely text based–written by LGBTQIA authors. Reading memoirs and personal essays offers an exceptional opportunity to step temporarily into another person’s experiences and point of view, try on their way of thinking, and understand the impact that circumstances beyond our own experiences can have on individual lives. In other words, reading memoir can help us to develop empathy toward others.

The reading selections for the course represent a wide range of experiences, orientations, and identities and may include the following: 

  • Lamya H. Hijab Butch Blues
  • Maia Kobabe, Gender Queer
  • Saeed Jones, How We Fight For Our Lives
  • Alison Bechdel, Fun Home: A Family Tragicomic
  • Carmen Maria Machado, In the Dreamhouse

Regardless of whether you are a part of LGBTQIA community or not, you will likely find aspects of the readings that you identify with and others that challenge you. While discussing our responses to the readings, we will seek to create a safe community in our classroom where we practice respect and openness toward one another. The overall aims of the course are to gain a greater understanding of LGBTQIA experience and to develop a stance of empathy toward others that we will carry with us beyond the classroom. 

The activities you will be completing in this course include:

  • Completing the assigned readings
  • Keeping a weekly reading journal
  • Participating in class discussions
  • Joining a “book club” with 2-3 other students. Each group will choose a book from a provided list to read together.
  • Giving a group presentation on your club’s selection
  • Submitting an end-of-quarter reflection on your course learning (2-3 pages)
OUTCOMES:
The primary learning objective of this course is to “cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy.” The assigned readings will give students an understanding of the wide spectrum of LGBTQIA experiences and perspectives. Prompts for the reading journal and the organization of the classroom discussions will also present students with opportunities to achieve this learning objective. For example, instead of analyzing the readings as one might in a literature course, students will be asked to reflect on their immediate and personal responses to the readings, consider which aspects of the readings resonate (or not) with them and to reflect on why, as well as to respond respectfully to their classmates’ perspectives. Students will learn what it means to take a stance of empathy toward others, and they will be able to differentiate between empathy and sympathy, as well as acceptance and tolerance. 

In addition to meeting the primary learning objective, students will also develop their communication skills–from finding ways to address sensitive subjects in informal discussions, to preparing to give a more formal presentation with their book group at the end of the quarter.
ASSIGNMENTS:
This will be a 2-unit p/np course. Students will be expected to complete the following to pass the course. 

  • Complete the assigned readings. (~2.5 hours/week) Reading time will be managed by assigning some shorter personal essays, some graphic memoirs, excerpts, and 2 text-based memoirs
  • Keep a weekly reading journal. (~.5 hours/week) Students will write a ~250 word reflection on each week’s reading.
  • Participate in class discussions. Class participation will be measured by writing and submitting a discussion question at the beginning of each class, as well as verbal contributions to full and small group discussions.  
  • Participate in a “book club” with 2-3 other students.(~1 hour/week)  Each group will choose a book from a provided list to read together.
  • Give a group presentation on the book club selection.
  • Submit an end-of-quarter course reflection (2-3 pages) reflecting on their course learning.

Students will demonstrate that they are processing the values related to the learning objectives in their weekly journal entries and the final course reflection. The prompts for both of these activities will directly ask students to reflect on this development.
GRADING:
This is a pass/no-pass course. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better. 

Grade Distribution

Weekly reading journal: 20%

Book club presentation: 20%

End-of-quarter course reflection: 30%

Participation in class: 15%

Participation in book club: 15%
INSTRUCTORBIO: Melissa Bender (she | her) wrote her doctoral dissertation on U.S. memoirs and completed her Ph.D. in English at UC Davis in 2009. She is now a Continuing Lecturer at the University Writing Center. She teaches upper-division courses in professional writing as well as a course on visual rhetoric. Her research and writing interests focus on writing pedagogy, visual rhetoric, and media/cultural representations of queer families. She identifies as a queer, cisgender woman.

TITLE: Rhythms and Greens: Understanding How Plants Tell Time
FOCUS: Are you interested in biology? Do you want to get started with genetic and molecular lab work? If so, this might be the course for you! This FYS CURE will explore molecular mechanisms underlying plant circadian rhythms. While you probably know that you have a circadian clock that influences things like when you wake up and when you get hungry, you may not realize that plants have circadian clocks as well. In plants, circadian clocks control key processes like gene expression, photosynthetic capacity, and pathogen resistance. While many circadian clock genes have been identified in plants, we still don’t know how they all function to generate the highly accurate and reproducible 24-hour rhythms that enhance plant fitness. One powerful method to identify genes that act together in a process is enhancer/suppressor forward genetic screens. In this course, you will carry out such a screen to identify new genes that act together with a mysterious factor, XAP5 CIRCADIAN TIMEKEEPER (XCT), to ensure the circadian clock maintains a 24-hour pace. Together, we will determine if putative mutants really do alter the ability of plants to keep accurate time. You’ll learn molecular methods such PCR, gel electrophoresis, and DNA extraction and use a bioluminescent reporter to measure circadian rhythms in the model plant Arabidopsis thaliana. In the process, you’ll learn basic wet-lab techniques, some biology, and bring technique and theory together to help us understand how plants can tell time.
OUTCOMES:
We expect that once you have successfully completed this course that you will be able to:

  • Display basic competence with key industry- and academically-relevant tools and techniques of molecular biology such as PCR and plant phenotyping
  • Generate and analyze plots of quantitative data
  • Successfully use genetics as a tool to dissect molecular pathways
  • Be able to describe the general molecular mechanisms that generate circadian rhythms and how they promote plant fitness
  • Determine whether putative mutants affect the pace of the circadian clock in Arabidopsis thaliana
  • Contribute results to an ongoing, federally-funded research project in the Harmer Lab (https://harmerlab.ucdavis.edu)
  • Collaborate with classmates to collect data and present your findings to the class
  • Use the scientific method to generate reliable data, including appropriate replication and troubleshooting of experiments as needed
  • Determine if you want to become a scientist!
ASSIGNMENTS:
1. Short pre-lab videos or reading that will prime students for that week’s activities.
  2. In-lab worksheets and reports where students will develop their experimental plans, collect data, and interpret their results.
  3. Analysis and display of data using software tools such as ImageJ, BioDare and Excel. 
  4. Active discussions with classmates and teaching team throughout the quarter.
  5. Oral presentation of final results.
