INSTRUCTORBIO:
Stacey Harmer: I’m a chronobiologist and professor in the Department of Plant Biology. I received my BA in Biochemistry at UC Berkeley and my PhD in Biochemistry at UC San Francisco. I switched from immunology to plant biology when I started my post-doc at the Scripps Research Institute. My lab uses genetic, biochemical, and functional genomics approaches to investigate fundamental mechanisms underlying the plant circadian clock and its role in the coordination of plant physiology. At UC Davis, I teach undergraduate courses in genetics (MCB 160L) and molecular biology (MCB 121). 

Sam De Riseis: I’m a current 3rd year PhD Candidate in Dr. Stacey Harmer’s Lab and I study the Arabidopsis thaliana circadian clock. I’m interested in understanding how the conserved eukaryotic factor, XAP5 CIRCADIAN TIMEKEEPER (XCT), mechanistically slows down clock pace. I’m also interested in pedagogy and developing effective teaching methods for STEM subjects. So far, I’ve successfully mentored three undergraduate students and a high school student in the research context and I’ve TA’d for MCB 160L and introductory biology courses both at UC Davis and UC Berkeley. In all contexts, I’ve led constructive discussions and provided support to students as they pursue their independent scientific inquiries.

TITLE: Salud publica
FOCUS: This first-year undergraduate seminar introduces students to the fundamentals of public health through a culturally and linguistically responsive curriculum taught entirely in Spanish. Designed for heritage speakers and advanced Spanish learners, the course fosters early academic engagement, bilingual health literacy, and a foundation in core public health concepts such as health equity, epidemiology, social determinants of health, and chronic disease prevention. Through case-based discussions and project-based learning, students will explore the intersection of public health and community realities, particularly within Latinx and Spanish-speaking populations.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

  1. Identify and explain key concepts in public health in Spanish.
  2. Discuss the impact of mental health, chronic disease, and social inequality on health outcomes.
  3. Analyze how social, political, and economic factors shape access to care and health disparities.
  4. Critically evaluate health information sources and understand the role of misinformation.
  5. Apply learned concepts in a final project focused on a health topic of their choice relevant to Spanish-speaking communities.
ASSIGNMENTS:
• Weekly Participation & Discussions (30%): Active engagement in Spanish, small group discussions, and class activities.
  • Short Reflection Assignments (20%): Written or verbal reflections responding to key class topics (2–3 total, in Spanish).
  • Final Project & Presentation (50%): A 15–20 minute group or individual presentation that explores a public health issue in depth (topic chosen by students, presented in Spanish).
GRADING: Each student will be expected to engage with weekly topics, participate actively in Spanish-language class discussions, and complete several short reflective assignments. Each student will also be asked to collaborate on a final public health project and give a 15–20 minute oral presentation in Spanish. Grading will be based on the short reflective assignments (30%), oral assignments (class participation and final presentation) (20%), and the final public health project (50%). Students will receive feedback on each assignment to support growth in both content understanding and Spanish language use.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Mónica Torreiro-Casal holds a Ph.D. in Counseling Psychology from Northeastern University, Boston, MA and a MFT (Marriage and Family therapist master’s degree) with Latinx mental health emphasis from Santa Clara University, CA. She is a Continuing Lecturer for the Chicana/o/x department where she teaches Latinx and Chicanx mental health and psychology classes.Her research, clinical and teaching interests include Latinx and Immigrant Community Mental Health, Feminist Chicanx Psychology and Minority Qualitative Research. She conducts research on immigration and mental health and mentors students on their research projects and academic journeys to contribute to their professional development.

Alfredo is a second-year medical student at the UC Davis School of Medicine and a graduate of the UC Davis, where they earned an MPH with a focus in Epidemiology. With a background in community-based research, clinical care, and health education, he is committed to expanding health equity for Spanish-speaking and undeserved populations. Prior to medical school, he led quality improvement and access-to-care initiatives at student-run clinics and conducted research on diabetes management and cancer survivorship in binational contexts. He is passionate about teaching and mentorship through influenced by his previous roles as a Teaching Assistant with the Department of Chicana/o Studies.

TITLE: Soccer Analytics
FOCUS:
This seminar will provide an introduction to soccer analytics without requiring a background in statistics or coding. The targeted student is a soccer or sports fan with an interest in sports analytics, who is not satisfied with general audience accounts, which generally do not provide specific guidelines on how to use data in order to analyze soccer games or bet on match outcomes. The goal of the seminar is to provide a “how to” manual aimed at bringing students up to speed on soccer analytics within ten weeks. Lectures are designed around particular skills such as 

  • downloading soccer data from the Web
  • predicting results of soccer games 
  • creating passing networks
  • performing statistical tests in a regression framework. 

Coding will be done in R, and in the course of the seminar students will learn to create fully functional R scripts they can then use for their own future analyses. The seminar will culminate with a project in which students use their acquired skills to analyze a new data set of their choosing, the results of which they will present to the class in the last two weeks of the quarter. While the focus of the seminar is on soccer data, the particular skill set students will develop in this seminar, transfer to general data analysis and will help them to develop quantitative reasoning.
OUTCOMES: Students will be introduced to data analysis and sports analytics, with soccer as the lead example. They will develop new cognitive and transferable skills based the statistical analysis of their own (and new) soccer data. Students will practice effective communication skills through written and oral presentation of the results of their analysis to the group.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Small, weekly homework assignments to reinforce the material covered in the lectures (about 2 hours per week for the first eight weeks). Homework solutions will be submitted individually at the beginning of the next seminar and students will receive feedback and are encouraged to iterate their solutions.

Active participation in class discussions (all ten weeks).

Students will give a 20-minute group presentation of a data analysis in week 9 or 10 (groups consist of two or three students depending on seminar size) and will compile a final report based on the presentation and feedback from instructor and students.

Students who are interested can voluntarily participate in watching international club soccer games (eg Champions League games) over lunch on Tuesdays.
GRADING:
The seminar is graded P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better according to the following breakdown

  • Homework: 40%
  • In-class participation: 20%
  • Presentation and write-up: 40%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Alexander Aue is a Professor in the Department of Statistics. His research is primarily in time series analysis, where he is interested in developing forecasting methodology for modern and complex data sets. He is a life-long and avid soccer fan and is enthusiastic about applying his professional skills to the beautiful game. 

Aue s a first-generation faculty member. He came to the US as a postdoc in 2004 after receiving a BS degree in Mathematics and a PhD in Applied Math from universities in Germany.

TITLE: Social Media Marketing with AI Agents
FOCUS:
We will design an integrated digital marketing strategy with current cutting-edge AI tools.

We start by learning about the social media business model, which is based on predictive analytics that guides recommender AI. To be able to compete in this data-driven world, the common solution, nowadays, consists of using AI (in the form of generative AI - think ChatGPT!) to generate campaigns for social media's AI: we fight (AI) fire with (AI) fire! The good news is that Large Language Models (LLMs), like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, DeepSeek, and Llama, all speak plain English, so no coding required!

We will research, explore, and discuss the different aspects required to build a successful social media strategy and use LLMs to catch the newest trends in a fast-changing environment.

After learning about prompt engineering for social media marketing campaigns, we explore the creation of marketing AI agents, and end with building entire teams of AI-multi-agent systems to help us in our campaign. The goal is to create and fine-tune postings for YouTube, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok, and other social media platforms to implement our social media strategy.

We measure its impact and discuss the lessons learned about what works and what doesn't work for our purposes.
OUTCOMES:
1. Research Process: Students are introduced to and researching appropriate social media marketing campaign methods and techniques.  
  2. Discovery: Students have the opportunity to make relevant discoveries in a fastly changing and very young field, that are previously unknown to both student and instructor and thus require both exploration and evidence-based reasoning.  
  3. Broadly Relevant Work: Students focus on learning to work with generative AI, which is of relevance beyond just the classroom setting, aiming at real-world marketing with these new tools. 
  4. Collaboration: Student collaboration and communication are emphasized and encouraged as critical research skills. 
  5. Iteration: Students learn the iterative nature of research and scholarship; new knowledge creation for social media channels builds on existing knowledge of traditional marketing techniques.
ASSIGNMENTS:
20% grade is attendance & participation. Being actively involved is important in applied research.

60% is the weekly hands-on lab assignments.

Session 1: The Social Media Business Model

Session 2: Explore different Large Language Models (LLMs)

Session 3: Personalization of LLMs: How to talk with an LLM?

Session 4: Contextualization of LLMs: Agentic Function calls

Session 5: Contextualization of LLMs: Workflow automation with AI Agents

Session 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration: AI teams for complex tasks

20% is for the final project, which consists of a series of real-world social media posts, elaborated with a multi-agent AI workflow. 

Session 7: Social Media Posts: first exploration

Sessions 8 - 10: Social Media Posts: final project
GRADING:
P/NP

20% grade is attendance & participation. 

60% is weekly hands-on labs. 

20% final project.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Martin Hilbert is Professor of Communication at the University of California, Davis, where he chairs the campus-wide emphasis on Computational Social Sciences. He studies the societal implications of digitalization in complex social systems. He holds doctorates in Economic and Social Sciences (2006), and in Communication (2012). His work is recognized in academia for the first study that assessed how much information there is in the world; in public policy for having designed the first digital action plan with the governments of Latin America and the Caribbean at the United Nations; and in the popular media for having alerted about the intervention of Cambridge Analytica in the campaign of Donald Trump a year before the scandal broke. Before he joined academia, he served as Economic Affairs Officer of the United Nations Secretariat for 15 years, where he provided technical assistance to more than 20 countries and dozens of publicly traded companies as digital strategist. His work has been published in the most recognized academic journals, such as Science, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Ecology and Evolution, and World Development, and regularly appears in popular magazines, including The Wall Street Journal, Washington Post, The Economist, NPR, BBC, among others. More: www.martinhilbert.net; https://www.youtube.com/@Prof.MartinHilbert

TITLE: Songs and Belonging: A South Asian Cultural Ensemble
FOCUS:
This course warmly invites students to explore South Asian culture through song, community, and shared experience. It is designed as a welcoming space where singing is not about perfection or performance, but about connection, curiosity, and joy. As an instructor who loves sharing South Asian culture, I guide students through songs that people sing in everyday life to express emotions, tell stories, and create a sense of belonging.

The course is experiential and participatory. Students will learn to sing together in a supportive environment, engaging with folk songs, ghazals, devotional music, and popular songs from South Asia and its diasporas. Alongside singing, we will talk about lyrics, language, cultural context, and the feelings these songs carry. No prior singing experience, musical training, or language background is required everyone’s voice is welcome.

By taking this course, students will develop cultural awareness, listening skills, and confidence in using their voice as a form of expression. Singing together helps build community, encourages reflection, and offers a meaningful way to experience culture from the inside. This course is for anyone who is curious, open, and interested in learning through shared cultural practice.
OUTCOMES: As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students will engage actively with South Asian musical traditions to explore culture, identity, and belonging. They will learn to communicate effectively through group singing and discussion, gaining confidence in using their voices in a supportive environment. Students will develop intercultural understanding by examining how songs convey history, emotion, and community across South Asia and its diasporas. Through listening, reflection, and participatory activities, they will think critically about the social and cultural roles of music and consider how art shapes lived experience. By connecting these practices to their own experiences of identity and community, students will establish foundations for lifelong learning, cultivating curiosity, empathy, and creative engagement. This seminar emphasizes experiential learning, encouraging students to discover culture through practice, reflection, and shared musical exploration.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to attend the rehearsals during class time, prepare the reading and pronunciation of songs, listen to links of the songs and of some of the music, read cultural handouts accompanying the songs they will be singing, and might submit a 1-2-page paper at the end of the class. The final group project is a low-key performance at the end of the quarter. Most of the project work will be done in class in preparation for the culminating performance at the end of the quarter. Students can practice the songs at home or in groups, but need to come prepared to class. This will involve listening to the music and the songs on their own for about one to two hours a week. Class participation is key to this course  Most pronunciation preparation will be done in class. Instructors will arrange for Middle Eastern music specialists to come in and demonstrate instruments and musical concepts to students in a practical manner. Students will be expected to do research and write one essay about one of the many presented subjects and/or about the visitors' presentations.
GRADING: Students are expected to attend all rehearsals, actively participate in group singing, and come prepared by practicing pronunciation and melodies of the songs. They will listen to recordings, review cultural handouts, and engage with the music outside class for 1 2 hours per week.Class participation is essential, as most rehearsal, pronunciation practice, and preparation for the final project a low-pressure group performance will happen . Guest South Asian music specialists will demonstrate instruments and musical concepts. Students will also write one 1–2 page reflective essay on a song, cultural context, or guest presentation, worth 20% of the final grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
I am a language instructor at the University of California, Davis, teaching Urdu and Hindi, and sharing South Asian culture through language, literature, and music. As a first-generation student, I understand the importance of belonging, mentorship, and discovering one’s voice in new environments. My own journey has shaped my commitment to creating inclusive and welcoming classrooms where all students feel empowered to participate and express themselves.

In my teaching and work on campus, I focus on experiential learning, helping students engage with poetry, song, and storytelling to explore identity, community, and cultural heritage. While I am not a trained singer, I love guiding students in singing, listening, and reflecting on South Asian musical traditions as a way to connect intellectually and emotionally with culture.

This seminar draws on my interests in cultural expression, intercultural learning, and community-building through music. Students will have the opportunity to experience South Asian culture hands-on, develop confidence in their voices, and reflect on their own sense of belonging all within a supportive, interactive, and joyful learning environment.

TITLE: Steampunk
FOCUS: Steampunk is a multimedia phenomenon that uses iconography of the Victorian nineteenth-century—bowler hats, fancy dresses, steam trains, brass goggles—as a springboard for science-fiction narrative and design. This course will examine steampunk as a subculture and as a critique of contemporary society. We will look at steampunk works of art and design (clothing, retro-fitted tools, video art) and contrast them to actual nineteenth-century works. We will discuss short examples of steampunk fiction and film, considering what parts of nineteenth-century culture are celebrated and what parts are critiqued or avoided altogether.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn how to consider a single cultural phenomenon from multiple perspectives (aesthetically, politically, historically, etc.) and explain that complexity clearly. Students will learn nineteenth-century history as we consider how actual historical occurrences and personages are utilized in steampunk art. Students will learn to analyze and contrast a variety of texts, including comic books, films, video games, and short fiction. It will stimulate students' interest in the history and literature of the nineteenth century. Students will also consider how design elements change the way we interact with the tools we use every day. The class will require synthesis of material and evaluation of multimedia texts, including two creative projects and multiple response papers; this will hone students' cognition skills and communication ability (1,2). We will also frequently discuss cultural appropriation in early steampunk, ways to avoid it, and the ongoing discussion in the steampunk community about responsible, empathic portrayals of race and gender that were not the norm during the 19th century (3).
ASSIGNMENTS: Reading will include one short novella (under 100 pages), approximately seven short stories (15-30 pages each), and one graphic novel (176 pages). Readings average approximately 40 pages per week. Four short (1 and 1/2 page long) response papers are required. Students will write responses to one of two steampunk films viewed, one graphic novel or novella, and any two out of approximately seven short stories. Due dates are determined by class session when material is covered. Two out-of-class, multimedia creative synthesis projects are included. One will be a redesign of a contemporary object in steampunk style, and the other will be a creation of a steampunk character for a novel, film, or video game. During different points of the quarter, groups of two or three will give one 10-minute presentation on selected topics such as the U.S> Civil War in steampunk, Victorian pickle jar collecting, Jules Verne's influence, steampunk and historical site tourism, and others. Students are expected to attend the class every week and discuss the material.
GRADING: Response essays (4) = 40%, Creative Projects (2) = 30%, Group Presentation = 10%, Class Discussion=20%. Creative projects will be graded based on thoughtful application of ideas we've discussed, not on purely artistic quality. These projects can be completed and submitted in students' choice of media: online video, computer slideshow, ink or crayon drawings, clipped images pasted to cardboard, and others. Group presentations will be graded based on content and depth of research, but not necessarily on proficiency in public speaking. Class discussion is based on frequency and quality of oral comments during class. Response essays (4) will involve reflection about the reading/viewing material, including one or two questions for class discussion. They will be graded on thoughtfulness, clarity, and demonstration of general understanding of material's content (no outside research is needed). All are graded on standard scale: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89 – 80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Nathaniel Williams is Continuing Lecturer in the University Writing Program. His book on nineteenth-century science fiction, Gears and God: Technocratic Fiction, Faith, and Empire in Mark Twain's America, was published in 2018 by University of Alabama Press. His articles have appeared in American Literature, Utopian Studies, and elsewhere; his short stories have appeared in Fantasy Magazine, Poor Mojo's Almanac(k), and elsewhere. His book chapters include "Science and Technology" for Mark Twain in Context (2020) and "American Edisonades" for The Cambridge History of Science Fiction (2019).

TITLE: Stoicism in Antiquity and Today
FOCUS: Stoicism is more popular today than ever before: everyone from influencers to best-selling authors is promoting this ancient philosophy as the way to happiness and fulfillment. But what is Stoicism and where did it come from? In this seminar, we will read widely in the works of the Roman Stoics (Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius) and consider selected modern adaptations of the Stoic philosophy they taught. We will ask what accounts for the enduring appeal of Stoicism and why so many people have embraced it in the twenty-first century. We will investigate the continuities and differences between ancient and modern Stoicism and consider what critical engagement with Stoicism can teach us about such enduring problems as learning from hardship, the role of the emotions in the good life, and the tension between the appeal of self-reliance and the bonds of community.
OUTCOMES:
Students in the course will develop a direct acquaintance with the writings of the ancient Stoics and will learn to think critically about contemporary appropriations and adaptations of the past. They will explore in depth why and how we seek to extract “wisdom” from the past, and they will consider the perils and promise of this effort. Most importantly, they will grapple with fundamental questions about how to live and gain a sophisticated understanding of the continuities and discontinuities in the ways people have sought to answer this question across time and across cultures.

Attainment of these outcomes will be assessed both through student class participation and through the five-page final paper. In evaluating the paper, the instructor will look for evidence that students have gained familiarity with the basic doctrines of Stoicism and thought deeply about the benefits and hazards of its contemporary revival. Students will be expected to discuss with knowledge and critical discernment the claims made in the present for the application of Stoic teaching to contemporary problems, individual and societal.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will read approximately thirty pages a week and will be expected to participate actively and thoughtfully in discussion. Our class discussions will be collective efforts to understand and consider the implications of the texts we are reading. The final grade will be based on class participation and on a five-page paper that offers a critical evaluation of an artifact of contemporary Stoicism in relation to the ancient texts on which that artifact purports to draw.
GRADING:
75%: Final paper (five pages)

25%: Class participation

Letter grades to be assigned as folllows:

100-90: A+ to A-

89-80: B+ to B-

79 to 70: C+ to C-

69 to 60: D+ to D-

Below 60: F
INSTRUCTORBIO: Carey Seal is Associate Professor of Classics. He teaches a wide range of course on ancient Greece and Rome. His work often has Roman Stoicism at its center, and he is the author of a book on Seneca's philosophical writings.

TITLE: Stories Across Time: Premodern worlds in rare texts
ROOM: Shields Archives and Special Collections Room 198
FOCUS: This course builds student awareness of global systems in the premodern world of roughly 1000 – 1700 CE through a multidisciplinary and dynamic hands-on exploration of books and rare texts. At the core will be an introduction to evocative objects and emerging technologies to explore how medieval and early modern people from cultures in Asia, the Americas, and Europe used different media—manuscripts, printed books, maps, travel narratives, and more—to make sense of a rapidly changing world. How, for example, did chocolate go from an unfamiliar New World drink to a popular luxury good across the world? How did the printing press encourage dissent and help launch an empire? How do you go from an armored Spanish conquistador to a naked slave of an Indigenous group to an itinerant powerful healer? Students will get the chance to encounter firsthand rare books held in UC Davis Archives and Special Collections as well viewing state of the art interactive digital texts. Individual case studies will be framed through recurring analytical themes on the circulation of knowledge, cross cultural encounter, empire, technologies, literacies and labor. Through lively discussions and interactive workshops led by faculty from the Medieval and Early Modern Studies Program, students will explore how a material history of books, book-making, and digital archives can illuminate how premodern societies engaged religion, art, and science to grapple with new economic networks, intellectual exchange, and the clash of empires in an emerging global community.
OUTCOMES:
In this seminar students will 1) develop a global perspective of early book technologies and knowledge mobilization, 2) develop an appreciation for the diversity of material culture in the premodern world, and 3) improve critical thinking skills to address the complexity of historic and contemporary global exchange.

Week 1: Introduction to material culture and the history of books (Tiffany Jo Werth and Christine Cheng), held in Special Collections 

Week 2: A Chinese Bestiary (Yuming He), held in Special Collections

Week 3:  Medieval Manuscripts  (Claire Waters), held in Special Collections 

Week 4: Cabeza de Vaca’s 1542 “Relación” (Andrés Reséndez) Digital Text 

  https://archive.org/details/larelacionquedio00nezc/page/n13/mode/2up

Week 5: English Travel Narratives and the Printing Press (Tiffany Jo Werth), held in Special Collections 

Week 6: Japanese Prints (David J. Gundry)

Week 7: Mughal Empire and Islamic Science (Lynna Dhanani), held in Special Collections

Week 8: "New World" Chocolate Treatises (Daniela Gutiérrez Flores), held in Special Collections 

Week 9: Hannah Woolley’s Queen-like Closet (1670) (Sal Nicolazzo), held in Special Collections

Week 10: Student pitch of proposed acquisitions
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will have short weekly readings that will prepare them for discussing the evocative book or text they will examine each week. These will be determined by the individual instructors and shared via PDF or other electronic source via a shared Canvas site.

Each week they will generate 2 questions to prepare for each class discussion and write an informal journal reflection of approximately 200 words each after viewing a text.

Students will receive a tutorial on handling rare materials from Christine Cheng at our initial meeting and faculty at subsequent meetings will reinforce and support students in proper care for rare materials and the use of digital texts. 

As a final assignment, each student will independently find and choose a book or text as an “evocative object” that they recommend our Archives and Special Collections to acquire and will write a one page pitch as to why this book would contribute to UC Davis’s Special Collections.
GRADING:
Grading will be pass / no pass dependent upon the completion of class assignments.

Readings: Students will be assigned short critical companion pieces to the various books and texts assigned by individual professors. These will be determined by the faculty member and circulated in digital format via class LMS.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Instructor Biographies

Tiffany Jo Werth is Professor of English and Program Director for Medieval and Early Modern Studies.  She enjoys introducing students to the weird and wonderful artifacts and facts of the Renaissance.

Lynna Dhanani is Assistant Professor of Religious Studies. She studies Jainism, Sanskrit, and Prakrit language and literature as well as medieval Indian alchemical traditions, yoga and tantra.  

Yuming He is Associate Professor of Chinese. Her recent work is focused on the history of the book, theater, and epistemological processes in early modern China. 

David J. Gundry is an Associate Professor of Japanese whose research focuses on the literature of 17th-century Japan. 

Daniela Gutiérrez Flores is Assistant Professor of Spanish. Her work focuses on the cultural history of food and cooking in early modern Spain and colonial Latin America.

Sal Nicolazzo is Associate Professor of English. Their research focuses on the literature and material culture of the British Empire in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Andrés Reséndez is a professor of History. His books include A Land So Strange about Cabeza de Vaca’s adventures in North America.

Claire Waters is Professor of English. Her research focuses on medieval religious literature and culture in English, French, and Latin.

TITLE: Strategies for Academic Success
FOCUS:
Academic success in college requires different skills than high school, such as time management and motivation. This first-year seminar will teach you strategies for successful learning. This course will help you set goals that align with your core values, develop quarterly and weekly plans to achieve your goals, and connect you to resources for achieving your personal and academic goals. This course emphasizes reflective learning, and assignments will follow that format. The overall goal is to help you develop lifelong skills and stay motivated this quarter. 

Topics include: writing SMART goals, developing a quarterly plan and weekly plans that you actually follow, the importance of self-care, imposter syndrome, reflecting and refocusing your goals and plans, motivation, studying and note-taking tips, preparing for exams, and celebrating your successes.

The format will include interactive lectures with small group discussions. Each week students will complete homework (e.g., brief reading or video) and write a reflection to keep them on track for achieving their goals this quarter. 

While students from all majors are welcome, the instructor's background is in engineering, so most of her experience and examples come from STEM fields. The instructor recommends this course for students who:

  • Want to improve their time management skills;
  • Have never heard of SMART goals;
  • Struggle to stay motivated (or suspect they may struggle to stay motivated) courses;
  • Sometimes feel like an impostor in academic settings (e.g., feel like everyone knows more than you); OR
  • Are interested based on the course description.
OUTCOMES:
The educational outcomes of this course are to:

  1. Develop leadership skills including goal setting, task prioritization, time management, motivation, and practicing self-care. Goals and time management skills will provide the basis of the course and most lessons will be building on the initial lessons taught through students making a quarter plan and weekly plans. For self-care, we rely on the eight dimensions of wellness model.
  2. Develop cognitive skills. Students will learn the higher cognitive skills necessary to continually observe, evaluate, and develop their own personal strategies for effective online learning. They will decide why a strategy is or is not working.
  3. Prepare for lifelong learning. The ability to develop and implement plans for success will be important for students after they leave UC Davis and enter the professional workforce.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students are expected to actively participate during class activities including readings and reflection, group brainstorming, and class discussion. Assignments outside of class will include reading (about 10 pages / 20 minutes weekly), videos (20-30 minutes weekly), and weekly reflections and responses to these materials (60 minutes weekly). Templates for several assignments will be provided.  Reflections should be typed (∼250 words), and assignments will be submitted online. Students will prepare and share updates and assignment products each week.
GRADING:
This seminar is a pass/no-pass course. A passing grade will be awarded for a course grade of 80% or higher. This is a higher threshold than standard P/NP classes, but is achievable by students who attend class and thoughtfully complete their reflective homeworks.

Students will be evaluated on in-class individual and group participation and discussion (35%), and weekly reflections and assignments from readings and videos (65%).
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Susan Gentry is an Associate Professor of Teaching in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering. She is interested in supporting students to be successful in college classes. She first taught this online seminar in Spring 2020 and found that the class was mutually beneficial for both her and the students as they discussed wellness and time management strategies. She is looking forward to collaborating with a new group of students in this journey during the upcoming quarter.

In addition to her professional activities, Prof. Gentry enjoys baking, hiking, and running.

TITLE: Sufism: The Art and Science of Self-Knowledge
FOCUS:
What is Sufism?
Sufism is a 1400-year-old discipline that teaches the individual to explore and discover their true self. Sufism teaches that each individual, underneath their social, cultural, and physical roles, and their definitions and labels of self, has a unique and stable identity. Who are you, really, beneath all the coverings of family, community, and society?

What is Tamarkoz®?
Tamarkoz, unique to MTO Shahmaghsoudi®, is the Sufi art and science of self-knowledge through concentration and meditation. As a meditative practice, Tamarkoz involves deep breathing, mind and body relaxation, slow meditative movements (Movazeneh®), visualization, energy work, and heart meditation. The main difference between Tamarkoz and other meditative traditions is its emphasis on the heart. In Sufism, the heart is considered to be the gateway between our physical and spiritual realms and the source of our true knowledge and wisdom.

Anyone can practice and benefit from Tamarkoz, since Sufism has no cultural, religious, or racial boundaries, and has no physical prerequisites. Tamarkoz grants physical, mental and spiritual benefits, providing a healthier, more balanced body, a powerful way to deal with stress, and an opportunity to explore the mysteries of the heart and discover the treasures within yourself.

This seminar includes both lectures on Sufism and the practice of Tamarkoz. The lectures provide context for the Tamarkoz practice. Tamarkoz students have reported that they are better able to enjoy, appreciate, and benefit from Tamarkoz when they have an understanding of the principles, history, and philosophy of Sufism, and of the role Tamarkoz plays in achieving the aim of self-knowledge. In the practice portion, you will be guided through mind relaxation exercises; deep breathing exercises; Movazeneh (slow movement meditation); deep relaxation; visualization; and heart concentration, the hallmark of Tamarkoz.
OUTCOMES:
-To foster self-reflection and self-exploration
-To learn the principles of Sufism
-To experience the key practices of Sufi meditation and to learn about its health benefits
-To learn how to stay relaxed and balanced during stressful times
-To develop enhanced concentration and focus
-To cultivate creativity, kindness, tolerance, acceptance, and empathy
-To develop inter-cultural perspective, and
-To prepare for lifelong learning
ASSIGNMENTS:
(1) Regular Attendance: presence and participation in class are essential since the course is experiential.

(2) Assigned weekly readings from the required short books, Sufism: A Bridge Between Religions & Manifestation of Thought. One chapter per week from each book, about 16 pages per week. On average, we expect 30-45 minutes of reading per week. Each week, it is further required to write at least a paragraph reflecting on the assigned reading.

(3) Self-reflection Exercise: To submit two self-reflection exercises using any form of creative expression, such as poetry, essay (250 words), or any kind of arts, to reflect on the weekly reading and daily meditation practices. We expect students to spend 2.5 hours completing each self-reflective exercise. (5 hours per quarter).
GRADING:
Your grade breakdown consists of

-20% for class participation,

-20% for the weekly assigned readings & weekly reflection writings,

-20% for the daily practice of meditation and keeping a meditation log,

-40% for self-expression exercise
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Mohammad Sadoghi is an Associate Professor in Computer Science at UC Davis. He leads the ExpoLab research group that aims to pioneer a new exploratory data platform to unify secure transactional and real-time analytics. Additionally, he has a keen interest in understanding the true nature of the human being and in uncovering one’s inner identity. He has been offering courses at UC Davis that focus on stress management and the student’s overall well-being centered around meditation.

Nasim Bahadorani is a wellness expert who specializes in lifestyle health behavior programs. She has her doctorate in public health from Loma Linda University and taught public health courses in the Health Sciences Department at California State University, Northridge. She holds a master’s degree in biomedical science from Midwestern University, College of Health Sciences. Her strong background in biomedical sciences coupled with her experience in public health provides unique expertise in understanding pathways of disease and knowing the strategic models and interventions to prevent them. She is a certified health education specialist and a certified health and wellness coach providing consultation for wellness programs. Her work is focused on lifestyle medicine with integrative approaches to disease prevention and health promotion.

TITLE: Supernatural Cinema
FOCUS: Harlan Ellison claims, “Science fiction and fantasy is a kind of literature that embodies the highest aspirations of the human race.” The "other" creatures we consider supernatural--mermaids, zombies, ghosts, aliens, unicorns, vampires, and trolls--are our mirrors, capturing our fears, our concerns, and our aspirations. Our course will examine supernatural cinema, both familiar and foreign, both horrific and comic, to understand our world, our society, and ourselves.
OUTCOMES:
Students will learn to connect contemporary film and dystopic visions with the reality of the world around them, as they increase their critical reading, viewing, oral participation, and writing skills.

Our films are all from the last fifty years, but from different countries and perspectives. Thus, students will encounter examples of supernatural film from generations before theirs, which we'll contextualize, as well as contemporary films, which we'll critically analyze. Examining the creatures we're familiar with (e.g. vampires) and contrasting them with creatures new to us (e.g. Finnish trolls) will allow us to enhance intercultural learning.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Students will participate in class, write short weekly journals (250-500 words), lead discussion one week with a small group of students (i.e., together, students will come up with a list of questions designed to further discussion that week), and create a term project, which might be a traditional essay (4-6 pages), an artistic piece, or a multimodal project.

We will watch 9 films over the quarter; students will also have short readings complementing the films. For example, the week we watch Shaun of the Dead, students will read two brief blog posts about how the film captured the zeitgeist.
GRADING:
Grading:

Term project: 40%

Participation: 20%

Weekly journals: 20%

Group leading of discussion: 20%
INSTRUCTORBIO: Karma Waltonen, a lecturer in the Writing Center, teaches upper division writing courses (Film Studies, International Relations, the Health Professions, etc.) and freshman seminars on The Simpsons, Margaret Atwood, Doctor Who, and Writing & Performing Stand-up Comedy. She leads a summer abroad course in Oxford on British cinema and mentors the stand-up comedy club at UCD. In 2015, she won the AF Excellence in Teaching Award.

TITLE: Symmetry in Art and Science
FOCUS: This class will explore aspects of symmetry in life, art and science. How do we perceive symmetry? How does it affect the way we see the world? This class will involve discussions that should deepen our understanding and perception of symmetry in our environment. How is symmetry involved in pattern making, in music, in biology? What is the role of repetition? Can symmetry be quantified? The format will involve discussions among the participants with each student expected to bring interesting examples of symmetric situations to the class room. Hopefully the class will involve students from a broad range of majors with interests in the arts and in science. The ability to count, add and subtract will be the only math skills required.
OUTCOMES: The course is designed to facilitate discussion between students in various disciplines with a common interest in symmetry. The class is also designed to increase the participants' awareness and perception of symmetry. The students should emerge form the course with greater appreciation of symmetry and some ability to quantify aspects of symmetry. Students will learn about symmetry operations, patterning, and related ways to quantify symmetry. The instructor will approach the topic from a visual, tactile standpoint, but students will be encouraged to consider other non-visual aspects of symmetry.
ASSIGNMENTS: The class will involve extensive discussion of symmetry and how we perceive symmetry. Students will be expected to bring examples of symmetric objects or situations to class for discussion. They should be prepared to explain the reasons for the examples they have chosen. Each student will be expected to participate in two in-class presentations focused on an aspect of symmetry that they find interesting and to write two 500-100 word essays
GRADING: Participation in classroom discussions will account for 25% of the grades. Another 50% of the grade will be based on the in class presentations.  The two essays will account for 25% of the grade.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Alan Balch is a Distinguished Professor Emeritus in the Department of Chemistry and was Associate editor of the journal, Inorganic Chemistry. His research involves the study of molecular structure and the interaction of molecular structure with physical and chemical properties of molecules. Much of his current work involves the highly symmetric fullerenes (Bucky Balls), which are closed cages comprised of only carbon atoms with a distinct inside and outside. He is also currently involved in the crystallization of luminescent gold(I) complexes, which may have sensor applications.  He is also interested in the visual arts and is a passionate museum goer. He collects various modern works of art, particularly work in and on paper, a few of which may be relevant to this course.

TITLE: Tamarkoz Meditation
FOCUS: We are witnessing a nation faced with stress epidemic, where 1 in 3 college freshmen show signs of mental health disorders. Overcoming stress is crucial in paving the way to promote a healthier lifestyle filled with joy & creativity. This experiential seminar presents a comprehensive stress management technique that revolves around a unique heart-centered meditation method, namely, Tamarkoz®. The Tamarkoz® method is the art of self-knowledge through concentration and meditation. Learning meditation is like learning how to swim. You cannot do it by reading a book. You have to get in the water and practice. True learning requires systematic training. This course will provide a "taste", it teaches how to attain the relaxation necessary for reaching the real state of concentration and meditation.
OUTCOMES: The beneficial effects of meditation are well documented in a massive amount of research literature. Participants report a number of positive results, with particular emphasis on stress reduction. People who meditate properly are calmer, less tense, less reactive to stress, healthier, feel better, have greater self-esteem, and above all are more creative and joyful than those who do not. Meditators also report a sense of increased well-being and enjoyment of life. Research has clearly demonstrated that meditation is helpful in a number of stress-related conditions such as high blood pressure, headaches, back problems, cardiac rehabilitation and pain reduction. Recent research indicates it is often more effective than psychotherapy in the treatment of some problems. The exercises included in the course can be of immediate practical value, and students usually enjoy doing them. To provide students with the following: (1) A foundation and working knowledge of basic theories of meditation; (2) The opportunity to participate as an ongoing member of a meditation group; (3) Working knowledge of meditative techniques; (4)An understanding of meditative principles; (5) The ability to practice deep breathing; (6) The ability to perform stretching and energy balancing exercises; (7) Working knowledge of stress reduction techniques; (8) Knowledge of the various types of meditative approaches and relevant therapeutic factors; (9) A safe environment, in which students feel free to examine and change behavioral habits; and (10) The capacity to sit still and silent for 30 minutes
ASSIGNMENTS: The basic outline of the course includes lecture, discussion, observation, analysis, physical exercises, meditative exercises, small group activities, and demonstrations will be included. Requirements are as follows. (1) Regular Attendance: presence and participation in class are essential since the course is experiential. (2) Daily Practice at home is required. This is one class in which everyone will know whether or not you have done your homework, for it will be physically obvious. You will be expected to demonstrate proficiency in the exercises, including the movements and the capacity to sit with your eyes closed without moving, being very still. Start with 5 minutes of meditation the first week, then increase at least 3 minutes each week thereafter, until you are meditating for 30 minutes each day. (3) Meditation Journal: Keeping a daily journal of your meditation and its results will help you recognize the effectiveness of the procedures, and aid the instructor in helping you deal with any problems.
GRADING: Students are expected to write a journal for at least 5 minutes daily (35 minutes per week) and daily practices of 10-20 minutes daily (1-2 hours per week) consisting of deep breathing, mind relaxation, Movazeneh® exercises (the art of balancing and harmonizing the body), visualization, progressive relaxation, and meditation. Your grade breakdown would be 25% for class participation, 50% for daily practices, and 25% of keeping a meditation journal.

TITLE: The Arboretum: A Living Laboratory
FOCUS:
The UC Davis Arboretum and Public Garden is a 5,300 acre urban greenspace incorporating stream, forest, grassland and urban ecosystems, demonstration gardens, scientific collections, over 1.5 miles of waterway, and over 3 miles of walking and biking trails. Managing the waterway is a significant task, employing many full-time staff and interns each year. A major component of waterway management is tracking and improving water quality. The goals of this CURE are to 1) educate students on the factors that influence water quality, including how water quality is measured and managed, and 2) provide Arboretum waterway managers with robust water quality data to inform decision-making. 

In this CURE, students will learn about the structure and operation of the Arboretum waterway, study different metrics of water quality, perform field and lab analyses on Arboretum water, and present their results as a report on waterway health to Arboretum waterway managers. Class sessions will incorporate a combination of activities including short lectures, walking tours, field sampling, lab measurements, data analysis and visualization, and written and oral reports. Students will be trained on water quality sampling methods such as pipetting and plating for bacterial counts and using meters to measure conductivity, dissolved oxygen, pH, and temperature. Data generated by students in the class will be provided to Arboretum waterway managers to assist management efforts and be expanded upon in future quarters of this CURE.
OUTCOMES:
The primary research outcome is to provide spatially and temporally robust water quality data to Arboretum waterway managers. Learning goals are to:

  • Describe the history of the Arboretum waterway, its hydrology, and the biotic and abiotic factors that drive water quality
  • Operate water quality meters
  • Plate water for bacterial assays (including performing dilutions, pipetting, and counting bacterial colonies)
  • Present data visually through graphs and calculate summary statistics
  • Communicate research progress and findings orally and in writing 


We will offer this CURE through the entirety of AY23/24 and AY25/26 (to different cohorts of students). Early cohorts will test multiple protocols and evaluate metrics of water quality. For example, different media grow different types of microorganisms, and thus provide a different view of the microbial community. There are also a variety of abiotic metrics of water quality, such as conductivity, pH, dissolved oxygen, and temperature. Students will review the advantages and limitations of different methods in terms of time and resource investment in performing the analyses and the aspect of water quality they represent. The final course product will be a report on the different analyses performed including a recommendation on the standard protocol to be used in the course moving forward.
ASSIGNMENTS: Assignments include field activities, lab journals, collecting/analyzing data, demonstrating proficiency in sampling techniques, and presenting written and oral results. Field activities include observational, field, and laboratory methods, and graphing of data and interpretation of results. Students will record data in a class archive for use by future iterations of the course. While the data to be collected are set; students have flexibility in the questions they ask for their data analysis projects. The class dataset includes 7 metrics sampled at 9 locations in the Arboretum, allowing students to consider a variety of questions, including spatial trends, correlation between metrics, and comparison of course data to previous sampling in the Arboretum. Students may also propose a hypothesis for future iterations of the course to test (e.g. seasonal trends), including predictions of data if the hypothesis is supported, and factors that could influence the predicted trends. Through these activities, students will be expected to demonstrate proficiency in techniques such as field sampling, pipetting, using a light microscope, performing dilutions and bacterial counts. The final product of the course is an oral presentation on a portion of the class data (to be shared with the Arboretum), and a final reflection paper.
