INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Laci Gerhart is an Associate Professor of Teaching (LSOE) in Evolution and Ecology. Her previous pedagogical research has included analyzing student engagement with video-based homework assignments, and the role of app-based participatory science projects in field courses. This CURE project was born out of her experiential field course EVE 16: Wild Davis in which students tested Arboretum water for fecal coliform bacteria. Her goal is to not only connect students to original research experiences, but also to provide Arboretum waterway managers with valuable data to inform decision-making. 

 

Dr. Brett Poulin is an Assistant Professor in Environmental Toxicology.His research focuses on the interface between water quality and ecosystem health, with an emphasis on metal contaminants that impact wildlife and humans. His active research projects span from the Florida Everglades to Arctic Alaska. His goal is to use the Arboretum as a living laboratory to connect new undergraduate students with research on their campus that will shape management of a local urban waterway.

TITLE: The Fabulous Singer Featherweight (Vintage Sewing Machine):  History, Mechanics, and Use
FOCUS: The Singer Featherweight comprises a series of lockstitch domestic sewing machines that were produced from 1933 to 1968.  The Singer Manufacturing Company started producing these sewing machines pre-war and they have continued popularity among sewers. Students who are interested in reduce, reuse, and recycle and the history of a vintage sewing machine series would benefit from this Freshman Seminar. It would also potentially help them add an activity to balance their overall life. Students who have some sewing experience or working with machines would benefit most from this seminar. This seminar will explore the history of the Singer Featherweight series, changes in the components of the machines over time, and will get time to use a machine. The goal of this course is to provide students with an overview of the history of a piece of machinery that was used in the everyday household historically and can still be used decades later to produce fabric items that can benefit society.
OUTCOMES: Students will learn about the history and production of Singer Featherweights. Goals of this seminar are to (1) cultivate ethics, responsibility, and respect, for reuse, reduce and recycle (2) learn about the mechanics of the machines (3) have the experience of using the machine to inspire them to explore sewing as a means to enhance life balance. This seminar is an opportunity to gain a more in-depth understanding of a vintage sewing machine that continues to hold its popularity.
ASSIGNMENTS: For the five sessions (2 hours total per session with a 10-minute break), students will be assigned to read 1-2 chapters from the book "Featherweight 221: The Perfect Portable." The chapter topics will then be discussed during the seminar sessions and students will be able to look at various machines so they can appreciate the design and functional changes over the years. During the quarter, students will be expected to design and produce a fabric postcard that they can work on during class sessions while they are experimenting with the different machines. The expectation is that they will have a finished product at the end of the seminar. This seminar will meet for a total of five sessions. Each session will be 2 hours in duration (10 am -11:50 AM) with a 10-minute break. Dates for the class will be the first five Tuesdays of the quarter.
GRADING: This is a pass/no pass course. A passing grade will be awarded to undergraduate students for work that would receive a grade of C– or better. The course grading for this seminar will be based on creating a fabric postcard (50%) according to the pattern instructions and verbal and quality of participation in class (50%). Class participation will entail students coming to class with questions and comments relative to the assigned reading for the seminar session, active participation in discussion sessions, and completing in-class activities (drawing/picking out a design, selecting/cutting fabric, sewing fabric).
INSTRUCTORBIO: Professor Tell is a member of the faculty in the School of Veterinary Medicine. Her research interests include the treatment of avian disease, avoidance of drug residues in poultry products consumed by humans, and avian health relative to the conservation of bird populations. Dr. Tell obtained her Bachelor of Science from UC Davis, did her residency at the National Zoo, then returned to UC Davis as a faculty member. She is boarded with the American College of Zoological Medicine and the American Board of Veterinary Practitioners (Avian Practice). Dr. Tell has been sewing since 1990 and has taught an honors art science fusion course on the UC Davis campus. She is interested in helping students be aware of the reduction of consumable products and to use fabric art as an outlet for creativity and overall mental health.

TITLE: The Fig
FOCUS: There are a lot of people who are interested in growing their own vegetables and fruits in their backyards. Their main motivation is often the reduction of cost and/or the production of quality organic food. In recent years, there is a significant increase in the interest of growing fig trees. This is the case due to the following facts: (1) figs are rarely available in markets (due to low shelf life), (2) fig trees are easy to propagate from cuttings, (3) figs have a massive number of varieties that produce fruits that are easily distinguishable among other varieties by taste, color, and size, and (4) figs can be easily adapted to grow in containers and hence can be grown in colder climates where they are often moved indoors while they are dormant in winter. Nowadays, fig trees and cuttings are significantly traded among online stores such as eBay and amazon. The students in this course will explore and learn various topics about figs. Every student in the class will research a selected topic and produce a paper and a presentation about that topic.
OUTCOMES: The students in this course will develop focus and depth in the field of figs. The students will learn several topics about figs including: The history of the fig, fig production around the world, fig varieties, fig tree propagation methods, characteristics of figs, diseases, nutritional content, trading fig trees/cuttings, and others. The students will also participate in an experiment to propagate fig cuttings and produce new fig trees. Finally, the assignments in this course are established to improve the effective communication skills of students.
ASSIGNMENTS: Students will be required to prepare a short paper (and a related presentation) on a topic related to the theme of the course which is going to be chosen in consultation with the instructor. The students are expected to do the following tasks throughout the quarter: (1) select the research topic, (2) do the proposed research, (3) prepare a presentation, and (4) write a final paper (5 to 7 pages). The overall workload in this course is expected to be 1 to 2 hours of out-of-class work per week.
GRADING: The course score will be based on the quality of the student work and computed as follows: a 10-minute oral presentation to the class (50%) and a final written research report (50%). The final course grade will be reported as pass/no pass, the grade of pass is awarded to students that otherwise would receive a grade of C– or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Hussain Al-Asaad is a professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering. His research interests include design verification, testing, and fault tolerant computing. He has published over 55 papers in archival journals and refereed conference/workshop proceedings. He teaches undergraduate and graduate courses on digital systems design, testing, and verification. Al-Asaad is a recipient of the National Science Foundation CAREER Award and the UC-Davis academic senate distinguished undergraduate teaching award. Al-Asaad has significant experience of growing various fruit trees, and he is currently growing and evaluating around 700 varieties of figs mostly grown in containers.

TITLE: The Legacy of Rachel Carson
FOCUS:
This course will explore the writing and activism of Rachel Carson. Rachel Carson is best known for writing Silent Spring, which is often described as a book that launched the environmental movement in the United States in the 1960's. Silent Spring was a call to action over the potential risks of chemical pesticides to the environment. However, her writing extends far beyond this book - and delves into multiple different topics including the wonder of the natural world, concerns about the directions of scientific research, and interactions between government and science. 

In this course, we will read Silent Spring, as well as a few of Carson's other writings, and review materials about her work. In each class period, we will explore scientists who are working today that carry on the legacy of Carson, via their research, writing, and activism.
OUTCOMES:
This course will focus on the following educational outcomes:

1) Develop effective communication skills

2) Cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy

3) Develop focus and depth in one or more discipline, and

4) Develop a global perspective

 

Through assignments, readings, and discussions, we will explore what it means to be a strong communicator about science. We will explore ethical qualms and challenges in science, and foster empathy towards global environmental challenges. We will focus on  natural and physical science perspectives on the environment, and feature environmental stories from around the world.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Course assignments will include:

20-30 pages of reading per week, including Silent Spring and other materials

Keeping a written nature journal, using provided reflection prompts

Participating fully in class discussions, including contributing discussion questions each week. 

One presentation for the class

 

Each class session will emphasize active participation via several techniques, including full class discussions and small group (or pair) discussions. We will emphasize evaluating information from a variety of sources including traditional media, social media, blogs, and videos.
GRADING:
Grading structure:

Presentation on topic of student's choice: 30%

Weekly journal writing: 30%

Weekly submitted discussion questions: 20%

Participation in class, and in group discussions: 20%

 

This class will be offered P/NP. The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Tessa Hill is a Professor in Earth & Planetary Sciences. Hill is an oceanographer, specializing in the consequences of climate change for oceans and coasts. Hill is the co-author of At Every Depth, a book that explores the human connection to the ocean and how that connection is evolving due to human impacts on the environment. Hill teaches upper and lower division oceanography courses at UC Davis, and enjoys delving into Rachel Carson's writing about the ocean.

TITLE: The Monsters of France
FOCUS: This First-Year Seminar explores the various iterations of monsters throughout French art and literature. Together, we will examine how societal and political anxieties contribute to the manifestation of literary monsters throughout the ages, such as (but not limited to) werewolves and other beasts, vampires, spirits and monstrous human forms. Students can expect to engage in discussions pertaining to race, class, gender, sex and sexuality, and political movements as they become tropes associated with specific manifestations of the monstrous. The course will be led through a mix of lecture and discussion and will guide students through various French historical periods (from the Middle Ages to the present). Assignments will be composed of written and oral responses that develop critical, analytical and even creative engagement with the primary reading material. Therefore, students will benefit from intercultural, literary and artistic enrichment. This course is taught in English, and all reading and writing will be in English.
OUTCOMES:
As a result of participating in this First-Year Seminar, students should be able to:

  • Communicate Effectively. Students will be asked to communicate in a variety of ways, such as presenting oral arguments and writing responses to readings and specific prompts. For all responses, students will need to clearly announce their argument/point of view and to use evidence (concretely pulled from primary texts, lectures, specific sources, etc.) in order to justify their arguments.
  • Think Critically. Students will be continuously asked to pause, think, and reflect on a variety of issues that may be presented in our readings and lectures/discussions. Issues such as race, class, gender, sex and sexuality, and politics will come up in our readings and discussions. Students will be asked to apply how these themes fit into depictions of the monstrous and what these monsters say about society at large.
  • Demonstrate intercultural learning. Students will attain better knowledge about French history, literature, art and society, and they will also be asked to reflect on their own cultural backgrounds as it compares to the French context. Students will demonstrate retention and application of this skill through communicative methods.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Once per week:

•300-word discussion post (1 hour). Students will engage with a prompt for after reading the assigned readings and then post responses in a Canvas Discussion board. They will then comment or post questions on other students' posts.

•15-20 pages of reading per week (1 to 1.5 hours). Readings must be completed before creating discussion posts and before coming to class. Students are required to retain the reading and be ready to engage in discussion posts on Canvas and in class. Readings will vary in form, style and length. May include viewing a film or TV series.

Once per quarter:

•In-class discussion lead (2 hours prep.). Each class session, one or two students will kick off our discussion. Students will briefly summarize the readings and offer key points and takeaways. 

•One essay, 3-5 pages (7 to 8 hours). Critical, argumentative engagement with the material in written form. A clear prompt and rubric will be given during the quarter.

•Invent a Monster Project (5-8 hours). Students will (individually or in small groups) create a portrait of a monster that represents a current socio-cultural or political anxiety. Specific guidelines and a rubric will be given in class.
GRADING:
Grade Breakdown

Participation and Preparation: 15%

Short Writing Responses on Canvas: 20%

Discussion Lead: 10%

Essay Assignment: 30%

Monster Project: 25%

Percentages required to earn specific letter grades:

A+ 100% to 97%
A < 97% to 93%
A- < 93% to 90%
B+ < 90% to 87%
B < 87% to 83%
B- < 83% to 80%
C+ < 80% to 77%
C < 77% to 73%
C- < 73% to 70%
D+ < 70% to 67%
D < 67% to 63%
D- < 63% to 60%
F < 60% to 0%
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Zack Scovel is a Lecturer in the Department of French and Italian. He holds a Master's and Ph.D in French and Francophone Studies and has a deeply rooted interest in horror literature and theory. His research focuses on the ways in which French art and literature depict monsters as manifestations of real-life social, cultural and political anxieties. At such intersections of art and society, Dr. Scovel aims to spark discussions around specific questions, such as, "Why is the monster depicted in this way?" or "Who is the 'real' monster in this situation?" and, most importantly, "What is the monster showing us about our own society, culture, nation, or ourselves?" 

Although this seminar is not specific to first-generation students only (all undergrads are welcome!), Dr. Scovel would like to mention that he was a first-generation student and always finds joy in connecting with other first-gen students and offering mentorship when needed.

TITLE: The Outdoor Classroom: Exploring How Children Grow and Learn in the Natural World
FOCUS: Children are multisensory, physical beings that thrive in outdoor spaces. Research conducted over the last 20 years confirms the need to provide children with quality green spaces in which to grow physically, cognitively, socially, and emotionally. This seminar will explore child development in the context of the natural world and provide an introduction to the design, implementation and evaluation of child-friendly, green spaces. Sample topics include: farm-to-table; "big body play" and other physical challenges; children as nature explorers; superhero play and social relationships. Emphasis will be placed on in-person observation and remote learning (using video) of real-world settings, including the certified Nature Explore outdoor classroom at the Center for Child and Family Studies (CCFS). Readings will be utilized to promote reflection and prompt dynamic discussion during weekly meetings. Written assignments are intended to explore applied research skills, and develop academic and professional writing. Informal lecture and video will be used to supplement learning. In lieu of a text, reading material and links to appropriate websites will be provided by instructor.
OUTCOMES:
By the end of this quarter, students will:

  1. Develop an understanding of and appreciation for the importance of outdoor play and connecting children with the natural world.
  2. Consider an interdisciplinary approach to early learning in applied settings.
  3. Connect research findings with observations and knowledge of the natural world; articulate practical implications.
  4. Practice important oral and written communication skills necessary for the successful participation in future academic and professional work.
  5. Articulate a critical need in the field and develop a well-researched proposal for intervention, formatted as a policy brief.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments and expectations for the course are as follows:

  • Students will complete one to two weekly readings (20-30 pages of journal articles and/or applied texts per week) intended to promote group discussion, critical thinking, and real-world connections.
  • Students will participate in a weekly discussion board to expand upon concepts introduced in readings.
  • Students will submit weekly applied writing assignments (1-2 pages) designed to develop observation skills, practice stylized writing, and make meaningful connections between research and applied program practice.
  • Students will complete a written policy brief (2-3 pages) to summarize their cumulative knowledge from the course.  Students will be encouraged to pace out their research and work throughout the final three weeks of class to complete this final assignment.
  • Students will attend and practice active engagement in all class meetings.
  • Students will complete two graded course surveys at different time points to provide feedback to course instructors regarding implementation of course learning objectives and course instructional practices.
GRADING:
The course grade will be based on the following breakdown:

  • Discussion Boards: 15%
  • Weekly Meeting Participation (including surveys): 25%
  • Applied Writing Assignments: 40%
  • Policy Brief: 20%

Each graded assignment will have a rubric highlighting the participation expectations and grading criteria.

The grade of 'pass' is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Hannah Minter Anderson, MAT, holds degrees in Human Development and Early Childhood Education.  She currently serves as a Child Development Demonstration Lecturer for the Department of Human Ecology.  She also works as a Program Coordinator at the Early Childhood Lab School (ECLS) at the Center for Child and Family Studies on campus.  The ECLS provides a model early childhood program and is a site for research, observation, and demonstration.  The ECLS is accredited through the National Association for the Education of Young Children (NAEYC) and contains a National Arbor Day Foundation certified Nature Explore Outdoor Classroom.  Hannah is a trained Project Specialist through The Outdoor Classroom Project and is a supporting member of the Children and Nature Network.  Hannah brings her personal experiences, knowledge, and affinity for the natural world and blends them with current research findings to design and implement high quality early childhood environments and experiences for the children in her care.

TITLE: The Pursuit of Happiness
FOCUS: In recent years, popular culture has become increasing focused on improving one's happiness. The World Happiness Report has ranked the happiest nations in the world since 2013, and the countries ranked in the top 10 are regularly highlighted in popular media in order to determine what makes residents in these countries so happy. For example, Denmark has begun to market their country’s concept of happiness, known as “hygge,” to countries around the world. Here in the US, a focus on how to foster happiness has grown in academic and popular presses alike. For example, the most popular course in the history of Yale University is now a course on happiness entitled Psychology and the Good Life by Dr. Laurie Santos. Research in this growing field indicates that one of the largest determining factors in an individual’s ability to achieve happiness is to experiencing gratitude on a regular basis (Singh, 24 Dec 2018). As such, this seminar will focus on defining and examining the concept of happiness as well as locating research-based practices that have been proven to increase happiness. The seminar will pay special focus on the role that gratitude and kindness plays in facilitating happiness. Students will read a variety of texts and media in order to conceptualize the scope of happiness in modern lives and to investigate research-based practices that have been proven to facilitate happiness. Students will also engage in self-reflective processes, such as keeping a gratitude journal and engaging in targeted acts of kindness, in order to determine if these processes may bring them happiness. Due to the highly personal nature of happiness, gratitude, and kindness, students must be prepared to actively create a safe classroom environment and to discuss sensitive topics with care.
OUTCOMES: Students will practice critical and close reading of written and visual sources, which will allow them to cultivate ethics, responsibility, honor, acceptance, respect, and empathy for different practices of cultivating happiness. Students will complete formal and informal writing, group and individual conversations among peers and with the instructor, and in-class as well as out of class happiness practices that will help students investigate issues related to the modern fascination of pursuing happiness. These activities will allow students to develop effective communication and cognitive skills by thinking critically about course concepts and representing their ideas both orally and through writing.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Each week students will read (or listen) to an academic or popular text regarding the pursuit of happiness in the modern world. Students will reflect on the the assigned readings in group discussions and/or write informal reading responses. Additionally, each week students will be assigned one happiness practice to complete and reflect on in writing.
Students will prepare a 15-minute formal presentation on a topic of their choice that examines a specific practice or tradition related to the pursuit of happiness. Students may also choose to present on what manifestations of happiness look like in different groups or cultures. For their final papers, students will be required to conduct a short (3-page) self-study, in which they reflect on the process of engaging in happiness-building activities (e.g., the gratitude journal and acts of kindness) throughout the class. Students will regularly complete informal, in-class writing assignments and activities, including a weekly gratitude journal. In order to successfully pass the course, student will regularly attend and actively participate in all class meetings. A passing grade will be earned by completion of all assignments with substantial insight and creativity, and active participation in class discussion and activities.
GRADING:
The grade of pass is awarded to undergraduate students for work in courses that otherwise would receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
Assignments and percentage of final grade:
1. Final paper: 20%
2. Presentation: 40%
3. Weekly reading completion and assigned happiness practices: 20%
3. Discussion and participation: 20%
A passing grade will be earned by completion of all assignments with substantial insight and creativity, and active participation in class discussion and activities.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Alison Bright is a continuing lecturer in the University Writing Program at the UC Davis and a teacher-consultant with the National Writing Project. She serves as the University California representative on the Advisory Board for the California Writing Project. Her research interests include: writing program administration; writing across the curriculum; writing centers; tutor preparation; teacher identity; and the professional development of teacher/writers. She has published the results of these research interests in English Education and Teaching/Writing. Alison and several of her colleagues collaboratively authored the English Education article “Beyond strategies: Teacher practice, writing process, and the influence of inquiry,” which was the recipient of the 2009 NCTE Janet Emig Award. She holds a Ph.D. in education from the University of California, Santa Barbara, an MA in English, with an emphasis in rhetoric and the teaching of English, from Sonoma State University, and BA in Spanish literature from the University of California, Santa Cruz.

TITLE: The Tarot in Theory and Practice
FOCUS:
Since the rise of social media in the last twenty tears-- and ESPECIALLY since the pandemic-- the Tarot has gained a greater prominence in popular culture than it has ever had before. But how well do you know the Tarot beyond the single-card draw and an "intuitive" literal impression of an image on a given card?

The Tarot has a rich and complex history; while many claims about that history are not necessarily supported by factual evidence, the Tarot is nevertheless a portable archive of the last 2500 years of mystical and esoteric thought; one can read the Tarot in literal terms, but it is a much richer experience if one has some awareness of the multiple symbolic systems-- including Hermeticism, Neoplatonism, the Qabalah, Hellenistic astrology, alchemy, and and Rosicrucianism among others-- which are simultaneously operating in any given Tarot spread.

This seminar will serve as an introduction to the Tarot; it will not only teach you how to do a Tarot reading, but will also serve as an introduction to the history of Esoteric spirituality. This class will NOT involve any fortune telling, nor will any of us be doing readings for other people. Instead, priority will be placed on the personal, individual use of the Tarot as a meditative tool that fosters self-knowledge and personal well-being. Most of all, we will regard Tarot in contemporary terms and consider its relation to mental health, to neurodiversity, and as a potential tool for social change.

This class will also coincide with World Tarot Day on May 25, and it is my hope that the class and its particpants might play a role in activities related to the campus celebration of that event I am currently planning that will emerge from a graduate course on the Tarot I am currently teaching.
OUTCOMES:
Educational goals for this course will include:

1. You will not only learn to read the symbolism of a given Tarot card, but how to read a sequence of cards in a multi-card reading; in this way you will not only learn alternative modes of reading (such as those utilized in Modernist poetry, something which the Tarot is closely related), but you will become more aware of the nature of interpretation itself.

2. In learning the migratory history and global background of the Tarot, you will be receiving an introduction to the certain facets of the fields of Literature, History, Sociology, Anthropology, Cultural Studies, and Psychology among others.

3. In learning the ways the Tarot can speak to the self through meditative practices, you will discovers ways in which the Tarot can contribute to one's mental health and can be an asset in recovery from trauma.

4. Finally, our final goal will concern the various ways the Tarot is increasingly used and regarded as a tool for social change-- whether for the self in regard to understanding the complexity of your own identity, or collectively in regard to the relationship the Tarot might have to our understanding of community.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments will include:

-- Frequent and active class participation; the first half of each class session will be devoted to discussion of brief reading assignments about various aspects of the Tarot, while the second half will be concern the symbolism of the cards and various reading techniques.

-- Students will keep a weekly journal tracking their progress through the Tarot; it will include a record of daily readings among other considerations of the Tarot.

-- As a final project, students will design their own Tarot "spread" (or, arrangement of cards for a reading); this won't be simply a random assemblage of cards in a shape, but will be organized and structured according to distinct interpretative/theoretical principles they have learned throughout the quarter. Students will present an explanation of the spread to the rest of the class on the final day of the quarter; this presentation will be accompanied by a 1500 word paper which explains how their spread works and situates it within the various reading assignments.

-- Students will not only in attend and participate in the events planned for World Tarot Day on May 25, but the class as a whole will collectively organize and present one of the events themselves.
GRADING: This class will be P/NP, and students are required to do ALL of the weekly exercises, the final project on their self-designed Tarot spread (including the related paper and oral presentation) and to contribute to the collective class-designed event on World Tarot Day. Participation will be a crucial part of student assessment, and students are required to be present in class to participate; therefore NO unexcused absences will be permitted and one unexcused absence is grounds for not passing the class.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Gregory Dobbins is an Associate Professor of English; his specialization is in Irish Modernism (particularly the writing of James Joyce, W.B. Yeats, and Samuel Beckett), Twentieth-Century British/Anglophone literature, Critical Theory, Neurodivergent Studies (with an emphasis on ADHD), Esoteric Studies, and Cultural Studies. He is currently writing two book projects: a collection of essays on the the relation between Neurodiversity and the emergence of Modernist literature (which will include his work on the Tarot), and a monograph about the relationship between the 19th century occult movement Theosophy and the structural form of the Irish Modernist novel. He has taught at UC Davis since 2002.

TITLE: The World's Game:  Soccer and Globalization
FOCUS: This seminar course introduces students to some of the major issues associated with soccer's growth into the world's most popular game and a multi-billion dollar business. Some issues we will discuss include the relationship between soccer's spread and colonialism; questions of race and racism in the game, both past and present; soccer as a tool of support for and resistance to totalitarian governments; and the conflict between nationalism and globalization.  Every week, we will divide our time equally between discussing a short weekly reading (average ca. 25 pages) and applying what we learn from the readings to analysis of media reportage and actual game footage. You read that right: you can get course credit for reading soccer websites and watching games.
OUTCOMES: This course will help students understand the human and financial impact of "big sports" in the 21st century in global perspective. They will leave the class understanding the subtext of what appears to be just a game. Further, since newspaper reports and match commentary will usually be the subjects of our discussion, they will learn to be critical consumers of media and entertainment -- a useful skill for lifelong learners. Weekly article readings will also help students to develop the basic skills (understanding argument and evidence) necessary to succeed in later humanities and social science courses. Particular attention will be devoted to the 2026 World Cup, hosted in the US, Mexico, and Canada this summer.
ASSIGNMENTS: The fundamental expectation for this course is that students do the assigned reading in advance (about 25 pp.) and come prepared to discuss questions about it. By engaging with their classmates and giving thoughtful answers to questions suggested by the reading, students will demonstrate that they have understood the reading. Students will also come prepared to watch soccer matches or read media reports and to discuss their relationship with the reading. The only paper in the course is a final assignment of roughly four pages, in which students will apply what they've learned from one or several readings to analysis of a contemporary media report. By completing this paper, students will demonstrate mastery of the reading and a critical attitude towards media and entertainment - the crucial long-term goal of the course.
GRADING: 25% of your course grade will be based on your attentive participation in our weekly discussions, and 25% will be based on your leading discussion (with a partner or in a team) of one weekly reading that interests you. The other half of your grade will be based on a four-page paper which will ask you to read one or several media reports and analyze them in light of our discussions and readings. We will devote one course session to discussing proposals for these final papers. Rubrics for participation, discussion leadership, and papers will be available on the course Canvas site from the first day of class; there will be no surprises. A passing grade will be between 70 and 100%.
INSTRUCTORBIO: Ian Campbell is Associate Professor in the Department of History, with a research focus on Russian imperialism in the 19th century. He is currently working on two books, one on frontier violence in Russian history, the other on settler colonialism in Central Asia. This course combines his longstanding pedagogical interest in global history (i.e. outside the traditional bounds of the nation-state) with his even-longer-standing love of soccer. He is currently developing a larger (four-credit) course on the history of sports and modernity. His favorite team is Zenit St. Petersburg, and he prefers to play defensive midfield.

TITLE: Vectors to Vecna: Playing with Roleplaying Systems
FOCUS:
This seminar uses Tabletop Role Playing Games (TTRPGs) as a creative framework to develop social intelligence, leadership, creative problem solving, and teamwork. Through games such as Dungeons & Dragons, Mausritter, Knave 2E, Mythic Bastionlands, Fabula Ultima, and Break!!, students will engage in collaborative storytelling, character creation, and group decision-making. The course emphasizes effective communication, negotiating tough conversations, and providing a safe space to practice leadership and interpersonal skills in a playful, narrative-driven environment.

 

Here is a tentative weekly schedule:

 

01. Introduction to RPGs: History, structure, and the core elements of collaboration.

02. Creating Characters: How attributes and backgrounds drive group dynamics.

03. Session Zero: Setting boundaries, expectations, and communication norms.

04. Worlds and Tropes: World-building techniques and subverting genre conventions.

05. Combat without Combat: Focus on negotiation systems and nonviolent diplomacy.

06. Improv & "Yes, And": Techniques to foster creativity and build on the ideas of others.

07. Shared Narratives: Methods for co-creating plots and weaving backstories.

08. Conflict Resolution: Managing different play styles and maintaining group cohesion.

09. Game Design: The basics of building your own TTRPG mechanics and themes.

10. The Game Master (GM): Leadership, preparation, and managing player engagement.

Each class consists of a 20-minute lecture followed by 40 minutes of participation.
OUTCOMES:
- Develop effective communication skills by engaging in collaborative storytelling, group discussion, and active listening during tabletop RPG sessions as well as engaging in creating strong teams.
- Strengthen high-level cognitive skills through creative problem solving, strategic thinking, and narrative analysis as part of gameplay and scenario resolution.
- Develop leadership skills through group dynamics, facilitating game sessions, and adapting to dynamic challenges.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Readings

Classes will be one hour per week. Students will be expected to read rulebook modules before coming to class, roughly 10 to 15 pages of reading each week.

Weekly Reflections 

Each week, students will submit a brief reflection consisting of two paragraphs. The reflection should summarize what you learned in class that week and any insights or takeaways you gained from the session (including the readings). Focus on how the material or activities contributed to your understanding of communication, teamwork, or problem-solving in tabletop RPGs. Responses should be no longer than a page.

Final

Create a scenario for a TTRPG that was covered in the class where players must resolve the conflict without combat, using negotiation, empathy, or creative problem-solving. If possible, playtest the scenario with friends and write a short essay on how it went. Did your players manage to deescalate the situation? If so then explain how you as the Dungeon Master made it possible. If not, then explain what you would do differently. If needed, you may also request the instructor to participate in a brief playtest.

If you are unable to form a group for the playtest, you may either:

  1. Write a detailed walkthrough of your scenario, describing possible player choices and outcomes, and reflect on the design process.
  2. Or, analyze one of the games we played in class, focusing on nonviolent encounters and how the game supports deescalation and communication.
GRADING:
P/NP

- 20% grade is attendance & participation. 
- 60% grade will come from participating in each week's activity
- 20% grade from the final project 

Pass will be given if the student has a 70% in the class. Anything below a 70% is a no pass.
INSTRUCTORBIO:
Josh McCoy, Associate Professor at UC Davis in Computer Science and Cinema and Digital Media, is a game developer and researcher exploring the intersection of AI, social science, and design. His work creates socially responsive, meaningful games that engage broad audiences and push the boundaries of interactive technology.


Arunpreet Sandhu is a PhD student in the Social Intelligence and Narrative Game (SING) lab at UC Davis.His focus is on Procedural Content Generation, Character Artificial Intelligence, and Emergent Narrative. He also is a hobbyist of creative writing and is willing to run a one shot whenever and wherever he can.

TITLE: What Are You Going to Do in Your 20s?
FOCUS: The question, What are you going to do after you graduate?, is only slightly less intimidating than, What are you going to do with your life? A better, though still daunting, question to ask yourself in college is this: What are you going to do with your 20s? In this course, we will combine the dreamy, the psychological and the practical. Guided by one primary text, Meg Jay’s The Defining Decade: Why your Twenties matter—and how to make the most of them now (2021), and selections from Richard N. Bolles’s What Color Is Your Parachute? Your Guide to a Lifetime of Meaningful Work and Career Success (2022), we will explore and write reflections on the professional and personal choices you will face in your 20s and discuss how to navigate them. As a bonus, each student will get targeted advice on building their resume and identifying the best next step in their search for meaningful professional experience.
OUTCOMES:
1. Explain some typical professional and personal challenges people face in their twenties, and strategies for success.

2. Demonstrate critical thinking skills in reflecting on the likely professional and personal challenges they will face as individuals.

3. Align personal interest and career goals.

4. Make informed choices about their post-graduation plans.

5. Feel ready to pursue the next step in their professional lives.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Week 1 Reflection on Expectations for the 20s: Personal and Professional Challenges and Goals, Post-Graduation Plans & Readiness: 15% of course grade

One short Reading Reflection per week (30% of course grade)

One turn as Discussion Leader (15% of course grade)

Participation (10% of course grade)

Final Reflection on Expectations for the 20s: Personal and Professional Challenges and Goals, Post-Graduation Plans & Readiness (10% of course grade): 30% of course grade

For the Week 1 Reflection, the rubric focuses on detailed development of ideas. I will compare the Final Reflection to the Week 1 Reflection, the rubric focuses on detailed development of ideas and the impact of the readings and discussion on the student's understanding of personal and professional challenges and goals and post-graduation plans and feelings of readiness.

For the Reading Reflections and Discussion Leaders, the rubrics focus on selection of quotations for discussion, posing generative discussion questions, and (for the latter) effectively co-leading discussion with the instructor.

For Participation, I will expect students to be present and demonstrate through discussion that they have done the required readings.
GRADING: The grade of pass will be awarded to undergraduate students who miss no more than 2 courses and for completing the assignments that would normally receive a grade of C- (70%) or better.
INSTRUCTORBIO: In my junior year in college, I took a job as a writing tutor. Ever since I have taught writing, in one way or another. I earned a B.A. in English with a French minor at Northwestern University, a Ph.D. in English at UC Davis, and an MFA in poetry at Warren Wilson College. For 15 years, I was an English professor at CSU Bakersfield. Since 2017 I’ve taught full-time in the University Writing Program and now in the Writing Center at UC Davis, and I also joined the Performance Studies Graduate Group as a Faculty Affiliate in 2019. In teaching writing, I frequently help students develop job or internship or graduate school applications, and I have come to appreciate the anxieties of making post-college plans when one's professional and future is hard to imagine. Indeed, I remember feeling those anxieties myself, and I look forward to learning more, alongside students, about how to help you navigate these challenges.

TITLE: What in the World Should I Do to Find a Satisfying Career?
FOCUS:
The course is intended for students in EVERY major and discipline at UC Davis and will promote diversity, equity, and inclusiveness in our campus community as a whole. While teaching it in previous quarters, I found the demand for this course was so high that even after exceeding the class limit students from all over the campus keep asking me "when will you teach this class again". Thus this proposal to teach it in Spring 2026.

This internship focused course is based on the instructor’s ten-year experience (2004-2014) as the Faculty Director of the UC Davis Internship and Career Center and an experienced teacher of Freshman seminars and undergraduate courses for over three decades. The course will discuss and describe the vast number and types of internships around the world that will guide and help our undergraduates toward their future professions. The goal will be to steer undecided students in the right direction and accelerate their growth through sharing personal insights and promoting an environment of decision-making and satisfaction in all they choose to do while studying at UC Davis. We will define education and internships as a blend of class work and hands-on learning and make students aware of what experiential training means through service, volunteer work, or part-time work. Decisions about life and the struggles encountered in the working world will be covered in some detail and pave the path towards creating plans that enhance emotional and personal happiness.
OUTCOMES: Students will be introduced to how to approach the world of work via internships,personal networking skills, and writing methods. The educational outcomes will be directed towards developing a global perspective of the workplace. Throughout the course students will learn strong communication and leadership skills and how to present themselves in a responsible and ethical fashion. Both for internships and career opportunities visits to industry/company sites will be integrated into the class schedule --an approach which was highly effective in previous FRS seminars taught by the instructor. Other activities include participation in field trips to potential employers or internship location possibilities (e.g. LLNL, MNRC, or LBL) as arranged by the instructor in previous FRS seminar classes.
ASSIGNMENTS: Weekly oral talks and/or written assignments will be a feature of the course as in all my past FRS seminars.Typically these will require reading of two 6 to 8 page published papers selected by the instructor or articles from popular current newspapers and magazines (like NY Times, TIME, National Geographic etc) requiring an average of 5 to 6 hours of out-of-class reading and review work for each student.  Once every 2 weeks a pre-assigned group of students will make PowerPoint presentations to the whole class to stimulate conversations on current topics like Artificial Intelligence, world conflicts, campus life changes.
GRADING: 50% of grades will be based on class participation and  presentations. The rest of the grade will be based on an well written essay type report (of 5 pages or so) submitted at the end of the quarter on a topic chosen by the student and discussed in class often. Students will meet the instructor in person as needed to seek help on chioce of topic and how to write the essay report well. The ambitious goal will be to submit the essay report for publication in an appropriate journal or a suitable magazine.
INSTRUCTORBIO: As a faculty member at UC Davis since 1990, I wish to share with students in this class what I have  learnt over three decades as a teacher trying to guide hundreds of students towards an internship and then towards a satisfying career choice. My experience as the Director of the UC-Davis Internship and Career Center (ICC) involved numerous student interactions and supervision of diverse staff, peer advisers, and interactions with  humanities and arts, business, counseling, psychology, physical sciences, engineering, and biology. My job covered  Diversity Resources, Liberal Arts and Business, Natural and Physical sciences, Government, and Agriculture. I led the ICC staff to create the new Diversity Leadership Development Program (DLDP) with the Leadership and Learning Center, Cross Cultural Center, Women's Resource and Research Center, LGBT Resource Center, and the Office of Campus Community Relations. The communication skills I learnt in these efforts led me to be sensitive to how one can create a diverse, safe and inclusive environment. As a final example, I saw how ICC staff applied for seed funding from the Target Corporation for the DLDP certificate program and built the collaboration with the the medical school to get a NIEHS grant on pollutants and health.

TITLE: Women in Science
FOCUS:
Women earn roughly half of M.D.s and Ph.D.'s in biological sciences awarded annually, yet are 
progressively underrepresented as they advance through the career pipeline. This discussion-based course will provide a brief overview of the history of women and other underrepresented groups in science and medicine, highlight their contributions to various fields, and examine current career obstacles, allowing students to think critically about intersectionality, privilege, and disparity in the STEMM (science, technology, engineering, mathematics, and medicine) fields.
OUTCOMES: The class structure will include discussion of current events, group presentations, reading primary research papers, and short written assignments. These critical-thinking assignments, along with literature searches and reviews, will culminate in a capstone project in which students will come up with solutions and strategies to help mitigate or overcome the obstacles discussed in the class. Students will also have the opportunity to learn more about STEMM careers through small group interactions with guest lecturers and learn how to create a competitive CV/résumé for success in the next steps of their academic careers.
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignment Points
Historical figures 5min presentation 10
Modern-day figures 5min presentation 10
Media/pop culture (1/2 page written + informal presentation) 10
Current events (1/2 page written + informal presentation) 10
CV/Resume 5
Literature review (5 pages): Modern Challenges 20
Final 10min presentation 20
Participation 15 (based on ~5-10 pages of reading/week)
Total points 100
GRADING:
Percentages required to earn specific letter grades: 100-90 (A+ to A-), 89-80 (B+ to B-), 79-70 (C+ to C-), 69-60 (D+ to D-), <60 (F)

Assignment Points

INSTRUCTORBIO:
Dr. Bauman is a professor in the Department of Physiology and Membrane Biology and a faculty member of the MIND Institute at UC Davis. She has a robust research portfolio and extensive experience leading large-scale interdisciplinary research and team-science approaches to complex biomedical research questions. Her program of research uses preclinical models to evaluate risk factors and develop novel therapeutic interventions for neurodevelopmental disorders. Her current research focuses on understanding how changes in the prenatal environment, in particular the maternal-fetal immune environment, may increase the risk for autism, schizophrenia and other neurodevelopmental disorders. 

In addition to her research interests, Dr. Bauman also has a strong commitment to advancing women's careers in science and medicine. She served as the Director of the UC Davis Health Women in Medicine and Health Sciences (WIMHS) program from 2018-2024 and led efforts to improve gender equity in academic medicine. She has participated in AAMC Early and Mid-Career Women Faculty Leadership Development Seminars and is an alumna of the 2022-2023 class in the national Executive Leadership in Academic Medicine (ELAM) program. Dr. Bauman developed and has also co-taught a first-year seminar on Women in Science for over 15 years.

TITLE: Writing About Scary Movies: Cultural Values and Fears in Recent American and Global Cinema
FOCUS:
This seminar explores the contemporary resurgence and reinvention of the horror film genre, especially in the past decade. We will explore thisrenaissance in artistic horror, paying special attention to how these films draw attention to cultural issues and illicit cultural fears and unease in both American and global contexts. 

The seminar will be a two-hour format, primarily discussion-based. Discussions will involve responses to the film previously screened the week before. The discussion will include personal reactions to the film as well as synthesis with concepts and arguments from the assigned readings.  

Important disclaimers:

  1. No previous experience in cinema and media studies is required. But some of our readings will introduce technical terms about film as well as critical reading methods and theoretical approaches from cultural studies. You will not be evaluated based on your mastery of cinematic vocabulary or cultural theory; however, you will be expected to begin applying some of these formal and cultural methods of reading by synthesizing and integrating them with your own ideas and interpretations of these films. Consider this an extension of critical thinking, reading, and writing practices from your other classes.
  2. Since this is a seminar that has horror as one of its main themes, many of the films that we screen will inevitably include disturbing images, language, and ideas. Likewise, our readings and discussions will also address sensitive and controversial cultural topics. Students should join this seminar only if they have experience watching this type of provocative and challenging cinema.
OUTCOMES:
1. Students will practice critical thinking, reading, and writing skills using different media.
  2. In learning how to critique recent films, students will also learn how to discuss cultural issues and problems that are explicitly and implicitly evoked by these films.
  3. In discussing these contemporary cultural issues, students will cultivate ethical values such as responsibility, respect, and empathy from both American and global perspectives.
  4. Students will be able to synthesize their critical reading and writing with a direct application to historical and contemporary cultural events and issues.
  5. Students will also bring their own knowledge of and personal experiences with film, media, and culture to bear in discussions.
  6. Moving beyond our course topics and themes, students will be able to transfer these critical strategies and communication skills into their future classes at Davis and in their public-facing lives and careers.

Cultural Themes: The seminar will engage with local and global issues, including: gender equality, fluidity, and discrimination; disability studies and ableist rhetoric;  nationalism, racism, and the precarious status of the refugee; ecological change, devastation, and extinction; global pandemics and dis-ease; monstrosity as a representation or projection of fears and insecurities about the unknown/ unnamed “other.”
ASSIGNMENTS:
Assignments:

We will screen and respond to American films from the past ten years, such as: Robert Eggers's “The Witch” (2015), Ari Aster’s “Hereditary” (2018), and Jordan Peele’s “Get Out” (2015); as well as recent international horror cinema, such as: Jenifer Kent's "The Babadook" (Australia 2014), Bong Joon-ho’s “Parasite” (Korea 2019), Goran Stolevski’s “You Won’t Be Alone” (Macedonia/International 2022), and Prano Bailey-Bond’s “Censor” (UK 2021). We will also screen one film chosen by the class by popular vote.

Students will be expected to devote at least four hours a week outside of class: two hours for screening the films and two hours for reading background content and posting responses. Students will be expected to read 10-30 pages per week. These will include cultural interpretations of the films as well as selections on form and genre. Students will be required to submit six short response/discussion board posts/papers throughout the course. For their final project, they will pick a recent horror/suspense film or streaming series (from those screened in class or of their own choice) and write a six-page paper that connects some of the themes and subthemes of that film to at least one contemporary cultural issue.
GRADING:
Discussion Board Posts: 60% (Six 300-word posts, and 200-word response(s) to colleagues)

Participation in Class Discussion, Reading Quizzes: 10% (Mixture of in-class participation and occasional reading quizzes)

Cultural Analysis Paper: 30% (6 pages total)

Grading Scale:

77.4%-72.6% = C  

72.5%-70% = C-

67.4%-62.6% = D

INSTRUCTORBIO: Brendan Johnston is a writing lecturer at the University Writing Center (UWC) where he teaches writing and advanced composition. He received his PhD in English from the University of Nevada, Reno in 2021, and his Masters in English with a Certificate in Teaching Composition from Sacramento State in 2012. He has taught classes in writing, literature, film, and the general humanities for over 15 years. His research interests include the relationship of science and technology to culture, modernism and poetics, contemporary theories of materialism (historical, scientific, and cultural), and cinema and media studies. He has presented his research at the Modernist Studies Association (MSA), the Society for Cinema and Media Studies (SCMS), and The Association for the Study of Literature and the Environment. He enjoys films of all kinds but has a special interest in arthouse horror cinema, both contemporary and historical.
